Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$: \begin{aligned} x_B &= r \cos\theta + L - \frac{r^2}{2L} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} \\ x_B &= r \cos\theta + L - \frac{r^2}{4L} + \frac{r^2}{4L} \cos 2\theta \end{aligned} **Step 2 explanation:** Substituted the half-angle identity to introduce the $\cos 2\theta$ term — this is the origin of the **secondary** term. ### 3.1.4 First Derivative — Velocity of Piston Differentiating $x_B$ with respect to time $t$ (noting $\dot{\theta} = \omega$): \begin{aligned} v_B = \frac{dx_B}{dt} &= -r \sin\theta \cdot \dot{\theta} + \frac{r^2}{4L}(-\sin 2\theta)(2\dot{\theta}) \\ v_B &= -r\omega \sin\theta - \frac{r^2 \omega}{2L} \sin 2\theta \end{aligned} **Step 3 explanation:** Applied chain rule. $\frac{d}{dt}\cos\theta = -\sin\theta \cdot \omega$, and $\frac{d}{dt}\cos 2\theta = -2\sin 2\theta \cdot \omega$. ### 3.1.5 Second Derivative — Acceleration of Piston Differentiating $v_B$ with respect to time: \begin{aligned} a_B = \frac{dv_B}{dt} &= -r \omega \cos\theta \cdot \omega - \frac{r^2 \omega}{2L} \cos 2\theta \cdot 2\omega \\ a_B &= -r \omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L} \cos 2\theta \right] \end{aligned} **Step 4 explanation:** Differentiated again. The negative sign indicates the acceleration is towards the crank center along the negative $x$-direction at typical crank angles. ### 3.1.6 Final Inertia Force Equation The inertia force on the piston is: $$\boxed{F_I = m \cdot a_B = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L} \cos 2\theta \right]}$$ The force is **positive** in the direction away from the crank (i.e., it acts on the piston pushing it away from the crank when accelerating, and pulling it towards the crank when decelerating — but in magnitude form, the formula above is the standard KTU form). ## 3.2 Derivation: Hammer Blow in a Locomotive Driving Wheel ### 3.2.1 Concept A locomotive has two cylinders, typically placed at 90° to each other (or 0° / 180° in some designs). The **primary inertia forces** from the two pistons do not completely cancel — the unbalanced component is transferred to the rails as a periodic vertical force called **Hammer Blow**. ### 3.2.2 Mathematical Derivation Consider a locomotive with two cylinders, the cranks set at 90° to each other. Let both pistons have mass $m$ and crank radius $r$. **Primary inertia force from cylinder 1 (crank at angle $\theta$):** $$F_{I1}^{(1)} = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$$ **Primary inertia force from cylinder 2 (crank at angle $\theta + 90°$):** $$F_{I1}^{(2)} = m r \omega^2 \cos(\theta + 90°) = -m r \omega^2 \sin\theta$$ **Unbalanced primary force (assuming pistons on same side):** $$F_{net} = m r \omega^2 (\cos\theta - \sin\theta)$$ **Maximum value** (when $\cos\theta = \sin\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, or when $\theta = 45°$): $$F_{net,max} = m r \omega^2 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot 2 = \sqrt{2} m r \omega^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Formula (90° cranks, opposite sides of wheels):** > > $$\boxed{F_{Hammer\,Blow} = 2 m r \omega^2}$$ > > This represents the maximum unbalanced vertical force on the rail per revolution. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Crank Angle on Inertia Force | **Crank Angle $\theta$** | **$\cos\theta$** | **$\cos 2\theta$** | **Inertia Force Type** | **Engineering Significance** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0° (IDC) | $+1$ | $+1$ | Max positive (Primary + Secondary) | Piston starts accelerating towards crank | | 90° | $0$ | $-1$ | Negative Secondary | Secondary reverses | | 180° (ODC) | $-1$ | $+1$ | Max negative (Primary dominates) | Piston decelerates, reverses | | 270° | $0$ | $-1$ | Negative Secondary | Piston accelerates back | | 45° | $+0.707$ | $0$ | Primary only zero Secondary | Secondary crosses zero | | 135° | $-0.707$ | $0$ | Primary only | Secondary crosses zero | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners often ask to plot the inertia force diagram. Remember the **secondary** component completes **two full cycles** for every one cycle of the **primary** component. ## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A reciprocating engine has the following data: > - Crank radius $r = 100$ mm > - Connecting rod length $L = 400$ mm > - Piston mass $m = 25$ kg > - Crank speed $N = 600$ rpm > > Find the inertia force on the piston at crank angle $\theta = 30°$ from IDC. ### Solution: **Step 1: Convert crank speed to angular velocity.** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 62.83 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2: Compute the trigonometric terms at $\theta = 30°$.** $$\cos 30° = 0.866, \quad \cos 60° = 0.5$$ **Step 3: Substitute into the inertia force formula.** \begin{aligned} F_I &= m r \omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L} \cos 2\theta \right] \\ F_I &= 25 \times 0.1 \times (62.83)^2 \left[ 0.866 + \frac{0.1}{0.4} \times 0.5 \right] \\ F_I &= 25 \times 0.1 \times 3947.8 \left[ 0.866 + 0.125 \right] \\ F_I &= 9869.5 \times 0.991 \\ \boxed{F_I} &\approx 9780.7 \text{ N} \end{aligned} **Step 4 explanation:** The first factor $m r \omega^2 = 25 \times 0.1 \times 3947.8 = 9869.5$ N is the **base inertia force**. The bracketed term $0.991$ is the **directional multiplier** depending on crank angle. > [!NOTE] > **Verification of typical magnitude:** A 25 kg piston at 600 rpm with 100 mm stroke generates nearly 10 kN of inertia force. This justifies why engines need heavy foundations and balance shafts! # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define inertia force and state D'Alembert's principle. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Model Answer:** **Inertia Force:** Inertia force is a fictitious force that appears to act on a body when its state of motion changes. It is the resistance offered by the mass of a body against any change in its velocity. Mathematically, it is given by: $$F_I = m \cdot a$$ where $m$ is the mass and $a$ is the acceleration. The direction of the inertia force is always **opposite to the direction of acceleration**. **D'Alembert's Principle:** It states that the inertia force acting on a body, when combined with the external applied forces, forms a system that is in **dynamic equilibrium**. Mathematically: $$\sum \vec{F}_{applied} + \vec{F}_{I} = 0$$ This principle allows dynamic problems to be solved using static equilibrium equations. --- ### Question 2: Distinguish between primary and secondary inertia forces in a reciprocating engine. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Primary Inertia Force** | **Secondary Inertia Force** | |---|---|---| | **Mathematical form** | $F_{I1} = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | $F_{I2} = m r \omega^2 (r/L) \cos 2\theta$ | | **Frequency** | Once per revolution ($\omega$) | Twice per revolution ($2\omega$) | | **Magnitude ratio** | Proportional to $r$ | Proportional to $r^2/L$ | | **Balancing** | Easy (single rotating mass) | Difficult (needs Lanchester balancer) | | **Value at $\theta=0$** | Maximum positive | Maximum positive | | **Value at $\theta=90°$** | Zero | Negative maximum | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** State and explain D'Alembert's principle with a suitable example. Derive the expression for the inertia force on the piston of a slider-crank mechanism, neglecting the mass of the connecting rod. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A reciprocating engine has a piston of mass 30 kg, crank radius 150 mm, connecting rod length 600 mm, running at 450 rpm. Determine: (i) the primary and secondary inertia forces, (ii) the maximum inertia force, and (iii) the crank position at which maximum inertia force occurs. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Question A: ### Part (a) — D'Alembert's Principle & Piston Inertia Force Derivation **D'Alembert's Principle Statement:** If a body of mass $m$ experiences an acceleration $a$ under the action of an external force $F$, then Newton's second law gives: $$F = m \cdot a$$ Rearranging: $$F + (-m \cdot a) = 0$$ The term $(-m \cdot a)$ is the inertia force $F_I$. Thus: $$F + F_I = 0$$ This means the external force and the inertia force form a balanced system, which is the statement of D'Alembert's Principle. **Example:** A body of mass $m$ is lifted vertically by a string with upward acceleration $a$. The forces are: - Tension $T$ upward - Weight $W = mg$ downward - Inertia force $F_I = m \cdot a$ downward (opposite to upward acceleration) For equilibrium: $T = W + F_I = mg + ma = m(g+a)$ **Derivation of Piston Inertia Force** (Refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation): The acceleration of the piston in a slider-crank mechanism is: $$a_B = r \omega^2 \left[\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right]$$ Hence, the inertia force on the piston is: $$\boxed{F_I = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left[\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right]}$$ ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given Data:** - Piston mass $m = 30$ kg - Crank radius $r = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m - Connecting rod length $L = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m - Speed $N = 450$ rpm **Step 1: Compute angular velocity.** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 450}{60} = 47.12 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2: Compute base factor $m r \omega^2$.** \begin{aligned} m r \omega^2 &= 30 \times 0.15 \times (47.12)^2 \\ &= 30 \times 0.15 \times 2220.3 \\ &= 9991.35 \text{ N} \approx 9.99 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} **(i) Primary and Secondary Inertia Forces:** $$F_{I1} = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta = 9991.35 \cos\theta \text{ N}$$ $$F_{I2} = m r \omega^2 \left(\frac{r}{L}\right) \cos 2\theta = 9991.35 \times \frac{0.15}{0.6} \cos 2\theta$$ $$F_{I2} = 9991.35 \times 0.25 \times \cos 2\theta = 2497.84 \cos 2\theta \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Maximum Inertia Force:** Maximum occurs when both $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\cos 2\theta = 1$, i.e., at $\theta = 0°$ (IDC). $$F_{I,\max} = 9991.35 \times (1 + 0.25) = 9991.35 \times 1.25$$ $$\boxed{F_{I,\max} = 12489.2 \text{ N} \approx 12.49 \text{ kN}}$$ **(iii) Crank position for maximum:** $\theta = 0°$ (Inner Dead Center) --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Alternative Choice] **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the concept of inertia force and inertia torque in rotating and translating bodies. Derive the expression for hammer blow in a locomotive with two cylinders set at 90°. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The two cylinders of a locomotive are set at 90° to each other. The crank radius is 300 mm, and the mass of each reciprocating part is 250 kg. The wheels have a diameter of 1.8 m. The speed of the locomotive is 80 km/h. Calculate: (i) the primary unbalanced force, (ii) the maximum hammer blow on the rails, and (iii) the variation of crank effort. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Question B: ### Part (a) — Inertia Force/Torque Concepts and Hammer Blow Derivation **Inertia Force in Translating Body:** For a body of mass $m$ moving with linear acceleration $a$, the inertia force is: $$F_I = m \cdot a \quad \text{(opposite to } a\text{)}$$ **Inertia Torque in Rotating Body:** For a body of mass moment of inertia $I$ rotating with angular acceleration $\alpha$: $$T_I = I \cdot \alpha \quad \text{(opposite to } \alpha\text{)}$$ **Hammer Blow Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.2 for the detailed derivation.) For two cylinders at 90°, the maximum hammer blow is: $$F_{Hammer\,Blow} = 2 m r \omega^2$$ ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given Data:** - Crank angle between cylinders $= 90°$ - Crank radius $r = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m - Reciprocating mass per cylinder $m = 250$ kg - Wheel diameter $D = 1.8$ m $\Rightarrow$ radius $R = 0.9$ m - Locomotive speed $V = 80$ km/h **Step 1: Find wheel speed and angular velocity.** \begin{aligned} V &= 80 \text{ km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \text{ m/s} \\ \omega &= \frac{V}{R} = \frac{22.22}{0.9} = 24.69 \text{ rad/s} \end{aligned} **Step 2: Primary Unbalanced Force (per cylinder).** $$F_{I1} = m r \omega^2 = 250 \times 0.3 \times (24.69)^2$$ $$F_{I1} = 75 \times 609.6 = 45720 \text{ N} \approx 45.72 \text{ kN}$$ **(i) Primary Unbalanced Force from one cylinder** = **45.72 kN** (varies sinusoidally with $\cos\theta$). **Step 3: Maximum Hammer Blow on Rails.** For two cylinders at 90° (assuming they act on the same side of the wheel): $$F_{Hammer\,Blow} = 2 m r \omega^2 = 2 \times 45720 = 91440 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_{Hammer\,Blow} \approx 91.44 \text{ kN}}$$ **(iii) Crank Effort Variation:** The crank effort $F_T$ is the tangential component of force at the crank pin: $$F_T = F_I \cdot \tan\phi \approx F_I \cdot \frac{r \sin\theta}{L}$$ But for analysis of variation, the net unbalanced vertical force on the rail per revolution varies from **$0$ to $2 m r \omega^2$** depending on the crank angle. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Unit conversion errors**: Forgetting to convert $r$ and $L$ from mm to m is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in KTU numericals. Always convert at the start. > > 2. **Confusing $\omega$ and $N$**: Mixing up rad/s with rpm will lead to a 60× error. Always use $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. > > 3. **Sign of inertia force**: KTU expects the magnitude of $F_I$ but the **direction is opposite to acceleration**. Mentioning this explicitly earns full credit. > > 4. **Neglecting connecting rod mass**: In Part (a) derivations, clearly state "neglecting the mass of the connecting rod" so the approximate formula is justified. > > 5. **Hammer blow formula variations**: Some textbooks use $F_{HB} = \sqrt{2} m r \omega^2$ for specific configurations. Always state the configuration (crank angle, cylinder side) before writing the formula. > > 6. **Mass vs Weight**: Never use weight $W$ in inertia force formulas. Convert $W$ to mass using $m = W/g$ first. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - ✅ **Inertia force** is a **fictitious (pseudo) force** — it is not a real applied force but the resistance of mass against acceleration. - ✅ **Direction:** Always **opposite** to the direction of acceleration of the center of mass. - ✅ **Magnitude:** $F_I = m \cdot a$ (linear) or $T_I = I \cdot \alpha$ (rotational). - ✅ **D'Alembert's Principle:** Converts dynamic problems into equivalent static equilibrium problems — foundation of all inertia force analysis. - ✅ **Piston Inertia Force (approximate):** $F_I = m r \omega^2 [\cos\theta + (r/L)\cos 2\theta]$ — memorize this formula completely. - ✅ **Primary Inertia Force:** Frequency $= \omega$ (once per revolution). Can be balanced by a single rotating counter-mass. - ✅ **Secondary Inertia Force:** Frequency $= 2\omega$ (twice per revolution). Requires Lanchester balancer or two counter-rotating masses. - ✅ **Centrifugal Force on rotating mass:** $F_c = m r \omega^2$ — always directed outward radially. - ✅ **Hammer Blow:** Maximum unbalanced vertical force on locomotive rails; for 90° cranks, $F_{HB} = 2 m r \omega^2$. - ✅ **Mass conversion:** $m = W/g$ where $g = 9.81$ m/s². - ✅ **Units check:** $F_I$ in N, $T_I$ in N·m, $m$ in kg, $a$ in m/s², $I$ in kg·m², $\omega$ in rad/s. - ✅ **Use the small-angle approximation** $\cos\phi \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}\sin^2\phi$ to derive the piston acceleration formula. - ✅ **KTU loves to test** the separation into **primary + secondary** and asks "which is harder to balance and why?" > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When solving a numerical, always start by listing given data with **units converted to SI** (kg, m, rad/s, N, N·m). This single habit can save 2–3 marks per question in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Inertia Torque
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inertia Torque

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inertia Torque** is the resisting (or driving) torque produced by a rotating mass due to its property of inertia, which opposes any change in its angular velocity. Mathematically, it is the product of the **mass moment of inertia** of the body about the axis of rotation and its **angular acceleration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Definition (Rigid Body Dynamics):**  
> Inertia Torque, $T_I$, is defined as the additional torque, over and above the externally applied torque, that must be supplied (or absorbed) to change the rotational state of a rigid body. It is given by:
>
> $$T_I = I \cdot \alpha$$
>
> where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia in **kg·m²** and $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration in **rad/s²**.

In the context of **slider-crank kinematics** (a major Module 1 topic for KTU), inertia torque specifically refers to the torque required by the crank to accelerate/decelerate the **equivalent reciprocating mass** of the piston, connecting rod, and other translating components.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a child on a **merry-go-round** at a playground.

- When the merry-go-round is **stationary**, you need a small push to start it rotating — that push overcomes the *static friction* and the inherent resistance of the wheel to start moving. This resistance is **inertia**.
- Once it is spinning at a **steady speed**, you barely need to push — only friction needs to be overcome.
- To **speed it up** (accelerate it), you must push harder. That extra effort is precisely the **inertia torque** you are supplying.
- To **stop** a spinning merry-go-round, the child has to drag their feet or grab the pole. That braking action absorbs the inertia torque.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Plain-English Intuition:**  
> Inertia torque is the "hidden" extra effort a machine's motor must deliver every time a rotating part speeds up or slows down. It does no useful work on the output shaft — it merely changes the kinetic energy stored in the moving masses.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Range (Engines) |
|----------|:------:|:-------:|:-----------------------:|
| Mass moment of inertia | $I$ | **kg·m²** | 0.01 – 50 |
| Angular acceleration | $\alpha$ | **rad/s²** | 10 – 1000 |
| Inertia torque | $T_I$ | **N·m** | 5 – 500 |
| Reciprocating mass | $m$ | **kg** | 0.5 – 10 |
| Piston acceleration | $a$ | **m/s²** | 100 – 5000 |

---

### 1.4 The Three Classes of Masses in a Mechanism

In KTU's standard treatment of **Engine Dynamics** (Module 1), every link of a mechanism is classified into one of three categories based on its motion:

1. **Stationary Masses** — Mounted on the engine frame (no motion, no inertia effects).
2. **Rotating Masses** — Crank, flywheel, belt pulleys, etc. (pure rotation about a fixed axis).
3. **Reciprocating Masses** — Piston, wrist pin, part of connecting rod (pure translation along cylinder axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Engine Dynamics):**  
> KTU examiners expect you to clearly distinguish between inertia effects from **rotating** masses (inertia *torque*) and **reciprocating** masses (inertia *force*). The connecting rod is a hybrid — part reciprocating, part rotating — and must be **dynamically split** into two equivalent masses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Inertia Torque Exists — The Physics

Every body possesses a fundamental property called **inertia** — the resistance to change in its state of motion. For translation, this is quantified by **mass** ($m$); for rotation, by the **mass moment of inertia** ($I$).

When a torque is applied to a shaft carrying a rotating mass:
- If the shaft is already rotating at constant speed → all torque does **useful work** (overcoming friction, doing output work).
- If the shaft is **accelerating** → part of the torque is consumed to **increase the rotational kinetic energy** of the mass. That consumed part is the **inertia torque**.

The energy stored in a rotating mass is:

$$\text{KE}_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

Differentiating w.r.t. time gives the power required to change this energy:

$$P = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2\right) = I \omega \alpha = T_I \cdot \omega$$

which is why $T_I = I\alpha$.

---

### 2.2 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identify the rotating mass and its axis of rotation.**
Locate the body's centre of mass and the axis about which it rotates. Compute the mass moment of inertia $I$ about that axis.

**Step 2 — Determine the angular acceleration $\alpha$.**
For a crank rotating with $\theta = \omega t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$, take the second time-derivative.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law for Rotation.**
$$\sum T = I \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Separate useful torque from inertia torque.**
$$T_{\text{applied}} = T_{\text{useful}} + T_{\text{inertia}} = T_{\text{useful}} + I\alpha$$

**Step 5 — For variable-speed engines, integrate over a cycle to get the mean and fluctuation of energy.**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight (Why It Matters in Design):**  
> The flywheel in an engine is sized precisely to *store* the excess kinetic energy during high-inertia-torque strokes (power stroke) and *release* it during low-torque strokes (idle stroke). Without the concept of inertia torque, flywheel design would be impossible.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:-----:|:------|
| 1 | Newton's II Law (Rotation) | $T = I\alpha$ | N·m | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Inertia Torque (Pure Rotation) | $T_I = I\alpha$ | N·m | Opposes $\Delta\omega$ |
| 3 | Inertia Force (Pure Translation) | $F_I = m \cdot a$ | N | $a$ is linear acceleration |
| 4 | Inertia Torque from Force | $T_I = F_I \cdot r$ | N·m | $r$ = moment arm |
| 5 | Rotational KE | $\text{KE} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ | J | Energy stored |
| 6 | Angular Acceleration | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$ | rad/s² | Time rate of change of $\omega$ |
| 7 | Piston Acceleration (Slider-Crank) | $a_B = r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | m/s² | $\theta$ = crank angle from IDC |
| 8 | Connecting Rod Mass Equivalence | $m_{\text{rec}} = m \cdot \dfrac{L_1}{L}$, $m_{\text{rot}} = m \cdot \dfrac{L_2}{L}$ | kg | $L_1$ = CG to piston pin |
| 9 | Equivalent System Inertia (Single Crank) | $I_{\text{eq}} = I_{\text{crank}} + m_{\text{rot}}r^2$ | kg·m² | $r$ = crank radius |
| 10 | Inertia Torque of Piston (≈) | $T_{I,B} = m_{\text{rec}} \cdot a_B \cdot r\!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)\Big/ \text{denom}$ | N·m | Exact form from virtual work |

> [!WARNING]  
> **LaTeX Pipeline Note:**  
> All absolute-value bars and norm symbols in the above table have been written using $\vert$ and $\mid$ — never the literal pipe character `\`|\`` — to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inertia torque is the **backbone of every rotating-machine design** discipline:

- **Automotive Engineering** — Sizing the **flywheel** of an IC engine so that the rotational kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ buffers the cyclic torque fluctuations from power strokes.
- **Robotics** — Selecting servo motors whose peak torque exceeds the inertia torque of the payload at the desired angular acceleration.
- **Turbomachinery** — Analysing **ramping** of turbines during startup to avoid torsional shaft failures.
- **CNC & Machine Tools** — Calculating the torque needed to accelerate spindle + tool + workpiece, which determines the feed-rate and acceleration limits.
- **Electric Vehicles** — Matching motor torque curves to the inertia torque demands during 0–100 km/h acceleration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inertia Torque of a Pure Rotating Mass

**Starting Premise (Newton's Second Law for a particle):**
A particle of mass $m$ moving in a circle of radius $r$ experiences a tangential force $F_t$ that produces tangential acceleration $a_t = r\alpha$.

$$F_t = m \cdot a_t = m \cdot r\alpha$$

The torque about the centre of rotation due to this force is:

$$T = F_t \cdot r = (m \cdot r\alpha) \cdot r = m r^2 \alpha$$

For a system of $n$ particles, sum the contributions:

$$T = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2\right) \alpha$$

By definition, the bracketed term is the **mass moment of inertia** of the body about the axis of rotation:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2 \quad \text{[kg·m}^2\text{]}$$

Therefore the net torque required is:

$$\boxed{T_I = I \alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Interpretation:**  
> The same derivation can be obtained by differentiating rotational kinetic energy:
>
> $$P = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2\right) = I\omega\frac{d\omega}{dt} = I\omega\alpha$$
>
> and since $P = T_I \cdot \omega$, dividing by $\omega$ (non-zero) gives $T_I = I\alpha$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inertia Torque from a Reciprocating Mass (Virtual Work Method)

Consider a slider-crank mechanism. Let:
- $m$ = total reciprocating mass (piston + $\frac{L_1}{L}$ of connecting rod)
- $r$ = crank radius, $L$ = connecting rod length
- $\theta$ = crank angle measured from the inner dead centre (IDC)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the crank (assumed constant for the *kinematic* part)

**Step 1 — Position of the piston B from the crank centre A:**

The piston displacement is the classic slider-crank relation:

$$x_B = r\cos\theta + L\cos\phi$$

where $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{L}\sin\theta$.

**Step 2 — Expand $\cos\phi$ using the binomial approximation (standard for $r/L \le 1/4$):**

$$\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2\sin^2\theta} \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2\sin^2\theta$$

Substituting back:

$$x_B \approx r\cos\theta + L\left[1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2\sin^2\theta\right] = r\cos\theta + L - \frac{r^2}{2L}\sin^2\theta$$

Using $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$x_B = r\cos\theta - \frac{r^2}{4L}(1 - \cos 2\theta) + \text{const}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate twice to get piston acceleration $a_B$:**

$$v_B = \dot{x}_B = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2}{2L}\omega\sin 2\theta$$

$$\boxed{a_B = \frac{d^2 x_B}{dt^2} = -r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)}$$

The negative sign indicates acceleration towards IDC when both cosines are positive. KTU convention often drops the sign and takes **magnitude**:

$$a_B = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

**Step 4 — Inertia force on the piston:**

$$F_I = m \cdot a_B = m \, r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert inertia force to inertia torque at the crank using virtual work:**

The piston moves $dx_B$ when the crank rotates $d\theta$. Equate the work done by the inertia torque on the crank to the work done by the inertia force on the piston:

$$T_I \, d\theta = F_I \, dx_B$$

Using $dx_B = v_B \, dt$ and $d\theta = \omega \, dt$:

$$T_I = F_I \cdot \frac{v_B}{\omega} = F_I \cdot \frac{1}{\omega}\left(-r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2}{2L}\omega\sin 2\theta\right)$$

Simplifying:

$$T_I = F_I \cdot r\left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$$

Substituting $F_I$:

$$\boxed{T_I = m \, r^2 \omega^2\left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)}$$

For engines with $r/L \ll 1$, the leading-order term is:

$$T_I \approx m \, r^2 \omega^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta = \frac{1}{2} m \, r^2 \omega^2 \sin 2\theta$$

This is the **approximate inertia torque** KTU textbooks use for hand-calculations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Mass Moment of Inertia of a Rotating System Referred to a Reference Shaft

Suppose a shaft A carries $n$ different rotating masses with moments of inertia $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n$ and angular velocities $\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_n$. We want to find the equivalent moment of inertia $I_{\text{eq}}$ about a reference shaft that spins at $\omega_{\text{ref}}$ such that the **total kinetic energy is conserved**.

Equate the kinetic energies:

$$\frac{1}{2} I_{\text{eq}} \omega_{\text{ref}}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2} I_i \omega_i^2$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{I_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \left(\frac{\omega_i}{\omega_{\text{ref}}}\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Crucial Application:**  
> This is the formula used to refer the inertia of a gear train, belt-pulley system, or cam-follower assembly back to the motor shaft. It is the rotational analogue of "equivalent mass" in a kinematic chain.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalence of Reciprocating and Rotating Masses

A reciprocating mass $m_{\text{rec}}$ has translational KE $\frac{1}{2}m_{\text{rec}} v_B^2$. To represent it as an equivalent rotating mass $m_{\text{rot}}$ at crank radius $r$:

$$\frac{1}{2} m_{\text{rot}} v_{\text{crank-pitch}}^2 = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{rec}} v_B^2$$

where $v_{\text{crank-pitch}} = r\omega$. The piston velocity to first order is $v_B \approx r\omega\sin\theta$, so at the worst case $\sin\theta = 1$:

$$m_{\text{rot,eq}} = m_{\text{rec}}\left(\frac{v_B}{r\omega}\right)^2 = m_{\text{rec}}\sin^2\theta \le m_{\text{rec}}$$

KTU standard practice uses the **mean** value $\langle\sin^2\theta\rangle = 1/2$, giving:

$$m_{\text{rot,eq}} = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{rec}}$$

referred to the crank pin, this gives an **equivalent moment of inertia**:

$$I_{\text{eq,rot}} = m_{\text{rot,eq}} \cdot r^2 = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{rec}} r^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Note:**  
> This conversion is essential when computing the **total turning moment** of a multi-cylinder engine where some masses reciprocate and some rotate. The total inertia torque is the sum of contributions from both.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A solid disc of mass $M = 12$ kg and radius $R = 0.25$ m is mounted on a shaft. The shaft accelerates uniformly from rest to $1500$ rpm in $8$ s. Find:
1. The mass moment of inertia of the disc.
2. The angular acceleration.
3. The inertia torque required.

**Solution:**

**Part (a)** — Mass moment of inertia of a solid disc about its central axis:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} M R^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times (0.25)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times 0.0625 = 0.375 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Part (b)** — Convert $1500$ rpm to rad/s:

$$\omega_f = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Uniform acceleration from rest, so $\omega_0 = 0$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\omega_f - \omega_0}{t} = \frac{157.08 - 0}{8} \approx 19.635 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Part (c)** — Inertia torque:

$$T_I = I\alpha = 0.375 \times 19.635 \approx 7.36 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Check units:** $\text{kg·m}^2 \times \text{rad/s}^2 = \text{kg·m}^2/\text{s}^2 = \text{N·m}$ ✓ (dimensionless rad does not affect units)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Inertia Torque in a Slider-Crank Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Torque from Crankshaft] --> B[Net Torque Balance Equation]
    B --> C{Component Identification}
    C --> D[Stationary Masses: No Inertia Contribution]
    C --> E[Rotating Masses: Crank, Flywheel, Pulleys]
    C --> F[Reciprocating Masses: Piston, Wrist Pin, Part of Con Rod]
    E --> E1[Compute I_eq = sum of I_i at crank axis]
    E1 --> E2[Torque_I_rot = I_eq x alpha]
    F --> F1[Compute piston acceleration a_B from kinematics]
    F1 --> F2[Inertia force F_I = m_rec x a_B]
    F2 --> F3[Virtual work: T_I_rec = F_I x dx_B/dtheta]
    E2 --> G[Net Inertia Torque on Crank]
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[T_net = T_useful + T_I_rot + T_I_rec]
    H --> I[Flywheel Sizing: I_flywheel from energy fluctuation]
    I --> J[Final Design Output]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **How to read this diagram:** The external torque from the combustion / motor is split by the **Net Torque Balance Equation** into the three classical categories. Each mass type contributes a separate inertia term (rotating mass → torque directly; reciprocating mass → force first, then torque via virtual work). These sum into the total inertia torque, which combined with useful work torque drives the **flywheel sizing** decision.

---

### 4.2 Inertia-Torque Generation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KIN[KINEMATIC INPUT]
        K1[Crank radius r]
        K2[Con rod length L]
        K3[Crank angle theta]
        K4[Crank omega constant]
    end
    subgraph DYN[DYNAMIC COMPUTATION]
        D1[Piston acceleration a_B = r w^2 cos theta + r w^2 r over L cos 2theta]
        D2[Inertia force F_I = m a_B]
        D3[Inertia torque T_I = F_I times r sin theta + r over 2L sin 2theta]
    end
    subgraph OUT[ENGINEERING OUTPUT]
        O1[Torque vs Crank Angle Curve]
        O2[Mean Torque and Fluctuation]
        O3[Flywheel Mass Moment of Inertia]
    end
    K1 --> D1
    K2 --> D1
    K3 --> D1
    K4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Mapping (Module 1):**  
> This block flow directly corresponds to the KTU Module 1 learning outcomes — deriving inertia torque from kinematics, plotting the turning-moment diagram, and using it to size the flywheel.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define the term **"Inertia Torque"** in the context of a slider-crank mechanism. State the expression for the inertia torque of a reciprocating mass in terms of crank angle.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Inertia torque is the additional torque required by the crank to **accelerate or decelerate** the reciprocating mass of the piston-connection-rod assembly due to its property of inertia. It opposes the change in angular velocity of the crank. **(1 mark)**

For a reciprocating mass $m$ at crank angle $\theta$:

$$T_I = m \, r^2 \omega^2 \left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

**(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation of how it is obtained via virtual work / piston acceleration.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Model]**

Distinguish between the **inertia force** of a reciprocating mass and the **inertia torque** of a rotating mass. Give one example of each from an IC engine.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Inertia Force | Inertia Torque |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Acts on | Translating mass | Rotating mass |
| Formula | $F_I = m \cdot a$ | $T_I = I \cdot \alpha$ |
| Nature | Linear resistance | Rotational resistance |
| Engine example | Piston acceleration reaction | Crank-shaft acceleration reaction |

**(1 mark for the distinction, 1 mark each for the two examples.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1 Full Question]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **inertia torque** of the reciprocating parts of a slider-crank mechanism, assuming the crank rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega$. Clearly state the simplifying assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal slider-crank mechanism has the following data: crank radius $r = 100$ mm, connecting-rod length $L = 400$ mm, crank speed $N = 1500$ rpm, and reciprocating mass $m = 1.5$ kg. Find the **inertia torque** at crank angles $\theta = 30°$ and $\theta = 90°$ from the inner dead centre. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The key steps to be shown on the answer script are:

1. Slider position: $x_B = r\cos\theta + L\cos\phi$ **(1 mark)**
2. Using $\sin\phi = (r/L)\sin\theta$ and binomial expansion of $\cos\phi$: 
   $x_B \approx r\cos\theta + L - \dfrac{r^2}{2L}\sin^2\theta$ **(1 mark)**
3. Apply double-angle identity and differentiate twice:
   $a_B = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$ **(1 mark)**
4. Inertia force: $F_I = m \, a_B$ **(0.5 mark)**
5. Virtual-work conversion: $T_I = F_I \cdot \dfrac{dx_B}{d\theta} = F_I \cdot r\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)$ **(2 marks)**
6. Final boxed expression and statement of assumptions (uniform $\omega$, $r/L \ll 1$, rigid links, no friction): **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.4$ m, $N = 1500$ rpm, $m = 1.5$ kg.

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r^2\omega^2$:**

$$r^2 \omega^2 = (0.1)^2 \times (50\pi)^2 = 0.01 \times 2500\pi^2 = 25\pi^2 \approx 246.74 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Inertia torque formula:**

$$T_I = m \cdot r^2 \omega^2 \left(\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta\right)\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$

With $r/L = 0.1/0.4 = 0.25$, so $r/(2L) = 0.125$.

**Case 1 — $\theta = 30°$:**

$$\sin 30° = 0.5, \quad \cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$
$$\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866, \quad \cos 60° = 0.5$$

First bracket: $\sin 30° + 0.125 \sin 60° = 0.5 + 0.125(0.866) = 0.5 + 0.1083 = 0.6083$

Second bracket: $\cos 30° + 0.25 \cos 60° = 0.866 + 0.25(0.5) = 0.866 + 0.125 = 0.991$

$$T_I = 1.5 \times 246.74 \times 0.6083 \times 0.991 \approx 223.16 \text{ N·m}$$

**Case 2 — $\theta = 90°$:**

$$\sin 90° = 1, \quad \cos 90° = 0$$
$$\sin 180° = 0, \quad \cos 180° = -1$$

First bracket: $\sin 90° + 0.125 \sin 180° = 1 + 0 = 1$

Second bracket: $\cos 90° + 0.25 \cos 180° = 0 + 0.25(-1) = -0.25$

$$T_I = 1.5 \times 246.74 \times 1 \times (-0.25) \approx -92.53 \text{ N·m}$$

**Interpretation:** At $\theta = 30°$ the inertia torque is **+223.16 N·m** (assisting the crank — piston is being decelerated as it approaches mid-stroke). At $\theta = 90°$ the torque is **−92.53 N·m** (opposing the crank). The sign convention must be **clearly stated** on the answer script. **(2 marks for interpretation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 1 Full Question]**

**(a)** What is the **principle of dynamic equivalence** as applied to an IC engine? Using this principle, derive the expression for the **equivalent mass moment of inertia** of the rotating masses referred to the crank axis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The turning moment diagram of a four-stroke single-cylinder engine has a maximum fluctuation of energy of $4000$ N·m per revolution. The mean speed is $200$ rpm with a permitted speed fluctuation of $\pm 2\%$. Determine the **mass moment of inertia of the flywheel**. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Dynamic Equivalence Principle (7 marks):**

The **principle of dynamic equivalence** states that a system of masses (some rotating, some reciprocating) can be replaced by an **equivalent system** of masses (all rotating about the crank axis or all reciprocating along the cylinder axis) such that the **kinetic energy** and hence the **dynamic forces/torques** of the two systems are identical at every instant. **(2 marks for definition)**

**Derivation:** Let there be $n$ rotating masses with $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n$ and angular velocities $\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_n$. The total rotational KE is:

$$\text{KE}_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \omega_i^2$$

Let $I_{\text{eq}}$ be the equivalent inertia at a reference angular velocity $\omega_r$ such that:

$$\frac{1}{2} I_{\text{eq}} \omega_r^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \omega_i^2$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{I_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \left(\frac{\omega_i}{\omega_r}\right)^2}$$

**(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for explaining the use of this in flywheel design and engine balancing.)**

**Part (b) — Flywheel Sizing (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Maximum fluctuation of energy: $\Delta E = 4000$ N·m = 4000 J (per revolution)
- Mean speed: $N = 200$ rpm
- Permitted speed fluctuation: $C_s = \pm 2\%$, so $C_s = 0.02$ (total range 4% of mean)

**Step 1 — Mean angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = \frac{20\pi}{3} \approx 20.94 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient of fluctuation of speed $C_s$:**

The energy fluctuation formula is:

$$\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I$:**

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{\omega^2 C_s} = \frac{4000}{(20.94)^2 \times 0.02} = \frac{4000}{438.5 \times 0.02} = \frac{4000}{8.77} \approx 456.1 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{flywheel}} \approx 456.1 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Note on Units of $\Delta E$:**  
> In KTU question papers, $\Delta E$ is often quoted in **N·m per revolution**. Since $1$ N·m = $1$ J, and the energy is added or subtracted **once per revolution** for a single-cylinder engine, the value in joules equals the numerical value in N·m. Be sure to verify this from the problem statement.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Inertia-Torque Problems:**  
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Failing to state and use a consistent sign convention (e.g., "positive torque is in the direction of crank rotation") leads to wrong signs in the final answer. Always state the convention first.  
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Square $r$ (–1 mark):** The most common algebraic slip. In $T_I = m r^2 \omega^2 (\cdots)$, the $r$ appears *squared*. Students often write $m r$ or $m r^3$.  
>
> 3. **Mixing Degrees and Radians (–1 to –3 marks):** When computing $\omega$ from rpm, ensure you convert to rad/s **before** using it in $\alpha$ or $T_I$. KTU examiners specifically check this.  
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Approximation Assumption (–1 mark):** The $\sin 2\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta$ terms come from the **binomial expansion** valid for $r/L \le 1/4$. If the given data violates this, the exact trigonometric form must be used. Always state the assumption.  
>
> 5. **Omitting the Connecting-Rod CG Position:** The connecting rod is **split** as $m_{\text{rec}} = m \cdot L_1/L$ and $m_{\text{rot}} = m \cdot L_2/L$. Omitting this split costs 1–2 marks.  
>
> 6. **Confusing "Mean Speed" with "Instantaneous Speed":** In flywheel problems, the energy stored uses the **mean** $\omega$, not the instantaneous one.  

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inertia torque is $T_I = I\alpha$ for pure rotation, and is derived via virtual work from the inertia force $F_I = m a_B$ for reciprocating parts.** *(Memorize both forms.)*  
- The **exact** inertia-torque expression contains $\sin 2\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta$ terms due to the connecting-rod obliquity.  
- The **approximate** expression, valid for $r/L \le 1/4$, reduces to $T_I \approx \frac{1}{2} m r^2 \omega^2 \sin 2\theta$.  
- Always **convert crank speed** from rpm to rad/s before plugging into torque equations.  
- The **principle of dynamic equivalence** allows lumping rotating and reciprocating masses into a single equivalent moment of inertia about the crank axis: $I_{\text{eq}} = \sum I_i (\omega_i/\omega_r)^2$.  
- Inertia torque is the **driving force** behind **flywheel design** — the flywheel stores the energy $\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ to smooth out the cyclic torque variation.  
- The connecting rod is a **hybrid** mass — split it as $m_{\text{rec}}$ (translating with piston) and $m_{\text{rot}}$ (rotating with crank) using the CG distances $L_1$ and $L_2$.  
- Sign convention: a **positive** inertia torque means it **assists** crank rotation; **negative** means it **opposes**.  
- The maximum inertia torque occurs near **IDC and ODC** (where $\cos\theta$ is maximum); the torque is zero at **90° and 270°** of crank angle.  
- Units check: $[I][\alpha] = (\text{kg·m}^2)(\text{rad/s}^2) = \text{N·m}$ — radian is dimensionless.  
- KTU Module 1 questions on this topic typically test (i) definition, (ii) derivation, (iii) numerical evaluation at specific crank angles, and (iv) flywheel sizing. Be prepared for any combination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Equivalent System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Equivalent System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **Dynamic Equivalent System** is an idealized, reduced-order mechanical model in which a complex multi-body mechanism (such as a multi-cylinder reciprocating engine, a planetary gear train, or a cam-driven slider mechanism) is mathematically condensed into a simpler kinematic equivalent — typically a single rotating mass with an **equivalent moment of inertia ($I_e$)** and a single translating mass with an **equivalent mass ($m_e$)** — such that the **kinetic energy** (or in some formulations, the **power**) of the original system at every instant is preserved in the equivalent system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *"A dynamic equivalent system is one in which the masses and forces/moments of the actual mechanism are replaced by equivalent masses concentrated at convenient reference points and equivalent forces/torques, such that the dynamic behavior (kinetic energy + power flow) of the actual system is faithfully reproduced by the simplified model."* The two pillars of dynamic equivalence are: | Equivalence Type | Preserved Quantity | Reference Frame | |---|---|---| | **Energy Equivalence (K.E.)** | Total kinetic energy $T$ | Inertial (fixed) frame | | **Power Equivalence** | Instantaneous power $P = \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{v}$ | Inertial (fixed) frame | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🎯 The "Bus Passengers Walking" Analogy** > > Imagine a long-distance bus with 47 passengers, each walking down the aisle at slightly different speeds toward the exit door. To the ticket checker at the door, this looks chaotic — 47 people arriving at different times. But to a **planner**, all that matters is the **total energy arriving per second** at the door. > > So, the planner invents a fictitious "**super-passenger**" — a single imaginary walker who carries the **combined kinetic energy** of all 47 passengers. Now the planner only needs to study **one** person instead of 47. > > That super-passenger is the **Dynamic Equivalent System**. The real bus is the actual multi-body mechanism. We trade realism for **solvability** — and gain enormous analytical power in return. > > In a reciprocating engine, the **connecting rod** is precisely this kind of reduction: an awkward, swinging, two-force member is replaced by a fictitious mass that reproduces its kinetic energy at the **crank pin** and the **crosshead (slider)**. ## 1.3 Why This Concept Is Central to KTU Board Examinations - It is the **gateway** to analyzing inertia forces, crank effort, and turning moment. - It enables the use of a **single equivalent rotating mass** to compute fluctuation of speed and energy in flywheel design (Module 2). - The **dynamic force** transmitted to the crankshaft bearings depends entirely on this equivalent system. --- > [!TIP] > **Pre-Requisite Knowledge Check (KTU Foundation):** > Before proceeding, ensure you are fluent with: > (i) Slider-crank kinematics ($r$, $l$, $\theta$, $n = l/r$), > (ii) Approximate inertia force of reciprocating parts ($m \omega^2 r (\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n})$), > (iii) Principle of virtual work / energy conservation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Two-Step Reduction Process A real mechanism is replaced by a dynamic equivalent system in **two logical steps**: ### Step 1 — Identify Reference Points The mechanism has parts that either **rotate** (crank, gears, flywheel) or **oscillate/translate** (piston, slider, connecting rod's CG). We pick: - A **reference rotating point** (typically the **crank pin** or the **crankshaft axis**). - A **reference sliding point** (typically the **piston pin / wrist pin**). ### Step 2 — Concentrate Inertia - All rotating masses are mathematically "moved" to the reference rotation radius using the **kinetic energy equivalence theorem**. - All reciprocating/translating masses are mathematically "moved" to the reference slider location using the same theorem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Principle (Verbatim Theorem):** > *"If a mass $m_i$ moving with velocity $v_i$ is to be replaced by an equivalent mass $m_e$ moving with velocity $v_e$, then equivalence requires that the kinetic energies be equal:"* > > $$\frac{1}{2} m_e v_e^2 \;=\; \sum_i \frac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2$$ This single equation, applied independently to the **rotational** and **translational** subsystems, yields all the $I_e$ and $m_e$ formulas used in KTU board papers. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Equivalent mass at slider (KE basis) | $m_e = m_r + m_{c,eq}$ | kg | $m_r$ = actual reciprocating mass; $m_{c,eq}$ = conn-rod equiv. mass | | 2 | Equivalent rotating mass at crank | $I_e = I_c + m_{c,eq}\, r^2$ | kg·m² | $I_c$ = crank moment of inertia about its axis | | 3 | Conn-rod equivalent mass (translation part) | $m_{c1} = m_c \dfrac{l_1}{l}$ | kg | Concentrated at slider (gudgeon pin) | | 4 | Conn-rod equivalent mass (rotation part) | $m_{c2} = m_c \dfrac{l_2}{l}$ | kg | Concentrated at crank pin | | 5 | Fractional lengths (conn-rod CG) | $l_1 + l_2 = l$ | m | $l_1$ = CG to big-end; $l_2$ = CG to small-end | | 6 | Equivalent mass at crank pin (combining) | $m_{cp} = m_{c2} + \dfrac{m_{c1}}{n^2}$ … (sometimes) | kg | For mass transfer along slider-crank | | 7 | Inertia force of reciprocating parts | $F_I = m_e \, r\,\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | N | **Most-tested** KTU equation | | 8 | Inertia torque (about crank axis) | $T_I = F_I \times r\!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)\Big/\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ … or $T_I = I_e \alpha$ | N·m | Used for crank-shaft torque analysis | | 9 | Angular velocity equivalence | $\omega_e = \omega$ (same shaft) | rad/s | Crank angular velocity is preserved | | 10 | Velocity of slider | $v_s = r\omega\!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ | m/s | Standard kinematic result | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Marking Trap:** Many students write $m_e = m_c$ (treating the entire connecting rod as slider mass). The correct treatment splits the rod into **two** equivalent masses ($m_{c1}$ at slider, $m_{c2}$ at crank pin). Examiners award **2 marks** specifically for this split — losing it costs a grade boundary. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Dynamic Equivalent System | |---|---| | **Internal Combustion Engines** | Computing crank effort, turning moment, flywheel sizing | | **Reciprocating Pumps/Compressors** | Predicting unbalanced inertia forces → foundation vibration analysis | | **Robotics & Mechanism Design** | Model-order reduction for control synthesis | | **Rotating Machinery (Turbines)** | Lumped-parameter modeling of blades + shaft as one equivalent rotor | | **Vehicle Dynamics** | Equivalent unsprung mass, equivalent sprung mass for suspension tuning | | **Structural Dynamics** | Rayleigh-Ritz reduction of distributed mass to a single SDOF equivalent | --- ## 2.4 Theoretical Foundation — The Equivalence Theorem (Energy Method) Consider a mechanism with masses $\{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n\}$ having velocities $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_n\}$ relative to an inertial frame. The **total kinetic energy** is: $$T \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2$$ A **dynamic equivalent system** has masses $\{M_1, M_2, \dots, M_k\}$ (with $k \ll n$) at velocities $\{V_1, V_2, \dots, V_k\}$, such that: $$\sum_{j=1}^{k} \tfrac{1}{2} M_j V_j^2 \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2 \quad \forall t \geq 0$$ This **pointwise equivalence** is what guarantees that the dynamic response (forces, accelerations, energy fluctuations) of the simplified model mirrors the original. > [!NOTE] > **Strong vs. Weak Equivalence:** > - **Strong (used in KTU):** Equivalence at every instant $t$. > - **Weak (used in FEM/model reduction):** Equivalence only in a least-squares / Galerkin sense over a time window. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Equivalent Mass of a Connecting Rod (Concentrated at Slider and Crank Pin) > **Given:** A uniform connecting rod of mass $m_c$, total length $l$, with center of gravity $G$ dividing it into $l_1$ (big-end side) and $l_2$ (small-end side), so $l_1 + l_2 = l$. ### Step A — Velocity of CG The small-end (slider) has velocity $v_s = v_B$, and the big-end (crank pin) has velocity $v_C = r\omega$. The velocity of the CG (using the rigid-body relation between two points): $$v_G \;=\; \frac{l_2}{l}\, v_B \;+\; \frac{l_1}{l}\, v_C$$ *(This follows from linear interpolation of velocity along a rigid link — the CG divides the link in the ratio $l_1:l_2$.)* ### Step B — Kinetic Energy of the Rod A rigid body in general plane motion has: $$T_{rod} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, m_c\, v_G^{\,2} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, I_G\, \omega_{rod}^{\,2}$$ ### Step C — Energy Equivalence (Split Form) We seek equivalent masses $m_{c1}$ at slider B and $m_{c2}$ at crank pin C such that: $$\tfrac{1}{2} m_{c1} v_B^2 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} m_{c2} v_C^2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m_c v_G^{\,2} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} I_G \omega_{rod}^{\,2}$$ ### Step D — Matching Coefficients (the Critical Algebraic Step) By **equating coefficients of $v_B^2$ and $v_C^2$** on both sides (since $v_B$ and $v_C$ are independent kinematic variables): $$\boxed{m_{c1} \;=\; m_c\,\frac{l_1}{l}}$$ $$\boxed{m_{c2} \;=\; m_c\,\frac{l_2}{l}}$$ These are added to the **actual reciprocating mass** $m_r$ (piston + gudgeon pin + partial rod) and the **actual rotating mass** (crank + partial rod) respectively, to obtain the full **dynamic equivalent system**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Inertia Force on the Piston (Complete KTU Working) We now derive the **equivalent inertia force at the slider**, which is the most-tested KTU result. ### Step 1 — Slider Acceleration (from kinematics) The slider position is: $$x \;=\; r\cos\theta \;+\; l\sqrt{1 - \left(\tfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta\right)^{\!2}}$$ Differentiating twice: $$a_{slider} \;=\; r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta \;+\; \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$ where $n = l/r$ (ratio of connecting rod to crank radius). ### Step 2 — Newton’s Second Law on the Equivalent Mass The total equivalent reciprocating mass is: $$m_e \;=\; m_r + m_{c1} \;=\; m_r + m_c\,\frac{l_1}{l}$$ ### Step 3 — Inertia Force By D'Alembert's principle, the inertia force (acting opposite to acceleration) is: $$\boxed{F_I \;=\; m_e\, r\,\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the single most important equation in Module 1 of PCAGT403.** It appears in **every KTU ESE paper** from this module. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Equivalent Torque on the Crankshaft The torque required to drive the slider against its inertia force is: $$T_{I} \;=\; F_I \cdot r\!\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$$ Substituting $F_I$: $$\boxed{T_{I} \;=\; m_e\, r^2\,\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)\!\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)}$$ Expanding and dropping $1/n^2$ terms (since $n \geq 3$ typically): $$T_{I} \;\approx\; m_e\, r^2\,\omega^2\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta \,-\, \tfrac{1}{4n}\sin 4\theta\right)$$ $$T_{I} \;\approx\; m_e\, r^2\,\omega^2\!\left(\sin 2\theta \;-\; \frac{\sin 4\theta}{4n}\right)$$ This is the **crank effort** due to inertia of reciprocating parts only — the foundation for the **turning moment diagram** and **flywheel design** in Module 2. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Situation | Reference Frame | Equivalent Quantity | Governing Equation | |---|---|---|---| | Single rotating mass at radius $r_i$ moved to radius $r_e$ | Rotation | $I_e = \sum m_i r_i^2$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} I_e \omega_e^2 = \sum \tfrac{1}{2} m_i r_i^2 \omega_i^2$ | | Translating mass moved to slider | Translation | $m_e = \sum m_i (v_i/v_s)^2$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} m_e v_s^2 = \sum \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2$ | | Connecting rod (rigid link) | Both | Split: $m_{c1}$ + $m_{c2}$ | Energy method, coefficient matching | | Multi-cylinder engine | Crank shaft | $I_{e,total} = \sum I_j + \sum m_{ej} r_j^2$ | Superposition across cylinders | | Cam + follower system | Slider | $m_{e,follower} = m_{follower} + m_{cam}\, (r_c/r_f)^2$ | Referenced through cam contact | --- ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** A slider-crank mechanism has crank radius $r = 100$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 400$ mm. The piston mass is $8$ kg. The connecting rod has mass $6$ kg with its CG at $l_1 = 200$ mm from the big-end. The crank rotates at $600$ rpm. Find the **inertia force** at $\theta = 30°$. ### Solution **Step 1 — Compute $n$:** $$n \;=\; \frac{l}{r} \;=\; \frac{400}{100} \;=\; 4$$ **Step 2 — Compute equivalent reciprocating mass:** $$m_{c1} \;=\; m_c \cdot \frac{l_1}{l} \;=\; 6 \cdot \frac{200}{400} \;=\; 3 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_e \;=\; m_r + m_{c1} \;=\; 8 + 3 \;=\; 11 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\omega$:** $$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} \;=\; 62.832 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the inertia force at $\theta = 30°$:** $$F_I \;=\; m_e\, r\, \omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$ $$\cos 30° = 0.8660, \qquad \cos 60° = 0.5$$ $$F_I \;=\; 11 \times 0.1 \times (62.832)^2 \times \left(0.8660 + \frac{0.5}{4}\right)$$ $$F_I \;=\; 11 \times 0.1 \times 3947.84 \times (0.8660 + 0.125)$$ $$F_I \;=\; 4342.62 \times 0.9910 \;=\; 4303.5 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{F_I \;\approx\; 4.30 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 5 — Equivalent torque on crank:** $$T_I \;=\; F_I \cdot r \cdot \frac{\sin\theta + \tfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}}{\cos\theta + \tfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}}$$ $$\sin 30° = 0.5, \qquad \sin 60° = 0.8660$$ $$T_I \;=\; 4303.5 \times 0.1 \times \frac{0.5 + \tfrac{0.8660}{8}}{0.9910} \;=\; 430.35 \times \frac{0.6083}{0.9910}$$ $$T_I \;\approx\; 264.2 \text{ N·m}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** Always carry **3 significant figures** in intermediate steps to avoid rounding drift. Final answers should be reported to **3–4 sig figs**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Dynamic Equivalence Reduction Pipeline The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete conceptual pipeline** for reducing a real multi-body mechanism to its dynamic equivalent system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["ACTUAL MULTI-BODY MECHANISM
Crank, Connecting Rod, Piston, Gudgeon Pin, Flywheel"] --> B["STEP 1 CLASSIFY KINEMATIC PARTS
Rotating Subsystem and Translating Subsystem"] B --> C["ROTATING SUBSYSTEM
Crank Webs, Crank Pin, Partial Rod"] B --> D["TRANSLATING SUBSYSTEM
Piston, Gudgeon Pin, Partial Rod"] C --> E["APPLY KINETIC ENERGY EQUIVALENCE AT CRANK SHAFT
Sum of 0.5 times m_i times r_i squared times omega squared"] E --> F["EQUIVALENT ROTATING MASS
Ie = Ic + mc2 times r squared"] D --> G["APPLY KINETIC ENERGY EQUIVALENCE AT SLIDER
Sum of 0.5 times mi times vi squared"] G --> H["EQUIVALENT TRANSLATING MASS
me = mr + mc1"] F --> I["DYNAMIC EQUIVALENT SYSTEM
Ie at crank radius plus me at slider"] H --> I I --> J["DYNAMIC ANALYSIS ENABLED
Inertia Force, Crank Effort, Turning Moment"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Information Flow in a Reciprocating Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN["ACTUAL MECHANISM"] A1["Piston mass mr"] A2["Connecting Rod mass mc
CG at l1 from big end"] A3["Crank mass mc with Ic"] end subgraph OUT["DYNAMIC EQUIVALENT"] B1["Equivalent slider mass
me = mr + mc times l1 over l"] B2["Equivalent crank inertia
Ie = Ic + mc times l2 over l times r squared"] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A3 --> B2 B1 --> C["FI equals me times r omega squared times bracket cos theta plus cos 2 theta over n"] B2 --> D["TI equals FI times r times bracket sin theta plus sin 2 theta over 2n"] C --> D ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Energy Equivalence Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Total Kinetic Energy T of actual mechanism"] --> Q["Partition T into T rotation plus T translation"] Q --> R["T rotation equals 0.5 times Ie times omega squared"] Q --> S["T translation equals 0.5 times me times v squared"] R --> T["Solve for Ie and me"] S --> T T --> U["Equivalent system has same T as actual at every instant"] ``` ## 4.4 Validation Checklist (Mermaid Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Define mechanism and reference points"] --> B["Are all velocities expressible in closed form?"] B -- Yes --> C["Apply KE equivalence directly"] B -- No --> D["Use Lagrangian reduction or Power equivalence"] C --> E["Compute Ie and me"] D --> E E --> F["Verify dimensional consistency"] F --> G["Verify energy conservation over one cycle"] G --> H["Equivalent system ready for dynamic analysis"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** > KTU paper-setters frequently ask students to **"draw the equivalent system diagram"**. The block-level architecture shown above directly maps to such questions — each block corresponds to a labeled node in the student's hand-drawn solution, and earns full marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "dynamic equivalent system" as applied to a slider-crank mechanism. Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **dynamic equivalent system** of a slider-crank mechanism is a simplified model in which the actual distributed masses (piston, connecting rod, crank, etc.) are replaced by: > - An **equivalent reciprocating mass $m_e$** assumed concentrated at the slider (gudgeon pin), and > - An **equivalent moment of inertia $I_e$** assumed concentrated on the crankshaft, > > such that the **kinetic energy** of the equivalent system equals that of the actual system at every instant. > > **Necessity:** Direct analysis of a multi-rigid-body mechanism involves solving coupled differential equations with multiple unknowns. By reducing to an equivalent system: > (1) The number of dynamic unknowns is minimized, > (2) Standard kinematic equations can be applied directly to compute inertia force, crank effort, and turning moment, > (3) Flywheel design and balancing analyses become tractable. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the principle used to determine the equivalent mass of a connecting rod at the slider and at the crank pin.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The principle is the **kinetic energy equivalence (Rayleigh's theorem)**: > *"The connecting rod is replaced by two point masses — one at the slider (small end) and one at the crank pin (big end) — such that the sum of their kinetic energies equals the actual kinetic energy of the rod."* > > Mathematically: > > $$\tfrac{1}{2}\, m_{c1}\, v_B^2 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, m_{c2}\, v_C^2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, m_c\, v_G^{\,2} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, I_G\, \omega_{rod}^{\,2}$$ > > Matching coefficients of $v_B^2$ and $v_C^2$ yields: > $$m_{c1} = m_c\,\frac{l_1}{l}, \qquad m_{c2} = m_c\,\frac{l_2}{l}$$ > > where $l_1$ is the distance from CG to the big end and $l_2$ to the small end. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the **inertia force** of the reciprocating parts of a slider-crank mechanism in terms of the crank angle, using the dynamic equivalent system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A single-cylinder vertical engine has the following data: - Crank radius $r = 75$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 225$ mm - Reciprocating mass $m_r = 12$ kg, connecting rod mass $m_c = 8$ kg - CG of connecting rod at $l_1 = 100$ mm from the big end - Speed $N = 900$ rpm Compute the **inertia force** and the **equivalent turning moment** at a crank angle of $\theta = 45°$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q1(a) **Step 1:** Identify the dynamic equivalent system: $$m_e = m_r + m_{c1}, \quad I_e = I_c + m_{c2}\,r^2$$ **Step 2:** Position of slider from crank center: $$x = r\cos\theta + l\sqrt{1 - \left(\tfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta\right)^{\!2}}$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate twice with respect to time using $\dot\theta = \omega$: $$\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega\sin\theta\cos\theta}{l\sqrt{1-\left(\tfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta\right)^2}}$$ Applying the standard approximation $\sqrt{1-x} \approx 1 - x/2$ with $x = (r\sin\theta/l)^2$: $$\dot{x} \approx r\omega\!\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right), \quad n = l/r$$ Differentiating again: $$\ddot{x} = r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Apply Newton's second law (D'Alembert): $$\boxed{F_I = m_e\, r\, \omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Q1(b) **Step 1 — Geometry:** $$n = l/r = 225/75 = 3$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent reciprocating mass:** $$m_{c1} = m_c\,\frac{l_1}{l} = 8 \times \frac{100}{225} = 3.556 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_e = 12 + 3.556 = 15.556 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 900}{60} = 94.248 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute trig values at $\theta = 45°$:** $$\cos 45° = 0.7071, \quad \cos 90° = 0$$ **Step 5 — Inertia force:** $$F_I = 15.556 \times 0.075 \times (94.248)^2 \times \left(0.7071 + \frac{0}{3}\right)$$ $$F_I = 15.556 \times 0.075 \times 8882.6 \times 0.7071$$ $$\boxed{F_I \approx 7332.5 \text{ N} \approx 7.33 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 6 — Equivalent turning moment:** $$\sin 45° = 0.7071, \quad \sin 90° = 1$$ $$T_I = F_I \times r \times \frac{\sin\theta + \tfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}}{\cos\theta + \tfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}}$$ $$T_I = 7332.5 \times 0.075 \times \frac{0.7071 + \tfrac{1}{6}}{0.7071}$$ $$T_I = 549.94 \times \frac{0.8738}{0.7071}$$ $$\boxed{T_I \approx 679.5 \text{ N·m}}$$ --- ### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **procedure to find the dynamic equivalent system** of a slider-crank mechanism. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A horizontal reciprocating engine has: - Two cylinders with cranks at $90°$ to each other - Each cylinder: $r = 60$ mm, $l = 240$ mm, $m_r = 10$ kg, $m_c = 6$ kg (CG at mid-length) - Speed $N = 1500$ rpm Calculate the **resultant inertia force** and the **resultant inertia torque** at crank angles $\theta_1 = 0°$ and $\theta_2 = 90°$ for cylinder 1. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q2(a) **Diagram (Block-Level):** ``` Actual Mechanism Dynamic Equivalent ┌──────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ mr (piston) ←→ │ │ me at slider pin │ │ mc (conn. rod) │ ⇒⇒⇒ │ Ie at crank axis │ │ Mc (crank) │ │ with same KE │ └──────────────────────┘ └──────────────────────┘ ``` **Procedure:** 1. **Step 1 — Classification:** Identify rotating parts (crank, flywheel) and reciprocating parts (piston, partial rod). 2. **Step 2 — Locate CG of connecting rod:** Measure $l_1$ and $l_2$ (or compute from known geometry). 3. **Step 3 — Compute split masses:** $$m_{c1} = m_c \cdot l_1/l \quad \text{(added to slider)}$$ $$m_{c2} = m_c \cdot l_2/l \quad \text{(added to crank pin)}$$ 4. **Step 4 — Form equivalent system:** $$m_e = m_r + m_{c1}, \quad I_e = I_c + m_{c2} \cdot r^2$$ 5. **Step 5 — Verify:** Check $\tfrac{1}{2}I_e\omega^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}m_e v^2 = T_{\text{actual}}$. --- #### Model Solution — Q2(b) **Step 1 — Geometry:** $$n = l/r = 240/60 = 4$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent mass (mid-length rod → $l_1 = l_2 = l/2$):** $$m_{c1} = 6 \times 0.5 = 3 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_e = 10 + 3 = 13 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 — Cylinder 1 at $\theta_1 = 0°$:** $$F_{I1} = 13 \times 0.06 \times (157.08)^2 \times \left(\cos 0° + \frac{\cos 0°}{4}\right)$$ $$F_{I1} = 13 \times 0.06 \times 24674 \times 1.25 = 24055 \text{ N} \approx 24.06 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Cylinder 2 at $\theta_2 = 90°$ (since crank 2 is $90°$ ahead):** $$\cos 90° = 0, \quad \cos 180° = -1$$ $$F_{I2} = 13 \times 0.06 \times 24674 \times \left(0 + \frac{-1}{4}\right) = -4811 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Resultant inertia force:** $$F_{I,R} = F_{I1} + F_{I2} = 24055 - 4811 = 19244 \text{ N} \approx 19.24 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 7 — Resultant inertia torque about crank axis:** $$T_{I,R} = F_{I1} \cdot r \cdot (\sin 0° + \tfrac{0}{8})/(1.25) + F_{I2} \cdot r \cdot \dots$$ Computing via the standard crank-effort formula: $$T_{I,1} = F_{I1} \times 0.06 \times 0 = 0 \text{ (at TDC)}$$ $$T_{I,2} = F_{I2} \times 0.06 \times \frac{\sin 90° + \tfrac{\sin 180°}{8}}{-0.25} = (-4811)(0.06)\times \frac{1}{-0.25}$$ $$T_{I,2} = +1154.6 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\boxed{T_{I,R} \approx 1154.6 \text{ N·m}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Dynamic Equivalent System Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the connecting rod's CG split** — losing 2 marks immediately on $m_{c1}$/$m_{c2}$ derivation. > 2. **Sign convention errors** — Inertia force in compression stroke vs. expansion stroke must carry correct signs in turning moment diagrams. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** per sign error. > 3. **Mixing $r$ and $l$ in the kinematic equation** — Always write $n = l/r$ explicitly before substituting. > 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing mm and m in the same expression. Convert **all lengths to meters** before computation. > 5. **Dropping the $\cos 2\theta/n$ term** — Students often write only $m_e r\omega^2\cos\theta$. The second harmonic term is **mandatory** in KTU papers. > 6. **Not stating the assumption of "negligible $1/n^2$ and higher"** when simplifying the torque expression — losing **0.5 mark** for missing justification. > 7. **Omitting the crank inertia $I_c$** in the equivalent system — examiners expect it added to $m_{c2}r^2$ explicitly. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Dynamic Equivalent System** - ✅ **Core Principle:** Kinetic energy equivalence — actual system KE = equivalent system KE at every instant. - ✅ **Connecting Rod Split:** $m_{c1} = m_c(l_1/l)$ at slider; $m_{c2} = m_c(l_2/l)$ at crank pin. - ✅ **Equivalent Slider Mass:** $m_e = m_r + m_{c1}$. - ✅ **Equivalent Crank Inertia:** $I_e = I_c + m_{c2}r^2$. - ✅ **Inertia Force Formula:** $F_I = m_e\, r\, \omega^2 (\cos\theta + \tfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n})$ — **THE** KTU equation. - ✅ **Crank Effort Formula:** $T_I = F_I \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{\sin\theta + \tfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}}{\cos\theta + \tfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}}$. - ✅ **Angular velocity:** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ — never forget units. - ✅ **Forces are vectors** — direction (sign) matters in turning moment diagrams. - ✅ **Always convert lengths to meters** before substituting into SI equations. - ✅ **State assumptions** when simplifying: $n \geq 3$, $1/n^2 \ll 1$, rigid link, etc. - ✅ **Equivalent system is a TOOL, not the actual mechanism** — it preserves energy, not exact geometry. - ✅ **Multi-cylinder superposition:** Total $F_I = \sum F_{I,j}$ with appropriate phase shifts. - ✅ **Link to Flywheel Design:** The $\Delta T$ in Module 2 is computed from $T_I$ derived here. - ✅ **Link to Balancing:** The inertia force derived here is what must be balanced in Module 3. > [!TIP] > **🎯 Final KTU Board Exam Tip:** > When the question says *"find the dynamic equivalent system"*, the examiner expects **four deliverables**: (i) the split $m_{c1}$, $m_{c2}$, (ii) the values $m_e$ and $I_e$, (iii) a labeled diagram showing the equivalent masses at slider and crank pin, and (iv) a verification statement that KE is conserved. Hitting all four = full marks every time.

topics.title: Dynamic Force Analysis of Four-Bar Mechanism and Slider Crank Mechanism (Explanation Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Force Analysis of Four-Bar Mechanism and Slider Crank Mechanism (Explanation Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Force Analysis** of a mechanism is the analytical process of determining the unknown bearing reactions, input torque (or driving force), and the internal forces in the members of a moving mechanism by considering the **inertia forces and inertia torques** developed due to the motion of the links, in addition to all externally applied loads and the weight of the members.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> Dynamic force analysis is the study of forces acting on the various parts of a machine when the machine is running, i.e., when there is a relative motion between the links. The forces include the static forces due to weight and externally applied loads, plus the dynamic forces due to inertia effects of the moving links.

**d'Alembert's Principle (The Foundation):**
When a system of rigid bodies is in motion, the external forces, the inertia forces, and the inertia torques acting on the system form a dynamically equivalent system. The principle states that the **inertia force** ($\vec{F_I} = -m\vec{a}$) and **inertia torque** ($\vec{T_I} = -I_G\vec{\alpha}$) can be treated as additional "pseudo-static" forces, allowing the dynamic equilibrium equations to be written exactly as in statics:

$$\sum \vec{F} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum \vec{M} = 0$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine pushing a heavy shopping cart at a constant speed — your hands apply a force that exactly balances the friction. This is **static force analysis**. Now imagine pushing the same cart and suddenly accelerating it — you feel a strong "kick-back" force from the cart pushing against you, resisting the motion. That resisting force is the **inertia force** ($F_I = m \cdot a$). 

In a real machine:
- A **piston** reciprocating in an engine cylinder generates enormous inertia forces (sometimes thousands of Newtons per kg of mass).
- A **connecting rod** not only translates but also rotates, so it has *both* translation inertia *and* rotational inertia.
- A **rotating crank** generates inertia torque but no net translation inertia (the center of mass is on the rotation axis or its path is a pure circle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this topic critical for KTU exams?**
> The dynamic force analysis determines the **shaking forces** transmitted to the frame, the **bearing loads**, the **size of the flywheel**, and the **input torque** required to drive the machine. An engineer who cannot perform this analysis will design unsafe, overweight, or over-stressed machinery.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Mass of the link ($m$):** measured in **kg**.
- **Mass moment of inertia ($I_G$ or $I_O$):** measured in **kg·m²**.
- **Linear acceleration ($a$):** measured in **m/s²**.
- **Angular acceleration ($\alpha$):** measured in **rad/s²**.
- **Angular velocity ($\omega$):** measured in **rad/s** (assumed constant for steady-state operation).
- **Inertia force magnitude:** $|F_I| = m \cdot a$, direction **opposite** to acceleration.
- **Inertia torque magnitude:** $|T_I| = I_G \cdot \alpha$, direction **opposite** to angular acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Emphasis:** Always write the inertia force vector with the **opposite sign** of the acceleration vector, and the inertia torque opposite to the angular acceleration. This is the most commonly skipped step in student answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Dynamic Analysis Needed Beyond Static Analysis?

Static analysis **assumes the mechanism is at rest** (or moving at *constant* velocity, which is physically impossible in reciprocating machinery). It ignores:
- Accelerations of links during operation.
- The reaction forces due to these accelerations.

These ignored forces are precisely the **inertia forces**, and they can be **2 to 10 times** the magnitude of the static weight forces in high-speed engines. Ignoring them leads to catastrophic design failure.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure for Dynamic Force Analysis

The standard KTU-board procedure for any mechanism (four-bar or slider-crank) follows this rigid sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Kinematic Analysis:** Determine the position, velocity, and acceleration of every link and its **center of mass** (using loop-closure equations, instant centres, or analytical methods).
2. **Step 2 — Identify Inertia Loads:** For every link:
   - Apply an inertia force $\vec{F_I} = -m \vec{a_G}$ at the **center of mass** $G$.
   - Apply an inertia torque $\vec{T_I} = -I_G \vec{\alpha}$ about the **center of mass** $G$.
3. **Step 3 — Free Body Diagrams (FBDs):** Draw the FBD of each link separately, showing:
   - All applied external forces (loads, gas pressure, etc.).
   - All weight forces ($W = mg$).
   - All bearing reactions ($F_{ij}$ at the pin joining link $i$ to link $j$).
   - The inertia force and inertia torque from Step 2.
4. **Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium Equations:** For each link, write:
   - $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$ (translational equilibrium).
   - $\sum M = 0$ about a strategic point (rotational equilibrium).
5. **Step 5 — Solve the System:** Since the inertia terms are *known* from Step 1, the equations are *linear* in the unknown bearing forces and the input torque. Solve algebraically (or numerically for complex mechanisms).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table 1: Inertia Loads for Different Types of Motion**

| Type of Motion of Link | Inertia Force | Inertia Torque | Where Applied |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Translation (e.g., slider) | $F_I = m \cdot a_G$ | $T_I = 0$ | At the **center of mass** $G$ |
| Pure Rotation about fixed axis through $G$ (e.g., a crank) | $F_I = m \cdot a_G = m \cdot r\omega^2$ (centripetal) | $T_I = I_G \cdot \alpha$ | Force at $G$, torque about $G$ |
| General Plane Motion (e.g., connecting rod) | $F_I = m \cdot a_G$ | $T_I = I_G \cdot \alpha$ | Force at $G$, torque about $G$ |
| Rotation about a fixed axis NOT through $G$ | $F_I = m \cdot a_G$ | $T_I = I_O \cdot \alpha$ (where $I_O = I_G + mr^2$) | Force at $G$, torque about the **fixed axis** $O$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table 2: Slider-Crank Inertia Force Formula (High-Yield)**

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Piston displacement | $x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$ | From crank centre $O$ to piston pin $B$ |
| Piston velocity | $v = -r\omega\left[\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2l}\sin 2\theta\right]$ | First derivative (approximate) |
| **Piston acceleration** | $a = -r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right]$ | **Second derivative (approximate)** |
| Inertia force of piston | $F_I = m_B \cdot a$ | Acts **opposite** to piston acceleration |
| Angular velocity of connecting rod | $\omega_{CR} = \frac{r\omega}{l}\cos\theta$ | Approximate |
| Angular acceleration of connecting rod | $\alpha_{CR} = -\frac{r\omega^2}{l}\sin\theta$ | Approximate |
| Inertia torque of connecting rod | $T_{CR} = I_G \cdot \alpha_{CR}$ | Acts **opposite** to $\alpha_{CR}$ |
| Effective inertia force at crank pin (approx.) | $F_{eff} = (m_B + m_{CR}) r\omega^2$ | Used for finding net torque |

> [!NOTE]
