- ✅ **Force Balance Equation:** $m_2 r_2 + m_3 r_3 = m_1 r_1$ (the sum of balancing mass products equals the unbalanced mass product).
- ✅ **Couple Balance about Plane L:** $m_3 r_3 \cdot L = m_1 r_1 \cdot a$.
- ✅ **Couple Balance about Plane R:** $m_2 r_2 \cdot L = m_1 r_1 \cdot b$.
- ✅ **Mass product in Plane L** (nearer plane) = $\dfrac{m_1 r_1 \cdot b}{L}$ — uses **far distance** $b$.
- ✅ **Mass product in Plane R** (farther plane) = $\dfrac{m_1 r_1 \cdot a}{L}$ — uses **near distance** $a$.
- ✅ **Angular position of both balancing masses** = **180°** from the unbalanced mass (diametrically opposite).
- ✅ **Centrifugal force** $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ — independent of gravity; balance is achieved at *all* speeds because $\omega^2$ cancels.
- ✅ **Static balance** = no resultant force. **Dynamic balance** = no resultant force AND no resultant couple.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Crankshafts, turbines, rotors, centrifuges, propellers, hard disk drives — anywhere rotating masses exist at high speeds.

> **Final Note:** This topic is a **guaranteed question** in KTU ESE Module 2. Master the two key formulas for different-plane balancing, practice 5–6 numerical problems, and you will secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balancing of Several Rotating Masses by Balancing Masses in Same Plane and in Different Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Balancing of Several Rotating Masses by Balancing Masses in Same Plane and in Different Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balancing** is the process of redistributing the masses of a rotating or reciprocating system such that the **centrifugal inertia forces** and **centrifugal couples** generated by the moving masses are either completely eliminated or reduced to an acceptable minimum within one revolution.

When several masses rotate about a common shaft axis, each mass produces a centrifugal force acting radially outward from the axis of rotation. The vector sum of all these inertia forces and the resultant moment (couple) produced by them must be **zero** for the system to be dynamically balanced.

Mathematically, the condition for a perfectly balanced rotor is:

$$\sum F_i = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i = 0 \quad \text{(Resultant force)}$$

$$\sum M_i = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \cdot l_i = 0 \quad \text{(Resultant couple)}$$

where $l_i$ is the axial distance of mass $m_i$ from a reference plane.

### 1.2 Classification of Balancing Problems

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies balancing problems into two broad categories:

| # | Category | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Balancing of Rotating Masses** | Multi-plane balancing involving both force and couple equilibrium |
| 2 | **Balancing of Reciprocating Masses** | Single-cylinder and multi-cylinder engine balancing |

Within **rotating masses**, the sub-classifications are:
- (a) Several masses in the **same plane**
- (b) Several masses in **different planes** (the most general industrial case)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine spinning a heavy hammer with one hand at its handle. You will feel the heavy head "tugging" sideways — this is the unbalanced centrifugal force trying to shake your hand. To eliminate this tug, you can either (i) **shorten the handle** (reduce radius — single-plane correction), or (ii) **add a counter-weight on the opposite end of the handle** (introduce a balancing mass). If you can only add mass to one end, you must place it at a different axial position — that is the essence of **multi-plane balancing**.

For a car wheel, a single balancing weight placed on the rim corrects all imbalances (same plane). For a **crankshaft** of an engine, you cannot move the crank throws; you must place counter-weights in **different axial planes** along the shaft length — this is multi-plane balancing.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- The standard reference rotational speed: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s (where $N$ is in **rpm**)
- Centrifugal force of any mass: $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ (in Newtons, N)
- For graphical balance problems, we use the proportional quantity $m \cdot r$ (mass-radius product) because $\omega^2$ is a common multiplier and cancels out in vector sums.

### 1.5 Why Balancing is Necessary

Unbalanced rotors cause:
- Excessive **vibrations** leading to fatigue failure
- **Bearing** reactions fluctuating, damaging journals
- **Noise** and discomfort in machinery cabins
- Loss of **precision** in machine tools
- Reduced **service life** of components

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Case 1: Several Masses in the Same Plane

When all rotating masses lie in a single transverse plane perpendicular to the shaft axis, the axial distance $l$ becomes irrelevant — only the **vector sum of centrifugal forces** must vanish.

**Condition:** $\sum (m_i \cdot r_i) = 0$ (vectorially)

**Methods of Solution:**

1. **Analytical (Algebraic) Method**
   - Resolve each $m_i \cdot r_i$ vector along two perpendicular axes (X and Y)
   - Sum components: $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ and $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$
   - Magnitude of balancing mass: $m_b \cdot r_b = \sqrt{(\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i)^2 + (\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i)^2}$
   - Angular position: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i}$

2. **Graphical (Vector Polygon) Method**
   - Draw vectors representing each $m_i r_i$ in its angular direction to scale
   - The closing side of the polygon, drawn in the opposite direction, gives the required balancing mass-radius product $m_b r_b$
   - **Rule:** The vector is drawn *from tail to head* in sequence; the resultant balancing vector is drawn *from head back to tail*

### 2.2 Case 2: Several Masses in Different Planes (Multi-Plane Balancing)

This is the **most general and industrially important** case — applies to crankshafts, multi-disc rotors, turbines.

**Two conditions must be satisfied:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition 1 — Static Balance (Force Polygon Closure):**
> The algebraic sum of $m \cdot r$ vectors of all masses (including balancing masses) projected onto any transverse plane must be zero.

$$\sum (m_i r_i) = 0$$

**Condition 2 — Dynamic Balance (Couple Polygon Closure):**
> The algebraic sum of couples produced by the inertia forces of all masses about any reference plane must be zero.

$$\sum (m_i r_i \cdot l_i) = 0$$

where $l_i$ is the axial distance of mass $i$ from a reference plane.

### 2.3 Procedure for Multi-Plane Balancing (Graphical Method)

**Step 1 — Force Polygon:**
- Construct a force polygon by laying out all $m_i r_i$ vectors to scale in proper angular sequence.
- If the polygon does not close, two balancing masses $m_a$ and $m_b$ in two reference planes A and B are introduced.
- The closing side is split into two vectors $\mathbf{F_a}$ and $\mathbf{F_b}$ (representing $m_a r_a$ and $m_b r_b$) using the **principle that any vector can be resolved into two components in given directions**.
- Note: $m_a r_a$ and $m_b r_b$ are assumed to be in known **angular directions** (or arbitrary, to be solved).

**Step 2 — Couple Polygon:**
- Construct a couple polygon by drawing vectors representing $m_i r_i \cdot l_i$ in the **same angular directions** as the corresponding $m_i r_i$ vectors, to a separate scale.
- Closing side gives the resultant unbalanced couple $C$.
- The balancing couples $C_a = m_a r_a \cdot L_a$ and $C_b = m_b r_b \cdot L_b$ must cancel $C$.

**Step 3 — Linking Equations:**
- From force polygon: $F_a \cos\alpha + F_b \cos\beta = F_c$ (horizontal closure)
- From couple polygon: $F_a \cdot L_a + F_b \cdot L_b = $ (couple closure, signed)

Solving these two equations gives the magnitudes of the two balancing masses.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Centrifugal Force | $F = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | N | Single mass at radius r |
| 2 | Centrifugal Couple | $C = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot l$ | N·m | About a reference plane |
| 3 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ | rad/s | N in rpm |
| 4 | Single-plane balance | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector) | kg·m | Resultant force = 0 |
| 5 | Multi-plane force balance | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector) | kg·m | Static condition |
| 6 | Multi-plane couple balance | $\sum m_i r_i \cdot l_i = 0$ (vector) | kg·m² | Dynamic condition |
| 7 | Balancing mass radius | $m_b = \dfrac{F_b}{\omega^2 \cdot r_b}$ | kg | Once $F_b$ is known |
| 8 | Balancing couple component | $F_a \cdot L_a$ | N·m² | Signed in couple polygon |
| 9 | Reference plane selection | Arbitrary | — | Convenience, no loss of generality |
| 10 | Number of balancing masses | 2 (minimum) | — | For a general unbalanced rotor |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive crankshafts** are designed with built-in counterweights specifically to balance the rotating mass system across multiple axial crank-pin planes.
- **Gas/steam turbine rotors:** Multi-disc balancing is critical because discs are mounted at different axial positions on the shaft.
- **Propeller shafts** of ships and aircraft: Must be dynamically balanced to prevent vibration at high speeds.
- **Centrifuge drums**, **flywheels**, **impellers of pumps/compressors** — all rely on multi-plane balancing principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Force on a Rotating Mass

Consider a rigid rotor rotating with constant angular velocity $\omega$ about a fixed axis. A particle of mass $m_i$ at radius $r_i$ from the axis executes uniform circular motion.

The radial (centripetal) acceleration is:

$$a_n = \omega^2 \cdot r_i$$

By Newton's second law, the centripetal force required is $F_c = m_i \cdot a_n = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i$. The **reactive centrifugal force** (the inertia force acting on the shaft bearing) is equal in magnitude and directed radially outward:

$$F_{ci} = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Conditions for a Completely Balanced Rotor (n Masses in Multiple Planes)

Let there be $n$ masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$ located in planes with axial distances $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ from a chosen reference plane (say, plane 1).

**For each mass**, the centrifugal force vector is:

$$\vec{F_i} = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \quad \text{(in the angular direction } \theta_i\text{)}$$

**Force Equilibrium (Static Balance):**

The vector sum of all inertia forces must vanish:

$$\vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} + \cdots + \vec{F_n} = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $\omega^2$ (non-zero):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i e^{j\theta_i} = 0$$

In Cartesian components:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(X-component)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(Y-component)}$$

**Couple Equilibrium (Dynamic Balance):**

Taking moments about the reference plane, the couple produced by each force is $F_i \cdot l_i$, with sign depending on whether $l_i$ is to the left or right of the reference plane.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cdot l_i = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i l_i e^{j\theta_i} = 0$$

Dividing by $\omega^2$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

**Result:** For $n$ masses, we have **4 scalar equations** (2 force + 2 couple). With **two balancing masses** (each contributing 2 unknowns: magnitude and angular position), we have exactly 4 unknowns. Hence the system is determinate.

### 3.3 Detailed Worked Example (Same Plane)

**Problem:** Three masses $m_1 = 10$ kg, $m_2 = 20$ kg, $m_3 = 30$ kg are mounted on a shaft at radii $r_1 = 0.3$ m, $r_2 = 0.2$ m, $r_3 = 0.1$ m at angular positions $\theta_1 = 0°$, $\theta_2 = 90°$, $\theta_3 = 180°$ respectively. Find the magnitude and angular position of the balancing mass if its radius is $r_b = 0.25$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute mass-radius products:**

$$m_1 r_1 = 10 \times 0.3 = 3 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_2 r_2 = 20 \times 0.2 = 4 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_3 r_3 = 30 \times 0.1 = 3 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve into X and Y components:**

$$\sum (m_i r_i \cos\theta_i) = 3\cos 0° + 4\cos 90° + 3\cos 180°$$
$$= 3(1) + 4(0) + 3(-1) = 3 + 0 - 3 = 0$$

$$\sum (m_i r_i \sin\theta_i) = 3\sin 0° + 4\sin 90° + 3\sin 180°$$
$$= 3(0) + 4(1) + 3(0) = 4$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of balancing mass-radius product:**

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{0^2 + 4^2} = 4 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 4 — Angular position:**

$$\tan\phi = \dfrac{4}{0} \implies \phi = 90°$$

**Step 5 — Mass:**

$$m_b = \dfrac{4}{0.25} = 16 \text{ kg}$$

### 3.4 Detailed Worked Example (Different Planes — Analytical Method)

**Problem:** A shaft carries four masses in four different planes:
- Plane 1: $m_1 = 12$ kg, $r_1 = 0.2$ m, $\theta_1 = 0°$
- Plane 2: $m_2 = 16$ kg, $r_2 = 0.15$ m, $\theta_2 = 60°$
- Plane 3: $m_3 = 20$ kg, $r_3 = 0.25$ m, $\theta_3 = 120°$
- Plane 4: $m_4 = 15$ kg, $r_4 = 0.2$ m, $\theta_4 = 200°$

Axial positions from left support: $L_1 = 0$, $L_2 = 200$ mm, $L_3 = 400$ mm, $L_4 = 600$ mm.

Two balancing masses $m_a$ and $m_b$ are placed at planes A and B located at $L_A = 100$ mm and $L_B = 500$ mm. Find the balancing masses at radii $r_a = r_b = 0.2$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute $m_i r_i$ for each plane:**

$$m_1 r_1 = 12 \times 0.2 = 2.4 \text{ kg·m, at } 0°$$

$$m_2 r_2 = 16 \times 0.15 = 2.4 \text{ kg·m, at } 60°$$

$$m_3 r_3 = 20 \times 0.25 = 5.0 \text{ kg·m, at } 120°$$

$$m_4 r_4 = 15 \times 0.2 = 3.0 \text{ kg·m, at } 200°$$

**Step 2 — Compute Resultant Inertia Force Vector $\vec{R}$:**

$$R_x = \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 2.4(1) + 2.4(0.5) + 5.0(-0.5) + 3.0(-0.9397)$$
$$= 2.4 + 1.2 - 2.5 - 2.819 = -1.719 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$R_y = \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 2.4(0) + 2.4(0.866) + 5.0(0.866) + 3.0(-0.342)$$
$$= 0 + 2.078 + 4.330 - 1.026 = 5.382 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(-1.719)^2 + (5.382)^2} = \sqrt{2.955 + 28.966} = \sqrt{31.921} = 5.650 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\phi_R = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5.382}{-1.719}\right) = 180° - 72.27° = 107.73°$$

**Step 3 — Compute Resultant Couple about Plane A (L_A = 100 mm):**

Distances from plane A: $d_1 = L_1 - L_A = -100$ mm, $d_2 = 100$ mm, $d_3 = 300$ mm, $d_4 = 500$ mm.

$$C_x = \sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 2.4(-100)(1) + 2.4(100)(0.5) + 5.0(300)(-0.5) + 3.0(500)(-0.9397)$$

Converting to meters for consistency:
$$= 2.4(-0.1)(1) + 2.4(0.1)(0.5) + 5.0(0.3)(-0.5) + 3.0(0.5)(-0.9397)$$
$$= -0.24 + 0.12 - 0.75 - 1.4096 = -2.2796 \text{ kg·m²}$$

$$C_y = 2.4(-0.1)(0) + 2.4(0.1)(0.866) + 5.0(0.3)(0.866) + 3.0(0.5)(-0.342)$$
$$= 0 + 0.2078 + 1.299 - 0.513 = 0.9938 \text{ kg·m²}$$

**Step 4 — Coupling Equation:**

The balancing mass $m_b$ at plane B (distance $L_B - L_A = 400$ mm = 0.4 m from plane A) must produce a couple to balance $\vec{C}$:

$$m_b r_b \cdot (L_B - L_A) = -C$$

$$m_b \cdot r_b \cdot 0.4 = -\sqrt{(-2.2796)^2 + (0.9938)^2}$$

$$m_b \cdot r_b \cdot 0.4 = -\sqrt{5.197 + 0.988} = -\sqrt{6.185} = -2.487 \text{ kg·m²}$$

The negative sign indicates opposite direction. Therefore:

$$m_b \cdot r_b = \dfrac{2.487}{0.4} = 6.218 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_b = \dfrac{6.218}{0.2} = 31.09 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Determine $m_a$ from Force Polygon:**

$$\vec{F_a} = \vec{R} - \vec{F_b}$$

Where $\vec{F_b}$ acts in the direction opposite to the resultant couple (perpendicular considerations are simplified in this scalar demonstration). In a full vector solution:

$$F_{bx} = -C_x / d_{AB} = -(-2.2796)/0.4 = 5.699$$
$$F_{by} = -C_y / d_{AB} = -(0.9938)/0.4 = -2.485$$

$$F_{ax} = R_x - F_{bx} = -1.719 - 5.699 = -7.418$$
$$F_{ay} = R_y - F_{by} = 5.382 - (-2.485) = 7.867$$

$$F_a = \sqrt{(-7.418)^2 + (7.867)^2} = \sqrt{55.03 + 61.89} = \sqrt{116.92} = 10.81 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_a = \dfrac{10.81}{0.2} = 54.06 \text{ kg}$$

This illustrates the analytical approach to multi-plane balancing — the graphical method uses the same principles but constructs polygons with a ruler and protractor.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Diagram: Single-Plane vs. Multi-Plane Balancing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["SINGLE PLANE BALANCING"]
        A1["Rotating Mass m1 at radius r1"] --> S1{"Force Polygon"}
        A2["Rotating Mass m2 at radius r2"] --> S1
        A3["Rotating Mass m3 at radius r3"] --> S1
        S1 --> S2["Resultant R = Sum of m_i r_i"]
        S2 --> S3["Balancing Mass m_b at radius r_b"]
        S3 --> S4{"Force = 0 condition"}
    end
    
    subgraph B["MULTI PLANE BALANCING"]
        B1["Mass m1 at Plane 1, distance L1"] --> P1{"Force Polygon"}
        B2["Mass m2 at Plane 2, distance L2"] --> P1
        B3["Mass m3 at Plane 3, distance L3"] --> P1
        B4["Mass m4 at Plane 4, distance L4"] --> P1
        P1 --> P2["Resultant Force R"]
        B1 --> Q1{"Couple Polygon"}
        B2 --> Q1
        B3 --> Q1
        B4 --> Q1
        Q1 --> Q2["Resultant Couple C"]
        P2 --> P3["Balancing Mass m_a at Plane A"]
        Q2 --> P3
        P2 --> P4["Balancing Mass m_b at Plane B"]
        Q2 --> P4
        P3 --> P5{"Force = 0 AND Couple = 0"}
        P4 --> P5
    end
```

### 4.2 Force Polygon Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["START: List all m_i, r_i, theta_i"] --> S2["Compute mass-radius products m_i r_i"]
    S2 --> S3["Choose scale, e.g. 1 cm = 1 kg-m"]
    S3 --> S4["Draw vector m1r1 at theta1"]
    S4 --> S5["From head of vector 1, draw vector m2r2 at theta2"]
    S5 --> S6["From head of vector 2, draw vector m3r3 at theta3"]
    S6 --> S7["Continue for all masses"]
    S7 --> S8{"Does polygon close?"}
    S8 -->|"YES"| S9["System is inherently balanced"]
    S8 -->|"NO"| S10["Closing side OA from tail O to final head A"]
    S10 --> S11["Vector AO represents balancing mass-radius product"]
    S11 --> S12["Measure magnitude and angle of AO"]
    S12 --> S13["Compute m_b = magnitude divided by r_b"]
    S13 --> S14["END: Balancing mass found"]
```

### 4.3 Multi-Plane Balancing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHAFT["ROTATING SHAFT ASSEMBLY"]
        direction LR
        P0["PLANE 0\n(Reference)"] --> P1["PLANE 1\nm1 at L1"]
        P1 --> PA["PLANE A\nBalancing mass m_a at L_A"]
        PA --> P2["PLANE 2\nm2 at L2"]
        P2 --> P3["PLANE 3\nm3 at L3"]
        P3 --> PB["PLANE B\nBalancing mass m_b at L_B"]
        PB --> P4["PLANE 4\nm4 at L4"]
    end
    
    SHAFT --> BL["BALANCING CONDITIONS"]
    BL --> BLC1["FORCE: Sum m_i r_i = 0"]
    BL --> BLC2["COUPLE: Sum m_i r_i l_i = 0"]
```

### 4.4 Vector Resolution for Balancing Mass Direction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["Inertia Force Vector F1\nMagnitude m1 r1\nDirection theta1"] --> RES
    V2["Inertia Force Vector F2\nMagnitude m2 r2\nDirection theta2"] --> RES
    V3["Inertia Force Vector F3\nMagnitude m3 r3\nDirection theta3"] --> RES
    RES[Vector Addition] --> RES2[Resultant R]
    RES2 --> NEG[Reverse direction]
    NEG --> FB[Balancing Force F_b = -R]
    FB --> COMP["F_b resolved into two components:\nF_a in plane A direction\nF_b in plane B direction"]
    COMP --> END[Balancing masses m_a and m_b]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term "static balancing" and "dynamic balancing" of a rotating mass system. Under what conditions is a rotor said to be in complete balance?

**Model Answer:**

**Static balancing** is a condition where the algebraic (vector) sum of the centrifugal forces of all the rotating masses of a system about the axis of rotation is zero. A statically balanced rotor will not cause any vertical reaction on its bearings when rotated slowly. The condition is:

$$\sum \vec{F_i} = \sum m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i = 0$$

**Dynamic balancing** is the additional condition where the algebraic sum of the couples produced by the centrifugal forces about any plane perpendicular to the axis of rotation is also zero. A dynamically balanced rotor will not cause any varying reactions on the bearings at any speed of rotation. The condition is:

$$\sum \vec{M_i} = \sum m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \cdot l_i = 0$$

A rotor is said to be in **complete balance** when both static and dynamic conditions are simultaneously satisfied.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Why is it not sufficient to balance a multi-disc rotor by treating each disc independently and adding a balancing mass in its own plane?

**Model Answer:**

When several discs are mounted at different axial positions along a common shaft, balancing each disc individually in its own plane creates a **balanced condition for individual discs but an unbalanced system as a whole**. This is because:

1. The balancing masses added in each plane introduce their own centrifugal forces and couples.
2. Although each disc has zero net inertia force in its own plane, the **couples** between planes do not sum to zero.
3. The result is a **wobbling motion** of the shaft axis and fluctuating bearing reactions.

**Correct approach:** A multi-plane balancing problem must satisfy **both** the force polygon closure AND the couple polygon closure simultaneously. Typically, only **two balancing masses** placed in two carefully chosen planes are needed to achieve complete balance for any number of unbalanced masses in different planes.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Four masses $m_1, m_2, m_3, m_4$ are mounted on a shaft in **different planes** at radii 200 mm, 150 mm, 200 mm, and 250 mm respectively. The masses and their angular positions are:

| Mass | Magnitude (kg) | Radius (mm) | Angular Position |
|------|---------------|-------------|------------------|
| $m_1$ | 8 | 200 | 0° |
| $m_2$ | 6 | 150 | 60° |
| $m_3$ | 10 | 200 | 135° |
| $m_4$ | 5 | 250 | 270° |

Determine graphically (or analytically) the magnitudes and angular positions of two balancing masses placed in planes A and B which are 150 mm and 600 mm from plane of $m_1$. Take radii of balancing masses as 250 mm each.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass-radius products:**

$$m_1 r_1 = 8 \times 0.2 = 1.6 \text{ kg·m at } 0°$$
$$m_2 r_2 = 6 \times 0.15 = 0.9 \text{ kg·m at } 60°$$
$$m_3 r_3 = 10 \times 0.2 = 2.0 \text{ kg·m at } 135°$$
$$m_4 r_4 = 5 \times 0.25 = 1.25 \text{ kg·m at } 270°$$

**Step 2 — Resultant Inertia Force (X and Y components):**

$$R_x = 1.6\cos 0° + 0.9\cos 60° + 2.0\cos 135° + 1.25\cos 270°$$
$$= 1.6 + 0.45 + (-1.414) + 0 = 0.636 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$R_y = 1.6\sin 0° + 0.9\sin 60° + 2.0\sin 135° + 1.25\sin 270°$$
$$= 0 + 0.7794 + 1.414 + (-1.25) = 0.9434 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$R = \sqrt{0.636^2 + 0.9434^2} = \sqrt{0.404 + 0.890} = \sqrt{1.294} = 1.138 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\phi_R = \tan^{-1}(0.9434/0.636) = \tan^{-1}(1.483) = 56.02°$$

**Step 3 — Couple about Plane A (taking distances from plane A):**

Assume plane of $m_1$ is at $L_1 = 0$, plane A at $L_A = 0.150$ m, plane of $m_2$ at $L_2 = 0.200$ m, plane of $m_3$ at $L_3 = 0.350$ m, plane of $m_4$ at $L_4 = 0.500$ m, plane B at $L_B = 0.600$ m.

Distances from plane A: $d_1 = -0.150$, $d_2 = +0.050$, $d_3 = +0.200$, $d_4 = +0.350$.

Couple of $m_a$ about A: **zero** (since $m_a$ is in plane A itself).

**Couple components about A:**

$$C_x = \sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 1.6(-0.150)(1) + 0.9(0.050)(0.5) + 2.0(0.200)(-0.707) + 1.25(0.350)(0)$$
$$= -0.24 + 0.0225 - 0.2828 + 0 = -0.5003 \text{ kg·m²}$$

$$C_y = 1.6(-0.150)(0) + 0.9(0.050)(0.866) + 2.0(0.200)(0.707) + 1.25(0.350)(-1)$$
$$= 0 + 0.0390 + 0.2828 - 0.4375 = -0.1157 \text{ kg·m²}$$

**Step 4 — Couple from $m_b$ about Plane A:**

Distance of B from A: $d_{AB} = 0.600 - 0.150 = 0.450$ m.

The couple of $m_b$ must balance $C$, so $m_b r_b$ acts opposite to $C$ at distance $d_{AB}$:

$$m_b r_b \cdot d_{AB} = -C \implies m_b r_b = -C / d_{AB}$$

$$F_{bx} = -C_x / d_{AB} = -(-0.5003)/0.450 = 1.112 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$F_{by} = -C_y / d_{AB} = -(-0.1157)/0.450 = 0.257 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$F_b = \sqrt{1.112^2 + 0.257^2} = \sqrt{1.237 + 0.066} = \sqrt{1.303} = 1.141 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\theta_b = \tan^{-1}(0.257/1.112) = 13.0°$$

$$m_b = \dfrac{F_b}{r_b} = \dfrac{1.141}{0.25} = 4.564 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Find $m_a$:**

$$\vec{F_a} = \vec{R} - \vec{F_b}$$
$$F_{ax} = 0.636 - 1.112 = -0.476 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$F_{ay} = 0.9434 - 0.257 = 0.6864 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$F_a = \sqrt{(-0.476)^2 + (0.6864)^2} = \sqrt{0.2266 + 0.4711} = \sqrt{0.6977} = 0.8353 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\theta_a = 180° - \tan^{-1}(0.6864/0.476) = 180° - 55.27° = 124.73°$$

$$m_a = \dfrac{0.8353}{0.25} = 3.341 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer:** $m_a \approx 3.34$ kg at angle $\approx 124.7°$ in plane A; $m_b \approx 4.56$ kg at angle $\approx 13°$ in plane B.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **graphical method** of balancing several masses lying in different planes of a rotating shaft. State clearly the construction of the force polygon and the couple polygon.

**Model Answer:**

**Setup:** Let masses $m_1, m_2, m_3, m_4$ be located in planes with axial distances $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ from a reference end. Two balancing masses $m_a$ and $m_b$ are placed in reference planes A and B.

**Step 1 — Construction of Force Polygon:**
- Choose a suitable scale, say, **1 cm = 1 kg·m**.
- Draw the vector $\vec{OA}$ representing $m_1 r_1$ at angle $\theta_1$ from a chosen datum line.
- From the head of $\vec{OA}$, draw vector $\vec{AB}$ representing $m_2 r_2$ at angle $\theta_2$.
- Similarly draw $\vec{BC}$ and $\vec{CD}$ for the remaining masses.
- If the polygon does not close (i.e., $\vec{OD}$ is non-zero), the closing vector $\vec{DO}$ (from head D to tail O) represents the resultant unbalanced force $\vec{R}$.
- The balancing force $\vec{R_b} = -\vec{R} = \vec{OD}$ can be resolved into two components $\vec{F_a}$ and $\vec{F_b}$ acting in the assumed angular directions of the two balancing masses.

**Step 2 — Construction of Couple Polygon:**
- Choose a different scale, say, **1 cm = 1 kg·m²**.
- Draw vectors representing $m_1 r_1 L_1$, $m_2 r_2 L_2$, ... in the **same angular directions** as the corresponding $m_1 r_1$ vectors in the force polygon.
- The closing side of this polygon gives the resultant couple $\vec{C}$.
- The balancing couple $\vec{C_b} = -\vec{C}$ must be produced by $m_b r_b$ acting at a distance $(L_B - L_A)$ from plane A.

**Step 3 — Linking:**
- From the force polygon, we have $\vec{F_a} + \vec{F_b} = -\vec{R}$.
- From the couple polygon, we have $F_b \cdot (L_B - L_A) = -C$ (when $m_a$ is in plane A).
- These two vector equations (equivalent to 4 scalar equations) uniquely determine $F_a, F_b$ and their angular positions.

**Final Step:** With known radii $r_a$ and $r_b$, compute the actual balancing masses:
$$m_a = \dfrac{F_a}{r_a}, \quad m_b = \dfrac{F_b}{r_b}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A shaft carries three masses in a **single plane** at the following positions:

| Mass (kg) | Radius (mm) | Angular Position |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| 12 | 100 | 30° |
| 18 | 150 | 120° |
| 15 | 200 | 240° |

Find the magnitude and angular position of a balancing mass placed at a radius of 250 mm.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass-radius products:**

$$m_1 r_1 = 12 \times 0.10 = 1.20 \text{ kg·m at } 30°$$
$$m_2 r_2 = 18 \times 0.15 = 2.70 \text{ kg·m at } 120°$$
$$m_3 r_3 = 15 \times 0.20 = 3.00 \text{ kg·m at } 240°$$

**Step 2 — Sum of X-components:**

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 1.20\cos 30° + 2.70\cos 120° + 3.00\cos 240°$$
$$= 1.20(0.866) + 2.70(-0.5) + 3.00(-0.5)$$
$$= 1.0392 - 1.35 - 1.50 = -1.8108 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 3 — Sum of Y-components:**

$$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 1.20\sin 30° + 2.70\sin 120° + 3.00\sin 240°$$
$$= 1.20(0.5) + 2.70(0.866) + 3.00(-0.866)$$
$$= 0.60 + 2.3382 - 2.598 = 0.3402 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of balancing mass-radius product:**

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{(-1.8108)^2 + (0.3402)^2} = \sqrt{3.279 + 0.1157} = \sqrt{3.3947} = 1.842 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 5 — Angular position:**

$$\tan\phi = \dfrac{0.3402}{-1.8108} = -0.1879$$

Since X is negative and Y is positive, the vector lies in the **second quadrant**:

$$\phi = 180° - 10.66° = 169.34°$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of balancing mass:**

$$m_b = \dfrac{1.842}{0.25} = 7.37 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer:** The balancing mass is $m_b \approx 7.37$ kg, placed at an angular position of $\approx 169.3°$ measured from the same datum.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the limitations of graphical methods for balancing rotating masses. Under what circumstances is the analytical method preferred?

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of Graphical Methods:**

1. **Accumulation of errors:** Each vector measurement introduces a small error, which propagates and accumulates through successive vector additions, especially in polygons with many sides.

2. **Dependence on drawing accuracy:** The accuracy is limited by the quality of the ruler, protractor, and the draughtsman's skill. Sharp angles between vectors lead to large uncertainties in the result.

3. **No algebraic verification:** It is difficult to check the result without repeating the entire construction, and any error becomes apparent only at the end.

4. **Difficulty with couple polygons:** When balancing masses in different planes, two polygons must be drawn, and the angular coupling between force and couple polygons can be confusing.

5. **Scale-dependent precision:** Choosing an inappropriate scale can lead to either a tiny, hard-to-read polygon or a too-large drawing that wastes space.

**Circumstances Where the Analytical Method is Preferred:**

1. **Large number of masses** (more than 5 or 6), where graphical construction becomes tedious and error-prone.

2. **High precision requirements**, such as in turbomachinery, where even small unbalanced forces are unacceptable.

3. **Computer-aided analysis** — the analytical formulation easily translates to spreadsheet or programming tools (in CS/automation contexts).

4. **Repeated calculations** during design iterations or optimization studies.

5. **Two balancing masses in different planes** where simultaneous vector equations must be solved — the analytical method gives exact trigonometric solutions without cumulative graphical error.

6. **Inverse problems** — where one wants to find the change in balancing mass if the original unbalance changes by a small amount.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Radii given in mm must be converted to meters before computing $m \cdot r$ in kg·m. Distances between planes must be in consistent units (all m or all mm).
>
> 2. **Sign errors in couple calculation:** Distances from the reference plane can be positive (to the right) or negative (to the left). Always adopt a strict sign convention and stick to it.
>
> 3. **Quadrant confusion in angle calculation:** When using $\tan^{-1}$, students often place the balancing mass in the wrong quadrant. Always check the signs of $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$ and $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$ first.
>
> 4. **Confusing single-plane and multi-plane problems:** A multi-plane problem CANNOT be solved by merely balancing each plane separately. Both force and couple conditions must be satisfied.
>
> 5. **Missing the factor $\omega^2$:** Since $\omega$ is common to all masses, the condition $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (instead of $\sum m_i \omega^2 r_i = 0$) is sufficient for **balancing**. Including $\omega^2$ earns no extra marks but forgetting the proportionality argument in conceptual questions costs marks.
>
> 6. **Direction convention:** When the question states "angular position measured clockwise from a certain axis," follow that convention precisely. Do not assume standard counter-clockwise convention.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for this topic:**

- [x] **Single-plane balance** requires only one balancing mass; the vector sum of all $m_i r_i$ must be zero.
- [x] **Multi-plane balance** requires a **minimum of two** balancing masses in two different axial planes.
- [x] **Static balance** = resultant force = 0; **Dynamic balance** = resultant couple = 0.
- [x] The proportionality $F \propto m \cdot r$ at constant $\omega$ simplifies graphical work.
- [x] In the **force polygon**, vectors are drawn head-to-tail; the closing side (reversed) gives the balancing force.
- [x] In the **couple polygon**, vectors are $m_i r_i \cdot l_i$ drawn in the **same angular directions** as the corresponding force vectors.
- [x] Reference plane for couple calculation can be **arbitrarily chosen**; choosing one of the balancing-mass planes simplifies equations.
- [x] The number of balancing masses (n = 2) equals the number of independent conditions (force closure + couple closure = 4 scalar equations → 4 unknowns: 2 magnitudes + 2 angles).
- [x] **Angles in standard position:** measured counter-clockwise from positive X-axis unless otherwise stated.
- [x] **Quadrant rule:** When $\tan\phi = Y/X$, the correct quadrant is determined by the signs of $X$ and $Y$, not just the ratio.
- [x] Practical applications: **crankshafts**, **turbine rotors**, **flywheels**, **pump impellers**, **propeller shafts**.
- [x] Common KTU 2024 keywords to remember: *centrifugal force, couple polygon, force polygon, balancing mass, plane of rotation, mass-radius product, static balance, dynamic balance, multi-plane balancing*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cams: Types of Cams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cams: Types of Cams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **cam** is a machine element having a curved surface (or a groove) which makes oscillating or reciprocating motion in a follower by direct contact. The cam, when rotating with uniform angular velocity (or sliding with uniform velocity), imparts a prescribed motion to a follower that oscillates or translates in a specific path.

A **follower** is the machine element that reciprocates, oscillates, or rotates in contact with the cam surface. The contact point between the cam and the follower is known as the **trace point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> "A cam is a rotating or sliding piece in a mechanical linkage, shaped so that it imparts a desired motion to a roller, a sliding member, or a piece of material in contact with it as it rotates or slides."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Taj Mahal Tour Bus & Curved Track**
> Imagine a small motor-operated tour bus moving at a *constant* speed along a flat, straight road. It can only travel horizontally. Now, suppose we place a curved ramp next to the road. The bus has a robotic arm whose tip touches the curve. As the bus moves at constant speed, the shape of the curve forces the arm to move **up and down** in a precise pattern. The road acts as the constant input motion, the curve acts as the **cam**, and the arm acts as the **follower**. The shape of the curve *programs* the output motion.

In a car engine, the camshaft is a rotating "curve" that pushes valves up and down in a precise pattern. The valve spring returns the valve. The cam converts *rotary motion → reciprocating motion* with an exact timing profile.

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters & Standard Units

The following standard geometric terms are universally used in KTU board answers:

| Term | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base Circle | $r_b$ | mm | Smallest circle drawn tangent to the cam profile |
| Pitch Circle | $r_p$ | mm | Circle through the pitch point of the follower |
| Prime Circle | $r'$ | mm | Smallest circle that the trace point traces |
| Lift (Stroke) | $S$ | mm | Maximum displacement of the follower |
| Pressure Angle | $\alpha$ | degrees | Angle between direction of follower motion and common normal |
| Eccentricity | $e$ | mm | Distance between cam axis and follower pivot |
| Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | rad/s | Speed of cam rotation |
| Pitch Curve | — | — | Locus of pitch point on cam surface |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cams (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

Cams are classified based on **shape**, **type of motion**, and **follower arrangement**.

### A. Classification Based on Shape

1. **Disc / Plate Cam (Eccentric Cam)**
   - The cam is a flat plate with an irregular profile. Operates by rotation about a fixed axis perpendicular to the plane. *Most common type.*
2. **Cylindrical / Barrel / Drum Cam**
   - A cylinder with a curved groove cut on its surface. Follower rides inside the groove. Motion imparted by cam rotation about its own axis.
3. **Conical Cam**
   - Cam profile cut on the surface of a cone. Follower oscillates as the cone rotates. Allows higher speeds and lower side thrust.
4. **Spherical / Globoidal Cam**
   - Cam profile cut on a sphere. Follower may be a knife-edge or roller. Used in automatic tool changers and gun-recoil mechanisms.
5. **Heart-Shaped Cam (Snail / Yoke Cam)**
   - Used in watches and clocks to convert rotary motion into uniform linear motion. The cam resembles a "heart" curve.
6. **Translating Cam (Wedge Cam)**
   - Cam moves in a straight line instead of rotating. The flat surface contacts a follower.

### B. Classification Based on Follower

1. **Radial Cam** — Follower moves radially (perpendicular to cam axis). Profile traced around the cam axis.
2. **Off-Set Cam** — Follower is offset from the cam axis by a distance $e$. Reduces the maximum pressure angle.

### C. Classification Based on Manner of Motion

1. **Uniform Velocity Cam** — Follower rises at a constant rate.
2. **Dwell Cam** — Follower remains at rest for a portion of cam rotation.

## 2.2 Classification of Followers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Followers are a mandatory 7- to 10-mark question in KTU Part B. Memorize the complete tree below.

### A. By Motion Type
- **Reciprocating (Sliding) Follower** — Moves in a straight line.
- **Oscillating (Rocking) Follower** — Rocks about a fixed pivot.

### B. By Shape of Contact
- **Knife-Edge Follower** — Point contact. High wear, simple.
- **Flat-Faced Follower** — Line contact. Can handle side thrust.
- **Roller Follower** — Point of contact replaced by a roller. Reduces wear.
- **Spherical-Faced (Mushroom) Follower** — Pointed spherical contact.

### C. By Position
- **In-line (Radial) Follower** — Line of motion passes through cam axis.
- **Off-set Follower** — Line of motion offset from cam axis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $S = h_{max}$ | Total lift (stroke) of the follower | mm |
| $r_p = r_b + r_r$ | Pitch radius (base radius + roller radius) | mm |
| $r' = r_b + r_f$ | Prime radius (base radius + follower axis offset) | mm |
| $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{v}{\omega \cdot (r_o + s)}$ | Pressure angle at any instant | degrees |
| $v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$ | Velocity of follower | mm/s |
| $a = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}$ | Acceleration of follower | mm/s² |
| $r_{min} = \dfrac{v}{\omega \cdot \tan(\alpha_{max})}$ | Minimum cam radius (to limit $\alpha$) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Cam mechanisms are the backbone of internal combustion engine valve trains, automatic knitting machines, printing presses, and sewing machines. By simply changing the cam profile, manufacturers can completely alter the motion timing without redesigning the rest of the system.

## 2.4 Comparison Table — Disc vs. Cylindrical vs. Conical Cam

| Property | Disc Cam | Cylindrical Cam | Conical Cam |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output follower motion | Reciprocating/oscillating | Reciprocating/oscillating | Reciprocating/oscillating |
| Surface profile | Flat plate | Groove on cylinder | Groove on cone |
| Side thrust on follower | Present | Reduced | Eliminated |
| Manufacturing ease | Easy | Moderate | Difficult |
| Typical application | IC engine valves | Watch escapement | Tool changers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration of a Cam-Driven Follower

Consider a disc cam rotating at a constant angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. The follower moves with displacement $s$ as a function of cam angle $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Time-Angle Conversion

Because the cam rotates at constant $\omega$, the cam angle $\theta$ and time $t$ are linked by:

$$
\theta = \omega \cdot t
$$

Hence, $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$.

### Step 2 — Follower Velocity (Differentiate Displacement w.r.t. Time)

The follower velocity is the rate of change of displacement with time:

$$
v = \frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{ds}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

Substituting $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$:

$$
v = \omega \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta}
$$

### Step 3 — Follower Acceleration (Differentiate Velocity w.r.t. Time)

$$
a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

Differentiating $v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$ with respect to $\theta$ (noting that $\omega$ is constant):

$$
\frac{dv}{d\theta} = \omega \cdot \frac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
a = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write the $\omega^2$ factor for acceleration. Many students drop it and lose 2 marks. The KTU board specifically tests whether you know that acceleration scales with the **square** of speed.

## 3.2 Derivation: Motion Profiles Used in Cams

For a rise of $h$ during cam angle $\beta$ (called the **outstroke angle**), we derive $s, v, a$ for the four standard motion profiles.

### Profile 1 — Uniform Velocity (Constant Velocity) Motion

Displacement is linear in $\theta$:

$$
s = h \cdot \frac{\theta}{\beta}
$$

Velocity (constant):

$$
v = \omega \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta} = \omega \cdot \frac{h}{\beta}
$$

Acceleration (zero during rise, infinite jerk at start/end):

$$
a = 0
$$

**Disadvantage:** Infinite jerk at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$. Not used in high-speed cams.

### Profile 2 — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

For SHM, the follower displacement is:

$$
s = \frac{h}{2} \left[ 1 - \cos\left( \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} \right) \right]
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\theta$:

$$
\frac{ds}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\beta} \cdot \sin\left( \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

Multiplying by $\omega$ for velocity:

$$
v = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta} \cdot \sin\left( \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

Differentiating once more w.r.t. $\theta$:

$$
\frac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2} = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi}{\beta}\right)^2 \cdot \cos\left( \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

Multiplying by $\omega^2$ for acceleration:

$$
a = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2 \beta^2} \cdot \cos\left( \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

**Property:** Acceleration is finite at start and end, but the **jerk is discontinuous** (still impulsive).

### Profile 3 — Cycloidal Motion (Used in KTU Numerical Problems)

Cycloidal motion is the **standard KTU answer** for "best cam motion" because it has zero jerk at start, end, and mid-point.

Displacement:

$$
s = h \left[ \frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \sin\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right) \right]
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{ds}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{\beta} \left[ 1 - \cos\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right) \right]
$$

Velocity:

$$
v = \frac{h \omega}{\beta} \left[ 1 - \cos\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right) \right]
$$

Second derivative:

$$
\frac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2} = \frac{2\pi h}{\beta^2} \cdot \sin\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

Acceleration:

$$
a = \frac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\beta^2} \cdot \sin\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

Third derivative (jerk) — vanishes everywhere, making cycloidal motion the smoothest:

$$
j = \frac{4\pi^2 h \omega^3}{\beta^3} \cdot \cos\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

### Profile 4 — Constant Acceleration and Deceleration (Parabolic Motion)

Divided into three segments:
- **First third** ($0 \le \theta \le \beta/3$): constant acceleration $a = c$.
- **Middle third** ($\beta/3 \le \theta \le 2\beta/3$): constant velocity.
- **Last third** ($2\beta/3 \le \theta \le \beta$): constant deceleration $a = -c$.

For $0 \le \theta \le \beta/3$:

$$
s = \frac{c \theta^2}{2}, \quad v = c\theta, \quad a = c
$$

For $\beta/3 \le \theta \le 2\beta/3$:

$$
s = \frac{c \beta}{2}\left(\theta - \frac{\beta}{6}\right), \quad v = \frac{c\beta}{2} = \text{const}, \quad a = 0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to apply the correct **boundary continuity** at $\theta = \beta/3$. The velocity must be continuous (no jump) at every transition, and acceleration must also be continuous for high-speed cams. Examiner deducts 2 marks per discontinuity.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Motion Profile | $s(\theta)$ form | $v$ range | $a$ range | Jerk | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform Velocity | Linear | Constant, finite | 0 during rise | Infinite at ends | Slow machines |
| Simple Harmonic | Cosine | Sine-shaped | Cosine-shaped | Finite jumps | IC engine valves |
| Cycloidal | Cosine-modulated | Cosine-shaped | Sine-shaped | **Zero at all critical points** | High-speed cams (best choice) |
| Constant Acc/Dec | Parabolic | Piecewise linear | Piecewise constant | Step changes | Punch press, low-speed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Cam-Follower Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Rotary or Linear Input Motion] --> camNodeA[Cam Driver Element]
    camNodeA --> contactA[Curved Contact Surface or Groove]
    contactA --> followerA[Follower Output Element]
    followerA --> outputA[Desired Reciprocating or Oscillating Motion]
    followerA -. force feedback .-> springA[Return Spring or Gravity]
    springA --> followerA

    style inputA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style camNodeA fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style contactA fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style followerA fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016
    style outputA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style springA fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Classification Tree of Cams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Cams] --> shapeA[Classification by Shape]
    rootA --> motionA[Classification by Manner of Motion]
    rootA --> followerA[Classification by Follower Type]

    shapeA --> discA[Disc or Plate Cam]
    shapeA --> cylinderA[Cylindrical or Barrel Cam]
    shapeA --> coneA[Conical Cam]
    shapeA --> sphereA[Spherical or Globoidal Cam]
    shapeA --> heartA[Heart Shaped Cam]
    shapeA --> wedgeA[Translating or Wedge Cam]

    motionA --> uniformA[Uniform Velocity Cam]
    motionA --> dwellA[Dwell Cam]

    followerA --> radialA[Radial or In line Cam]
    followerA --> offsetA[Off set Cam]

    style rootA fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style shapeA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style motionA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style followerA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Follower Type Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootB[Followers] --> motionB[By Motion]
    rootB --> contactB[By Contact Type]
    rootB --> positionB[By Position]

    motionB --> reciprocatingB[Reciprocating or Sliding Follower]
    motionB --> oscillatingB[Oscillating or Rocking Follower]

    contactB --> knifeB[Knife Edge Follower]
    contactB --> flatB[Flat Faced Follower]
    contactB --> rollerB[Roller Follower]
    contactB --> sphericalB[Spherical Faced Follower]

    positionB --> inlineB[In line or Radial Follower]
    positionB --> offsetB[Off set Follower]

    style rootB fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style motionB fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style contactB fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style positionB fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Cam Profile Generation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a cam and a follower. List the essential elements of a cam mechanism.**

**Model Answer (Expected KTU Pattern):**

A cam is a rotating or sliding mechanical member which pushes against a lever or follower to impart a predetermined motion. A follower is the output member that reciprocates, oscillates, or rotates in contact with the cam.

Essential elements of a cam mechanism:
1. **The driver (cam)** – provides the input motion.
2. **The follower** – receives the programmed motion.
3. **The frame** – provides the fixed reference for relative motion.

Sometimes a **return spring** is included to keep the follower in continuous contact with the cam.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between a knife-edge follower and a roller follower. State one disadvantage of the knife-edge follower.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Knife-Edge Follower | Roller Follower |
|---|---|---|
| Contact type | Point contact | Rolling contact |
| Wear | Very high | Low |
| Side thrust handling | Poor | Good |
| Use | Slow, low-load mechanisms | High-speed, high-load mechanisms |
| Manufacturing | Very simple | Slightly complex |

**Disadvantage of knife-edge follower:** It has a sharp edge that wears out rapidly under load, leading to early failure of the cam mechanism. Also, it cannot handle any side thrust on the follower stem.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**Q.** (a) Classify cams and followers with neat sketches. State at least **two engineering applications** of cam mechanisms. **(7 Marks)**

(b) For a disc cam rotating at $\omega = 20$ rad/s, the follower rises with **simple harmonic motion** through a stroke of $h = 30$ mm in an outstroke angle of $\beta = 60°$. Determine the **maximum velocity** and **maximum acceleration** of the follower. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Classification of Cams (with sketches referenced in words):**

1. **Disc Cam:** A flat plate rotating about a fixed axis perpendicular to its plane. The cam profile is cut on the edge of the disc.
2. **Cylindrical Cam:** A cylinder with a curved groove cut on its outer surface. The follower rides inside the groove.
3. **Conical Cam:** A cone-shaped body with the cam profile on its lateral surface.
4. **Heart-Shaped Cam:** Profile resembles a heart, used in watches and clocks.

**Classification of Followers (sketches referenced in words):**

- **Knife-Edge Follower:** Sharply pointed contact at a single point.
- **Roller Follower:** A cylindrical roller at the tip reduces friction and wear.
- **Flat-Faced Follower:** A flat disc at the follower tip contacts the cam.
- **Oscillating Follower:** Rocks about a fixed pivot instead of sliding.

**Two Engineering Applications:**
1. **Internal combustion engine valve gear** — cam opens and closes valves in a precise timing sequence.
2. **Automatic copying / turning lathes** — cam profiles the cross-slide to generate the desired workpiece contour.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given Data:**

$$
\omega = 20 \text{ rad/s}, \quad h = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad \beta = 60° = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Maximum Velocity (SHM profile):**

From the SHM derivation in Section 3.2:

$$
v = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta} \cdot \sin\left( \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

The sine function has a maximum value of 1. Therefore:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{\pi \times 30 \times 20}{2 \times (\pi/3)} = \frac{30 \times 20}{2/3} = 900 \text{ mm/s}
$$

So $v_{max} = 900$ mm/s $= 0.9$ m/s.

**Maximum Acceleration (SHM profile):**

From the SHM derivation:

$$
a = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2 \beta^2} \cdot \cos\left( \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

The cosine function has a maximum magnitude of 1. Therefore:

$$
a_{max} = \frac{\pi^2 h \omega^2}{2 \beta^2}
$$

Substituting values (converting $\beta$ to radians):

$$
a_{max} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 30 \times 20^2}{2 \times (\pi/3)^2} = \frac{30 \times 400}{2 \times \pi^2/9} = \frac{12000 \times 9}{2\pi^2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
a_{max} = \frac{108000}{2 \times 9.8696} = \frac{108000}{19.739} \approx 5470.6 \text{ mm/s}^2
$$

So $a_{max} \approx 5.47$ m/s².

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**Q.** (a) Explain with neat sketches the **disc cam with knife-edge follower**, **disc cam with roller follower**, and **cylindrical cam**. Why is the roller follower preferred over the knife-edge follower in high-speed machinery? **(7 Marks)**

(b) A cam rotates at $N = 200$ rpm and imparts **cycloidal motion** to a follower with a lift of $h = 40$ mm in an outstroke angle of $\beta = 90°$. Determine the **maximum velocity**, **maximum acceleration**, and **time of outstroke**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Disc Cam with Knife-Edge Follower:**
- A flat circular disc rotates about a fixed axis.
- The follower has a sharp pointed tip that slides on the cam profile.
- Simple, low cost, but high wear at the contact point.

**Disc Cam with Roller Follower:**
- Same rotating disc.
- A cylindrical roller is mounted at the follower tip.
- The roller rolls along the cam profile, reducing friction and wear.

**Cylindrical Cam:**
- A solid cylinder rotates about its own axis.
- A curved groove is cut on the cylinder surface.
- The follower roller rides inside the groove.

**Why roller follower is preferred in high-speed machinery:**

1. **Rolling contact reduces friction** dramatically compared to sliding contact of a knife edge.
2. **Wear is distributed** over the rolling surface, increasing cam life.
3. **Can handle side thrust** without scoring the cam profile.
4. **Reduces noise and vibration** at high rotational speeds.
5. **Maintains profile accuracy** over long service life.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given Data:**

$$
N = 200 \text{ rpm}, \quad h = 40 \text{ mm}, \quad \beta = 90° = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Angular Velocity $\omega$:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = \frac{20\pi}{3} \text{ rad/s} \approx 20.94 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Velocity (Cycloidal Profile):**

From the cycloidal derivation in Section 3.2:

$$
v = \frac{h \omega}{\beta} \left[ 1 - \cos\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right) \right]
$$

The term $\left[1 - \cos(\cdot)\right]$ has a maximum value of 2 (when $\cos = -1$). Therefore:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{2 h \omega}{\beta}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{2 \times 40 \times (20\pi/3)}{\pi/2} = \frac{2 \times 40 \times 20\pi/3 \times 2}{\pi} = \frac{3200}{3} \text{ mm/s}
$$

So $v_{max} \approx 1066.67$ mm/s $\approx 1.067$ m/s.

**Step 3 — Maximum Acceleration (Cycloidal Profile):**

From the cycloidal derivation:

$$
a = \frac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\beta^2} \cdot \sin\left( \frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} \right)
$$

The sine function has a maximum value of 1. Therefore:

$$
a_{max} = \frac{2\pi h \omega^2}{\beta^2}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
a_{max} = \frac{2\pi \times 40 \times (20\pi/3)^2}{(\pi/2)^2} = \frac{2\pi \times 40 \times 400\pi^2/9}{\pi^2/4} = \frac{32000\pi^3/9 \times 4}{\pi^2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
a_{max} = \frac{128000 \pi}{9} = \frac{128000 \times 3.1416}{9} \approx 44670 \text{ mm/s}^2
$$

So $a_{max} \approx 44.67$ m/s².

**Step 4 — Time of Outstroke:**

The outstroke time $t_o$ is the time taken for the cam to rotate through $\beta$:

$$
t_o = \frac{\beta}{\omega} = \frac{\pi/2}{20\pi/3} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{20} = \frac{3}{40} \text{ s} = 0.075 \text{ s}
$$

So $t_o = 0.075$ seconds or **75 milliseconds**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Critical Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\omega^2$ factor in acceleration** — KTU examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this. Acceleration is not just the second derivative of $s$ w.r.t. $\theta$; it is $\omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}$.
> 2. **Mixing degrees and radians** — The angle $\beta$ **must** be in radians when used in sine/cosine arguments. KTU 2024 board specifically checks this.
> 3. **Confusing velocity and acceleration peaks** — In SHM, velocity peaks at mid-stroke while acceleration peaks at start and end. In cycloidal motion, both velocity and acceleration peak at mid-stroke.
> 4. **Omitting units in final answer** — Always write **mm/s** for velocity and **mm/s²** for acceleration. Missing units = 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Wrong maximum of trig function** — For SHM, $v_{max}$ uses $\sin = 1$; for cycloidal, $v_{max}$ uses $(1 - \cos) = 2$. Confusing these is a common 2-mark error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **Mandatory KTU High-Yield Points:**

- **Cam** = driver element with curved profile; **Follower** = output element in contact with cam.
- **Disc cam** is the most common type; **Cylindrical cam** is used when side thrust must be avoided.
- **Knife-edge follower** has highest wear; **Roller follower** is preferred for high-speed, high-load applications.
- **Flat-faced follower** can handle side thrust; **Oscillating follower** rocks about a pivot.
- **Pressure angle** $\alpha$ = angle between follower's direction of motion and common normal at the contact point. It must be limited to about $30°$ for efficient transmission.
- **Base circle** is the smallest circle tangent to the cam profile; **Pitch circle** passes through the pitch point of the follower.
- **Off-set follower** reduces the maximum pressure angle and is preferred for better force transmission.
- **Velocity** $v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$; **Acceleration** $a = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}$ (always remember the $\omega^2$ factor).
- **Uniform velocity** has infinite jerk — never use for high-speed cams.
- **Simple harmonic motion (SHM)** has finite acceleration at endpoints but discontinuous jerk.
- **Cycloidal motion** is the **best** profile for high-speed cams because it has **zero jerk** at all critical points (start, mid, end).
- **Constant acceleration/deceleration** (parabolic) motion has step changes in acceleration and jerk — use only for slow machines.
- **Outstroke** = rise of follower; **Return stroke** = return; **Dwell** = rest period.
- For a disc cam with roller follower, the cam profile = **pitch curve offset by roller radius** (in the direction normal to the profile).
- **Minimum cam radius** for a given follower is governed by the maximum allowable pressure angle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Followers; Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Followers; Displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Cam–Follower Pair: Foundational Definition

A **cam** is a rotating (or sliding) machine element having a curved or specially profiled working surface, designed to impart a prescribed motion (displacement, velocity, and acceleration) to a second element called the **follower**. The follower maintains contact with the cam either through gravity, a spring, or a positive mechanical lock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A cam mechanism is a higher pair kinematic pair in which the motion of the follower is completely determined by the geometry (profile) of the cam. This makes it a **function-generating mechanism** — the follower motion is a *function* of the cam rotation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Music Box Pin Drum"

Imagine the rotating drum of a music box. As the cylinder turns, little pins (which act as **miniature cams**) lift the steel comb teeth (the **followers**). Each pin's height determines how far that particular tooth rises (its displacement), and the spacing of pins determines the timing. A cam mechanism is exactly this idea — the cam is the rotating input, and the follower is the element whose up-and-down (or oscillating) motion you wish to programme.

> [!NOTE]
> The **cam** is the **input member** (driver), and the **follower** is the **output member**. The *shape* of the cam profile is the *program* of motion.

### 1.3 Classification of Followers — Overview

KTU examiners most frequently frame questions around **two classification axes**:

**A. Classification by the Shape of the Contacting Surface (Foot of Follower):**

1. **Knife-edge Follower** — A sharp knife edge bears against the cam.
2. **Flat-faced Follower** — A flat face (foot) bears against the cam.
3. **Roller Follower** — A roller rotates freely at the foot of the follower.
4. **Spherical-faced (Mushroom) Follower** — A spherical foot bears against the cam.

**B. Classification by the Type of Follower Motion:**

1. **Translating (Reciprocating) Follower** — Slides in straight-line guides.
2. **Oscillating (Pivoted) Follower** — Rotates about a fixed pivot.

**C. Classification by the Position of the Cam Axis:**

1. **Radial Follower** — Axis of follower passes through cam centre.
2. **Offset Follower** — Axis of follower is offset from cam centre.

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Explanation of Each Follower Type

| Follower Type | Real-world Analogy | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Knife-edge** | The tip of a needle pressing on a rotating disc | Rarely used in industry due to high wear |
| **Flat-faced** | A flat wood block sliding on a tilted surface | Automotive fuel pumps, printing presses |
| **Roller** | A ball-bearing rolling on a contour | Most common — IC engine valves, sewing machines |
| **Mushroom** | A door stopper with a rounded head | High-stress valve applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **roller follower** is the *industry workhorse* because rolling contact drastically reduces wear compared to sliding contact. The **flat-faced follower** is favoured when the cam must resist side thrust or operate with very high speeds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Follower Type

#### 2.1.1 Knife-Edge Follower
- **Geometry:** A sharp wedge contacts the cam at a single theoretical point.
- **Advantage:** Simplest geometry; mathematically easiest to derive cam profile.
- **Disadvantage:** Extremely high contact stress → rapid wear; cannot transmit side loads; the sharp edge tends to dig into the cam.
- **KTU Note:** Generally avoided in real machines; used mainly as a *theoretical reference* in textbooks.

#### 2.1.2 Flat-Faced (Mushroom-type, when face is planar) Follower
- **Geometry:** The follower foot is a flat plate, and the cam surface is in sliding contact with this plate.
- **Advantage:** Can handle side thrust because the face is broad; compact design; useful for high speeds since the contact region is well defined.
- **Disadvantage:** Sliding friction across the entire face causes wear; cam profile must be generated carefully.
- **KTU Note:** When designing, the cam profile is the *envelope* of the flat face positions — not simply traced from a point.

#### 2.1.3 Roller Follower ⭐ (Most Important for KTU)
- **Geometry:** A steel roller is mounted on a pin at the end of the follower stem; the roller rolls without slipping on the cam surface.
- **Advantage:** Rolling contact → minimal wear; can handle higher speeds and loads.
- **Disadvantage:** Roller adds mass and inertia; the contact stress depends on roller diameter.
- **KTU Note:** The cam profile is generated such that its **base curve (pitch curve)** is what is mathematically designed; the actual cam profile is offset by the roller radius.

#### 2.1.4 Spherical-faced (Mushroom) Follower
- **Geometry:** A convex spherical head (like a button) contacts the cam.
- **Advantage:** Self-aligning, useful when the cam-follower axis may deviate slightly from true alignment.
- **Disadvantage:** Contact stress is high due to small contact area.
- **KTU Note:** Used in some valve gear applications.

#### 2.1.5 Translating vs Oscillating Follower
- **Translating follower:** Moves in pure translation; the displacement is a linear function along a fixed line. Common in poppet valves.
- **Oscillating follower:** Pivots about a fixed point; displacement is an angular function. Used in mechanisms where space is constrained.

#### 2.1.6 Radial vs Offset Follower
- **Radial follower:** Axis passes through cam centre. Mathematical analysis is simpler because the pressure angle is symmetric.
- **Offset follower:** Axis is offset by a distance `e`. The pressure angle is *reduced* on one side but increased on the other; this is exploited to obtain smoother motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The choice of follower type **directly influences the cam profile generation method**. For a roller follower, the cam profile is offset by the roller radius from the base (pitch) curve. For a knife-edge follower, the cam profile *is* the pitch curve.

### 2.2 Displacement Diagrams — Conceptual Foundation

A **displacement diagram** is a plot of the **displacement `y` of the follower** (vertical axis) versus the **cam rotation angle `θ`** (horizontal axis). It is the *graphical specification* of the desired follower motion.

**The Four Standard Motions used in KTU Examinations:**

1. **Uniform Velocity** (Uniform motion)
2. **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**
3. **Cycloidal Motion**
4. **Constant Acceleration and Retardation** (Parabolic motion)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Displacement, Velocity & Acceleration

Let `h` = total follower lift (stroke), `β₀` = angular interval of the outstroke (in radians), `ω` = angular velocity of cam (rad/s), `θ` = instantaneous cam angle measured from start of outstroke.

> [!WARNING]
> In every formula below, ensure that `θ` is expressed in **radians** when multiplied by `ω`, and that the angles of each phase sum correctly to `2π` for the full cam revolution. KTU examiners frequently test whether students respect the **boundary conditions** at the start and end of each phase.

| Motion Type | Displacement `y(θ)` | Velocity `v = dy/dt` | Acceleration `a = d²y/dt²` | Max Velocity | Max Acceleration |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Velocity** | `y = (h / β₀) · θ` | `v = (h · ω) / β₀` (constant) | `a = 0` | `v_max = (h · ω) / β₀` | `a_max = 0` |
| **Simple Harmonic Motion** | `y = (h/2) · [1 − cos(πθ / β₀)]` | `v = (π h ω) / (2 β₀) · sin(πθ / β₀)` | `a = −(π² h ω²) / (2 β₀²) · cos(πθ / β₀)` | `v_max = (π h ω) / (2 β₀)` | `a_max = (π² h ω²) / (2 β₀²)` |
| **Cycloidal Motion** | `y = h · [θ / β₀ − (1/(2π)) · sin(2πθ / β₀)]` | `v = (h ω / β₀) · [1 − cos(2πθ / β₀)]` | `a = (2π h ω² / β₀²) · sin(2πθ / β₀)` | `v_max = (2 h ω) / β₀` | `a_max = (2π h ω²) / β₀²` |
| **Parabolic — Accn Phase** (`0 ≤ θ ≤ β₀/2`) | `y = 2 h · (θ / β₀)²` | `v = (4 h ω / β₀²) · θ` | `a = (4 h ω²) / β₀²` (constant) | `v_max = (2 h ω) / β₀` | `a_max = (4 h ω²) / β₀²` |
| **Parabolic — Retardn Phase** (`β₀/2 ≤ θ ≤ β₀`) | `y = h − 2 h · (1 − θ/β₀)²` | `v = (4 h ω / β₀²) · (1 − θ/β₀)` | `a = −(4 h ω²) / β₀²` | same | same magnitude |

### 2.4 Comparative Engineering Appraisal

| Criterion | Uniform Velocity | SHM | Cycloidal | Parabolic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Velocity** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | Moderate-High |
| **Maximum Acceleration** | Infinite at start & end ⛔ | Finite | Finite & bounded | Finite but discontinuous in derivative |
| **Jerk (da/dt)** | Infinite at boundaries | Cosinusoidal | Bounded & continuous | Step function at mid-stroke |
| **Practical Suitability** | Poor (impact at boundaries) | Good for low-speed | Excellent for high-speed | Moderate |
| **KTU Verdict** | Rarely used | Often used | Best for IC engine valves | Commonly used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** The **cycloidal motion** is the *theoretically perfect* motion because both the acceleration and the **jerk (rate of change of acceleration)** are continuous. This eliminates shock loading, which is critical at high cam speeds (e.g., racing engine valves). **SHM** is a close second and is easier to manufacture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below follow the **standard KTU board convention**: the outstroke occurs during cam rotation from `θ = 0` to `θ = β₀`, the dwell from `β₀` to `β₁`, the return stroke from `β₁` to `β₂`, and the final dwell from `β₂` to `2π`. We assume the follower moves with the prescribed motion over `0 ≤ θ ≤ β₀`.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uniform Velocity Motion

**Step 1: Set the boundary conditions.**
At the start of the outstroke (`θ = 0`), displacement `y = 0`. At the end (`θ = β₀`), displacement `y = h`.

**Step 2: Assume a linear relationship.**
For uniform velocity, displacement must be a linear function of `θ` because `v = dy/dt = (dy/dθ) · ω` must be constant.

$$
y = A \cdot \theta + B
$$

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions.**

Apply `y(0) = 0`:
$$
0 = A \cdot 0 + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 0
$$

Apply `y(β₀) = h`:
$$
h = A \cdot \beta_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{h}{\beta_0}
$$

**Step 4: Write the displacement equation.**

$$
\boxed{y = \frac{h}{\beta_0} \cdot \theta}
$$

**Step 5: Differentiate to obtain velocity.**

$$
v = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{h}{\beta_0} \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 6: Differentiate again for acceleration.**

$$
a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2 y}{d\theta^2} \cdot \omega^2 = 0
$$

**Step 7: Identify the flaw.**
Although `v` is finite, the acceleration is *theoretically infinite* at the boundaries `θ = 0` and `θ = β₀` because the velocity changes instantaneously from 0 to `v`. This makes uniform velocity **unsuitable** for high-speed cams.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

**Step 1: Visualize the construction.**
SHM is constructed by projecting uniform circular motion onto a diameter. Consider a circle of diameter `h`. A point `P` moves on this circle with uniform angular velocity `πω/β₀` (chosen so that the point covers a semicircle during the outstroke). The vertical projection of `P` onto the diameter gives the follower displacement.

**Step 2: Express the projection.**

Let `φ` be the angle subtended by the radius at the centre of the SHM circle. For the projection to start at 0 and reach `h`, `φ` must vary from `π/2` to `3π/2`. Define `φ = π/2 + πθ/β₀`.

The displacement `y` is measured from the bottom of the diameter (i.e., `y = 0` when `φ = π/2`):

$$
y = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{h}{2} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**Step 3: Substitute `φ`.**

$$
y = \frac{h}{2} - \frac{h}{2} \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

Using `cos(π/2 + x) = −sin(x)`:

$$
y = \frac{h}{2} + \frac{h}{2} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Convert to the standard form using a phase shift.**

Apply the identity `sin(x) = cos(π/2 − x)`:

$$
\boxed{y = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]}
$$

**Step 5: Differentiate to obtain velocity.**

$$
v = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

Compute `dy/dθ`:

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right) \cdot \frac{\pi}{\beta_0}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v = \frac{\pi h \, \omega}{2 \beta_0} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

**Step 6: Differentiate to obtain acceleration.**

$$
a = \frac{dv}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

$$
\frac{dv}{d\theta} = \frac{\pi h \, \omega}{2 \beta_0} \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right) \cdot \frac{\pi}{\beta_0}
$$

$$
\boxed{a = -\frac{\pi^2 h \, \omega^2}{2 \beta_0^{2}} \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)}
$$

**Step 7: Locate the maximum values.**

Velocity is maximum when `sin(πθ/β₀) = 1` ⟹ `v_max = (π h ω) / (2 β₀)`.
Acceleration is maximum when `cos(πθ/β₀) = −1` ⟹ `a_max = (π² h ω²) / (2 β₀²)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** At the **start** (`θ = 0`) and **end** (`θ = β₀`) of the outstroke, acceleration has magnitudes `−(π² h ω²)/(2β₀²)` and `+(π² h ω²)/(2β₀²)`. This finite acceleration is what makes SHM usable in practice.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cycloidal Motion

**Step 1: The cycloid construction.**
A **cycloid** is the curve traced by a point on the circumference of a circle rolling without slipping along a straight line. If the diameter of the rolling circle is `h` and the line length is `πh` (so the rolling circle completes one full revolution over the outstroke), the follower displacement is the *ordinate of the cycloid*.

**Step 2: Parametric equations of the cycloid.**

Let the rolling circle have radius `r = h / π` (so its diameter `2r = 2h/π` — note: this ensures the maximum ordinate equals `h`). Parametric equations:

$$
x = r(\phi - \sin\phi), \qquad y = r(1 - \cos\phi)
$$

**Step 3: Express in terms of cam angle `θ`.**

Since the circle completes one revolution as `θ` goes from `0` to `β₀`, we have `φ = 2π θ / β₀`. Also `x = θ · (π r) / β₀` (the distance rolled equals the arc), so `r(φ − sin φ) = π r θ / β₀`. Both sides are satisfied when we use the standard cycloid identity.

The displacement of the follower is the vertical rise, scaled by the ratio `h / 2r = h / (2h/π) = π/2`:

$$
y = \frac{h}{2r} \cdot r(1 - \cos\phi) = \frac{h}{2}(1 - \cos\phi)
$$

Wait — we need `y` to reach `h` when `θ = β₀`. Substituting `φ = 2π`:

$$
y = \frac{h}{2}(1 - \cos 2\pi) = \frac{h}{2}(1 - 1) = 0
$$

This is wrong because the standard cycloid returns to zero at `φ = 2π`. The trick is to use the **flank of the cycloid**, but offset. The standard result used in KTU textbooks is:

$$
y = h \cdot \left[\frac{\theta}{\beta_0} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4: Verify boundary conditions.**

At `θ = 0`: `y = h · [0 − 0] = 0` ✓
At `θ = β₀`: `y = h · [1 − (1/(2π)) · sin(2π)] = h · [1 − 0] = h` ✓

**Step 5: Differentiate for velocity.**

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} = h \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\beta_0} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right) \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\beta_0}\right]
$$

$$
\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{\beta_0} \cdot \left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

Multiply by `ω`:

$$
\boxed{v = \frac{h \omega}{\beta_0} \cdot \left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]}
$$

**Step 6: Differentiate for acceleration.**

$$
\frac{dv}{d\theta} = \frac{h \omega}{\beta_0} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right) \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\beta_0}
$$

Multiply by `ω`:

$$
\boxed{a = \frac{2\pi h \omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)}
$$

**Step 7: Locate extrema.**

- Maximum velocity occurs when `cos(2πθ/β₀) = −1` ⟹ `v_max = 2 h ω / β₀` (at `θ = β₀/2`).
- Maximum acceleration occurs when `sin(2πθ/β₀) = ±1` ⟹ `a_max = 2π h ω² / β₀²` (at `θ = β₀/4` and `θ = 3β₀/4`).

**Step 8: Compute the jerk (rate of change of acceleration).**

$$
j = \frac{da}{dt} = \frac{da}{d\theta} \cdot \omega = \frac{(2\pi)^{2} h \omega^{3}}{\beta_0^{3}} \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

Notice that jerk is **finite and continuous everywhere** — this is why cycloidal motion is the *gold standard* for high-speed cams.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Constant Acceleration and Retardation (Parabolic)

**Step 1: Concept.**
The outstroke is split into two equal halves:
- **Acceleration half:** `0 ≤ θ ≤ β₀/2`, follower accelerates uniformly from rest.
- **Retardation half:** `β₀/2 ≤ θ ≤ β₀`, follower decelerates uniformly to rest.

**Step 2: Acceleration phase derivation.**

For uniform acceleration from rest, displacement is proportional to `θ²`:

$$
y = A \cdot \theta^{2}
$$

Apply boundary condition at `θ = β₀/2`, `y = h/2`:

$$
\frac{h}{2} = A \cdot \left(\frac{\beta_0}{2}\right)^{2} = A \cdot \frac{\beta_0^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{2h}{\beta_0^{2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{y = 2h \cdot \left(\frac{\theta}{\beta_0}\right)^{2}}
$$

Differentiating:

$$
v = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{d\theta} \cdot \omega = \frac{4 h \omega}{\beta_0^{2}} \cdot \theta
$$

$$
a = \frac{dv}{d\theta} \cdot \omega = \frac{4 h \omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}
$$

**Step 3: Retardation phase derivation.**

By symmetry, displacement is:

$$
y = h - 2h \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\theta}{\beta_0}\right)^{2}
$$

Differentiating:

$$
v = \frac{4 h \omega}{\beta_0^{2}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

$$
a = -\frac{4 h \omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}}
$$

**Step 4: Note the discontinuity in jerk.**

At `θ = β₀/2`, acceleration abruptly switches from `+4hω²/β₀²` to `−4hω²/β₀²`. Hence the **jerk is a step function**, which is why parabolic motion is slightly inferior to cycloidal at very high speeds.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A cam rotates at **240 rpm** and gives a follower a lift of **30 mm** during an outstroke of **60°**, followed by a dwell. The follower moves with **simple harmonic motion**. Find the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration during the outstroke.

**Step 1: Convert given quantities.**

- `h = 30 mm = 0.030 m`
- `ω = 2π · 240 / 60 = 8π rad/s ≈ 25.133 rad/s`
- `β₀ = 60° = π/3 rad ≈ 1.0472 rad`

**Step 2: Compute maximum velocity.**

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta_0} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.030 \cdot 8\pi}{2 \cdot (\pi/3)}
$$

Simplify:

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{8 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.030}{2 \pi / 3} = \frac{8 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.030 \cdot 3}{2 \pi} = \frac{24 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.030}{2 \pi} = 12 \pi \cdot 0.030
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{\max} = 0.36 \pi \approx 1.131 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute maximum acceleration.**

$$
a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta_0^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \cdot 0.030 \cdot (8\pi)^{2}}{2 \cdot (\pi/3)^{2}}
$$

Simplify:

$$
a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} \cdot 0.030 \cdot 64 \pi^{2}}{2 \cdot \pi^{2} / 9} = \frac{64 \pi^{4} \cdot 0.030 \cdot 9}{2 \pi^{2}} = \frac{576 \pi^{4} \cdot 0.030}{2 \pi^{2}}
$$

$$
a_{\max} = 288 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.030 = 8.64 \pi^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{\max} \approx 8.64 \cdot 9.8696 \approx 85.27 \text{ m/s}^{2}}
$$
