## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cam–Follower Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    camRoot["CAM FOLLOWER SYSTEM"]
    camRoot --> clsA["CLASSIFICATION AXIS A"]
    camRoot --> clsB["CLASSIFICATION AXIS B"]
    camRoot --> clsC["CLASSIFICATION AXIS C"]

    clsA --> type1["BY CONTACT SHAPE"]
    clsA --> type2["BY MOTION TYPE"]
    clsA --> type3["BY AXIS OFFSET"]

    type1 --> folA["KNIFE EDGE FOLLOWER"]
    type1 --> folB["FLAT FACED FOLLOWER"]
    type1 --> folC["ROLLER FOLLOWER"]
    type1 --> folD["SPHERICAL MUSHROOM FOLLOWER"]

    type2 --> motA["TRANSLATING RECIPROCATING"]
    type2 --> motB["OSCILLATING PIVOTED"]

    type3 --> axA["RADIAL AXIS THROUGH CAM CENTER"]
    type3 --> axB["OFFSET AXIS PARALLEL TO CAM CENTER"]

    folC --> noteC["KTU MOST FREQUENT TYPE IN IC ENGINE VALVES"]
    folB --> noteB["USED IN FUEL PUMPS AND HIGH SPEED CAMS"]
    motA --> noteMA["MOST COMMON OUTPUT MOTION"]
    motB --> noteMB["USED WHERE STROKE IS LIMITED BY SPACE"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Displacement Diagram Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["INPUT SPECIFICATIONS"]
    inA --> in1["FOLLOWER LIFT H"]
    inA --> in2["OUTSTROKE ANGLE BETA0"]
    inA --> in3["CAM SPEED N RPM"]

    in1 --> procA["SELECT MOTION LAW"]
    in2 --> procA
    in3 --> procA

    procA --> law1["UNIFORM VELOCITY"]
    procA --> law2["SIMPLE HARMONIC"]
    procA --> law3["CYCLOIDAL"]
    procA --> law4["PARABOLIC ACCN RETARDN"]

    law1 --> outA["DISPLACEMENT Y THETA"]
    law2 --> outA
    law3 --> outA
    law4 --> outA

    outA --> stepV["DIFFERENTIATE TO GET VELOCITY"]
    outA --> stepA["DIFFERENTIATE AGAIN TO GET ACCELERATION"]

    stepV --> final["OUTPUT VMAX AND AMAX FOR DESIGN VERIFICATION"]
    stepA --> final

    final --> camProf["GENERATE CAM PROFILE USING PITCH CURVE AND OFFSET"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Cycloidal Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cycRoot["CYCLOIDAL MOTION CONSTRUCTION"]
    cycRoot --> cycA["DRAW ROLLING CIRCLE OF RADIUS H OVER PI"]
    cycA --> cycB["DIVIDE CIRCUMFERENCE INTO EQUAL ARCS"]
    cycB --> cycC["ROLL CIRCLE ALONG STRAIGHT LINE OF LENGTH BETA0"]
    cycC --> cycD["MARK SUCCESSIVE POSITIONS OF THE TRACING POINT"]
    cycD --> cycE["JOIN SMOOTH CURVE THROUGH TRACING POINTS"]

    cycF --> cycG["OBTAIN Y VS THETA PLOT FOR DESIGN"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Classify followers used in cam mechanisms. Compare roller follower and flat-faced follower.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Followers are classified based on (i) shape of contacting surface — knife-edge, flat-faced, roller, spherical; (ii) type of motion — translating, oscillating; and (iii) axis position — radial, offset.

| Parameter | Roller Follower | Flat-Faced Follower |
|---|---|---|
| Contact | Rolling | Sliding |
| Wear | Very low | High |
| Side thrust capacity | Limited (needs guide) | Excellent |
| Cam profile | Offset from pitch curve | Envelope of face positions |
| Typical use | IC engine valves | Printing presses, fuel pumps |

*(Full 3-mark answer requires the classification list + comparison table.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define displacement diagram. List the different types of motions used in cam design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A displacement diagram is a plot of follower displacement `y` versus cam rotation angle `θ`. It graphically specifies the desired motion of the follower and is the starting point for cam profile design.

The four standard motions used in cam design are:

1. **Uniform velocity** motion
2. **Simple harmonic** motion (SHM)
3. **Cycloidal** motion
4. **Constant acceleration and retardation** (parabolic) motion

Among these, cycloidal motion is preferred for high-speed cams because the acceleration and jerk are both bounded and continuous.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of followers used in cam mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A follower is the output member of a cam mechanism. The shape of its contacting surface and the type of its motion classify the follower.

**Knife-edge follower (1 mark):**
A sharp edge contacts the cam at a single point. Simple mathematically but impractical due to high wear and inability to handle side thrust.

**Flat-faced follower (1.5 marks):**
The follower foot is a flat plate. The cam profile is the envelope of all positions of the flat plate. Advantages: handles side thrust, good for high speed. Disadvantage: sliding friction across face.

**Roller follower (2 marks):**
A roller is mounted on a pin at the foot of the follower. Rolling contact minimises wear. The cam profile is offset from the pitch (base) curve by the roller radius. Most widely used in IC engine valves.

**Spherical (mushroom) follower (1 mark):**
A convex spherical foot provides self-alignment. Used in valve gears of some engines.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The roller follower is the industry standard; flat-faced is used when side thrust is high; knife-edge is largely theoretical.

---

**(b)** A cam rotates at **300 rpm** and drives a follower with **simple harmonic motion**. The lift is **40 mm** during an outstroke of **90°**. Determine (i) the maximum velocity during the outstroke, (ii) the maximum acceleration during the outstroke, and (iii) the time required for the outstroke. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
- `N = 300 rpm`
- `h = 40 mm = 0.040 m`
- `β₀ = 90° = π/2 rad`

**Step 1: Compute cam angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 300}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute time for outstroke.**

$$
t = \frac{\beta_0}{\omega} = \frac{\pi/2}{10\pi} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{out}} = 0.05 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum velocity (SHM formula).**

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta_0} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.040 \cdot 10\pi}{2 \cdot (\pi/2)}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{10 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.040}{\pi} = 10 \pi \cdot 0.040 = 0.4 \pi
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{\max} \approx 1.257 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum acceleration (SHM formula).**

$$
a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta_0^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \cdot 0.040 \cdot (10\pi)^{2}}{2 \cdot (\pi/2)^{2}}
$$

$$
a_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} \cdot 0.040 \cdot 100 \pi^{2}}{2 \cdot \pi^{2}/4} = \frac{100 \pi^{4} \cdot 0.040 \cdot 4}{2 \pi^{2}} = \frac{400 \pi^{4} \cdot 0.040}{2 \pi^{2}}
$$

$$
a_{\max} = 200 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.040 = 8 \pi^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{\max} \approx 78.96 \text{ m/s}^{2}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for displacement, velocity, and acceleration of a follower moving with **cycloidal motion** during the outstroke of angle `β₀` and total lift `h`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cycloid construction (1 mark).**
A cycloid is traced by a point on a circle of diameter `2r = 2h/π` rolling without slipping along a straight line of length `πh`. As the cam rotates through `β₀`, the circle completes one revolution. The vertical rise gives the follower displacement.

**Step 2: Displacement (2 marks).**
By the standard cycloid equation, the follower displacement is:

$$
y = h \cdot \left[\frac{\theta}{\beta_0} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Verification of boundary conditions:**
- At `θ = 0`: `y = h · [0 − 0] = 0` ✓
- At `θ = β₀`: `y = h · [1 − (1/(2π)) · sin(2π)] = h` ✓

**Step 3: Velocity (2 marks).**
Differentiating `y` with respect to `θ` and multiplying by `ω`:

$$
v = \frac{h \omega}{\beta_0} \cdot \left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4: Acceleration (2 marks).**
Differentiating once more:

$$
a = \frac{2\pi h \omega^{2}}{\beta_0^{2}} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta_0}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.**
Both `v` and `a` are continuous functions of `θ`, with `v_max = 2hω/β₀` at mid-stroke and `a_max = 2πhω²/β₀²` at quarter and three-quarter stroke. Cycloidal motion ensures bounded jerk, making it ideal for high-speed cams.

---

**(b)** A cam with **outstroke angle 75°**, **dwell 30°**, **return stroke 60°**, and **dwell 195°** operates a roller follower through **40 mm** of lift. The follower moves with **SHM during outstroke** and **uniform velocity during return**. The cam runs at **150 rpm**. Determine the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration during the outstroke and the maximum velocity during the return stroke. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
- `β₀_out = 75° = 5π/12 rad`
- `h = 0.040 m`
- `ω = 2π · 150/60 = 5π rad/s`
- Return stroke `β_r = 60° = π/3 rad`

**Part i — Outstroke maximum velocity (SHM):**

$$
v_{\max, \text{out}} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta_0} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.040 \cdot 5\pi}{2 \cdot 5\pi/12}
$$

$$
v_{\max, \text{out}} = \frac{5\pi^{2} \cdot 0.040 \cdot 12}{10\pi} = \frac{60 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.040}{10\pi} = 6\pi \cdot 0.040 = 0.24\pi
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{\max, \text{out}} \approx 0.754 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Part ii — Outstroke maximum acceleration (SHM):**

$$
a_{\max, \text{out}} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta_0^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \cdot 0.040 \cdot 25 \pi^{2}}{2 \cdot (5\pi/12)^{2}}
$$

$$
a_{\max, \text{out}} = \frac{25 \pi^{4} \cdot 0.040 \cdot 144}{2 \cdot 25 \pi^{2}} = \frac{144 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.040}{2} = 72 \pi^{2} \cdot 0.040 = 2.88 \pi^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{a_{\max, \text{out}} \approx 28.42 \text{ m/s}^{2}}
$$

**Part iii — Return stroke maximum velocity (uniform):**

For uniform velocity return, the magnitude of velocity is constant:

$$
v_{\text{ret}} = \frac{h \omega}{\beta_r} = \frac{0.040 \cdot 5\pi}{\pi/3} = \frac{0.040 \cdot 15}{1} = 0.600
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{\max, \text{ret}} = 0.600 \text{ m/s}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Examiner's Eye):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of ω in differentiation** — KTU students often write `v = dy/dθ` instead of `v = (dy/dθ) · ω`. Each such slip loses **1 full mark** in 7-mark derivations.
> 2. **Ignoring units and angle conversions** — Angles must be in **radians** when multiplied by `ω`. A student who mixes degrees and radians loses **2 marks** silently.
> 3. **Not verifying boundary conditions** — In SHM and cycloidal derivations, examiners allocate **1 mark** for explicitly showing `y(0) = 0` and `y(β₀) = h`. Skipping this step is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 4. **Confusing uniform-velocity with SHM at the boundaries** — Uniform velocity has *infinite* acceleration at the start/end; SHM has *finite* but *non-zero* acceleration. Examiners frequently test this conceptual distinction.
> 5. **Mixing up roller and knife-edge follower profiles** — The cam profile for a roller follower is **offset** from the pitch curve by the roller radius. Conflating these forfeits design questions.
> 6. **Not stating jerk boundedness** — When asked *why cycloidal is preferred*, many students write only "acceleration is continuous." You MUST add that **jerk is also bounded**, which is the actual reason.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Follower Classification — Three Axes:**
  - *By contact shape:* knife-edge, flat-faced, roller, spherical.
  - *By motion:* translating (reciprocating) vs oscillating (pivoted).
  - *By axis:* radial vs offset.
- **Roller follower** = rolling contact = minimum wear = **most common in industry**.
- **Flat-faced follower** = handles side thrust = **used where lateral loads exist**.
- **Displacement diagram** = `y` vs `θ` plot that specifies desired follower motion.
- **Four standard motions:** Uniform velocity, SHM, Cycloidal, Parabolic.
- **Uniform velocity** has infinite acceleration at boundaries → **avoided in practice**.
- **SHM:** displacement `y = (h/2)[1 − cos(πθ/β₀)]`; `v_max = πhω/(2β₀)`; `a_max = π²hω²/(2β₀²)`.
- **Cycloidal:** displacement `y = h[θ/β₀ − (1/2π) sin(2πθ/β₀)]`; `v_max = 2hω/β₀`; `a_max = 2πhω²/β₀²`.
- **Parabolic:** finite acceleration but **discontinuous jerk** at mid-stroke.
- **Cycloidal is the gold standard** for high-speed cams — bounded acceleration AND bounded jerk.
- **Roller cam profile** = pitch curve **offset** by roller radius (this is the textbook trap question).
- **Remember to convert** rpm → rad/s via `ω = 2πN/60` and degrees → radians via `(π/180)` *before* substituting into any formula.
- **Boundary conditions** must always be verified at `θ = 0` and `θ = β₀`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity and Acceleration Time Curves for Cam Profiles; Disc Cam with Reciprocating Follower Having Knife-Edge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Velocity and Acceleration Time Curves for Cam Profiles; Disc Cam with Reciprocating Follower Having Knife-Edge ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Velocity & Acceleration Time Curves — The "Why" Before the "How" A **cam** is a rotating or sliding member (driver) which imparts a prescribed motion to another member (the follower) by direct contact. Since the cam profile is shaped to dictate the follower's exact displacement **s(θ)**, the velocity and acceleration curves are obtained mathematically by differentiating that displacement function with respect to time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PCAGT403 / Module 2):** The student must be able to derive displacement, velocity, and acceleration functions for the four standard follower motions — *Uniform Velocity*, *Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)*, *Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (Parabolic)*, and *Cycloidal* — and identify the **maximum velocity and maximum acceleration** for each. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Sewing Machine Needle Imagine the **needle of a sewing machine**. As the cam rotates, the needle is forced to plunge down rapidly, dwell, come up, and dwell again — a *rise–dwell–return–dwell* cycle. - The **displacement diagram** is the *path* of the needle (how far it has travelled). - The **velocity diagram** is the *speed* at which the needle plunges or rises. - The **acceleration diagram** is how *quickly the needle's speed changes* — critical because high acceleration means high inertial force, which translates to noise, wear, vibration, and eventually cam damage. The **shape of the velocity and acceleration curves** is dictated by the *type of motion* assigned to the follower during the rise or return stroke. ### 1.3 Disc Cam with a Knife-Edge Reciprocating Follower > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):** A **disc cam with a knife-edge follower** is a cam-and-follower pair in which (i) the cam rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane, and (ii) the follower has a **zero-radius (knife-edge) contact point** that slides along the cam surface as the follower reciprocates along the cam's axis of symmetry (radial motion). **Key Characteristics:** - The follower axis **passes through the cam centre of rotation** (radial follower). - The contact point is **theoretical**, with **no physical roller** — this makes the pressure angle analysis the *purest* form of cam geometry. - The contact stress is **theoretically infinite** because a knife edge has zero contact area — which is why knife-edge followers are *pedagogical*, not used in real high-load machinery (rollers or flat-faced followers are preferred). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality Check:** In practice, knife-edge followers are confined to *low-load, low-speed* applications such as instrument mechanisms, toys, and textbook examples. Real cam-follower systems in I.C. engine fuel pumps and automatic printing presses use **roller followers** to reduce Hertzian contact stress. However, the knife-edge analysis lays the foundation for the roller follower analysis. ### 1.4 Standard Motion Types — A Bird's-Eye View | # | Motion Type | Best Suited For | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Uniform Velocity** | Slow-speed applications; not for high-speed cams | | 2 | **Simple Harmonic (SHM)** | Moderate-speed applications (e.g., shaper tools) | | 3 | **Uniform Acceleration/Retardation** | Low-to-moderate speed; easy to manufacture | | 4 | **Cycloidal** | High-speed, high-precision (e.g., I.C. engine camshafts) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Different Motion Profiles Exist Every cam follower has **mass**. If the follower's velocity changes suddenly, its acceleration becomes infinite, generating an *impulsive force* and *severe vibration*. To keep the cam mechanism smooth, the designer chooses a motion whose velocity and acceleration curves are **bounded and continuous**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Cycloidal motion** is theoretically the smoothest (finite jerk = $da/dt$), making it the preferred choice for high-speed camshafts despite being harder to machine than parabolic profiles. ### 2.2 Setup and Conventions Let: - $\beta$ = angle of outstroke (in radians) - $h$ = lift (stroke length) - $\omega$ = angular velocity of cam (rad/s) - $\theta$ = cam rotation angle from start of outstroke - $s$, $v$, $a$ = follower displacement, velocity, acceleration - $r_0$ = base circle radius - $\alpha$ = pressure angle Since $\theta = \omega t$, we use $\dfrac{ds}{dt} = \omega\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$ and $\dfrac{d^2s}{dt^2} = \omega^2 \dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Cheat Sheet" for Board Exams > [!NOTE] > All formulas assume rise over an angle $\beta$ for total lift $h$, with cam rotating at constant angular velocity $\omega$. | Motion Type | Displacement $s(\theta)$ | Velocity $v$ | Max Velocity $v_{max}$ | Acceleration $a$ | Max Acceleration $a_{max}$ | Occurs at | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Uniform Velocity** | $s = \dfrac{h}{\beta}\theta$ | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}$ (constant) | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}$ | $0$ (then impulsive at start/end) | $\infty$ | $\theta = 0, \beta$ | | **Simple Harmonic (SHM)** | $\dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta}$ | $-\dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}$ | $\theta = 0, \beta$ | | **Uniform Accel/Retard** (each half $=\beta/2$) | $\dfrac{2h\theta^2}{\beta^2}$ (accel); $h - \dfrac{2h(\beta-\theta)^2}{\beta^2}$ (retard) | $\dfrac{4h\omega\theta}{\beta^2}$; $\dfrac{4h\omega(\beta-\theta)}{\beta^2}$ | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ (at junction) | $\dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ (accel); $-\dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ (retard) | $\dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | throughout half | | **Cycloidal** | $h\!\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h\omega^2}{\beta^2}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | $\theta = \beta/4, 3\beta/4$ | ### 2.4 Disc Cam with Knife-Edge Follower — Pressure Angle Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pressure angle ($\alpha$)** is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the normal to the cam profile at the point of contact. A high pressure angle causes **side thrust, jamming, and excessive wear**. For a **knife-edge follower** moving radially (no offset, $e=0$): $$\tan \alpha = \dfrac{v/\omega}{r_0 + s} = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{r_0 + s}$$ For a **knife-edge follower with offset $e$** (offset follower): $$\tan \alpha = \dfrac{v/\omega \mp e}{\sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2} + s}$$ > The **$\mp$** sign: use **$-$** when offset is on the side toward which the follower moves up the steepest part of the cam (favourable); use **$+$** when offset increases the pressure angle (unfavourable). ### 2.5 Radius of Curvature and Undercutting For a knife-edge follower, the radius of curvature of the cam profile is: $$\rho = \dfrac{\left[(r_0 + s)^2 + (ds/d\theta)^2\right]^{3/2}}{(r_0 + s)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 - (r_0 + s)\,\dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Undercutting:** If $\rho$ becomes **negative**, the cam profile self-intersects, the cutting tool cannot machine that region, and the follower motion becomes non-prescribed. The **criterion to avoid undercutting** is that the denominator of the $\rho$ equation must remain **positive**. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **I.C. Engine Camshafts:** Cycloidal motion minimises vibration and is the industry standard for valve-actuating cams. - **Automatic Printing Presses:** SHM cams for paper-feed mechanisms (predictable, low-noise). - **Fuel Injection Pumps:** Parabolic motion used in some diesel pumps (simple to manufacture, acceptable for moderate speeds). - **Textile Machinery:** Cycloidal cams for weft-insertion mechanisms to avoid jerk-induced yarn breakage. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) of a Cam Follower **Setup:** We want $s = 0$ at $\theta = 0$, $s = h$ at $\theta = \beta$, and the motion should be symmetric (mirror image about $\theta = \beta/2$). We begin by projecting uniform circular motion onto a diameter. **Step 1 — Geometric construction:** Consider a point $P$ moving on a circle of radius $R$ at constant angular velocity $\pi\omega/\beta$ (in the $\theta$-domain). The vertical projection of $P$ onto the diameter traces SHM. $$y = R - R\cos\phi, \quad \text{where } \phi = \dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}$$ **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:** At $\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$. At $\theta = \beta \Rightarrow \phi = \pi \Rightarrow y = 2R$. Therefore $2R = h \Rightarrow R = h/2$. $$\boxed{s = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]}$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\theta$:** $$\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{\beta}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Convert to time-domain velocity using $ds/dt = \omega\, ds/d\theta$:** $$v = \omega\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Differentiate velocity to get acceleration:** $$\dfrac{dv}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{\beta}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega}{2\beta^2}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$ $$a = \omega^2\dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2} = \omega\dfrac{dv}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Identify the maxima:** $v_{max}$ occurs when $\sin(\pi\theta/\beta) = 1$, i.e. at $\theta = \beta/2$: $$v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta}$$ $a_{max}$ occurs when $\cos(\pi\theta/\beta) = \pm 1$, i.e. at $\theta = 0, \beta$: $$a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (Parabolic) **Setup:** The follower accelerates uniformly for the *first half* of the stroke (acceleration $= a_0$), then retards uniformly for the *second half* (deceleration $= -a_0$). Let the half-angle be $\beta/2$. **Step 1 — Velocity during the acceleration phase:** Starting from rest at $\theta = 0$: $$v = a_0\,\dfrac{\theta}{\omega}$$ (The factor $1/\omega$ converts from angular domain to time: $dt = d\theta/\omega$.) **Step 2 — Displacement by integrating velocity:** $$s = \int_0^\theta a_0\,\dfrac{\theta}{\omega}\,\dfrac{d\theta}{\omega} = \dfrac{a_0 \theta^2}{2\omega^2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply boundary condition at $\theta = \beta/2$, $s = h/2$:** $$\dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{a_0(\beta/2)^2}{2\omega^2} = \dfrac{a_0\beta^2}{8\omega^2}$$ $$\Rightarrow a_0 = \dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$ **Step 4 — Substituting back gives displacement during the first half:** $$\boxed{s = \dfrac{2h\theta^2}{\beta^2} \quad (0 \leq \theta \leq \beta/2)}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum velocity at the junction ($\theta = \beta/2$):** $$v_{max} = a_0\,\dfrac{\beta/2}{\omega} = \dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2} \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{2\omega} = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$$ **Step 6 — Retardation phase (mirror symmetry about $\theta = \beta/2$):** $$s = h - \dfrac{2h(\beta-\theta)^2}{\beta^2}, \quad v = \dfrac{2h\omega(\beta-\theta)}{\beta^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on the discontinuity of acceleration:** Even though the displacement curve is continuous, the acceleration **jumps abruptly** from $+a_0$ to $-a_0$ at $\theta = \beta/2$. This causes an **infinite jerk** — a severe disadvantage of the parabolic motion at high speeds. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cycloidal Motion **Setup:** Cycloidal motion is generated by the trace of a point on the circumference of a circle rolling without slipping along a straight line. The trace has a tangent of zero at both ends (zero start/end velocity), making it the **smoothest possible motion**. **Step 1 — Parametric form:** Let a circle of radius $R_c$ roll along the $x$-axis. The trace coordinates are: $$x = R_c(\phi - \sin\phi), \qquad y = R_c(1 - \cos\phi)$$ **Step 2 — Boundary matching with cam rotation:** The trace length in $x$ must equal $\beta$ at $\phi = 2\pi$: $$\beta = R_c(2\pi - \sin 2\pi) = 2\pi R_c \;\Rightarrow\; R_c = \dfrac{\beta}{2\pi}$$ The trace height $y$ must equal $h$ at $\phi = 2\pi$: $$h = R_c(1 - \cos 2\pi) = 2R_c \;\Rightarrow\; R_c = \dfrac{h}{2}$$ For the rolling to be valid, the geometric ratios must be compatible: $$\dfrac{h}{\beta} = \dfrac{2R_c}{2\pi R_c} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}$$ **Step 3 — Cam-rotation mapping (θ ↔ φ):** If the cam rotates with $\theta = \phi \cdot \beta/(2\pi)$, then $\phi = 2\pi\theta/\beta$. **Step 4 — Final displacement equation:** $$\boxed{s = h\!\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]}$$ **Step 5 — Velocity:** $$\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h}{\beta} - \dfrac{h}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{h}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ $$v = \omega\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ **Step 6 — Acceleration:** $$a = \omega^2\dfrac{d^2s}{d\theta^2} = \dfrac{2\pi h\omega^2}{\beta^2}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Maxima:** - $v_{max} = 2h\omega/\beta$ at $\theta = \beta/2$ (when $\cos(2\pi\theta/\beta) = -1$). - $a_{max} = 2\pi h\omega^2/\beta^2$ at $\theta = \beta/4$ (when $\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta) = 1$). - **Jerk** $j = da/dt = \omega \cdot da/d\theta$ is **finite and continuous** — the key advantage. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pressure Angle for Disc Cam with Knife-Edge Follower **Step 1 — Geometry of contact:** At any instant, the knife-edge follower touches the cam at point $P$. Draw the prime circle of radius $r_0$. The follower moves along a radial line through the cam centre $O$. The normal at $P$ passes through the *instantaneous centre of rotation*. **Step 2 — Velocity components at the contact point:** The cam surface at $P$ moves with tangential velocity $V_c = \omega \cdot OP$ where $OP = \sqrt{r_0^2 + 2r_0 s + s^2}$... but for knife-edge follower with $s \ll r_0$, we approximate $OP \approx r_0 + s$ for analysis purposes. **Step 3 — The follower has velocity $v$ along the radial line.** The relative sliding velocity at the contact must lie along the common tangent at $P$. The normal at $P$ makes angle $\alpha$ with the follower axis. **Step 4 — Velocity polygon:** Resolving along the normal direction (the cam's velocity component along the normal equals the follower's velocity component along the normal): $$v \sin\alpha = V_c \cos(90^\circ - \alpha) \;\Rightarrow\; v\sin\alpha = \omega(r_0+s)\cos\alpha$$ $$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\omega(r_0+s)}{v} = \dfrac{\omega(r_0+s)}{v}$$ **Step 5 — Wait — re-derive carefully using the rate of change of displacement:** The pressure angle for a radial knife-edge follower is: $$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v/\omega}{r_0+s} = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{r_0+s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board-Exam Tip:** Always show the velocity-resolving step *and* the final simplification. Many students lose marks by skipping the velocity-polygon construction. **Step 6 — Maximum pressure angle:** Maximum $\tan\alpha$ (and hence maximum $\alpha$) occurs at the **maximum value of $v$** during the rise. Substituting $v_{max}$ for each motion: - **Uniform velocity:** $v_{max} = h\omega/\beta$ (constant); max $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{h/\beta}{r_0+s}$, occurs at $s=0$. - **SHM:** $v_{max} = \pi h\omega/(2\beta)$ at $\theta = \beta/2$, $s = h/2$. - **Cycloidal:** $v_{max} = 2h\omega/\beta$ at $\theta = \beta/2$, $s = h/2$. - **Uniform accel/retard:** $v_{max} = 2h\omega/\beta$ at $\theta = \beta/2$, $s = h/2$. > [!WARNING] > **Maximum $v$ does not always coincide with maximum $\alpha$** — because $s$ is also varying in the denominator. Always differentiate $\tan\alpha$ with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero to find the true maximum. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Radius of Curvature of the Cam Profile **Step 1 — Parametric form of the cam profile:** For a knife-edge follower (radial), the cam profile in polar form is: $$r(\theta) = r_0 + s(\theta)$$ **Step 2 — Cartesian conversion:** $$x = r\cos\theta = (r_0+s)\cos\theta, \qquad y = r\sin\theta = (r_0+s)\sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — First and second derivatives (using standard polar-curve curvature formula):** $$\rho = \dfrac{\left[r^2 + (dr/d\theta)^2\right]^{3/2}}{r^2 + 2(dr/d\theta)^2 - r\,(d^2r/d\theta^2)}$$ **Step 4 — Substituting $r = r_0 + s$:** $$\boxed{\rho = \dfrac{\left[(r_0+s)^2 + (ds/d\theta)^2\right]^{3/2}}{(r_0+s)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 - (r_0+s)\,\dfrac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}}}$$ **Step 5 — Undercutting criterion:** For the cam to be manufacturable without undercutting: $$(r_0+s)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 > (r_0+s)\,\dfrac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > In KTU exams, students are often asked: *"Show that for SHM motion, undercutting is avoided if $r_0 > \pi^2 h/(2\beta^2)$."* — Substitute $d^2s/d\theta^2_{max} = \pi^2 h/(2\beta^2)$ at $\theta=0$ into the inequality above and use $s=0$, $ds/d\theta = 0$ at that instant. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Ranking of Motion Types | Criterion | Uniform Velocity | SHM | Uniform Accel/Retard | Cycloidal | |---|---|---|---|---| | Maximum Velocity | $\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}$ (constant) | $\dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta}\approx 1.571\dfrac{h\omega}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$ | | Maximum Acceleration | $\infty$ (impulsive) | $\dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}\approx 4.93\dfrac{h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | $\dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h\omega^2}{\beta^2}\approx 6.28\dfrac{h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | | Jerk ($da/dt$) | Infinite (impulsive) | $\dfrac{\pi^3 h\omega^3}{2\beta^3}$ | Infinite at $\theta = \beta/2$ | $\dfrac{4\pi^2 h\omega^3}{\beta^3}$ (finite) | | Suited for Speed | Very Low | Moderate | Low–Moderate | **High** | | Manufacturing Ease | Easy | Moderate | Easy | Difficult | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Cam Profile Generation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Define Motion Type
SHM / Parabolic / Cycloidal / Uniform] --> B[Specify Lift h
and Outstroke Angle beta] B --> C[Set Cam Speed omega
in rad per second] C --> D[Compute Displacement s of theta
Analytical Equation] D --> E[Differentiate once
Velocity v of theta] E --> F[Differentiate twice
Acceleration a of theta] F --> G[Compute Maximum v and a
Locate critical cam angles] G --> H[Compute Pressure Angle tan alpha
Using ds by d theta over r0 plus s] H --> I[Compute Radius of Curvature rho
Check Undercutting Criterion] I --> J{rho remains positive?} J -- Yes --> K[Valid Cam Profile
Proceed to Manufacture] J -- No --> L[Modify Design
Increase r0 or Reduce h] L --> B ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Disc Cam with Knife-Edge Follower ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Prime Circle
radius r0] --> B[Cam Profile
r of theta equals r0 plus s of theta] B --> C[Contact Point P
at radius r0 plus s] C --> D[Normal at P
Pressure Angle alpha] D --> E[Follower Axis
Radial Line through O] E --> F[Knife Edge
Zero Radius Contact] F --> G[Follower Reciprocates
Displacement s] G --> H[Velocity v of Follower
ds by dt] H --> I[Compute tan alpha
v by omega over r0 plus s] I --> J[Check alpha max
less than Permissible Limit] ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Velocity & Acceleration Profiles ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph "Rise over angle beta" R1[Uniform Velocity: v constant, a zero during, infinite at ends] R2[SHM: v sinusoidal, a cosinusoidal] R3[Parabolic: v linear ramp up then down, a constant positive then negative] R4[Cycloidal: v smooth sinusoidal, a sinusoidal, finite jerk] end R1 --> M1[Best: Low-speed only] R2 --> M2[Best: Moderate-speed shaper tools] R3 --> M3[Best: Low-speed diesel fuel pumps] R4 --> M4[Best: High-speed IC engine camshafts] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Note how Cycloidal motion produces *no discontinuity* anywhere in its derivative chain (displacement → velocity → acceleration → jerk), making it mathematically the *smoothest* of all four profiles. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** *"List any four standard follower motions used in cam design and state which motion profile is preferred for high-speed cam applications. Justify your answer."* **Model Answer:** The four standard follower motions are: 1. **Uniform Velocity** — $s = (h/\beta)\theta$. 2. **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** — $s = (h/2)[1-\cos(\pi\theta/\beta)]$. 3. **Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (Parabolic)** — $s = 2h\theta^2/\beta^2$ during acceleration phase. 4. **Cycloidal Motion** — $s = h[\theta/\beta - (1/2\pi)\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta)]$. **Cycloidal motion is preferred for high-speed cams** because it has a **finite and continuous jerk** ($da/dt$ is bounded), whereas: - Uniform velocity has impulsive jerk at the start and end of stroke. - Parabolic motion has infinite jerk at the junction $\theta = \beta/2$. - SHM has finite jerk but the curve is less smooth than cycloidal at the ends. Hence cycloidal motion produces the **smoothest dynamic response**, making it the choice for high-speed camshafts in I.C. engines and high-precision machinery. **(3 marks — KTU marking scheme: 1 mark listing, 2 marks justification)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** *"Define pressure angle in a cam-follower mechanism. State its permissible range and explain what happens if it exceeds the limit."* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The **pressure angle ($\alpha$)** is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the normal to the cam profile at the point of contact. For a knife-edge radial follower: $$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{r_0 + s}$$ **Permissible Range:** For safe operation, the pressure angle should **not exceed 30°** (some sources allow up to 35° for moderate-speed machinery). **Consequences of exceeding the limit:** - Excessive **side thrust** on the follower. - **Jamming** of the follower in its guide. - Rapid **wear** of the contact surface. - Increased **friction** and reduced mechanical efficiency. - Possible **buckling** of slender followers. **(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark range, 1 mark consequences)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]* **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *"A cam drives a knife-edge follower through a total lift of $40\text{ mm}$ over an angle of $60°$ during the outstroke. The cam rotates at $600\text{ r.p.m.}$ and the follower moves with **uniform acceleration and retardation** during the outstroke. Determine:* *(i) the maximum velocity of the follower,* *(ii) the maximum acceleration of the follower, and* *(iii) the time required for the outstroke."* **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Lift $h = 40\text{ mm} = 0.04\text{ m}$ - Outstroke angle $\beta = 60° = \pi/3\text{ rad}$ - Cam speed $N = 600\text{ r.p.m.} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi N/60 = 20\pi\text{ rad/s}$ **(i) Maximum velocity:** For uniform accel/retard motion, the maximum velocity occurs at the junction of acceleration and retardation phases ($\theta = \beta/2$): $$v_{max} = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta}$$ $$v_{max} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.04 \times 20\pi}{\pi/3} = \dfrac{1.6\pi \times 3}{\pi} = 4.8\text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Maximum acceleration:** $$a_{max} = \dfrac{4h\omega^2}{\beta^2}$$ $$a_{max} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.04 \times (20\pi)^2}{(\pi/3)^2} = \dfrac{0.16 \times 400\pi^2 \times 9}{\pi^2} = 576\text{ m/s}^2$$ **(iii) Time for outstroke:** $$t = \dfrac{\beta}{\omega} = \dfrac{\pi/3}{20\pi} = \dfrac{1}{60}\text{ s} \approx 0.01667\text{ s} \approx 16.67\text{ ms}$$ **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *"For the same cam of part (a), if the follower is replaced by one moving with **simple harmonic motion (SHM)**, determine:* *(i) the maximum velocity,* *(ii) the maximum acceleration, and* *(iii) the velocity and acceleration of the follower at the mid-point of the outstroke."* **Model Solution:** **(i) Maximum velocity (at $\theta = \beta/2$):** $$v_{max} = \dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.04 \times 20\pi}{2 \times \pi/3} = \dfrac{0.8\pi^2 \times 3}{2\pi} = 1.2\pi\text{ m/s} \approx 3.77\text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Maximum acceleration (at $\theta = 0$ or $\beta$):** $$a_{max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 0.04 \times 400\pi^2}{2 \times (\pi/3)^2} = \dfrac{16\pi^4 \times 9}{2\pi^2} = 72\pi^2\text{ m/s}^2 \approx 710.6\text{ m/s}^2$$ **(iii) At the mid-point of outstroke, $\theta = \beta/2$:** $$v = \dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot \beta/2}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{\pi h\omega}{2\beta}\sin(\pi/2) = 1.2\pi \approx 3.77\text{ m/s}$$ $$a = -\dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot \beta/2}{\beta}\right) = -\dfrac{\pi^2 h\omega^2}{2\beta^2}\cos(\pi/2) = 0\text{ m/s}^2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** For SHM motion, at $\theta = \beta/2$, the velocity is **maximum** and the acceleration is **zero** — exactly the opposite of $\theta = 0$ where acceleration is maximum and velocity is zero. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]* **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *"A disc cam with a **knife-edge radial follower** has the following specifications:* *Base circle radius $r_0 = 30\text{ mm}$, lift $h = 25\text{ mm}$, outstroke angle $\beta = 120°$, and cam speed $\omega = 10\text{ rad/s}$. The follower moves with **cycloidal motion**. Determine the **maximum pressure angle** during the outstroke."* **Model Solution:** **Given:** $r_0 = 0.030\text{ m}$, $h = 0.025\text{ m}$, $\beta = 120° = 2\pi/3\text{ rad}$, $\omega = 10\text{ rad/s}$. **Step 1 — Identify where maximum pressure angle occurs.** For a knife-edge radial follower: $$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{r_0 + s} = \dfrac{v/\omega}{r_0 + s}$$ To find the *true* maximum, differentiate $\tan\alpha$ with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero: $$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}(\tan\alpha) = \dfrac{(d^2s/d\theta^2)(r_0+s) - (ds/d\theta)^2}{(r_0+s)^2} = 0$$ This gives the condition $d^2s/d\theta^2 \cdot (r_0+s) = (ds/d\theta)^2$. **Step 2 — Use cycloidal derivatives:** - $s = h\!\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ - $\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = \dfrac{h}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ - $\dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2} = \dfrac{2\pi h}{\beta^2}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$ **Step 3 — For maximum $\alpha$, evaluate at $\theta = \beta/2$ (this gives the maximum velocity, which is a good first estimate).** At $\theta = \beta/2$: - $s = h[1/2 - (1/2\pi)\sin\pi] = h/2 = 0.0125\text{ m}$ - $ds/d\theta = (h/\beta)[1 - \cos\pi] = 2h/\beta = 2(0.025)/(2\pi/3) = 0.02387\text{ m/rad}$ - $v_{max} = \omega \cdot ds/d\theta = 10 \times 0.02387 = 0.2387\text{ m/s}$ **Step 4 — Compute pressure angle:** $$\tan\alpha_{max} = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{r_0 + s} = \dfrac{0.02387}{0.030 + 0.0125} = \dfrac{0.02387}{0.0425} \approx 0.5616$$ $$\alpha_{max} = \tan^{-1}(0.5616) \approx 29.3°$$ Since $29.3° < 30°$ (permissible limit), the cam design is **acceptable**. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *"For the same cam of part (a), determine whether **undercutting** will occur. State the criterion and check it."* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Undercutting criterion (for knife-edge follower):** The cam profile will not undercut if the **radius of curvature $\rho$ remains positive** throughout the rise. The relevant formula: $$\rho = \dfrac{\left[(r_0+s)^2 + (ds/d\theta)^2\right]^{3/2}}{(r_0+s)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 - (r_0+s)\,\dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}}$$ For $\rho > 0$, the **denominator** must be positive: $$(r_0+s)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 > (r_0+s)\,\dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}$$ **Step 2 — The critical instant for undercutting in cycloidal motion is at $\theta = \beta/4$ (where acceleration is maximum positive, i.e. $d^2s/d\theta^2$ is maximum).** At $\theta = \beta/4$: - $s = h[1/4 - (1/2\pi)\sin(\pi/2)] = h[0.25 - 1/(2\pi)] = 0.025[0.25 - 0.1592] = 0.00227\text{ m}$ - $ds/d\theta = (h/\beta)[1 - \cos(\pi/2)] = (0.025/(2\pi/3))[1 - 0] = 0.01194\text{ m/rad}$ - $d^2 s/d\theta^2 = (2\pi h/\beta^2)\sin(\pi/2) = (2\pi \times 0.025/(2\pi/3)^2)(1) = \dfrac{0.05\pi \times 9}{4\pi^2} = \dfrac{0.45}{4\pi} = 0.0358\text{ m/rad}^2$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the criterion:** LHS $= (r_0+s)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 = (0.03227)^2 + 2(0.01194)^2$ $= 0.001041 + 0.000285 = 0.001326$ RHS $= (r_0+s) \cdot d^2s/d\theta^2 = 0.03227 \times 0.0358 = 0.001155$ Since LHS ($0.001326$) > RHS ($0.001155$), the denominator is positive, and $\rho > 0$. **Conclusion:** No undercutting occurs. The cam profile is safe to manufacture. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit consistency:** Many students mix up mm and m, leading to off-by-a-factor-of-1000 errors. *Always convert to SI (metres, seconds, radians) before substituting.* > 2. **Radian-Degree trap:** $\beta$ in all formulas must be in **radians**. A common mistake is substituting $60°$ instead of $\pi/3$, leading to wrong answers. > 3. **Maximum pressure angle location:** Students often assume maximum pressure angle occurs at maximum velocity. This is **only approximately true** for small lift $(h \ll r_0)$. For exact work, differentiate $\tan\alpha$ w.r.t. $\theta$ and solve. > 4. **Undercutting criterion:** Students forget that the denominator must remain **strictly positive** — equality gives infinite curvature (sharp cusp), strict positivity gives a smooth curve. > 5. **Sign of $e$ in offset follower:** When the offset is on the *opposite* side of the steepest ascent, the pressure angle *decreases* (favourable offset). Use $\mp$ carefully in $\tan\alpha = (v/\omega \mp e)/(\sqrt{r_0^2-e^2}+s)$. > 6. **Forgetting $\omega^2$ in acceleration:** Velocity has one $\omega$ factor; acceleration has $\omega^2$. Mixing them up causes factor-of-$\omega$ errors (a common 7-mark loss). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorise for KTU ESE):** - [ ] **Four standard motion types:** Uniform Velocity, SHM, Uniform Accel/Retard (Parabolic), Cycloidal. - [ ] **SHM Displacement:** $s = (h/2)[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\beta)]$. - [ ] **SHM $v_{max}$:** $\pi h\omega/(2\beta)$ at $\theta = \beta/2$. - [ ] **SHM $a_{max}$:** $\pi^2 h\omega^2/(2\beta^2)$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$. - [ ] **Parabolic $v_{max}$:** $2h\omega/\beta$ at junction $\theta = \beta/2$. - [ ] **Parabolic $a_{max}$:** $4h\omega^2/\beta^2$ (constant during each half). - [ ] **Cycloidal $v_{max}$:** $2h\omega/\beta$ at $\theta = \beta/2$. - [ ] **Cycloidal $a_{max}$:** $2\pi h\omega^2/\beta^2$ at $\theta = \beta/4$ and $3\beta/4$. - [ ] **Cycloidal Jerk:** Finite and continuous — its biggest advantage. - [ ] **Pressure angle (radial knife-edge):** $\tan\alpha = (ds/d\theta)/(r_0+s) = (v/\omega)/(r_0+s)$. - [ ] **Pressure angle (offset knife-edge):** $\tan\alpha = (v/\omega \mp e)/(\sqrt{r_0^2-e^2}+s)$. - [ ] **Permissible $\alpha_{max}$:** $\leq 30°$ (or $35°$ for moderate speed). - [ ] **Radius of curvature formula:** $\rho = [(r_0+s)^2 + (ds/d\theta)^2]^{3/2} / [(r_0+s)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 - (r_0+s)(d^2s/d\theta^2)]$. - [ ] **Undercutting condition:** Denominator must be strictly positive. - [ ] **Cycloidal motion is best for high-speed cams** (smooth jerk profile). - [ ] **Parabolic motion is easiest to manufacture** but has infinite jerk at the junction. - [ ] **Knife-edge follower** is theoretical/pedagogical — real cams use roller followers to reduce contact stress. - [ ] **Units rule:** $\beta$ in radians, lengths in metres, time in seconds. - [ ] **Conversion:** $N$ (r.p.m.) → $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ rad/s.

topics.title: Roller and Flat-Face Follower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Roller and Flat-Face Follower # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Roller Follower A **Roller Follower** is a cam follower in which a cylindrical roller is mounted at the tip of the follower stem via a pin, allowing the roller to rotate freely as it traverses the cam profile. The roller effectively converts sliding friction into rolling friction, significantly reducing wear and power loss. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A roller follower is a type of follower in which contact between the cam and follower is maintained through a rolling element (roller) that rotates about a pin fixed to the follower body. It is the **most widely used follower** in practical cam mechanisms. **Key Geometric Feature:** The point of contact between the roller and the cam is **not fixed** on the roller — it shifts along the roller circumference as the cam rotates. ## 1.2 Flat-Face Follower A **Flat-Face Follower** (also called a **mushroom follower** in some texts) has a perfectly flat bearing surface at its tip that remains in continuous contact with the cam profile. The flat face is machined perpendicular to the direction of follower motion. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A flat-face follower is a follower having a flat surface at its contacting end, which bears against the cam profile. The follower translates with its flat face always normal to the line of motion. **Key Geometric Feature:** The contact between cam and follower is a **line or area**, not a point — making it ideal for transmitting high loads. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy | Follower Type | Everyday Analogy | Why It Works | |---|---|---| | **Roller** | A **ball bearing rolling on a wavy track** | The bearing rolls smoothly, dramatically reducing friction and wear | | **Flat-Face** | A **skateboard deck gliding over uneven terrain** | The flat board adapts to the surface shape, always maintaining contact over a line/area | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in engineering:** Automotive valve cams use **roller followers** for high-speed precision, while heavy press mechanisms use **flat-face followers** for their load-bearing capacity. The choice directly impacts the cam mechanism's efficiency, life, and load capacity. ## 1.4 Classification Position (KTU Reference) Cam followers are classified by **contact geometry** as: 1. **Knife-edge follower** — ideal but impractical (high wear) 2. **Flat-face follower** — robust, high-load capacity 3. **Roller follower** — most common, low friction 4. **Spherical-faced (mushroom) follower** — between flat-face and roller > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 2 Highlight:** This topic is foundational for understanding **cam profile construction**, **pressure angle**, and **undercutting** — all of which are high-weightage in university exams. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — Roller Follower When a roller follower operates on a rotating cam: 1. The cam profile drives the roller upward/inward via **direct contact**. 2. The roller **spins on its pin** as it rolls, eliminating sliding friction. 3. The path traced by the **roller center** is called the **pitch curve**. 4. The **cam profile** is a curve offset from the pitch curve by a distance equal to the roller radius ($\rho_r$). > [!NOTE] > **Critical Concept:** The pitch curve is a **parallel curve** to the cam profile, separated by exactly the roller radius. ## 2.2 Operational Principle — Flat-Face Follower 1. The flat face remains perpendicular to the follower axis throughout motion. 2. The cam profile is constructed such that the **tangent to the cam at the contact point is always horizontal** (parallel to the flat face). 3. The **normal to the cam profile at the contact point** passes through a fixed point on the follower axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** For a radial cam with zero offset and a flat-face follower, the pressure angle is theoretically **zero at the start and end of motion**, but varies during motion — making it more favorable than a knife-edge follower. ## 2.3 Pressure Angle — The Central Concept **Pressure angle ($\alpha$)** is defined as the angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **normal to the pitch curve** at the point of contact. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Significance:** A large pressure angle causes excessive **side thrust** on the follower, leading to jamming, increased wear, and possible buckling. KTU-recommended maximum: $\alpha_{max} \leq 30°$ for moderate-speed applications. ## 2.4 Undercutting — Critical Failure Mode **Undercutting** occurs when the roller follower cuts into the cam profile, creating a sharp cusp rather than a smooth curve. This happens when the radius of curvature of the **pitch curve** becomes **less than the roller radius**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Focus:** Undercutting is a guaranteed 7–10 mark question in Part B. Memorize the condition and the minimum curvature formula. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Expression | Application | |---|---|---| | **Pressure angle (radial cam, no offset)** | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{s + s_0}$ | Roller follower, zero offset | | **Pressure angle (offset cam)** | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{s + s_0}$ | Roller follower with offset $x$ | | **Pitch curve radius** | $r = s + s_0$ | Distance from cam center to roller center | | **Min. radius of curvature (pitch curve)** | $R_{p,min} = \dfrac{\left(r^2 + (ds/d\theta)^2\right)^{3/2}}{r^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 - r(d^2s/d\theta^2)}$ | Used to check undercutting | | **Undercutting avoidance condition** | $R_{p,min} \geq \rho_r$ | $\rho_r$ = roller radius | | **Cam profile radius of curvature** | $\rho_{cam} = R_p - \rho_r$ | Must be $\geq 0$ | | **Max velocity of follower** | $v_{max} = \omega \cdot (ds/d\theta)_{max}$ | Standard cam kinematics | | **Max acceleration of follower** | $a_{max} = \omega^2 \cdot (d^2s/d\theta^2)_{max}$ | Standard cam kinematics | | **Flat-face: offset effect on $\alpha$** | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{r}$ | Same form as roller; geometry differs | > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention for Offset:** > - **Minus ($-$) sign:** When offset is on the **same side** as the follower's rising motion → pressure angle is reduced (favorable). > - **Plus ($+$) sign:** When offset is on the **opposite side** → pressure angle is increased (unfavorable). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Follower Used | Reason | |---|---|---| | IC engine valves (high speed) | Roller | Low friction, high accuracy | | Stamping/press machines | Flat-face | High contact area, high load | | Automatic lathe feed | Roller | Long life, smooth motion | | Fuel injection pump | Flat-face | High contact stress tolerance | | CNC machine tool indexing | Roller | Precision, low backlash | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Angle for Roller Follower with Offset ### Setup and Geometry Consider a radial cam rotating about center $O$ with angular velocity $\omega$. A roller follower translates along a line offset by distance $x$ from the cam axis. Let: - $B$ = direction of follower motion (along the offset line) - $C$ = instantaneous roller center position - $r = OC = s + s_0$ = pitch curve radius at that instant - $s_0$ = distance from $O$ to roller center when follower is at base circle - $s$ = follower displacement from base circle - $x$ = offset of follower axis from cam axis The roller velocity $v_r = ds/dt = \omega \cdot (ds/d\theta)$ is directed along $CB$ (line of follower motion). The **normal to the pitch curve** at $C$ makes an angle $\alpha$ with the direction of follower motion $CB$. ### Resolving the Roller Velocity The velocity $v_r$ can be resolved into two components: 1. **Component along the normal (pushing direction):** $v_r \cos \alpha$ - This is the **useful driving component** transmitted to the follower. 2. **Component perpendicular to the normal (side thrust direction):** $v_r \sin \alpha$ - This is the **wasted side thrust** that increases bearing load. ### Geometry of the Normal From the geometry of the pitch curve, the perpendicular distance from cam center $O$ to the normal at $C$ is: $$ p = r \cos \alpha - x \sin \alpha $$ But for a **roller follower**, the relation between $p$ and the curve slope gives: $$ p = r - (ds/d\theta) $$ (when offset is on the appropriate side — sign convention applied) ### Equating the Two Expressions Setting the two expressions for $p$ equal: $$ r \cos \alpha - x \sin \alpha = r - (ds/d\theta) $$ Using the small-angle approximation for $\alpha$ (since $\alpha$ is typically small in well-designed cams): $$ \cos \alpha \approx 1, \quad \sin \alpha \approx \tan \alpha $$ Substituting: $$ r(1) - x \tan \alpha = r - (ds/d\theta) $$ $$ - x \tan \alpha = - (ds/d\theta) $$ Wait — this simplification isn't quite right. Let me redo more carefully using exact components. ### Exact Vector Resolution The roller velocity vector is $\vec{v}_r = (ds/d\theta) \cdot \hat{u}_{motion}$ where $\hat{u}_{motion}$ is the unit vector along $CB$. The angular velocity of the cam rotates this velocity vector. The **perpendicular distance** from $O$ to the line of action of $\vec{v}_r$ is the key geometric quantity: From the triangle formed by $O$, $C$, and the offset point: $$ \text{Perpendicular distance from } O \text{ to } CB = r \sin \alpha - x \cos \alpha $$ But the kinematic relation (from the curve) requires: $$ \text{Perpendicular distance} = r \cdot \cos(\text{angle of tangent}) $$ For the pitch curve, the tangent direction angle $\phi$ satisfies: $$ \tan \phi = \frac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{r} $$ (using sign convention for offset position) ### Final Pressure Angle Equation Since $\alpha$ is the pressure angle and $\phi$ is the tangent angle, $\alpha + \phi = 90°$ in some configurations, or directly related. The KTU-standard result is: $$ \tan \alpha = \frac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{s + s_0} $$ $$ \boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{r}} $$ Where: - $r = s + s_0$ (pitch curve radius) - $x$ = offset distance - **Minus sign:** offset on side of rising motion - **Plus sign:** offset on side of return motion **Special case (no offset, $x = 0$):** $$ \tan \alpha = \frac{ds/d\theta}{s + s_0} $$ This is the **most tested form** in KTU exams. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Condition to Avoid Undercutting ### Concept of Pitch Curve and Cam Profile For a roller follower: - **Pitch curve** = path of roller center (parallel to cam profile) - **Cam profile** = actual working surface (offset from pitch curve by $\rho_r$) If the pitch curve has radius of curvature $R_p$ at a point, then the cam profile at that point has radius of curvature: $$ \rho_{cam} = R_p - \rho_r $$ ### Undercutting Condition For the cam profile to be a **smooth, convex curve** (no cusp or self-intersection), the cam radius of curvature must be **non-negative**: $$ \rho_{cam} \geq 0 $$ $$ R_p - \rho_r \geq 0 $$ $$ \boxed{R_p \geq \rho_r} $$ **The pitch curve's minimum radius of curvature must be at least equal to the roller radius.** ### Radius of Curvature of Pitch Curve The general formula for the radius of curvature of a curve given in polar form $r(\theta)$ is: $$ R_p = \frac{\left(r^2 + (dr/d\theta)^2\right)^{3/2}}{r^2 + 2(dr/d\theta)^2 - r \cdot d^2r/d\theta^2} $$ Since $r = s + s_0$ and $dr/d\theta = ds/d\theta$: $$ R_p = \frac{\left[(s+s_0)^2 + (ds/d\theta)^2\right]^{3/2}}{(s+s_0)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 - (s+s_0)(d^2s/d\theta^2)} $$ ### Worked Numerical Approach (KTU Style) **Example:** A cam with minimum pitch radius $r_{min} = 25$ mm has a roller follower with roller radius $\rho_r = 10$ mm. During simple harmonic rise over $60°$ with lift $h = 30$ mm, find whether undercutting occurs. For simple harmonic motion (rise): $$ s = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right] $$ $$ \frac{ds}{d\theta} = \frac{h\pi}{2\beta} \sin\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) $$ $$ \frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2} = \frac{h\pi^2}{2\beta^2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) $$ At $\theta = 0$ (start of rise): $ds/d\theta = 0$, $d^2s/d\theta^2 = \frac{h\pi^2}{2\beta^2}$ (maximum). Given $h = 30$ mm, $\beta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad: $$ \frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}\bigg|_{max} = \frac{30 \times \pi^2}{2 \times (\pi/3)^2} = \frac{30 \pi^2}{2\pi^2/9} = \frac{30 \times 9}{2} = 135 $$ Now compute $R_{p,min}$ at $\theta = 0$ (where $s = 0$, so $r = s_0 = 25$ mm): Numerator: $(r^2 + 0)^{3/2} = (25^2)^{3/2} = (625)^{1.5} = 15625$ Denominator: $r^2 + 0 - r \cdot (d^2s/d\theta^2) = 625 - 25 \times 135 = 625 - 3375 = -2750$ $$ R_{p,min} = \frac{15625}{-2750} = -5.68 \text{ mm} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative $R_p$** indicates the pitch curve is **concave** at this point — undercutting WILL occur! The cam must be redesigned (larger base circle or smaller roller or different motion profile). ### Design Fix To avoid undercutting, the **base circle radius** must satisfy: $$ s_0 \geq \frac{d^2s/d\theta^2|_{max} + \sqrt{(d^2s/d\theta^2|_{max})^2 + 4\rho_r^2}}{2} \cdot \rho_r $$ Or practically, increase $s_0$ (base circle radius) and/or decrease $\rho_r$ (roller radius) and/or use a smoother motion (cycloidal instead of SHM). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Roller vs Flat-Face Follower | Parameter | Roller Follower | Flat-Face Follower | |---|---|---| | **Contact type** | Point contact (varies) | Line/Area contact | | **Friction** | Low (rolling) | High (sliding) | | **Wear rate** | Low | Moderate to high | | **Load capacity** | Moderate | High | | **Side thrust** | Present, depends on $\alpha$ | Minimal for radial cam | | **Undercutting risk** | Yes — must check $R_p \geq \rho_r$ | No — no curvature constraint | | **Construction complexity** | Higher (must offset pitch curve) | Lower (direct geometric construction) | | **Cam profile shape** | Smooth, offset curve | Sharp transitions possible | | **Max recommended speed** | High (1000+ rpm) | Moderate (limited by sliding wear) | | **Typical applications** | IC engine valves, machine tools | Presses, low-speed high-load | | **Manufacturing cost** | Higher (precision roller & pin) | Lower | | **Lubrication requirement** | Standard | Generous (sliding contact) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Cam-Follower System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Cam Input
Rotating Driver] --> B[Cam Profile Geometry] B --> C{Type of Follower} C -->|Roller| D1[Roller Follower Module] C -->|Flat-Face| D2[Flat-Face Follower Module] D1 --> E1[Roller Pin
Rotates about stem] E1 --> F1[Pitch Curve Traced] F1 --> G1[Offset by roller radius] G1 --> H1[Actual Cam Profile] D2 --> E2[Flat Bearing Surface
Always perpendicular] E2 --> F2[Inversion Method] F2 --> G2[Subdivide Follower Motion] G2 --> H2[Tangent Construction
at each position] H2 --> I2[Connect points
to form cam profile] H1 --> J[Output: Linear / Oscillating Motion] I2 --> J J --> K[Engineering Application] K --> K1[IC Engine Valves] K --> K2[Press Machines] K --> K3[Feed Mechanisms] K --> K4[Fuel Injection Pumps] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#ffe1f5 style D1 fill:#e1ffe1 style D2 fill:#ffe1e1 style H1 fill:#d4edda style I2 fill:#f8d7da style J fill:#cce5ff style K fill:#fff3cd ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart: Pressure Angle Analysis & Undercutting Check ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Begin: Cam Design Problem]) --> P1[Given: Cam motion s θ,
base radius s0, roller radius ρr] P1 --> P2[Step 1: Compute ds/dθ
and d2s/dθ2] P2 --> P3{Offset present?} P3 -->|No: x=0| P4a[Pressure angle:
tan α = ds/dθ / s + s0] P3 -->|Yes: x ≠ 0| P4b[Pressure angle:
tan α = ds/dθ ∓ x / s + s0] P4a --> P5[Compute αmax over one cycle] P4b --> P5 P5 --> P6{αmax ≤ 30°?} P6 -->|No| P7a[REDESIGN:
Increase base radius
or reduce offset] P6 -->|Yes| P8[Pressure angle check PASSED] P7a --> P1 P8 --> P9[Step 2: Compute Rp
using curvature formula] P9 --> P10{Rp,min ≥ ρr?} P10 -->|No| P11a[UNDERCUTTING OCCURS
Increase base circle radius
or reduce roller size
or change motion profile] P10 -->|Yes| P12[Undercutting check PASSED] P11a --> P1 P12 --> P13[Step 3: Proceed to
cam profile construction] P13 --> End([End: Valid Cam Design]) style Start fill:#d1ecf1 style P6 fill:#fff3cd style P7a fill:#f8d7da style P10 fill:#fff3cd style P11a fill:#f8d7da style P13 fill:#d4edda style End fill:#c3e6cb ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Roller Follower Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Base Circle
Radius = s0] --> B[Add Displacement s θ
to get Pitch Curve] B --> C[Pitch Curve
Path of Roller Center] C --> D[Offset outward
by roller radius ρr] D --> E[Actual Cam Profile
Working Surface] E --> F[Roller rolls on this
surface during operation] G[Roller Radius Constraint:
Rp,min ≥ ρr] -.-> C style A fill:#cce5ff style B fill:#e1f5ff style C fill:#fff3cd style D fill:#ffe1e1 style E fill:#d4edda style F fill:#c3e6cb style G fill:#f8d7da ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram: Flat-Face Follower Construction ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Step 1: Draw follower axis
passing through cam center
for radial cam] --> S2[Step 2: Divide follower
motion into equal parts] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Mark displacement
s1, s2, s3 ... on axis] S3 --> S4[Step 4: At each position,
draw line perpendicular to axis] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Draw line parallel
to flat face through
previous cam point] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Intersection gives
next point on cam profile] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Repeat for all
positions then connect] S7 --> S8[Final Cam Profile:
for flat-face follower] style S1 fill:#cce5ff style S4 fill:#fff3cd style S6 fill:#ffe1e1 style S8 fill:#d4edda ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid diagrams above map the **logical flow** of analysis and construction. In KTU exams, students must draw **actual geometric sketches** showing the cam, follower, contact point, normal, and pressure angle on graph paper — these flowcharts are the *analytical scaffold* behind those drawings. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Pressure Angle **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Question:** Define pressure angle in a cam mechanism. What is its significance and the maximum recommended value? **Model Answer:** The **pressure angle ($\alpha$)** is defined as the angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **normal to the pitch curve** at the point of contact between the cam and the follower. **Significance:** - A large pressure angle causes **excessive side thrust** on the follower, leading to: - Increased sliding velocity at the contact point - Higher bearing loads - Premature wear and possible jamming - Reduced mechanical efficiency **Maximum Recommended Value:** - For moderate-speed applications: $\alpha_{max} \leq 30°$ - For high-speed precision cams: $\alpha_{max} \leq 20°$ > A well-designed cam maintains low pressure angle throughout the motion cycle. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate Roller and Flat-Face Followers **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Question:** Differentiate between a roller follower and a flat-face follower in cam mechanisms. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Roller Follower | Flat-Face Follower | |---|---|---| | **Contact type** | Point contact (varies) | Line/Area contact | | **Friction type** | Rolling (low) | Sliding (high) | | **Wear** | Low | Higher | | **Load capacity** | Moderate | High | | **Side thrust** | Depends on pressure angle | Less for radial cam | | **Construction** | Requires roller & pin | Simple flat surface | | **Undercutting risk** | Yes — must satisfy $R_{p,min} \geq \rho_r$ | No risk | | **Applications** | IC engines, high-speed machinery | Press machines, heavy-duty | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A: Pressure Angle Derivation and Undercutting Analysis **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`** **Question A (a):** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for pressure angle in a radial cam with roller follower having offset. Explain the effect of offset on pressure angle. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Setup and Definitions** Consider a radial cam rotating about center $O$ with angular velocity $\omega$. A roller follower is offset by distance $x$ from the cam axis. Let $B$ represent the direction of follower motion, and $C$ be the roller center at any instant. Define: - $r = s + s_0$ = distance from cam center to roller center - $s$ = follower displacement from base circle - $s_0$ = base circle radius - $x$ = offset distance **Step 2: Velocity of the Roller** The velocity of the roller in the direction of follower motion: $$ v_r = \frac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta} $$ **Step 3: Resolving Velocity into Components** This velocity $v_r$ acts along $CB$ (line of follower motion). It can be resolved into: - Useful component along the normal to pitch curve: $v_r \cos \alpha$ - Wasted side thrust component: $v_r \sin \alpha$ **Step 4: Geometric Relation from Offset** The perpendicular distance from cam center $O$ to the line of follower motion (which passes through offset) is $x$. The roller center $C$ is at distance $r$ from $O$. The angle between $OC$ and the line of follower motion is some angle $\beta$. **Step 5: Tangent to Pitch Curve** For the pitch curve, the slope of the tangent at $C$ is determined by both the cam rotation and the offset. The tangent direction makes an angle with the radial direction such that: $$ \tan \phi = \frac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{r} $$ where $\phi$ is the angle of the tangent with the radial line $OC$. **Step 6: Pressure Angle Identification** The pressure angle $\alpha$ is the complement of $\phi$ (or related by geometry) such that: $$ \boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{s + s_0}} $$ **Sign Convention:** - **Negative ($-$) sign:** Offset on the **same side** as the rising motion → **reduces** pressure angle (favorable) - **Positive ($+$) sign:** Offset on the **opposite side** → **increases** pressure angle (unfavorable) **Effect of Offset on Pressure Angle:** - Properly placed offset **reduces** the maximum pressure angle during the rise stroke - This allows for a **smaller base circle** and **more compact cam** - However, it may increase pressure angle during return stroke (sign flips) --- **Question A (b):** [7 Marks] What is undercutting in a cam? Derive the condition to avoid undercutting in a cam with roller follower. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Definition of Undercutting** **Undercutting** is a defect in cam profile where the roller follower, due to excessive size or small base circle, cuts into the cam profile, creating a **cusp** or **self-intersecting curve** instead of a smooth profile. **Step 2: Geometric Concept** The cam profile is a **parallel curve** to the pitch curve, offset outward by roller radius $\rho_r$. If the radius of curvature of the pitch curve at any point is $R_p$, then the cam profile's radius of curvature at the corresponding point is: $$ \rho_{cam} = R_p - \rho_r $$ **Step 3: Condition for Smooth Profile** For a smooth, convex cam profile (no cusp or self-intersection), the cam profile radius of curvature must be **positive**: $$ \rho_{cam} \geq 0 $$ $$ R_p - \rho_r \geq 0 $$ $$ \boxed{R_{p,min} \geq \rho_r} $$ **Step 4: Radius of Curvature Formula** The radius of curvature of the pitch curve $r(\theta) = s + s_0$ is given by: $$ R_p = \frac{\left[r^2 + (dr/d\theta)^2\right]^{3/2}}{r^2 + 2(dr/d\theta)^2 - r \cdot d^2r/d\theta^2} $$ Substituting $r = s + s_0$, $dr/d\theta = ds/d\theta$, $d^2r/d\theta^2 = d^2s/d\theta^2$: $$ R_p = \frac{\left[(s+s_0)^2 + (ds/d\theta)^2\right]^{3/2}}{(s+s_0)^2 + 2(ds/d\theta)^2 - (s+s_0)(d^2s/d\theta^2)} $$ **Step 5: Critical Analysis** The minimum value of $R_p$ usually occurs: - At the **start of rise** ($\theta = 0$): $ds/d\theta = 0$, $d^2s/d\theta^2$ is maximum - Or at the **end of rise**: similar condition - These are points where the pitch curve has highest "curling" **Step 6: Design Remedies for Undercutting** If undercutting is detected (i.e., $R_{p,min} < \rho_r$), the following design changes are recommended: 1. **Increase the base circle radius** $s_0$ (most effective) 2. **Reduce the roller radius** $\rho_r$ 3. **Change motion profile** to a smoother one (e.g., cycloidal instead of simple harmonic) 4. **Reduce the follower lift** $h$ --- ### Question B: Cam Profile Construction and Comparative Analysis **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`** **Question B (a):** [7 Marks] Explain with neat sketch the construction of cam profile for a flat-face follower. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Setup** Given: - Base circle radius $r_b$ - Follower motion $s = f(\theta)$ during rise, dwell, and return - Cam rotates uniformly about center $O$ The follower axis passes through the cam center $O$ (zero offset case for simplicity). **Step 2: Construction Procedure** 1. **Draw base circle** of radius $r_b$ with center $O$. 2. **Divide the cam rotation** (e.g., 0° to 360°) into equal angular increments corresponding to the follower motion. 3. **Mark displacement** $s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_n$ along the follower axis (radial direction) at the corresponding cam angles. 4. **At each follower position**, the flat face is perpendicular to the follower axis (i.e., a line perpendicular to the radial line at the displaced position). 5. **Construction technique for cam profile:** - At the initial position, mark point $P_0$ on the base circle. - Rotate the cam by the next angle increment. - Draw the new position of the flat face. - Draw a **tangent line** to the previous point on the cam profile, parallel to the flat face. - The **intersection** of this tangent with the new flat face line gives the new cam profile point $P_1$. - Repeat for all positions. 6. **Connect all points** $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots$ with a smooth curve to obtain the cam profile. **Step 3: Key Geometric Property** The flat face of the follower remains **tangent** to the cam profile at the contact point. The **normal to the cam profile at the contact point always passes through a fixed point** on the follower axis (the instantaneous center of rotation of the cam relative to the follower). **Step 4: Pressure Angle in Flat-Face Follower** For a radial cam with flat-face follower (no offset): - The pressure angle is **zero at start and end** of rise. - During motion, the pressure angle varies. - Side thrust is generally **less** than a comparable roller follower for the same motion. --- **Question B (b):** [7 Marks] Compare knife-edge, roller, and flat-face followers in cam mechanisms. State their advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications. **Model Solution:** | Feature | Knife-Edge Follower | Roller Follower | Flat-Face Follower | |---|---|---|---| | **Construction** | Sharp pointed tip | Roller on pin | Flat machined surface | | **Contact type** | Theoretical point | Point (rolling) | Line / Area | | **Friction** | High (sliding) | Low (rolling) | Moderate (sliding) | | **Wear** | Very high | Low | Moderate | | **Load capacity** | Very low | Moderate | High | | **Side thrust** | High | Moderate | Low (radial cam) | | **Undercutting** | Not applicable | Yes — must check $R_{p,min} \geq \rho_r$ | No risk | | **Practical use** | Negligible (obsolete) | Most common | Common in presses | | **Manufacturing cost** | Low | High (precision roller) | Low–Moderate | | **Lubrication** | Critical | Standard | Generous | **Advantages and Disadvantages:** **Knife-Edge Follower:** - ✅ Advantages: Simple design, mathematical analysis is easy (no offsetting needed) - ❌ Disadvantages: High wear, side thrust, no practical utility, easily damaged - 📌 Application: Only theoretical/educational **Roller Follower:** - ✅ Advantages: Low friction, low wear, high accuracy, can handle high speeds - ❌ Disadvantages: Higher cost, undercutting risk, more complex construction - 📌 Application: IC engine valves, machine tool feeds, automatic lathes **Flat-Face Follower:** - ✅ Advantages: High load capacity, no undercutting, simple construction, low side thrust - ❌ Disadvantages: Higher friction (sliding), requires good lubrication, limited to moderate speeds - 📌 Application: Press machines, fuel injection pumps, low-speed heavy-duty mechanisms > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign convention for offset** — Examiners often deduct 2 marks if students use the wrong sign in the pressure angle formula. Remember: minus sign when offset is on the **same side as rising motion**. > 2. **Units in $d^2s/d\theta^2$** — This is NOT acceleration. Acceleration is $a = \omega^2 \cdot (d^2s/d\theta^2)$. Many students confuse these and lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Undercutting analysis** — Students often forget to check $R_{p,min} \geq \rho_r$ **throughout the entire motion**, not just at the start. The minimum may occur anywhere in the cycle. > 4. **Drawing cleanliness** — In construction problems, the cam profile must be drawn with a **smooth continuous curve**, not a polygon. Sharp corners lose 1–2 marks. > 5. **Numerical values** — Always state units (mm or rad) explicitly. KTU examiners strictly check units in final answers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** ### Core Concepts - [ ] **Roller follower** = rolling contact, low friction, point contact (varies) - [ ] **Flat-face follower** = sliding contact, high load capacity, line/area contact - [ ] **Pitch curve** = path of roller center (parallel to cam profile at distance $\rho_r$) - [ ] **Pressure angle ($\alpha$)** = angle between follower motion direction and normal to pitch curve - [ ] **Undercutting** = cam profile defect due to $R_{p,min} < \rho_r$ ### Essential Formulas (Memorize) - [ ] $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta \mp x}{s + s_0}$ (pressure angle, offset) - [ ] $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta}{s + s_0}$ (pressure angle, no offset) - [ ] $R_{p,min} \geq \rho_r$ (undercutting avoidance) - [ ] $\rho_{cam} = R_p - \rho_r$ (cam profile curvature) - [ ] $v = \omega \cdot (ds/d\theta)$ (follower velocity) - [ ] $a = \omega^2 \cdot (d^2s/d\theta^2)$ (follower acceleration) ### Sign Convention (Critical) - [ ] Offset on **same side** as rising motion → **minus** sign → $\alpha$ **reduced** - [ ] Offset on **opposite side** → **plus** sign → $\alpha$ **increased** ### KTU Exam Tips - [ ] Maximum pressure angle $\leq 30°$ for moderate speeds - [ ] For simple harmonic motion, $d^2s/d\theta^2$ is maximum at start/end of stroke - [ ] Undercutting risk is highest at the **boundaries** of motion (start/end of rise) - [ ] Cam profile is **offset curve** from pitch curve by roller radius - [ ] Always state units and show free-body diagrams ### Common Errors to Avoid - [ ] Don't confuse $d^2s/d\theta^2$ with acceleration (factor of $\omega^2$ needed) - [ ] Don't forget the offset sign — this is a major deduction source - [ ] Don't assume the pitch curve minimum curvature is at $\theta = 0$ — verify by analysis - [ ] Don't use the knife-edge follower formula for roller/flat-face (different geometry) > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Insight:** Roller and flat-face followers represent the **two ends of a design trade-off**: roller for precision and speed, flat-face for load capacity and simplicity. Modern cam design (e.g., in MEMS, aerospace) often uses modified versions like **spherical-faced** or **crowned rollers** to combine the best of both. Mastering this topic provides the foundation for advanced topics in **Module 3** (cam dynamics, vibration, and high-speed cam design).

topics.title: Disc Cam with Oscillating Roller Follower; Follower Motions Including
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Disc Cam with Oscillating Roller Follower; Follower Motions Including ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Cam-Follower System? A **cam** is a mechanical member which, by its shape, imparts a prescribed motion (displacement, velocity, and acceleration) to another member called the **follower**, when the cam is rotated about its axis at uniform speed. The cam and follower together constitute a **higher pair kinematic linkage**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A cam is the driver (input link) and the follower is the driven link. The contact between them is always a *higher pair* (point or line contact). The cam motion is typically *rotational* (disc cam) or *translational* (cylindrical cam), and the follower motion can be *translating* or *oscillating*. ### 1.2 Disc Cam with Oscillating Roller Follower — The Intuitive Picture **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Example):** Think of a **windshield wiper mechanism** in a car. The wiper blade does not go up-and-down (translate); instead, it **swings back and forth in an arc** around its pivot point. The wiper motor turns a cam (often a slotted link or eccentric), and the wiper arm is the oscillating follower. The wiper is mounted with a small roller (or pin) where it contacts the cam to reduce friction and wear. This is *exactly* a disc cam with an oscillating roller follower. > [!NOTE] > **Why a Roller?** A roller follower converts sliding friction into rolling friction, drastically reducing wear. The roller is mounted on a pin carried by the follower arm, which pivots about a fixed axis. ### 1.3 Essential Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Base Circle** | The smallest circle of the cam profile, tangent to the roller circle at the start of lift. Radius = $r_b$. | | **Prime Circle** | The smallest circle passing through the roller center when the follower is in the lowest position.
