Radius = $r_p = r_b + r_r$ (for radial follower). | | **Pitch Curve** | The path traced by the roller center as the cam rotates through one cycle. | | **Cam Profile (Working Curve)** | The actual outer surface of the cam, offset from the pitch curve by the roller radius $r_r$. | | **Trace Point** | A theoretical point on the follower (replacing the roller) used to generate the cam profile. | | **Pressure Angle ($\alpha$)** | The angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the normal to the pitch curve at the point of contact. | | **Lift (Stroke, $s$)** | The maximum displacement of the follower from its lowest (or initial) position. | | **Angle of Rotation of Cam** | The total angle through which the cam rotates during one complete follower motion cycle (rise $\beta$, dwell $\theta_d$, return $\gamma$, dwell $\theta_d$). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pearl:** For an **oscillating follower**, the "lift" is replaced by the **total angular swing** of the follower, denoted as $\Phi$ (capital phi). The follower does not move in a straight line; it rotates about a fixed pivot $O_1$. ### 1.4 Schematic of Disc Cam with Oscillating Roller Follower The cam center is $O$, the follower pivot is $O_1$, the roller is at $A$ (contact point), and the roller center is $A_0$. The line $O O_1$ has length $d$ (center distance). When the cam rotates, the roller center $A_0$ moves along the pitch curve, causing the follower arm $O_1 A_0$ to oscillate. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Geometric Relationship During Operation Consider the cam rotating by an angle $\theta$ from the start of the rise. Let the corresponding angular displacement of the follower be $\phi$. The roller center position is $A_0$, and the geometry of the quadrilateral $O A_0 O_1$ (at start) gives: $$O A_0 = r_p \quad \text{(prime circle radius)}$$ $$O_1 A_0 = L \quad \text{(length of follower arm)}$$ $$O O_1 = d \quad \text{(fixed center distance)}$$ By the **sine rule** in triangle $O A_0 O_1$: $$\frac{d}{\sin(\pi - \alpha - \beta_0)} = \frac{r_p}{\sin \beta_0} = \frac{L}{\sin \alpha}$$ where $\alpha$ is the **pressure angle** and $\beta_0$ is the angle $\angle A_0 O_1 O$ in the base position. ### 2.2 The Pressure Angle Formula (The Most Tested Equation!) For an oscillating roller follower, the **pressure angle** at any instant is the angle between the normal to the pitch curve (line $O A_0$) and the velocity direction of the roller center (which is perpendicular to $O_1 A_0$). The **velocity of the follower** (perpendicular to $O_1 A_0$) has magnitude: $$v_f = L \cdot \dot{\phi}$$ The **velocity of the cam surface contact point** in the direction of $v_f$ is $\omega \cdot r_p \cos\alpha$ (projection of cam surface velocity). Balancing these along the common normal: $$\tan \alpha = \frac{v_f}{\omega \cdot r_p \cos\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\alpha}$$ The **clean, exam-ready KTU formula** is: $$\boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{L \cdot \dot{\phi}}{\omega \cdot O A_0} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos(\text{geometry correction})}}$$ In simplified form using $s$ as instantaneous roller center displacement along the tangent to pitch curve: $$\boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{d s / d t}{\omega \sqrt{(O A_0)^2 - s^2}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU Board problems, you will usually be given or asked to compute the *maximum pressure angle* — the critical design parameter. The maximum occurs at the steepest portion of the displacement diagram. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet #### A. Motion Equations for an Oscillating Roller Follower | Motion Type | Displacement $\phi$ (during rise, $0 \le \theta \le \beta$) | Velocity $\dot{\phi}$ | Acceleration $\ddot{\phi}$ | |---|---|---|---| | **Uniform Velocity** | $\phi = \Phi \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\beta}$ | $\dot{\phi} = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot \omega}{\beta}$ | $\ddot{\phi} = 0$ | | **Simple Harmonic (SHM)** | $\phi = \dfrac{\Phi}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dot{\phi} = \dfrac{\pi \Phi \omega}{2\beta}\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$ | $\ddot{\phi} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2}{2\beta^2}\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$ | | **Uniform Acceleration & Retardation** | For $0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$:
$\phi = \dfrac{2\Phi}{\beta^2}\theta^2$ | $\dot{\phi} = \dfrac{4\Phi \omega}{\beta^2}\theta$ | $\ddot{\phi} = \dfrac{4\Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | | **Uniform Acceleration & Retardation** | For $\beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta$:
$\phi = \Phi\left[1 - 2\left(1 - \dfrac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^2\right]$ | $\dot{\phi} = \dfrac{4\Phi \omega}{\beta^2}(\beta - \theta)$ | $\ddot{\phi} = -\dfrac{4\Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | | **Cycloidal Motion** | $\phi = \Phi\left[\dfrac{\theta}{\beta} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\dot{\phi} = \dfrac{\Phi \omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | $\ddot{\phi} = \dfrac{2\pi \Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}\sin\left(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$ | #### B. Maximum Velocity & Acceleration Summary | Motion | Max Velocity | Max Acceleration | |---|---|---| | Uniform Velocity | $\dfrac{\Phi \omega}{\beta}$ | $\infty$ (jump at start/end) | | SHM | $\dfrac{\pi \Phi \omega}{2\beta}$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2}{2\beta^2}$ | | Uniform Acc/Ret | $\dfrac{2\Phi \omega}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{4\Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | | Cycloidal | $\dfrac{2\Phi \omega}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi \Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Cycloidal motion** is the gold standard for high-speed cams because it has **zero acceleration at start and end** (no shock). It is preferred over SHM for the same reason. SHM has finite acceleration at start and end (mild shock). Uniform velocity has infinite acceleration jumps (severe shock — avoided in practice except for very low speeds). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Cam mechanisms with oscillating followers are used in: - **Internal combustion engines** (valve operating mechanisms, especially in overhead valve systems) - **Textile machinery** (shuttle control) - **Printing presses** (ink roller actuation) - **Automatic screw machines and CNC tool changers** - **Robotic gripper actuation** and **fuel injection pumps** in diesel engines ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Angle for Oscillating Roller Follower **Setup:** Cam center is $O$, follower pivot is $O_1$ at distance $d$. Roller center at $A_0$ is at the pitch curve. $O A_0 = R_c$ (instantaneous distance from cam center to roller center), $O_1 A_0 = L$ (follower arm length). Cam angular velocity is $\omega$ (constant), follower angular velocity is $\dot{\phi}$. **Step 1 — Velocity of the roller center (point on follower):** The roller center moves in a circular arc about $O_1$, so its velocity is purely tangential, perpendicular to $O_1 A_0$, with magnitude: $$v_f = L \cdot \dot{\phi}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity of the corresponding point on the cam surface (at contact):** The cam surface at the contact point has velocity $v_c = \omega \cdot R_c$ perpendicular to $O A_0$ (since $R_c$ is measured from the cam center $O$). **Step 3 — Decompose $v_f$ into components along and perpendicular to the common normal (which is line $O A_0$):** The angle between the follower arm $O_1 A_0$ and the line $O A_0$ (the common normal) is the pressure angle $\alpha$. So: $$v_{f, \text{normal}} = v_f \sin \alpha = L \dot{\phi} \sin \alpha$$ $$v_{c, \text{normal}} = v_c = \omega R_c$$ **Step 4 — Apply the higher-pair kinematic constraint (no penetration / no separation):** The component of relative velocity along the common normal must be zero (the bodies stay in contact without separating or interpenetrating): $$v_c \cos 0 = v_f \sin \alpha$$ $$\omega R_c = L \dot{\phi} \sin \alpha$$ **Step 5 — Solve for pressure angle $\alpha$:** $$\sin \alpha = \frac{\omega R_c}{L \dot{\phi}}$$ Or equivalently (often the form KTU expects): $$\boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{L \dot{\phi}}{\omega R_c \cos \alpha}}$$ Since the geometric correction is often small, the most common **KTU board form** is: $$\boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{L \dot{\phi}}{\omega \cdot O A_0}}$$ where $O A_0 = R_c = \sqrt{d^2 + L^2 - 2 d L \cos(\beta_0 + \phi)}$ (changing with follower rotation $\phi$). > [!NOTE] > **Geometric insight:** $O A_0$ is *not* constant during motion. It changes as the follower swings. This is what makes the oscillating follower analysis slightly more involved than the radial translating follower case. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Displacement Equation for SHM Motion **Given:** During rise, cam rotates by angle $\beta$ and follower must swing by total angle $\Phi$. **Assumption:** The follower motion is to be **simple harmonic**. Imagine a point $P$ moving uniformly around a circle of radius $\Phi/2$ with constant angular velocity $\pi\omega/\beta$. The projection of $P$ on a diameter gives the follower displacement. **Step 1 — Set up the SHM reference circle:** The reference circle has radius $\Phi/2$. The point moves with angular velocity $\omega_{ref} = \pi \omega / \beta$ (such that it completes half a revolution while the cam rotates through $\beta$). **Step 2 — Project onto the diameter:** When the reference point has moved through angle $\pi\theta/\beta$, its projection (measured from the center) is: $$\phi = \frac{\Phi}{2} - \frac{\Phi}{2}\cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$\boxed{\phi = \frac{\Phi}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to time** (using $\theta = \omega t$, so $d\theta/dt = \omega$): $$\dot{\phi} = \frac{\Phi}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi \omega}{\beta} \sin\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ $$\ddot{\phi} = \frac{\Phi}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi \omega}{\beta}\right)^2 \cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Find maximum values (at $\theta = \beta/2$ for velocity; at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$ for acceleration):** $$\dot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{\pi \Phi \omega}{2 \beta}$$ $$\ddot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2}{2 \beta^2}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Displacement Equation for Uniform Acceleration & Retardation **Concept:** During the **first half** of the rise ($\theta$ from $0$ to $\beta/2$), the follower is **uniformly accelerated** from rest. During the **second half** ($\theta$ from $\beta/2$ to $\beta$), it is **uniformly retarded** to rest at the top. **Step 1 — First half: $0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$** Let $\ddot{\phi} = C$ (constant). With initial conditions $\dot{\phi}(0) = 0$ and $\phi(0) = 0$: $$\dot{\phi} = C \cdot \frac{\theta}{\omega}$$ $$\phi = \frac{C}{2} \cdot \frac{\theta^2}{\omega^2} = \frac{C \theta^2}{2 \omega^2}$$ **Step 2 — Second half: $\beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta$** At $\theta = \beta/2$, $\phi = \Phi/2$ and $\dot{\phi} = \dot{\phi}_{max} = C \beta / (2 \omega)$. Let $\ddot{\phi} = -C$ (constant deceleration). With these boundary conditions: $$\phi = \Phi - \frac{C}{2 \omega^2}(\beta - \theta)^2$$ **Step 3 — Determine constant $C$ from $\phi(\beta) = \Phi$:** $$\Phi = \frac{C}{2 \omega^2} \cdot \frac{\beta^2}{4} + \Phi - \frac{C}{2 \omega^2} \cdot \frac{\beta^2}{4}$$ This is automatically satisfied, so the matching condition at $\theta = \beta/2$ gives: $$\frac{\Phi}{2} = \frac{C \beta^2}{8 \omega^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{4 \Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}$$ **Step 4 — Final equations:** For $0 \le \theta \le \beta/2$: $$\boxed{\phi = \frac{2 \Phi}{\beta^2} \theta^2}$$ For $\beta/2 \le \theta \le \beta$: $$\boxed{\phi = \Phi - \frac{2 \Phi}{\beta^2}(\beta - \theta)^2 = \Phi \left[1 - 2\left(1 - \frac{\theta}{\beta}\right)^2\right]}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum velocity** (at $\theta = \beta/2$): $$\dot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{2 \Phi \omega}{\beta}$$ **Step 6 — Maximum acceleration** (constant in each half): $$\ddot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{4 \Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Discontinuity in acceleration:** At $\theta = \beta/2$, the acceleration instantaneously changes from $+4\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2$ to $-4\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2$. This is a **finite jerk** (rate of change of acceleration), causing vibration at high speeds. Cycloidal motion avoids this. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Cycloidal Motion Displacement Equation **Concept:** The cycloid is the curve traced by a point on a circle rolling along a straight line. It is the smoothest possible motion because acceleration varies sinusoidally and is zero at both ends. **Step 1 — Set up rolling circle of radius $R$ rolling along a straight line of length $\Phi$ (the total lift).** For the rolling circle to traverse length $\Phi$ while the cam rotates through $\beta$, the rolling angular velocity must be $\omega_{roll} = 2\pi \omega / \beta$ (so the circle makes one full revolution in angle $\beta$). **Step 2 — Position of the tracing point (geometric construction):** The tracing point is on the *circumference* of the rolling circle. The total displacement of the follower is the **x-coordinate** of the tracing point minus a constant (such that it starts at 0). The parametric form of a cycloid is: $$x = R(\psi - \sin \psi), \quad y = R(1 - \cos \psi)$$ where $\psi$ is the angle of rotation of the rolling circle. **Step 3 — Substitute** $\psi = 2\pi\theta/\beta$ and require $x(0) = 0$, $x(\beta) = \Phi$: $$\phi = \frac{\Phi}{2\pi}\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta} - \sin\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\phi = \Phi\left[\frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate** with respect to time: $$\dot{\phi} = \frac{\Phi \omega}{\beta}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$ $$\ddot{\phi} = \frac{2\pi \Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Verify boundary conditions:** - At $\theta = 0$: $\phi = 0$, $\dot{\phi} = 0$, $\ddot{\phi} = 0$ ✓ - At $\theta = \beta$: $\phi = \Phi$, $\dot{\phi} = 0$, $\ddot{\phi} = 0$ ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > Cycloidal motion is **uniquely superior** because both velocity and acceleration are zero at the boundaries — no shock, no vibration, ideal for high-speed machinery. This is a favorite KTU viva and short-note question. --- ### 3.5 Summary: Comparison of Motion Profiles | Property | Uniform Velocity | SHM | Uniform Acc/Ret | Cycloidal | |---|---|---|---|---| | Initial Acceleration | $\infty$ (impulse) | $\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2 / (2\beta^2)$ (finite, non-zero) | $4\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2$ (finite) | $0$ ✓ | | Final Acceleration | $\infty$ (impulse) | $\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2 / (2\beta^2)$ (finite, non-zero) | $-4\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2$ (finite) | $0$ ✓ | | Acceleration continuity | Discontinuous (severe shock) | Continuous but non-zero at ends (mild shock) | Discontinuous at $\beta/2$ (mild shock) | Continuous everywhere ✓ | | Max Velocity | $\Phi\omega/\beta$ | $\pi\Phi\omega/(2\beta) \approx 1.57 \Phi\omega/\beta$ | $2\Phi\omega/\beta$ | $2\Phi\omega/\beta$ | | Max Acceleration | — | $\pi^2\Phi\omega^2/(2\beta^2) \approx 4.93\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2$ | $4\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2$ | $2\pi\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2 \approx 6.28\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2$ | | Use case | Very low speed, simple tools | Moderate speed | Quick indexing | High-speed precision | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Disc Cam with Oscillating Roller Follower — Functional Architecture The following diagram shows the **geometric and kinematic architecture** of the system. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain no markdown formatting. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["CAM DISC ROTATING ABOUT O"] -->|ANGULAR VELOCITY ω| nodeB["PITCH CURVE OF CAM"] nodeB -->|CONTACT NORMAL| nodeC["ROLLER CENTER A0 MOVING ALONG PITCH CURVE"] nodeC -->|PERPENDICULAR DISTANCE| nodeD["FOLLOWER ARM O1A0"] nodeD -->|PIVOTS ABOUT| nodeE["FIXED PIVOT O1 OF FOLLOWER"] nodeC -->|RADIAL VECTOR| nodeF["OA0 RADIUS VARIES DURING MOTION"] nodeF -->|TOGETHER WITH| nodeD nodeF -->|COMPUTED| nodeG["PRESSURE ANGLE α AT CONTACT"] nodeC -->|PROJECTS TO| nodeH["ANGULAR DISPLACEMENT φ OF FOLLOWER"] nodeH -->|DIFFERENTIATE| nodeI["ANGULAR VELOCITY dφ BY dt"] nodeI -->|DIFFERENTIATE| nodeJ["ANGULAR ACCELERATION d²φ BY dt²"] nodeG -->|DESIGN CHECK| nodeK["MAX PRESSURE αmax ≤ 30 DEG FOR TRANSLATING AND ≤ 45 DEG FOR OSCILLATING"] nodeB -->|OFFSET BY ROLLER RADIUS| nodeL["ACTUAL CAM PROFILE WORKING CURVE"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Rise-Dwell-Return Cycle (Outflow Timing) This sequence diagram maps the timing of follower motion through one complete cam rotation cycle. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP["START θ EQUALS 0"] -->|RISE β| nodeQ["FOLLOWER SWEEPS Φ USING SHM OR CYCLOIDAL"] nodeQ -->|OUTER DWELL θd1| nodeR["FOLLOWER STATIONARY AT TOP"] nodeR -->|RETURN γ| nodeS["FOLLOWER RETURNS Φ TO ORIGINAL POSITION"] nodeS -->|INNER DWELL θd2| nodeT["FOLLOWER STATIONARY AT BOTTOM"] nodeT -->|CYCLE REPEATS| nodeP nodeQ -->|VELOCITY dφ BY dt PROFILE| nodeQ nodeQ -->|ACCELERATION d²φ BY dt² PROFILE| nodeQ ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Graph: Comparison of Displacement Profiles This graph visualizes the four follower motion types qualitatively. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeM1["FOUR STANDARD FOLLOWER MOTIONS"] --> nodeN1["UNIFORM VELOCITY - STRAIGHT LINE"] nodeM1 --> nodeN2["SHM - SINE WAVE STARTING AND ENDING HORIZONTAL"] nodeM1 --> nodeN3["UNIFORM ACC AND RET - TWO PARABOLAS JOINED AT MIDDLE"] nodeM1 --> nodeN4["CYCLOIDAL - SMOOTH SINE START AND END WITH ZERO ACCELERATION"] nodeN1 -->|NEGATIVE| nodeO1["INFINITE ACC AT JUMPS AVOID AT HIGH SPEED"] nodeN2 -->|POSITIVE| nodeO2["FINITE ACC AT ENDS MILD SHOCK"] nodeN3 -->|PARTIAL| nodeO3["DISCONTINUOUS ACC AT MIDPOINT MILD SHOCK"] nodeN4 -->|BEST| nodeO4["ZERO ACC AT ENDS NO SHOCK IDEAL FOR HIGH SPEED"] ``` ### 4.4 ASCII Schematic of Disc Cam with Oscillating Roller Follower For students who need to draw this in the KTU exam, here is a clean text-based representation: ``` ROLLER (radius r_r) ___ / \ | A | ← Roller center A₀ on pitch curve \___/ |←— Pitch curve passes through A₀ | ___________________ __|__ ___________________ / \/ \/ \ / CAM PROFILE O \ | (base circle ↑ Cam center O | \ r_b) | / \___________________ _|__ ___________________/ | | d (center distance O to O₁) | O₁ ← Follower pivot (fixed) |←— L (follower arm length O₁A₀) | A₀ (roller center at start of rise) ``` **Key geometric parameters used in KTU problems:** - $d$ = center distance $O O_1$ (given or to be designed) - $L$ = follower arm length $O_1 A_0$ - $r_b$ = base circle radius - $r_r$ = roller radius - $R_c = O A_0$ = instantaneous radius (varies during motion) - $\phi_0$ = angle $\angle A_0 O_1 O$ in the lowest (base) position - $\Phi$ = total angular swing of the follower ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Define pressure angle for a cam with an oscillating roller follower. Why is the maximum permissible value larger for oscillating followers than for translating followers?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The pressure angle is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower (perpendicular to the follower arm $O_1 A_0$) and the **common normal** to the contact surfaces (the line joining the cam center $O$ to the roller center $A_0$) at the point of contact. It is denoted by $\alpha$ and is given by $\tan \alpha = L\dot{\phi}/(\omega \cdot O A_0)$. **Reason for higher permissible value (1.5 marks):** For an oscillating follower, the follower arm $L$ acts as a lever; the side thrust is reacted by the pivot $O_1$ rather than transmitted into the slideways as in a translating follower. Hence, the bearing loads are lower, and the side thrust does not cause jamming. The standard KTU limits are: - Translating follower: $\alpha_{max} \le 30°$ - Oscillating follower: $\alpha_{max} \le 45°$ --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Compare cycloidal and simple harmonic motions for a cam follower. Why is cycloidal motion preferred for high-speed cams?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect (1.5 marks) | SHM | Cycloidal | |---|---|---| | Acceleration at start and end of stroke | Finite but **non-zero** (causes mild shock) | **Zero** (no shock) | | Maximum acceleration | $\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2 / (2\beta^2) \approx 4.93 \Phi \omega^2/\beta^2$ | $2\pi \Phi \omega^2 / \beta^2 \approx 6.28 \Phi \omega^2/\beta^2$ | | Velocity profile | Sinusoidal | Cosinusoidal | **Why cycloidal is preferred (1.5 marks):** In cycloidal motion, both the velocity and acceleration are **zero at the start and end of the rise and return strokes**. This eliminates the inertial shock that occurs at the start of each stroke. The acceleration varies continuously and sinusoidally, producing no sudden change (no jerk) in the system. This makes cycloidal motion ideal for high-speed machinery where dynamic loads and vibration must be minimized. --- ### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 1]** **(a) [7 marks]** A disc cam operates an oscillating roller follower through an angle of $30°$ during the rise, which takes place with **simple harmonic motion**. The cam rotates at $240$ rpm. If the cam rotation angle during the rise is $120°$, determine: 1. The maximum angular velocity of the follower during the rise. 2. The maximum angular acceleration of the follower during the rise. 3. The angular acceleration of the follower when the cam has rotated $45°$ from the start of the rise. **(b) [7 marks)** For a disc cam with an oscillating roller follower, the cam center $O$ and follower pivot $O_1$ are $150$ mm apart. The roller center at the lowest position is at $A_0$, with $O A_0 = 100$ mm and $O_1 A_0 = 120$ mm. The follower is to rise with **uniform velocity**. If the cam angular velocity is $20$ rad/s and the follower swings through $20°$ in $60°$ of cam rotation, find the **maximum pressure angle** during the rise. --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a):** **Given:** - Total swing $\Phi = 30° = \pi/6$ rad - Cam speed $N = 240$ rpm, so $\omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 8\pi$ rad/s - Cam angle during rise $\beta = 120° = 2\pi/3$ rad **1) Maximum angular velocity of follower (SHM):** $$\dot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{\pi \Phi \omega}{2 \beta}$$ $$\dot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{\pi \cdot (\pi/6) \cdot 8\pi}{2 \cdot (2\pi/3)} = \frac{8\pi^3 / 6}{4\pi/3} = \frac{8\pi^3}{6} \cdot \frac{3}{4\pi} = \pi^2 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{\phi}_{max} \approx 9.87 \text{ rad/s}}$$ **2) Maximum angular acceleration of follower (SHM):** $$\ddot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2}{2 \beta^2}$$ $$\ddot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot (\pi/6) \cdot (8\pi)^2}{2 \cdot (2\pi/3)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot \pi/6 \cdot 64\pi^2}{2 \cdot 4\pi^2/9}$$ $$= \frac{64\pi^5 / 6}{8\pi^2 / 9} = \frac{64\pi^5}{6} \cdot \frac{9}{8\pi^2} = \frac{64 \cdot 9 \pi^3}{48} = 12\pi^3$$ $$\boxed{\ddot{\phi}_{max} \approx 372.0 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$ **3) Angular acceleration when cam has rotated $45°$ from start of rise:** For SHM, $\ddot{\phi} = \frac{\pi^2 \Phi \omega^2}{2\beta^2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$ At $\theta = 45° = \pi/4$ rad, $\beta = 2\pi/3$ rad: $$\frac{\pi \theta}{\beta} = \frac{\pi \cdot \pi/4}{2\pi/3} = \frac{3\pi}{8}$$ $$\cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{8}\right) = \cos 67.5° \approx 0.3827$$ $$\ddot{\phi} = 12\pi^3 \times 0.3827 \approx 4.59 \pi^3 \approx 142.4 \text{ rad/s}^2$$ $$\boxed{\ddot{\phi} \approx 142.4 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$ **Part (b):** **Given:** - $d = O O_1 = 150$ mm - $r_p = O A_0$ (at start) = $100$ mm - $L = O_1 A_0 = 120$ mm - $\omega = 20$ rad/s - Total swing $\Phi = 20° = \pi/9$ rad - Cam angle during rise $\beta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad - Motion: Uniform velocity **Step 1 — Angular velocity of follower (uniform velocity):** For uniform velocity motion, $\dot{\phi}$ is constant throughout the rise: $$\dot{\phi} = \frac{\Phi \cdot \omega}{\beta} = \frac{(\pi/9) \cdot 20}{\pi/3} = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.667 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $O A_0$ as a function of follower angle $\phi$:** Using the cosine rule in triangle $O A_0 O_1$ (with angle $\angle A_0 O_1 O = \beta_0$ at start): At start ($\phi = 0$): by cosine rule, $r_p^2 = d^2 + L^2 - 2 d L \cos \beta_0$: $$100^2 = 150^2 + 120^2 - 2(150)(120) \cos \beta_0$$ $$10000 = 22500 + 14400 - 36000 \cos \beta_0$$ $$\cos \beta_0 = \frac{36900 - 10000}{36000} = \frac{26900}{36000} \approx 0.7472$$ $$\beta_0 \approx 41.6°$$ **Step 3 — When the follower has rotated by $\phi$, the new angle $\angle A_0 O_1 O$ becomes $\beta_0 + \phi$.** The new $O A_0$ length is: $$R_c^2 = d^2 + L^2 - 2 d L \cos(\beta_0 + \phi)$$ **Step 4 — Pressure angle formula for oscillating follower:** $$\tan \alpha = \frac{L \dot{\phi}}{\omega \cdot R_c}$$ Since $L \dot{\phi}$ is constant (= $120 \times 6.667 = 800$ mm·rad/s) and $\omega = 20$ rad/s, the pressure angle is maximum when $R_c$ is **minimum**. **Step 5 — Minimize $R_c$:** $R_c$ is minimum when $\cos(\beta_0 + \phi)$ is maximum, i.e., when $\beta_0 + \phi = 0$ — but $\phi$ is positive during rise, so $\cos(\beta_0 + \phi)$ **decreases** as $\phi$ increases. Thus $R_c$ **increases** during rise, so $\tan \alpha$ is **maximum at the start** of the rise. Therefore, the maximum pressure angle occurs at $\phi = 0$: $$R_{c, \min} = r_p = 100 \text{ mm}$$ $$\tan \alpha_{max} = \frac{800}{20 \times 100} = \frac{800}{2000} = 0.4$$ $$\boxed{\alpha_{max} = \tan^{-1}(0.4) \approx 21.8°}$$ This is well within the KTU design limit of $45°$ for oscillating followers, so the design is safe. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2 Alternative]** **(a) [7 marks]** A disc cam with an oscillating roller follower is to provide a rise of $20°$ during $90°$ of cam rotation, a dwell for $30°$, a return of $20°$ during $90°$ of cam rotation, and a final dwell for the remaining $150°$ of cam rotation. The follower motion during rise and return is **uniform acceleration and retardation**. If the cam speed is $600$ rpm, calculate: 1. The maximum angular velocity of the follower during the rise. 2. The maximum angular acceleration during the rise. 3. The time required for the rise. **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the construction of the displacement diagram for **cycloidal motion** rise of a follower. State its advantages over simple harmonic motion. --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a):** **Given:** - Total swing $\Phi = 20° = \pi/9$ rad - Cam angle during rise $\beta = 90° = \pi/2$ rad - Cam speed $N = 600$ rpm, so $\omega = 2\pi \times 600/60 = 20\pi$ rad/s - Motion: Uniform acceleration and retardation **1) Maximum angular velocity of follower (uniform acc/ret):** $$\dot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{2 \Phi \omega}{\beta} = \frac{2 \times (\pi/9) \times 20\pi}{\pi/2}$$ $$= \frac{40\pi^2/9}{\pi/2} = \frac{80\pi}{9} \approx 27.93 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{\phi}_{max} \approx 27.93 \text{ rad/s}}$$ **2) Maximum angular acceleration during rise:** $$\ddot{\phi}_{max} = \frac{4 \Phi \omega^2}{\beta^2} = \frac{4 \times (\pi/9) \times (20\pi)^2}{(\pi/2)^2}$$ $$= \frac{4 \times \pi/9 \times 400\pi^2}{\pi^2/4} = \frac{1600\pi^3/9}{\pi^2/4} = \frac{6400\pi}{9} \approx 2233.6 \text{ rad/s}^2$$ $$\boxed{\ddot{\phi}_{max} \approx 2233.6 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$ **3) Time required for the rise:** $$t_{rise} = \frac{\beta}{\omega} = \frac{\pi/2}{20\pi} = \frac{1}{40} \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{t_{rise} = 0.025 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms}}$$ **Part (b): Construction of Cycloidal Displacement Diagram (7 marks):** **Construction Steps (4 marks):** 1. Draw the displacement diagram with cam angle $\beta$ on the x-axis (length $L_1$) and total lift $\Phi$ on the y-axis (length $L_2$). Mark the rise portion from $O$ to $B$ (length $L_1$ corresponding to $\beta$). 2. Divide $OB$ horizontally into **two equal parts** at point $C$ (i.e., $OC = CB = L_1/2$). 3. Divide the **vertical** $A A_1$ (representing the lift $\Phi$) also into **two equal parts**, marking $A_2$ at the mid-height. 4. Draw a **semicircle** on the horizontal $A_2 A_3$ (the upper half) with diameter $A_2 A_3 = L_2$ as the y-axis. 5. Divide the semicircle into any number of equal parts (say 6), naming the divisions $1', 2', 3', 4', 5', 6'$ starting from $A_3$ (top). 6. Draw vertical lines from these points on the semicircle to intersect the horizontal line $A_2 A_1$ at points $1'', 2'', 3'', \ldots$ 7. Now divide the base $OB$ into the **same number of equal parts** as the semicircle (6 parts here), marking points $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$. 8. From each division point $i$ on $OB$, draw a vertical line; from the corresponding point $i''$ on the horizontal $A_2 A_1$, draw a horizontal line. Their intersection is a point on the cycloidal displacement curve. 9. Connect all such intersections with a smooth curve — this is the **cycloidal displacement diagram**. **Mathematical form (1.5 marks):** The resulting curve satisfies: $$\phi = \Phi \left[ \frac{\theta}{\beta} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \sin\left(\frac{2\pi \theta}{\beta}\right) \right]$$ **Advantages over SHM (1.5 marks):** 1. **Zero acceleration at both ends** of the rise (and return), giving zero shock at the beginning and end of stroke. 2. The acceleration varies sinusoidally and is **continuous** throughout the motion — no abrupt changes (no infinite jerk). 3. The **maximum acceleration is lower** for the same lift, cam angle, and speed (only $2\pi \approx 6.28$ vs. SHM's $\pi^2/2 \approx 4.93$ — but cycloidal's lower bound zero wins out in vibration reduction). 4. Cycloidal motion is **uniquely the best** for high-speed cam design where vibration, noise, and dynamic loads are critical. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in $\Phi$, $\beta$, $\theta$ before substituting into sine/cosine. **Always convert to radians for trig functions.** > 2. **Mixing up $O A_0$ with $L$.** The pressure angle formula uses $O A_0$ (cam center to roller center), which is *not* the same as the follower arm length $L = O_1 A_0$. > 3. **Forgetting the geometric constraint** that the pressure angle for an oscillating follower is not at a fixed point but varies as the roller traces the pitch curve. Always check whether $O A_0$ is constant or varying. > 4. **Confusing "uniform velocity" with "uniform angular velocity"** of the cam. The cam speed $\omega$ is *uniform*; it's the *follower* motion that varies by type. > 5. **Not checking the design limit** $\alpha_{max} \le 45°$ for oscillating followers. A perfectly correct calculation that gives $\alpha = 50°$ should be flagged as an unsafe design. > 6. **Mixing up maximum velocity locations:** For SHM, max velocity is at mid-stroke ($\theta = \beta/2$); for uniform acc/ret, also at mid-stroke; for cycloidal, max velocity is also at mid-stroke. Max *acceleration* for SHM is at ends; for uniform acc/ret, the whole first half; for cycloidal, at $\theta = \beta/4$ and $3\beta/4$. --- ### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — High-Density Summary** - ✅ **Cam**: Driver with curved/irregular profile; **Follower**: Driven link. - ✅ **Oscillating roller follower** = follower that **swings** about a fixed pivot, with a **roller** at the contact point. - ✅ **Lift $s$** is replaced by **angular swing $\Phi$** for oscillating followers. - ✅ **Pressure angle $\alpha$**: angle between follower motion direction and common normal to the contact surfaces. For oscillating follower: $\tan \alpha = L\dot{\phi}/(\omega \cdot O A_0)$. - ✅ **Design limits**: $\alpha_{max} \le 30°$ (translating) and $\alpha_{max} \le 45°$ (oscillating). - ✅ **Four standard follower motions**: 1. **Uniform Velocity** — simple, but infinite acceleration jumps (avoid for high speed). 2. **Simple Harmonic (SHM)** — sine curve, finite acceleration at ends (mild shock). 3. **Uniform Acceleration & Retardation** — two parabolas, discontinuous acceleration at mid-stroke (mild shock). 4. **Cycloidal** — smoothest; **zero acceleration at both ends**; **best for high-speed cams** (no shock). - ✅ **Maximum velocity comparison** (for same $\Phi$, $\beta$, $\omega$): Uniform velocity < SHM < Uniform Acc/Ret = Cycloidal = $2\Phi\omega/\beta$. - ✅ **Maximum acceleration comparison**: Cycloidal ($2\pi\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2 \approx 6.28$) > SHM ($\pi^2\Phi\omega^2/(2\beta^2) \approx 4.93$) > Uniform Acc/Ret ($4\Phi\omega^2/\beta^2 = 4$). - ✅ **Roller follower advantage**: converts sliding friction to rolling friction — **less wear**, **longer cam life**. - ✅ **Cam profile** = pitch curve **offset** outward by roller radius $r_r$ (use the concept of the *equivalent radial translating follower* method for graphical construction). - ✅ **Construction of cycloidal displacement diagram**: half-circle on top half of lift, divide into $n$ parts, project down to find cycloidal ordinates. - ✅ **Boundary conditions for every motion type**: $\phi(0) = 0$, $\phi(\beta) = \Phi$, $\dot{\phi}(0) = 0$, $\dot{\phi}(\beta) = 0$ (except uniform velocity, which has velocity jump at boundaries). - ✅ **Time for rise**: $t_{rise} = \beta / \omega = \beta \cdot 60 / (2\pi N)$ minutes. - ✅ **For SHM motion**: $\phi = (\Phi/2)[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\beta)]$ — derived from projection of uniform circular motion. - ✅ **For uniform acc/ret motion**: First half is $\phi = (2\Phi/\beta^2)\theta^2$; second half is $\phi = \Phi[1 - 2(1-\theta/\beta)^2]$. - ✅ **For cycloidal motion**: $\phi = \Phi[\theta/\beta - (1/2\pi)\sin(2\pi\theta/\beta)]$ — derived from a cycloid generated by a rolling circle. > **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations! Master the derivations, practice numerical problems from past papers, and remember the design limits for pressure angle. These notes cover the complete Module 3 syllabus topic on disc cam with oscillating roller follower and follower motions.** 🎓

topics.title: Uniform Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Uniform Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Uniform Velocity Motion** is a fundamental follower motion profile used in cam kinematics in which the follower translates (or rotates) with a *constant linear (or angular) velocity* during its outward and inward strokes. The defining characteristic is that the slope of the displacement-time curve remains perfectly constant throughout each of the rise and return phases, while theoretically infinite acceleration is demanded at the transition points (start and end of stroke) to instantly impart or arrest the velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGT403 - Module 3):** The follower motion with uniform velocity is a *primary-displacement* profile, classified under "Standard Cam Follower Motions." The student is required to derive the displacement, velocity, and acceleration expressions and interpret the cam profile characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a passenger elevator that starts moving **instantly** at full speed the moment the doors close, travels at exactly the same speed on every floor, and then stops **instantly** the moment it aligns with the destination floor. That is *Uniform Velocity*. The rider feels a violent jerk on entering and leaving the motion. In real life, elevators use a *trapezoidal* velocity profile to avoid this jerk — but in the idealized world of kinematic cam design, this is the starting case.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Stroke length (S)**: Total displacement of the follower in **mm** or **m**.
- **Cam speed (N)**: Rotational speed in **rpm** (revolutions per minute).
- **Cam angular velocity (ω)**: 2πN/60 in **rad/s**.
- **Angle of rise (β)**: Cam rotation in **radians** during outstroke.
- **Angle of return (δ)**: Cam rotation in **radians** during return stroke.
- **Periodicity (T)**: 2π/ω in **seconds** per cam revolution.

> [!NOTE]
> In all derivations following, the cam is assumed to rotate at *constant* angular velocity ω, and the follower axis is taken to pass through the cam's axis of rotation (radial translating follower), unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases of One Cam Revolution

A typical cam operating with uniform-velocity motion is described in four distinct kinematic segments, each occupying a specific angular span of the cam's 2π revolution:

1. **Outstroke (Rise)** — Angular span **β**, follower moves from 0 to S at constant velocity.
2. **Outer Dwell** — Angular span **Φd1**, follower remains stationary at height S.
3. **Return Stroke** — Angular span **δ**, follower returns from S to 0 at constant velocity.
4. **Inner Dwell** — Angular span **Φd2**, follower remains at the base position.

The angle-closure identity is:

$$\beta + \delta + \Phi_{d1} + \Phi_{d2} = 2\pi$$

> [!NOTE]
> Many textbook cam problems *omit* dwells (Φd1 = Φd2 = 0) for simplicity. In that case, β + δ = 2π.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Each Phase

### 2.2.1 Outstroke (Rise)
- **Why?** The mechanism must transmit a controlled, predictable motion to the follower.
- **How?** The cam profile is constructed as an Archimedean spiral (for a radial follower). Each equal increment of cam angle θ produces an equal increment in follower position y.
- **Result:** Constant velocity = S·ω/β, but theoretically *infinite* acceleration at the very start and end of the rise.

### 2.2.2 Outer Dwell
- **Why?** In some applications (e.g., a fuel injection pump, a forming press dwell), the follower must pause at the topmost position.
- **How?** The cam profile is a perfect circle concentric with the base circle.

### 2.2.3 Return Stroke
- **Why?** Restore the follower to the starting position for the next cycle.
- **How?** Reverse spiral — but if β ≠ δ, the "spiral pitch" on the return is different from the rise.
- **Result:** Constant negative velocity = –S·ω/δ.

### 2.2.4 Inner Dwell
- **Why?** Required to allow other operations in the machine cycle (e.g., exhaust in an internal-combustion engine).
- **How?** Cam profile coincides with the base circle.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Rise (0 ≤ θ ≤ β)** | **Outer Dwell (β ≤ θ ≤ β + Φd1)** | **Return (β + Φd1 ≤ θ ≤ β + Φd1 + δ)** | **Inner Dwell (remaining 2π)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement $y$ | $\dfrac{S \cdot \theta}{\beta}$ | $S$ | $S \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\theta - \beta - \Phi_{d1}}{\delta}\right)$ | $0$ |
| Velocity $v = \dot y$ | $\dfrac{S \cdot \omega}{\beta}$ (constant) | $0$ | $-\dfrac{S \cdot \omega}{\delta}$ (constant) | $0$ |
| Acceleration $a = \ddot y$ | $0$ (theoretically) | $0$ | $0$ (theoretically) | $0$ |
| Jerk $j = \dddot y$ | $\infty$ at θ = 0 and θ = β | $0$ | $\infty$ at boundaries | $0$ |
| Max velocity | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{S \cdot \omega}{\beta}$ | – | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{S \cdot \omega}{\delta}$ | – |
| Max acceleration | $\infty$ (impulse at boundaries) | – | $\infty$ (impulse at boundaries) | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\|` in any inline expressions to preserve the KTU markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Uniform Velocity** |
|---|---|
| **Automatic Screw Machines** | Linear feed of the work-piece at constant rate for uniform depth of cut. |
| **Printing Presses** | Ink-roller or paper-feed indexing requiring constant traverse speed. |
| **Packaging Machinery** | Label-feed cams producing constant web speed. |
| **Hydraulic/Pneumatic Valve Gear** | Low-speed auxiliary cams where dynamic smoothness is not critical. |
| **Toy Mechanisms** | Wind-up toys with simple cams — mass-production cost outweighs smoothness. |

In high-speed machinery, uniform velocity is *avoided* in favour of **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**, **Cycloidal**, or **Modified Trapezoidal** profiles because the impulsive accelerations cause unacceptable vibration, noise, and impact loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup and Variables

We begin with the following givens for the derivation:

- Cam rotates at a uniform angular velocity **ω** rad/s.
- The follower is a **translating radial follower** (most common textbook case).
- Let **θ** be the angular position of the cam measured from the *start of the rise*.
- Let **y** be the instantaneous displacement of the follower from its lowest position.
- Stroke length is **S**, angle of rise is **β**, angle of return is **δ**.

The fundamental relationship between time and cam angle is:

$$\theta = \omega \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{\theta}{\omega}$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \quad \text{(constant, since the cam runs at steady speed)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Outstroke (Rise Phase)

### 3.2.1 Displacement y(θ)

By the very definition of uniform velocity, the displacement grows *linearly* with the cam angle θ. At the start of the rise (θ = 0), the displacement is **y = 0**. At the end of the rise (θ = β), the displacement is **y = S**. The graph of y vs. θ is a straight line. The equation of a straight line passing through (0, 0) and (β, S) is:

$$y = \frac{S - 0}{\beta - 0} \cdot \theta + 0 = \frac{S \cdot \theta}{\beta}$$

Therefore, during the rise, the displacement is:

$$y = \frac{S \cdot \theta}{\beta} \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le \theta \le \beta$$

### 3.2.2 Velocity v(θ)

Velocity is the first time-derivative of displacement. Using the chain rule:

$$v = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{dy}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{dy}{d\theta} \cdot \omega$$

Computing dy/dθ from the displacement expression:

$$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{d}{d\theta}\left(\frac{S \cdot \theta}{\beta}\right) = \frac{S}{\beta}$$

Substituting back:

$$v = \frac{S}{\beta} \cdot \omega = \frac{S \cdot \omega}{\beta}$$

Hence, during the rise, the velocity is a **constant** equal to S·ω/β.

### 3.2.3 Acceleration a(θ)

Acceleration is the derivative of velocity with respect to time:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{S \cdot \omega}{\beta}\right) = 0 \quad \text{(since S, ω, β are all constants)}$$

Hence, during the rise, the acceleration is **theoretically zero**.

### 3.2.4 Jerk j(θ)

Jerk is the time-derivative of acceleration:

$$j = \frac{da}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{(within the rise interval)}$$

However, at the **boundaries** of the rise (θ = 0 and θ = β), the follower velocity suddenly changes from 0 to S·ω/β (or vice-versa). The change in velocity is Δv = S·ω/β, occurring over an infinitesimally small time interval Δt → 0. Therefore:

$$a_{\text{boundary}} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \infty$$

This infinite theoretical acceleration corresponds to the *jerk pulses* (Dirac delta functions) at the transition points.

## 3.3 Derivation — Outer Dwell (Phase 2)

The follower remains stationary at y = S. By inspection:

$$y = S, \quad v = 0, \quad a = 0, \quad j = 0$$

## 3.4 Derivation — Return Stroke (Phase 3)

Define a local angle φ for the return stroke such that φ = 0 at the start of return (i.e., φ = θ − β − Φd1). At φ = 0, the displacement is **y = S**. At φ = δ, the displacement is **y = 0**. The straight line through (0, S) and (δ, 0):

$$y = S + \frac{0 - S}{\delta - 0} \cdot \varphi = S - \frac{S \cdot \varphi}{\delta} = S \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\varphi}{\delta}\right)$$

In terms of the global cam angle θ:

$$y = S \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\theta - \beta - \Phi_{d1}}{\delta}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad \beta + \Phi_{d1} \le \theta \le \beta + \Phi_{d1} + \delta$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = -\frac{S}{\delta}$$

Multiplying by ω:

$$v = -\frac{S \cdot \omega}{\delta} \quad \text{(negative sign indicates direction of motion)}$$

Acceleration:

$$a = 0 \quad \text{within the return interval}$$

Jerk is theoretically infinite at φ = 0 and φ = δ (the transition points).

## 3.5 Derivation — Inner Dwell (Phase 4)

The follower is at rest at y = 0:

$$y = 0, \quad v = 0, \quad a = 0, \quad j = 0$$

## 3.6 Compact Displacement–Velocity–Acceleration Master Table

| **Phase** | **Angular Interval** | **Displacement y** | **Velocity v** | **Acceleration a** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rise | $0 \le \theta \le \beta$ | $\dfrac{S \theta}{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{S \omega}{\beta}$ | $0$ |
| Outer dwell | $\beta \le \theta \le \beta + \Phi_{d1}$ | $S$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Return | $\beta + \Phi_{d1} \le \theta \le \beta + \Phi_{d1} + \delta$ | $S \left(1 - \dfrac{\theta - \beta - \Phi_{d1}}{\delta}\right)$ | $-\dfrac{S \omega}{\delta}$ | $0$ |
| Inner dwell | remaining $2\pi$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

## 3.7 Maximum Velocity Theorem

The *peak* velocity attained by the follower is the **larger** of the two constant velocities (rise or return). It is a direct function of which stroke is *shorter* in angular span:

$$v_{\text{peak}} = S \cdot \omega \cdot \max\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta}, \frac{1}{\delta}\right)$$

If β = δ (symmetrical cam), then the peak velocity is identical in magnitude on both strokes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Making the rise slower (larger β) *reduces* the rise velocity, but it simultaneously *shortens* the available time for the return — increasing return velocity. The cam designer must balance both.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Cam Cycle with Uniform Velocity Motion

The following block diagram captures the state machine governing the cam-follower motion. Each state corresponds to one of the four kinematic phases derived above. The transition arrows are tagged with the *trigger condition* (cam angle θ crossing a threshold) and the *boundary effects* (infinite jerk).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateA
    StateA: STATE A : Outstroke (Rise)
    StateA: y = (S*theta)/beta
    StateA: v = S*omega/beta (constant)
    StateA: a = 0
    StateB: STATE B : Outer Dwell
    StateB: y = S (constant)
    StateB: v = 0, a = 0
    StateC: STATE C : Return Stroke
    StateC: y = S*(1 - phi/delta)
    StateC: v = -S*omega/delta
    StateC: a = 0
    StateD: STATE D : Inner Dwell
    StateD: y = 0 (constant)
    StateD: v = 0, a = 0
    StateA --> StateB : theta = beta ; infinite jerk
    StateB --> StateC : theta = beta + Phi_d1 ; infinite jerk
    StateC --> StateD : theta = beta + Phi_d1 + delta ; infinite jerk
    StateD --> StateA : theta = 2*pi ; infinite jerk
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Kinematic Profile Curves

The three orthogonal graphs (displacement, velocity, acceleration) of uniform-velocity motion. The displacement rises *linearly*, the velocity is a *rectangular pulse*, and the acceleration is *zero everywhere* with Dirac-impulses at the boundaries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DISPLACEMENT y vs theta] -->|Linear ramp up| B1[Rise: y = S*theta/beta]
    A1 -->|Flat top| C1[Dwell: y = S]
    A1 -->|Linear ramp down| D1[Return: y = S*(1 - phi/delta)]
    A1 -->|Flat bottom| E1[Dwell: y = 0]

    A2[VELOCITY v vs theta] -->|Constant rectangle positive| B2[Rise: v = S*omega/beta]
    A2 -->|Zero level| C2[Dwell: v = 0]
    A2 -->|Constant rectangle negative| D2[Return: v = -S*omega/delta]
    A2 -->|Zero level| E2[Dwell: v = 0]

    A3[ACCELERATION a vs theta] -->|Zero level| B3[Rise: a = 0]
    A3 -->|Impulse spike| C3[Boundary at theta = beta]
    A3 -->|Zero level| D3[Return: a = 0]
    A3 -->|Impulse spike| E3[Boundary at theta = 2*pi]

    B1 -.->|Coupled| B2
    C1 -.->|Coupled| C2
    D1 -.->|Coupled| D2
    B2 -.->|Derivative| B3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cam Profile Construction

For a radial disc cam with translating follower, the radial distance from the cam centre to the contact point at any angle θ is:

$$r(\theta) = R_b + r_r + y(\theta)$$

where $R_b$ is the base-circle radius, $r_r$ is the roller radius, and y(θ) is the follower displacement profile. For uniform-velocity motion, y(θ) is linear in θ — therefore, **r(θ) traces an Archimedean spiral** during the rise and a mirrored Archimedean spiral during the return.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[/Given: S, beta, delta, omega, Rb, rr/] --> Phase1[Phase 1: Outstroke Polar Equation]
    Input --> Phase2[Phase 2: Outer Dwell Polar Equation]
    Input --> Phase3[Phase 3: Return Polar Equation]
    Input --> Phase4[Phase 4: Inner Dwell Polar Equation]
    Phase1 --> Spiral1[Archimedean Spiral Ascending : r = Rb + rr + S*theta/beta]
    Phase2 --> Circle1[Concentric Circle : r = Rb + rr + S]
    Phase3 --> Spiral2[Archimedean Spiral Descending : r = Rb + rr + S - S*phi/delta]
    Phase4 --> Circle2[Base Circle : r = Rb]
    Spiral1 --> CamProfile[Final Cam Profile Polar Curve]
    Circle1 --> CamProfile
    Spiral2 --> CamProfile
    Circle2 --> CamProfile
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Define uniform velocity motion as applied to cam followers. Why is it generally not preferred for high-speed cam mechanisms?**

**Model Answer:**
Uniform velocity motion is a follower-displacement programme in which the follower moves with a **constant linear velocity** during both the outstroke and the return stroke. Mathematically, dy/dt = constant = S·ω/β (rise) and dy/dt = –S·ω/δ (return), while the acceleration is theoretically zero *within* each stroke.

It is *not* preferred for high-speed cam mechanisms because the **infinite impulsive accelerations** at the start and end of each stroke produce severe vibration, noise, and impact loading — often leading to follower jump, cam wear, and dynamic failure. Hence, smoother profiles such as **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**, **Cycloidal**, or **Modified Trapezoidal** are used instead.

*(Word count: 95 — fits the 3-mark KTU answer length norm.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Sketch the displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams for a cam follower executing uniform velocity motion during rise. Indicate the key features.**

**Model Answer:**
The three diagrams are:
- **Displacement vs. cam angle θ:** A straight line rising linearly from 0 to S over the angular interval β.
- **Velocity vs. θ:** A rectangular block of constant height S·ω/β over the same interval.
- **Acceleration vs. θ:** A zero line (a = 0) within the interval, with two *theoretical impulse spikes* (infinite magnitude, zero duration) at θ = 0 and θ = β, indicating the instantaneous changes in velocity.

Key features:
1. Constant velocity throughout the rise.
2. Zero acceleration during the rise (smooth kinematics inside the stroke).
3. Infinite impulsive acceleration at the *boundaries*, which is the chief disadvantage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - A *very common* mistake is forgetting to convert the cam speed from **rpm to rad/s** before applying ω = 2πN/60. Marks are routinely lost here.
> - Many students compute the *rise* velocity only and forget the *return* velocity comparison, even when β ≠ δ.
> - Do **not** write the acceleration as "zero everywhere" — it is zero *within* the stroke but infinite *at the boundaries*. Phrase this carefully to secure full marks.
> - Always state the units explicitly in the final answer.
> - In cam-profile construction, drawing the displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams to scale is essential — KTU evaluators allot at least 2 marks for scaled sketches.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A radial translating follower is to rise through 40 mm with uniform velocity in 90° of cam rotation, dwell for 60° at the top, and return with uniform velocity in 120° of cam rotation. The cam rotates at 240 rpm clockwise. Find:**

**(a)** The maximum velocity of the follower during rise and return.
**(b)** The acceleration of the follower during the rise and return strokes. Comment on the result.

#### Part (a) — Maximum Velocities

**Step 1 — Given data and unit conversion:**
- Stroke S = 40 mm = 0.04 m
- Angle of rise β = 90° = π/2 rad ≈ 1.5708 rad
- Angle of return δ = 120° = 2π/3 rad ≈ 2.0944 rad
- Top dwell Φd1 = 60° = π/3 rad
- Cam speed N = 240 rpm

Cam angular velocity in rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 240}{60} = 2\pi \cdot 4 = 8\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity during rise:**

$$v_{\text{rise}} = \frac{S \cdot \omega}{\beta} = \frac{0.04 \cdot 8\pi}{\pi/2} = \frac{0.32\pi}{\pi/2} = 0.32 \cdot 2 = 0.64 \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to mm/s: v_rise = 640 mm/s.

**Step 3 — Velocity during return:**

$$v_{\text{return}} = \frac{S \cdot \omega}{\delta} = \frac{0.04 \cdot 8\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.32\pi}{2\pi/3} = 0.32 \cdot \frac{3}{2} = 0.48 \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to mm/s: v_return = 480 mm/s.

**Step 4 — Identifying the maximum:**

$$v_{\max} = \max(0.64, 0.48) = 0.64 \text{ m/s} = 640 \text{ mm/s (during the rise)}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Acceleration & Comment

**Step 1 — Acceleration within each stroke:**

For uniform velocity, the acceleration is theoretically zero throughout the rise and return strokes:

$$a_{\text{rise}} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le \theta \le \beta$$
$$a_{\text{return}} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \beta + \Phi_{d1} \le \theta \le \beta + \Phi_{d1} + \delta$$

**Step 2 — Acceleration at the boundaries:**

At θ = 0 (start of rise), θ = β (end of rise), and at the corresponding return boundaries, the follower velocity changes instantaneously by Δv = 0.64 m/s (rise) or 0.48 m/s (return) over Δt → 0, yielding:

$$a_{\text{boundary}} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \infty \quad \text{(theoretical impulse)}$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**

The acceleration diagram is **identically zero** during the rise and return strokes, but contains *four infinite spikes* at the transition points. Physically, this means:
- The cam-follower system is **kinematically smooth within each stroke**, requiring no inertial force from the follower mass during the actual rise/return.
- However, the **infinite impulsive acceleration** at the stroke boundaries causes **severe dynamic shocks**, leading to vibration, noise, impact wear on the cam-roller interface, and possible follower "jump" at high speeds. Hence, for this high cam speed (240 rpm) with such a substantial stroke (40 mm), the uniform-velocity profile is **not recommended** in practice. Engineers would prefer a **Simple Harmonic Motion** or **Cycloidal** profile for smoother operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**A cam drives an in-line translating knife-edge follower with uniform velocity during the outstroke of 25 mm in 120° of cam rotation and uniform velocity return in 90°. The remaining angle is the outer dwell. The cam rotates at 150 rpm.**

**(a)** Derive the displacement, velocity, and acceleration equations for the rise and return strokes.
**(b)** Plot the displacement-time, velocity-time, and acceleration-time diagrams with their peak values clearly marked.

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Equations

**Step 1 — Given data and unit conversion:**
- Stroke S = 25 mm = 0.025 m
- Angle of rise β = 120° = 2π/3 rad ≈ 2.0944 rad
- Angle of return δ = 90° = π/2 rad ≈ 1.5708 rad
- Outer dwell Φd1 = 2π − β − δ = 2π − 2π/3 − π/2 = 2π · (1 − 2/3 − 1/4) = 2π · (12/12 − 8/12 − 3/12) = 2π · (1/12) = π/6 rad = 30°
- Cam speed N = 150 rpm

Cam angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 150}{60} = 5\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 15.708 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Outstroke equations:**

Displacement is a linear function of cam angle θ, passing through (0, 0) and (β, S):

$$y = \frac{S \cdot \theta}{\beta} = \frac{0.025 \cdot \theta}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.025 \cdot 3 \cdot \theta}{2\pi} = \frac{0.0375 \cdot \theta}{\pi} \quad \text{[m]}$$

In terms of time t (since θ = ωt = 5π·t):

$$y = \frac{0.0375 \cdot 5\pi \cdot t}{\pi} = 0.1875 \cdot t \quad \text{[m]}$$

Velocity is the time-derivative:

$$v = \frac{S \cdot \omega}{\beta} = \frac{0.025 \cdot 5\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.125\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{0.125 \cdot 3}{2} = 0.1875 \text{ m/s} = 187.5 \text{ mm/s}$$

Acceleration during the rise is theoretically zero:

$$a = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le t \le t_{\beta}, \quad t_{\beta} = \frac{\beta}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi/3}{5\pi} = \frac{2}{15} \text{ s} \approx 0.1333 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Return stroke equations:**

Defining a local angle φ for the return (φ = 0 at the start of return, φ = δ at the end), displacement is a linear function passing through (0, S) and (δ, 0):

$$y = S \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\varphi}{\delta}\right) = 0.025 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\varphi}{\pi/2}\right) = 0.025 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\varphi}{\pi}\right)$$

In terms of global cam angle θ with the start of return at θ = β + Φd1 = 2π/3 + π/6 = 5π/6:

$$y = 0.025 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2(\theta - 5\pi/6)}{\pi}\right)$$

Velocity during return:

$$v = -\frac{S \cdot \omega}{\delta} = -\frac{0.025 \cdot 5\pi}{\pi/2} = -\frac{0.125\pi}{\pi/2} = -0.25 \text{ m/s} = -250 \text{ mm/s}$$

The negative sign indicates motion in the *return* direction.

Acceleration during the return is theoretically zero:

$$a = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad t_{\beta} + t_{\Phi d1} \le t \le t_{\beta} + t_{\Phi d1} + t_{\delta}$$

where $t_{\delta} = \delta/\omega = (\pi/2)/(5\pi) = 1/10$ s = 0.1 s, and $t_{\Phi d1} = \Phi_{d1}/\omega = (\pi/6)/(5\pi) = 1/30$ s ≈ 0.0333 s.

**Step 4 — Summary of derived equations:**

| **Quantity** | **Rise** | **Return** |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement $y$ | $y = 0.1875 \cdot t$ [m] | $y = 0.025 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\theta - 5\pi/6}{\pi/2}\right)$ [m] |
| Velocity $v$ | $+0.1875$ m/s | $-0.25$ m/s |
| Acceleration $a$ | $0$ | $0$ |

---

#### Part (b) — Plots of Kinematic Profiles

**Step 1 — Time domain parameters:**

- Rise duration: $t_{\beta} = 0.1333$ s
- Outer dwell duration: $t_{\Phi d1} = 0.0333$ s
- Return duration: $t_{\delta} = 0.1000$ s
- Total period: $T = 2\pi/\omega = 0.4$ s
- Inner dwell duration: $T - t_{\beta} - t_{\Phi d1} - t_{\delta} = 0.4 - 0.1333 - 0.0333 - 0.1 = 0.1334$ s

**Step 2 — Peak values marked:**

- Displacement peak: $y_{\max} = S = 25$ mm (held constant during the outer dwell).
- Velocity peaks: $v_{\text{rise,peak}} = 187.5$ mm/s (positive); $v_{\text{return,peak}} = 250$ mm/s (negative, larger in magnitude).
- Acceleration peaks: 0 mm/s² (theoretically) within each stroke; $\pm \infty$ at the four boundary instants.

**Step 3 — Sketched curves (description for KTU answer sheet):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Displacement vs Time: Linear ramp 0 to 25 mm in 0.1333 s]
    P2[Outer dwell flat at 25 mm for 0.0333 s]
    P3[Linear ramp down from 25 mm to 0 mm in 0.1 s]
    P4[Inner dwell flat at 0 mm for 0.1334 s]

    V1[Velocity vs Time: Rectangular block at +187.5 mm/s for 0.1333 s]
    V2[Zero level during outer dwell 0.0333 s]
    V3[Rectangular block at -250 mm/s for 0.1 s]
    V4[Zero level during inner dwell 0.1334 s]

    A1[Acceleration vs Time: Zero level with infinite impulse spike at t = 0]
    A2[Zero level during outer dwell]
    A3[Infinite impulse spike at end of rise]
    A4[Zero level during return with infinite spikes at boundaries]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
```

**Reading the graphs for the exam:**
- The **displacement-time** graph consists of a positive-slope line, a horizontal line, a negative-slope line, and a horizontal line at zero.
- The **velocity-time** graph shows a positive rectangle (+187.5 mm/s), a zero line, a *larger* negative rectangle (–250 mm/s), and a final zero line.
- The **acceleration-time** graph is *mostly* zero, punctuated by four vertical spikes of infinite height (Dirac impulses) at the transition points. In KTU examination sketches, these spikes are conventionally drawn as upward arrows of indefinite height with a label "∞".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Uniform Velocity Motion (Cam Followers)**

- ✅ **Core definition:** The follower translates with a *constant velocity* (zero acceleration) during the rise and return strokes; impulses occur at the boundaries.
- ✅ **Displacement equations:** $y_{\text{rise}} = (S/\beta) \cdot \theta$ and $y_{\text{return}} = S(1 - \varphi/\delta)$.
- ✅ **Velocity equation:** $v_{\text{rise}} = S \cdot \omega/\beta$, $v_{\text{return}} = -S \cdot \omega/\delta$.
- ✅ **Acceleration:** Theoretically zero *within* strokes, infinite *at* the four transition points.
- ✅ **Unit conversion (mandatory):** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ — never use $N$ directly in rad/s formulas.
- ✅ **Angle closure:** $\beta + \delta + \Phi_{d1} + \Phi_{d2} = 2\pi$.
- ✅ **Polar cam profile:** Archimedean spiral during rise and return; concentric circle during dwells.
- ✅ **Maximum velocity theorem:** $v_{\text{peak}} = S \cdot \omega \cdot \max(1/\beta, 1/\delta)$.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Impulsive acceleration, severe vibration, follower jump risk — not preferred for high-speed cams.
- ✅ **Comparison peers:** SHM (sinusoidal), Cycloidal, Modified Trapezoidal — all smoother, all preferred.
- ✅ **Engineering use cases:** Low-speed, low-cost machines (toy mechanisms, slow automatic screw machines, indexing cams).
- ✅ **Marker trap to avoid:** Do **not** state "acceleration is zero" without qualifying "within the stroke, infinite at boundaries." A common KTU answer defect that costs 1–2 marks.
- ✅ **Sketch standard:** Always draw the displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams *on the same time/angle axis* with the same abscissa scale; mark the peak values numerically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-mile KTU tip:** If a problem gives N in rpm and angles in degrees, *immediately* convert angles to radians and N to rad/s. Many students skip this and get a numerical answer that is exactly off by a factor of 57.3 or 9.55 — and the KTU answer-key check is unforgiving. Carry out unit-conversions as a *separate displayed step* in your answer script to gain the method mark even if the final number is miscalculated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
