> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Uniform Acceleration and Retardation and Cycloidal Motion; Introduction to the Design Process ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR) Motion **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR), also known as **Parabolic Motion**, is a follower motion profile in which the follower undergoes a constant positive acceleration during the first half of the rise (or return) stroke and an equal constant negative acceleration (deceleration/retardation) during the second half. The displacement diagram consists of two parabolic arcs that meet at mid-stroke, producing a sharp peak in velocity at the midpoint. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine you are riding a bicycle and you start pedaling as hard as you can (uniform acceleration), reach maximum speed at the middle of the road, and then apply the brakes with the same force until you stop exactly at the end of the road. Your velocity vs. time graph will look like a triangle — rising linearly to a peak and then falling. Now, if you plot your *position* vs. *time*, you get a parabolic shape. This is exactly what a UAR cam does to a follower. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The UAR profile is widely used in low-to-medium speed machines. The principal disadvantage is that the **acceleration becomes infinite at the start and end of the stroke** (a step discontinuity in acceleration), which causes severe vibration and noise at high speeds. This is the reason engineers often prefer the **Modified UAR** (which inserts a constant-velocity dwell in the middle) or the **Cycloidal Motion** (which smooths out the acceleration). --- ### 1.2 Cycloidal Motion **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Cycloidal motion is a follower displacement law in which the displacement of the follower traces the path of a point on the circumference of a circle (a cycloid) as the circle rolls without slipping along the base circle of the cam. Mathematically, the displacement `y` of the follower is expressed as a trigonometric combination of sine and linear terms, ensuring **zero velocity AND zero acceleration at both the start and end of the stroke**. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Picture a small coin rolling along the inside of a large circular plate. If you mark a point on the edge of that coin and trace its path, you get a beautiful, smooth, wave-like curve called a **cycloid**. A cycloidal cam motion is exactly this — the follower moves as if it were glued to the edge of a rolling wheel. The result is butter-smooth starting and stopping, with no jerks, no vibrations, and no sudden shocks. This is why **high-speed printing presses, textile machines, and internal combustion engine valve trains** frequently use cycloidal cams. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Note:** Because the cycloidal motion is **doubly-damped** (both velocity and acceleration are zero at the boundaries), it is classified as an excellent high-speed cam profile. The trade-off is that it requires **slightly more complex machining** of the cam profile compared to simple parabolic motion. --- ### 1.3 Introduction to the Design Process **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** The Design Process is a systematic, iterative sequence of engineering activities — starting from the recognition of a need and progressing through problem definition, conceptual synthesis, analytical and numerical evaluation, optimization, and final production documentation — that converts an abstract idea into a manufacturable, reliable, and economically viable engineering product or system. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of designing a new smartphone. The engineers do not simply pick up tools and start building. Instead, they: (1) ask *what problem are we solving?*, (2) brainstorm *what the phone should look like*, (3) draw rough sketches, (4) use computer simulations to test strength and battery life, (5) build prototypes, and (6) iterate until perfect. The **Design Process** is this exact sequence of steps — a *recipe* for turning ideas into machines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - **Adaptive Design** → Minor changes to an existing product. > - **Variant Design** → Modifying size/dimensions for new capacity. > - **New (Original) Design** → Creating a fundamentally new product from first principles. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Kinematics of Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR) The UAR profile divides the rise (or return) stroke into two equal time intervals of duration `t₁` each. Let `h` be the total follower lift (stroke) and `T = 2t₁` be the total rise time. **Phase 1 — Uniform Acceleration (0 ≤ t ≤ t₁):** - The follower accelerates at a constant rate `A` from rest. - Velocity grows linearly: `V(t) = A·t` - Displacement grows parabolically: `s(t) = ½·A·t²` **Phase 2 — Uniform Retardation (t₁ ≤ t ≤ T):** - The follower decelerates at a constant rate `-A` from maximum velocity `Vm`. - Velocity falls linearly: `V(t) = Vm − A·(t − t₁)` - Displacement grows parabolically: `s(t) = h − ½·A·(T − t)²` **Boundary Conditions Applied:** - At `t = t₁` → `s = h/2` (mid-stroke) and `V = Vm` (peak velocity). - At `t = T` → `s = h` and `V = 0`. --- ### 2.2 Kinematics of Cycloidal Motion The cycloidal displacement law is defined as: $$y(t) = h\left[\frac{t}{T} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\right]$$ Differentiating once gives velocity; differentiating twice gives acceleration. The function is bounded by `0` at `t = 0` and `h` at `t = T`, and it has **zero first AND second derivatives at both endpoints** — making it infinitely smooth at the boundaries. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Uniform Accel. & Retardation (UAR)** | **Cycloidal Motion** | |---|---|---| | Displacement `s(t)` | `½·A·t²` for accel phase; `h − ½·A·(T−t)²` for retard phase | `h[t/T − (1/2π)·sin(2πt/T)]` | | Velocity `V(t)` | `A·t` for accel phase; `Vm − A·(t − t₁)` for retard phase | `(h/T)·[1 − cos(2πt/T)]` | | Acceleration `A(t)` | Constant magnitude `±A` (step jumps at boundaries) | `(2πh/T²)·sin(2πt/T)` (smooth sinusoid) | | Maximum Velocity `Vm` | `2h/T` | `2h/T` | | Maximum Acceleration `Am` | `4h/T²` | `2πh/T²` ≈ `6.283·h/T²` | | Maximum Jerk `Jm` | **Infinite** (theoretical) at `t = 0, t₁, T` | `(4π²h/T³)·cos(2πt/T)`; `Jm = 4π²h/T³` ≈ `39.48·h/T³` | | Velocity at start/end | Zero, but **gradient is discontinuous** | Zero, with **zero gradient** | | Acceleration at start/end | **Infinite (step jump)** | **Zero (smooth)** | | Stroke time `T` (typical) | `2t₁` where `t₁ = h/Vm` | `T` (single parameter) | | Best suited for | Low–medium speed followers | High-speed followers | | Complexity of manufacture | Simple | Moderate (requires sinusoidal flank) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Insight:** Although both UAR and Cycloidal motion have the same `Vm = 2h/T`, the Cycloidal motion has only **`2πh/T² ≈ 1.57 × (4h/T²)` = 1.57 times the UAR maximum acceleration**. However, Cycloidal motion has a **finite, bounded maximum jerk**, which is the true secret to its vibration-free behavior at high speeds. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **UAR Profile** → Used in automatic lathe mechanisms, low-speed actuator systems, slow-opening valves, and governor linkages where simplicity of cam manufacture outweighs smoothness requirements. - **Cycloidal Profile** → Used in high-speed printing machinery, IC engine valve camshafts, textile looms, packaging machines, and high-precision robotic actuators. - **Design Process** → The backbone of any mechanical, civil, electrical, or computer-aided design project, from designing a single gear to launching a satellite. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of UAR Profile Equations **Given:** A follower lifts through a total stroke `h` in total time `T`. The motion consists of uniform acceleration for time `t₁` and uniform retardation for the remaining time `(T − t₁)`. **Step 1 — Apply boundary condition at end of acceleration phase.** At `t = t₁`, the follower has travelled exactly half the stroke (by symmetry of constant `±A`). $$\begin{aligned} s(t_1) &= \frac{1}{2} A\, t_1^{\,2} = \frac{h}{2} \\ \Rightarrow A &= \frac{h}{t_1^{\,2}} \end{aligned}$$ This row states that the **constant acceleration magnitude `A` equals the stroke divided by the square of the half-rise time**, derived by substituting `s = h/2` and `t = t₁` into the kinematic equation `s = ½At²`. **Step 2 — Express the maximum velocity.** Maximum velocity occurs at the end of the acceleration phase. $$V_m = A\, t_1 = \frac{h}{t_1^{\,2}} \cdot t_1 = \frac{h}{t_1}$$ This row uses the relation `V = A·t` evaluated at `t = t₁`, and substitutes the value of `A` from Step 1 to obtain the peak velocity. **Step 3 — Relate maximum velocity to total time `T`.** For the basic UAR profile (no constant-velocity dwell in the middle), `T = 2t₁`, hence `t₁ = T/2`. $$V_m = \frac{h}{T/2} = \frac{2h}{T}$$ This row shows that the maximum velocity of UAR is **twice the average velocity** — a characteristic shared with simple harmonic motion (SHM). **Step 4 — Derive maximum acceleration.** $$A_m = \frac{h}{t_1^{\,2}} = \frac{h}{(T/2)^2} = \frac{4h}{T^2}$$ This row squares the half-rise time and substitutes it back into the expression for `A` from Step 1 to give the **maximum acceleration magnitude** of the UAR follower. **Step 5 — Write the complete displacement-time equation.** For the **acceleration phase** (`0 ≤ t ≤ t₁ = T/2`): $$s(t) = \frac{1}{2} A t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{4h}{T^2} \cdot t^2 = \frac{2h\,t^2}{T^2}$$ For the **retardation phase** (`T/2 ≤ t ≤ T`): $$s(t) = h - \frac{1}{2} A (T-t)^2 = h - \frac{2h\,(T-t)^2}{T^2}$$ These two rows piece together the parabolic displacement curve — the first parabola is convex up to `T/2`, the second parabola is convex down from `T/2` to `T`, meeting at the mid-stroke peak with equal tangents (continuous velocity) but with **opposite curvature** (discontinuous acceleration). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Cycloidal Motion Equations **Step 1 — Define the displacement law.** The cycloidal cam motion is constructed by rolling a circle of radius `R = T/(2π)` along a straight line such that a point on its circumference traces the follower lift. $$y(t) = h\left[\frac{t}{T} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\right]$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate once to obtain velocity.** $$\begin{aligned} V(t) &= \frac{dy}{dt} = h\left[\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\cdot\frac{2\pi}{T}\right] \\[4pt] V(t) &= \frac{h}{T}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\right] \end{aligned}$$ This row applies the chain rule — the derivative of `sin(2πt/T)` is `(2π/T)·cos(2πt/T)` — and simplifies the `2π` factors to give the compact velocity expression. **Step 3 — Differentiate again to obtain acceleration.** $$\begin{aligned} A(t) &= \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{h}{T}\left[\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\cdot\frac{2\pi}{T}\right] \\[4pt] A(t) &= \frac{2\pi h}{T^2}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right) \end{aligned}$$ This row differentiates `cos(2πt/T)` to obtain `-(2π/T)·sin(2πt/T)`, and the leading negative sign cancels with the existing negative sign in the bracket. **Step 4 — Identify the maximum velocity.** Maximum velocity occurs when `cos(2πt/T) = −1`, i.e., when `2πt/T = π`, which gives `t = T/2`. $$V_m = \frac{h}{T}[1 - (-1)] = \frac{2h}{T}$$ This row substitutes `t = T/2` into the velocity equation. The maximum velocity is **2h/T**, identical to UAR. **Step 5 — Identify the maximum acceleration.** Maximum acceleration occurs when `sin(2πt/T) = 1`, i.e., when `2πt/T = π/2`, which gives `t = T/4`. $$A_m = \frac{2\pi h}{T^2}$$ This row substitutes `t = T/4` (and equivalently `t = 3T/4` for maximum negative acceleration) into the acceleration equation. **Step 6 — Verify smoothness at boundaries.** - At `t = 0`: `V(0) = (h/T)(1 − 1) = 0` ✓ - At `t = 0`: `A(0) = (2πh/T²)·sin(0) = 0` ✓ - At `t = T`: `V(T) = (h/T)(1 − cos 2π) = 0` ✓ - At `t = T`: `A(T) = (2πh/T²)·sin 2π = 0` ✓ This row confirms that the cycloidal motion is **doubly-damped** — both velocity and acceleration are zero at start and end of stroke, ensuring **no impact loading** on the follower. --- ### 3.3 Comparative Numerical Example (Worked Out) **Problem:** A cam follower lifts `h = 40 mm` in `T = 1 second` during the rise stroke. Compute `Vm`, `Am`, and `Jm` for (a) UAR and (b) Cycloidal motion. **For UAR:** $$V_m = \frac{2h}{T} = \frac{2 \times 40 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 0.080\ \text{m/s} = 80\ \text{mm/s}$$ $$A_m = \frac{4h}{T^2} = \frac{4 \times 40 \times 10^{-3}}{1^2} = 0.160\ \text{m/s}^2 = 160\ \text{mm/s}^2$$ **For Cycloidal:** $$V_m = \frac{2h}{T} = 80\ \text{mm/s}\ (\text{same as UAR})$$ $$A_m = \frac{2\pi h}{T^2} = \frac{2\pi \times 40 \times 10^{-3}}{1^2} \approx 0.2513\ \text{m/s}^2 = 251.33\ \text{mm/s}^2$$ **Observations:** The cycloidal cam has a **57% higher peak acceleration** than UAR, but its **bounded jerk** (≈ 3948 mm/s³) is what makes it vibration-free at high speeds. In UAR, jerk is theoretically infinite at the boundaries, which is the source of all the noise and wear. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cam-Follower Motion Profile Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph UAR[UAR PARABOLIC PROFILE] U1[Phase 1: Uniform Acceleration
0 to T/2
V rises linearly from 0 to Vm] U2[Phase 2: Uniform Retardation
T/2 to T
V falls linearly from Vm to 0] end subgraph CYC[CYCLOIDAL PROFILE] C1[Phase 1: Sinusoidal Acceleration
0 to T/4
A rises from 0 to Am] C2[Phase 2: Sinusoidal Deceleration
T/4 to T/2
A falls from Am to 0] C3[Phase 3: Sinusoidal Retardation
T/2 to 3T/4
A rises from 0 to -Am] C4[Phase 4: Sinusoidal Recovery
3T/4 to T
A rises from -Am to 0] end U1 ==> U2 C1 ==> C2 ==> C3 ==> C4 ``` ### 4.2 Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration Curves — Cycloidal Motion ```mermaid flowchart TB start([Stroke Time Axis t]) --> s_curve s_curve[Displacement s of t
Shape: Cycloid
Smooth, S-shaped] --> s_end s_end([Ends at s = h]) start --> v_curve v_curve[Velocity V of t
V = h per T times 1 minus cos 2 pi t per T
Smooth, bell-shaped
Peaks at t = T/2] --> v_end v_end([Ends at V = 0]) start --> a_curve a_curve[Acceleration A of t
A = 2 pi h per T squared times sin 2 pi t per T
Pure sinusoid
Zero at start and end] --> a_end a_end([Ends at A = 0]) ``` ### 4.3 The Engineering Design Process Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Recognition of Need
Identify a problem or opportunity] --> B B[Step 2: Definition of Problem
State specifications, constraints, and requirements] --> C C[Step 3: Conceptual Design and Synthesis
Generate alternative design ideas] --> D D[Step 4: Analysis and Mathematical Modeling
Apply kinematics, dynamics, and strength of materials] --> E E[Step 5: Evaluation and Decision Making
Compare alternatives using cost, performance, and safety] --> F F{Step 6: Design Acceptable?} -->|No| G G[Step 7: Iteration and Optimization
Revise design and re-analyze] --> D F -->|Yes| H H[Step 8: Detailed Design and Production Drawings
Create manufacturing specifications] --> I I[Step 9: Prototype Fabrication and Testing
Build and validate] --> J J{Step 10: Test Results Satisfactory?} -->|No| G J -->|Yes| K K[Step 11: Final Production and Marketing
Manufacture and launch product] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of Cam Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT[Inputs
Stroke h
Time T
Motion Type] --> PROC PROC[Analytical Engine
Equations of UAR or Cycloid
Differentiate s of t to get V and A] --> OUTPUT OUTPUT[Outputs
Vm, Am, Jm
Boundary values of V and A
Suitability for high speed] --> VALID VALID[Validation
Compare Vm and Am with KTU standards
Check smoothness at boundaries] --> REC REC[Recommendation
UAR for low speed
Cycloid for high speed] --> DESIGN DESIGN[Next Step
Cam Profile Synthesis
Cutter Radius Correction] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR) motion of a cam follower. State its main drawback.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR) motion is a follower displacement law in which the follower is subjected to a **constant positive acceleration** during the first half of the rise (or return) stroke and an **equal constant retardation** during the second half, producing a parabolic displacement curve. **Main Drawback (1 mark):** The acceleration becomes theoretically **infinite (a step discontinuity) at the start and end of the stroke**, causing severe vibration, noise, and impact loading — making it unsuitable for high-speed applications. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State two advantages of cycloidal motion over UAR motion.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Zero acceleration at the start and end of the stroke** — ensures smooth, vibration-free starting and stopping of the follower. 2. **Bounded (finite) maximum jerk** — prevents the impulsive loading and noise that plague UAR cams at high speeds. 3. (Bonus) The motion is **doubly-damped** — both velocity and acceleration vanish at the boundaries. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) Derive the expressions for maximum velocity and maximum acceleration of a cam follower executing cycloidal motion during the rise stroke. Assume a total lift of `h` and a rise time of `T`. (7 marks)** **(b) A cam is to give the following motion to a follower: rise of `40 mm` in `90°` of cam rotation, dwell for `30°`, return of `40 mm` in `120°` of cam rotation, and dwell for the remaining `120°`. The cam rotates at `240 rpm`. Determine the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration during the rise for:** **(i) Uniform Acceleration and Retardation motion,** **(ii) Cycloidal motion. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** The cycloidal displacement law is: $$y(t) = h\left[\frac{t}{T} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\right]$$ Differentiating once: $$V(t) = \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{h}{T}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)\right]$$ Differentiating twice: $$A(t) = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{2\pi h}{T^2}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\right)$$ **Maximum Velocity** occurs when `cos(2πt/T) = −1`, i.e., at `t = T/2`: $$V_m = \frac{h}{T}[1 - (-1)] = \frac{2h}{T}$$ **Maximum Acceleration** occurs when `sin(2πt/T) = 1`, i.e., at `t = T/4`: $$A_m = \frac{2\pi h}{T^2}$$ **Note (1 mark):** Both `Vm` and `Am` are attained **at well-defined instants** and are **finite**, unlike UAR where acceleration has step discontinuities. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given data:** - Stroke `h = 40 mm = 0.040 m` - Rise angle `θ_r = 90°` - Cam speed `N = 240 rpm` → angular velocity `ω = 2πN/60 = 2π·240/60 = 8π rad/s ≈ 25.133 rad/s` **Compute rise time `T`:** $$T = \frac{\theta_r}{\omega} = \frac{90° \cdot \pi/180°}{8\pi} = \frac{\pi/2}{8\pi} = \frac{1}{16}\ \text{s} = 0.0625\ \text{s}$$ **Case (i) — UAR Motion:** $$V_m = \frac{2h}{T} = \frac{2 \times 0.040}{0.0625} = 1.28\ \text{m/s}$$ $$A_m = \frac{4h}{T^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.040}{(0.0625)^2} = \frac{0.160}{0.003906} = 40.96\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **Case (ii) — Cycloidal Motion:** $$V_m = \frac{2h}{T} = \frac{2 \times 0.040}{0.0625} = 1.28\ \text{m/s}$$ $$A_m = \frac{2\pi h}{T^2} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.040}{(0.0625)^2} = \frac{0.0800\pi}{0.003906} = 64.34\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **Observation (1 mark):** The cycloidal cam has a higher peak acceleration (by a factor of π/2 ≈ 1.57), but it is smooth and bounded — making it far superior to UAR for high-speed machinery. --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) A cam follower is to rise `h = 50 mm` in `T = 0.5 s` using UAR motion. Determine: (i) the maximum velocity, (ii) the maximum acceleration, (iii) the displacement, velocity, and acceleration at `t = 0.1 s` and `t = 0.3 s`. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the major phases of the engineering design process in detail. Distinguish between adaptive, variant, and new design. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) UAR Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** `h = 50 mm = 0.050 m`, `T = 0.5 s`. For pure UAR, `t₁ = T/2 = 0.25 s`. **Compute constant acceleration `A`:** $$A = \frac{h}{t_1^{\,2}} = \frac{0.050}{(0.25)^2} = \frac{0.050}{0.0625} = 0.80\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **Maximum velocity:** $$V_m = A \cdot t_1 = 0.80 \times 0.25 = 0.20\ \text{m/s} = 200\ \text{mm/s}$$ **Maximum acceleration:** `Am = 0.80 m/s²` (constant in magnitude during each phase). **Evaluation at `t = 0.1 s` (acceleration phase, since `t < t₁ = 0.25 s`):** $$s = \frac{1}{2} A t^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.80 \times (0.1)^2 = 0.004\ \text{m} = 4\ \text{mm}$$ $$V = A t = 0.80 \times 0.1 = 0.08\ \text{m/s} = 80\ \text{mm/s}$$ $$A_{\text{inst}} = +0.80\ \text{m/s}^2$$ **Evaluation at `t = 0.3 s` (retardation phase, since `t > t₁ = 0.25 s`):** $$s = h - \frac{1}{2} A (T - t)^2 = 0.050 - \frac{1}{2} \times 0.80 \times (0.5 - 0.3)^2$$ $$s = 0.050 - 0.5 \times 0.80 \times 0.04 = 0.050 - 0.016 = 0.034\ \text{m} = 34\ \text{mm}$$ $$V = A \cdot t_1 - A \cdot (t - t_1) = A(2t_1 - t) = 0.80 \times (0.5 - 0.3) = 0.16\ \text{m/s} = 160\ \text{mm/s}$$ $$A_{\text{inst}} = -0.80\ \text{m/s}^2\ (\text{retardation})$$ --- **(b) Engineering Design Process (7 marks):** The engineering design process consists of the following iterative phases: 1. **Recognition of Need (1 mark):** Identifying a problem that requires a mechanical/engineering solution. Example: The need for fuel-efficient vehicles. 2. **Definition of Problem (1 mark):** Stating specifications (loads, speeds, life, environment), constraints, and customer requirements quantitatively. 3. **Synthesis and Conceptual Design (1 mark):** Generating multiple alternative concepts, often using brainstorming, TRIZ, or morphological analysis. 4. **Analysis and Modeling (1 mark):** Using kinematics, dynamics, strength of materials, FEA, and simulations to evaluate each concept. 5. **Evaluation and Optimization (1 mark):** Comparing alternatives on cost, weight, performance, and reliability; iterating to optimize. 6. **Detailed Design and Production (1 mark):** Preparing detailed engineering drawings, BOM, GD&T, and manufacturing process plans. 7. **Testing and Iteration (1 mark):** Building prototypes, conducting tests, identifying failures, and looping back to the analysis phase. **Distinction Between Design Types (bonus marks):** | **Type** | **Description** | **Example** | |---|---|---| | **Adaptive Design** | Minor modifications to an existing product to suit a slightly different need. | Adding a spoiler to an existing car model. | | **Variant Design** | Changing dimensions/parameters of an existing product to obtain a new capacity. | Scaling up a 5 HP motor to 10 HP using the same design principles. | | **New (Original) Design** | Creating a fundamentally new product from first principles, often involving new technology. | Designing a fully reusable rocket — no precedent exists. | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit mistakes (2 marks lost commonly):** Convert all dimensions to **meters** and all angles to **radians** before substituting into formulas. Mixing `mm` and `m` is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers in cam problems. > 2. **Forgetting `2π` in cycloidal jerk:** Maximum jerk is `4π²h/T³` (NOT `2πh/T³`). Many students write the wrong coefficient. > 3. **Confusing UAR with Modified UAR:** Modified UAR has a constant-velocity dwell of `t₂` in the middle. Pure UAR does NOT. Read the question carefully! > 4. **Direction of acceleration:** In the rise stroke, retardation is still positive (just decelerating). In the return stroke, retardation is negative. Sign conventions matter in KTU valuation. > 5. **Design Process is a description question:** Examiners expect **bullet-pointed phases with examples**, not long paragraphs. Use headings to grab easy marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **UAR (Parabolic) Motion:** Displacement is parabolic; `Vm = 2h/T`; `Am = 4h/T²`; **infinite jerk at boundaries**. - **Cycloidal Motion:** Displacement is sinusoidal-mixed; `Vm = 2h/T`; `Am = 2πh/T²`; **zero jerk at boundaries (bounded jerk = `4π²h/T³`)**. - **Pure UAR has NO constant-velocity dwell** — it consists of only two phases: acceleration and retardation. **Modified UAR** inserts a dwell. - **Cycloidal motion is doubly-damped:** `V(0) = V(T) = 0` AND `A(0) = A(T) = 0`. - **UAR is preferred for simplicity and low cost**; **Cycloidal is preferred for high-speed, low-vibration applications**. - **Design Process is iterative** — never linear. Always loop back from testing to analysis. - **Three design types:** Adaptive (minor), Variant (scaling), New (first principles). - **Always convert `mm → m` and `rpm → rad/s`** before plugging into any kinematic formula. - **Maximum velocity occurs at mid-stroke** for BOTH UAR and Cycloidal motion. - **Maximum acceleration occurs at `T/4` and `3T/4`** for Cycloidal motion, but occurs **at every instant in each phase** (step function) for UAR. - **Cam angle to time conversion:** `T = θ / ω` where `ω = 2πN/60`. - **Boundary value check:** Always verify `V(0) = 0`, `V(T) = 0`, and `s(0) = 0`, `s(T) = h` to catch algebraic errors early. - **Kinematic order:** `s → V → A → J` (displacement, velocity, acceleration, jerk). Each is the derivative of the previous. - **Jerk matters at high speeds** because it controls the rate of change of inertia force, which is what causes vibration and noise. - **Design specifications** must include: functional requirements, constraints, cost targets, life expectancy, and safety factors.

topics.title: Factors Influencing Machine Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Machine Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Factors influencing machine design* are the comprehensive set of **functional, structural, environmental, economic, and material constraints** that a design engineer must systematically evaluate and incorporate during the conceptualization, calculation, and detailing phase of any machine element or mechanical assembly to ensure the manufactured product performs its intended function **safely, reliably, economically, and durably** throughout its specified service life.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic directly maps to:

- **CO3:** *Apply principles of design to machine elements subjected to various loading and operating conditions.*
- **PO1 (Engineering Knowledge):** Demonstrates understanding of multidisciplinary design constraints.
- **PO3 (Design/Development of Solutions):** Develops safe, sustainable, and economically viable design solutions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Building" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of designing a machine like building a house.**

When an architect plans a house, they do not just calculate the area of rooms. They consider:

| House Building Factor | Machine Design Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Number of people living (load) | Magnitude & type of load |
| Coastal humidity (corrosion) | Environmental conditions |
| Family budget (cost) | Economic constraints |
| Earthquake zone (dynamic forces) | Nature of stress / shock loads |
| 50-year lifespan expectation | Fatigue life / durability |
| Material availability in local market | Manufacturability & material sourcing |
| Aesthetic appeal | Ergonomics & aesthetics |

Similarly, a machine designer cannot work in isolation with just *strength of materials* equations. The designer must consider a **holistic web of interdependent factors** that determine whether a machine is fit for purpose.

---

## 1.3 High-Yield Classification of Design Factors (Syllabus Snapshot)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these factors into the following major heads. Memorize this taxonomy — examiners frequently ask **"List and briefly explain the various factors influencing machine design."**

1. **Functional & Load-Related Factors** — Type of load, magnitude, stress nature.
2. **Material-Related Factors** — Strength, stiffness, ductility, fatigue strength, cost.
3. **Manufacturing & Process Factors** — Castability, machinability, weldability, availability.
4. **Operational & Environmental Factors** — Temperature, corrosion, wear, lubrication, noise.
5. **Economic Factors** — Material cost, machining cost, assembly cost, life-cycle cost.
6. **Safety, Reliability & Human Factors** — Factor of safety, service life, ergonomics.
7. **Aesthetic & Standardization Factors** — Appearance, codes, IS/ISO standards.

---

## 1.4 Explicit KTU Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following quantitative metrics form the engineering vocabulary of this topic. They are flagged in **bold** as they appear verbatim in KTU answer keys:

- **Factor of Safety (FoS)** — `FOS = Failure Load / Working Load`
- **Service Factor (Ks)** — Empirical multiplier accounting for dynamic loading (typically **1.0 to 1.75** for general machinery).
- **Design Stress (σd)** — The maximum allowable stress a designer permits, equal to `σd = σultimate / FoS`.
- **Endurance Limit (σe)** — For steels, `σe ≈ 0.5 × σu` (Rotating beam test) for `σu < 1400 MPa`.
- **Standard Reference Temperature** — **20 °C** (mechanical property baseline in IS/ISO standards).
- **Standard Gravitational Acceleration** — **9.81 m/s²** for converting masses to weights in load calculations.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Exhaustive Factor-Wise Breakdown (Syllabus-Aligned)

### **2.1.1 Type of Load**

The first design decision is identifying **how the load will be applied** during service. This governs the choice of theory of failure (Rankine, Guest, Von Mises, etc.) and the formula set used.

- **Static Load:** Applied gradually, magnitude constant. Example: Weight of a storage tank. → Use **static stress analysis**.
- **Dynamic Load:** Load varies with time. Includes:
  - *Fluctuating/Variable Load:* Cyclic (e.g., connecting rod of IC engine).
  - *Shock/Impact Load:* Sudden application (e.g., punch press, hammer blow).
  - *Repeated/Reversed Load:* Stress alternates between tension and compression (e.g., leaf spring, axle).
- **Consequence:** Dynamic loads invoke **fatigue analysis** (Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, ASME-elliptic criteria).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A 10 kN *static* load and a 10 kN *impact* load are NOT equivalent for design. Impact loads can produce stresses **5×–10× higher** than equivalent static loads.

---

### **2.1.2 Magnitude of Load**

- Must be quantified from power rating, torque, pressure, or reaction analysis.
- Designers typically work with **maximum expected load** plus a safety multiplier, NOT the rated load.
- For KTU numericals: $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ converts torque $T$ (N·m) and speed $N$ (rpm) into tangential power-transmitting load $P$.

---

### **2.1.3 Nature of Stress**

Stress states that occur in machine members:

- **Tensile** — Pulling apart (tie rods, chains).
- **Compressive** — Pushing together (columns, piston).
- **Shear** — Tangential (rivets, keys, bolts in single shear).
- **Bending** — Transverse loading (shafts, beams).
- **Torsional** — Twisting (drive shafts, drill bits).
- **Combined / Compound Stress** — Multiple stresses acting simultaneously (most real machine elements).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** When asked *"Why is combined stress analysis essential in machine design?"*, the examiner expects: *"Because the majority of machine elements (shafts, cranks, springs) experience simultaneous bending, torsion, axial and shear stresses. Failure theories such as Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's) or Distortion Energy Theory (Von Mises) are then applied to obtain equivalent stress for safe design."*

---

### **2.1.4 Material Selection Factors**

A material's suitability is judged by the following **mechanical properties**:

| Property | Symbol | Practical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ | Resistance to fracture under tension |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | Onset of permanent deformation |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | Stiffness (resistance to elastic deflection) |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | Stiffness under torsion |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | Lateral strain behaviour |
| Ductility | % elongation | Formability, warning before fracture |
| Hardness | HB / HV / HRC | Resistance to surface indentation/wear |
| Impact Strength | $I$ | Energy absorbed before fracture (Izod/Charpy) |
| Fatigue Strength | $\sigma_e$ | Endurance under cyclic loading |
| Creep Resistance | — | Behaviour at elevated temperature |
| Corrosion Resistance | — | Service life in aggressive media |

> **Engineering Utility:** Aerospace components prioritise **strength-to-weight ratio**; chemical pumps prioritise **corrosion resistance**; cutting tools prioritise **hardness and red hardness**.

---

### **2.1.5 Manufacturing Considerations**

Even a theoretically perfect design fails if it cannot be manufactured. KTU tests this through design-for-manufacture questions:

- **Castability** — For cast iron, cast steel; fluidity, shrinkage allowance.
- **Machinability** — Free-cutting steels (e.g., AISI 1118) for high-volume parts.
- **Weldability** — Low-carbon steels preferred; high-carbon steels crack.
- **Forgeability** — Hot working range.
- **Heat-Treatability** — Hardenability (Jominy distance) for shafts, gears.
- **Availability** — Standard sections (rolled, drawn) preferred over custom profiles.
- **Standardization** — Use of standard sizes reduces cost via economy of scale.

---

### **2.1.6 Operational & Environmental Factors**

- **Temperature Effects:**
  - Above ~300 °C, steels experience **creep** → creep strength becomes governing.
  - Below ~−20 °C, many steels become **brittle** → notch toughness matters.
  - Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$ governs thermal stresses: $\sigma_t = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$.

- **Corrosion & Wear:**
  - Reduces effective cross-section over time.
  - Mitigation: coatings, alloying, lubrication, surface treatment.

- **Lubrication:** Reduces wear and heat; dictates surface finish requirements.

- **Vibration & Noise:** Dynamic instability can cause fatigue failure; must be checked using critical speed / natural frequency analysis.

---

### **2.1.7 Economic & Commercial Factors**

> [!NOTE]
> *"The best design is not the one that is strongest, but the one that gives the required performance at minimum overall cost."* — **Classic KTU Board Quote.**

Cost components:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{manufacturing}} + C_{\text{assembly}} + C_{\text{operation}} + C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{scrap}}
$$

A cheaper material that requires frequent replacement may be **more expensive** over the life cycle than a costlier durable material.

---

### **2.1.8 Safety, Reliability & Human Factors**

- **Factor of Safety (FoS):** Higher for:
  - Human-carrying applications (lifts, cranes).
  - Brittle materials (cast iron, ceramics).
  - Uncertain loading conditions.
  - Critical applications (aerospace, nuclear).

- **Reliability:** Probability of performing intended function over a specified period.

- **Ergonomics & Safety Standards:** Must comply with **IS 2064**, **IS 12343**, OSHA norms, etc.

---

### **2.1.9 Aesthetics, Weight & Size**

- Aesthetic appeal influences consumer products but not industrial machinery.
- Weight reduction is critical in **automotive, aerospace, portable equipment** (mobility, fuel efficiency).
- Size constraints arise from packaging, installation space, and portability.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{failure}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$ | Factor of Safety | Always stated in design problem preamble |
| 2 | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\text{FoS}}$ | Design stress | Sets allowable stress limit |
| 3 | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$ | Endurance limit (for steels, $\sigma_u < 1400$ MPa) | Shaft and spring design |
| 4 | $\sigma_{te} = 0.5 \, \sigma_e$ | Endurance limit in tension for brittle materials | Cast iron shafts |
| 5 | $\tau_e \approx 0.3 \, \sigma_u$ | Endurance limit in shear | Keys, pins, rivets |
| 6 | $\sigma_{e, \text{combined}} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3} \left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right]$ | Equivalent bending stress (Max Shear Stress Theory) | Shafts under combined bending + torsion |
| 7 | $\sigma_{e, \text{VM}} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$ | Equivalent stress (Von Mises) | Ductile shaft design |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Power in terms of torque | Load derivation |
| 9 | $F_t = \dfrac{P}{v} = \dfrac{2T}{d}$ | Tangential tooth force | Gears, pulleys |
| 10 | $\sigma_t = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained member) | Shrink-fit, hot assembly |

> **Real-World Utility:** A mechanical engineer designing a turbine blade uses rows 1, 3, 5, 9, 10 together; a machine-tool designer focuses on rows 1, 2, 6; a chemical-pump designer emphasizes corrosion factor alongside rows 1 and 2.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Design Stress Derivation Chain (Mandatory KTU Working)

This is the **fundamental logical chain** that every KTU numerical answer on machine design must begin with. We will derive it step by step.

---

### **3.1.1 From Ultimate Strength to Design Stress**

A material's laboratory-measured Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$) is the stress at which the specimen fractures under a **static, uniaxial, ideal** tensile test. A real machine element never operates at this stress for three reasons:

1. The actual service load is uncertain (impact, fatigue, misalignment).
2. The material has internal flaws, inclusions, and residual stresses.
3. The consequences of failure may be catastrophic (human, economic).

Hence, we define a **Factor of Safety** as the *ratio of the maximum stress a material can withstand to the maximum stress it is permitted to experience* in service.

$$
\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate (or yield)}}}{\sigma_{\text{allowable}}}
$$

Rearranging gives the allowable / design stress:

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_u}{\text{FoS}} \quad \text{(for brittle materials)}
$$

$$
\sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}} \quad \text{(for ductile materials)}
$$

> **Why the two forms?** Ductile materials warn of failure (they yield first); brittle materials do not (they fracture suddenly). The conservative design philosophy is to design against *yield* for ductile and *ultimate* for brittle.

---

### **3.1.2 The Generalised Working Stress Equation**

For a machine element subjected to combined loading, the design must simultaneously satisfy **strength, stiffness, wear, temperature, and buckling** conditions. Mathematically:

$$
\sigma_{\text{working}} \le \min \left\{ \frac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}}, \frac{\sigma_e}{\text{FoS}_{\text{fatigue}}}, \frac{\sigma_{\text{creep}}}{\text{FoS}_{\text{creep}}}, \sigma_{\text{thermal}}, \sigma_{\text{buckling}} \right\}
$$

The **governing** minimum stress dictates the final design.

---

### **3.1.3 Working Out the Design Diameter — Worked Skeleton**

A solid circular shaft transmits torque $T$ and bending moment $M$. Using the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's Theory)**, the equivalent torque approach is standard in KTU solutions:

$$
T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}
$$

The shear stress in a solid shaft of diameter $d$ is:

$$
\tau = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, d^3}
$$

Setting $\tau$ equal to the design shear stress $\tau_d$:

$$
\tau_d = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, d^3}
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
d^3 = \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_d}
$$

$$
d = \left( \frac{16 \, T_e}{\pi \, \tau_d} \right)^{1/3}
$$

This is the **canonical diameter equation** appearing in ~70% of KTU shaft-design numericals.

---

### **3.1.4 Derivation of the Equivalent Bending Moment**

From the equation $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$, we can also express an equivalent *bending moment* $M_e$ by replacing $T$ with $2M$ (since $\tau_y = 0.5 \sigma_y$ for ductile materials per Tresca):

$$
M_e = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2}
$$

Therefore the **equivalent bending stress** becomes:

$$
\sigma_{e,b} = \frac{32 \, M_e}{\pi \, d^3} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right]
$$

This matches row 6 in the KTU Formula Sheet.

---

### **3.1.5 Incorporating the Fatigue Factor**

For fluctuating loads, the KTU board expects the use of the **Soderberg Line** as the safest design line:

$$
\frac{1}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}
$$

Where:

- $\sigma_a$ = stress amplitude (alternating component)
- $\sigma_m$ = mean stress
- $\sigma_e$ = endurance limit
- $\sigma_y$ = yield strength

The design variable (e.g., shaft diameter) is then solved from the largest of $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$, ensuring both terms are bounded by the Soderberg envelope.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Factor Governs?"

| Operating Scenario | Governing Factor | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Connecting rod of an IC engine (rotating, fluctuating) | **Fatigue (Endurance Limit)** | Cyclic stress → fatigue failure dominates |
| Crane hook lifting a heavy load | **Static Strength + FoS** | Predominantly static, human-safety critical |
| Furnace conveyor belt at 600 °C | **Creep Strength** | High temperature → time-dependent deformation |
| Cryogenic storage tank support | **Notch Toughness** | Low temperature → brittle fracture risk |
| Marine propeller shaft | **Corrosion Fatigue** | Salt water + cyclic stress |
| Press tool punch | **Impact / Shock Load** | Sudden energy release |
| Injection mould cavity | **Surface Finish + Hardness** | Wear-dominated, abrasive polymer flow |
| Lathe bed | **Stiffness (Young's Modulus)** | Deflection governs accuracy, not strength |
| Column supporting a roof | **Buckling** | Slenderness governs failure |
| Bolted joint under thermal cycling | **Thermal Stress + Loosening** | Differential expansion |

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a numerical gives you a problem, immediately identify which row of the matrix above applies. The factor-of-safety value, theory of failure, and formula all change based on this single decision.

---

## 3.3 A Quantitative Illustration — Effect of FoS on Diameter

Suppose a circular shaft must transmit a torque $T = 1000$ N·m, with no bending. Using $\tau_d = \sigma_y / \text{FoS}$, with $\sigma_y = 400$ MPa:

- **Case 1 (FoS = 2):**

$$
\tau_d = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ MPa} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{16 \times 1000}{\pi \times 200 \times 10^6} = 2.546 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
d = 0.0294 \text{ m} = 29.4 \text{ mm}
$$

- **Case 2 (FoS = 4):**

$$
\tau_d = \frac{400}{4} = 100 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{16 \times 1000}{\pi \times 100 \times 10^6} = 5.093 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
d = 0.0370 \text{ m} = 37.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Observation:** Doubling the FoS increases the diameter by only **~26%**, but the volume (and mass) increases by **~100%**. This is precisely why a designer must choose the **lowest justified FoS** — economic and weight optimization are themselves design factors.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define 'Factor of Safety'. Mention its typical values for static and dynamic loading. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Factor of Safety (FoS)** is the ratio of the material's failure stress (ultimate strength for brittle, yield strength for ductile) to the **maximum allowable working stress** in service.

$$
\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{failure}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}
$$

Typical KTU-standard values:

- **Static loading, ductile material, well-defined load:** **1.5 to 2.0**
- **Static loading, brittle material or uncertain load:** **3.0 to 4.0**
- **Dynamic / shock loading:** **4.0 to 8.0**
- **Human-safety-critical (cranes, lifts, pressure vessels):** **6.0 to 10.0**

Higher FoS implies safer but bulkier and costlier design. *([1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark typical values])*

---

### **Q2. List any six factors influencing machine design. Briefly explain 'manufacturability' as one such factor. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six factors are: **(i)** type of load, **(ii)** magnitude of load, **(iii)** material properties, **(iv)** operating conditions, **(v)** safety, **(vi)** cost. *(1.5 marks for listing)*

**Manufacturability** is the ease with which a chosen material can be shaped, joined, and finished using available manufacturing processes. A design that mandates an exotic alloy with poor machinability or requires complex castings when a simple forging would suffice is a **poorly manufactured** design. The designer must choose a material and geometry that align with the available foundry/machine shop capabilities. *(1.5 marks for explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks. The student answers **either** OR-style choice. Below we present the standard internal-choice pair (A or B), each with two sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Essay Type**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]**
>
> *Explain in detail the various factors influencing the design of a machine element. Discuss with neat sketches the classification of loads and the corresponding theories of failure used in design.*

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Classification and Explanation of Design Factors [7 marks]**

The design of a machine element is governed by the following inter-related factors:

1. **Type of Load** — static, dynamic (fluctuating, shock, impact). Determines whether static or fatigue analysis applies.
2. **Magnitude of Load** — derived from power, torque, or pressure. Always use the **maximum expected** load, not the average.
3. **Nature of Stress** — tensile, compressive, shear, bending, torsional, or combined. For combined stress, equivalent stress is computed.
4. **Material Selection** — judged by strength, stiffness, ductility, hardness, fatigue, corrosion, cost, and availability.
5. **Manufacturing Considerations** — castability, machinability, weldability, forgeability, heat-treatability. Standardized parts reduce cost.
6. **Operating Conditions** — temperature (creep, brittleness), humidity, dust, chemicals, vibration, lubrication.
7. **Safety & Reliability** — quantified via Factor of Safety; reliability via probability of survival.
8. **Economic Factors** — total cost = material + fabrication + operation + maintenance. Optimise life-cycle cost, not first cost.
9. **Aesthetic & Ergonomic Factors** — relevant for consumer products; must conform to IS 2064, IS 12343.
10. **Standards & Codes** — IS, ISO, BIS, AGMA, DIN — mandatory for legal and interchangeability reasons.

*Each sub-point carries ~0.7 marks. Sketches/textual flow chart earns remaining marks.*

**(b) Classification of Loads and Theories of Failure [7 marks]**

**Classification of Loads:**

```
Loads
├── Static Loads       (constant magnitude)
│     ├── Dead Loads    (self-weight)
│     └── Live Loads    (storage, fluid pressure)
└── Dynamic Loads      (varying with time)
      ├── Fluctuating (constant direction, varying magnitude)
      ├── Shock        (sudden, large magnitude)
      ├── Impact       (collision of two bodies)
      └── Fatigue / Reversed (alternating tension-compression)
```

**Theories of Failure (for ductile vs brittle materials):**

| Material | Recommended Theory | Equivalent Stress Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Ductile | Max Shear Stress Theory (Guest) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 4\tau^2}$ |
| Ductile | Distortion Energy (Von Mises) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ |
| Brittle | Max Normal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1$ (max principal) |
| Brittle | Max Principal Strain (St. Venant) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$ |

**Sketches expected by KTU valuator:**

1. **Stress-strain diagram** showing brittle vs ductile behaviour and the location of allowable stress.
2. **Block diagram** of the design loop: Requirement → Stress Analysis → Failure Theory → Design → Test → Production.

*End of solution. Award 7 marks per sub-part following the sub-division above.*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Numerical-Integrated Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**
>
> *(a) A solid circular shaft is required to transmit 25 kW at 240 rpm. The maximum permissible shear stress is 50 MPa. The shaft is also subjected to a bending moment of 1200 N·m due to a pulley mounted between bearings. Using Maximum Shear Stress Theory, determine the shaft diameter. Take FoS = 3.* (7 marks)
>
> *(b) Explain the influence of 'operating temperature' and 'fatigue loading' on the selection of factor of safety in machine design. Why is the Soderberg line preferred over the Goodman line for safe design?* (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Numerical: Shaft Diameter under Combined Loading [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Torque from power:**

$$
T = \frac{60 \times P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 25 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 240}
$$

$$
T = 994.72 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Bending moment (given):**

$$
M = 1200 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent torque (Max Shear Stress / Guest Theory):**

$$
T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(1200)^2 + (994.72)^2}
$$

$$
T_e = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^6 + 0.9895 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_e = \sqrt{2.4295 \times 10^6} = 1558.7 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Design shear stress:**

$$
\tau_d = \frac{\tau_{\text{permissible}}}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{3} = 16.667 \text{ MPa} = 16.667 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Diameter equation:**

$$
\tau_d = \frac{16 T_e}{\pi d^3}
$$

$$
d^3 = \frac{16 \times 1558.7}{\pi \times 16.667 \times 10^6} = 4.762 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
d = 0.0780 \text{ m} = 78.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Standard size selection:** Round up to the next preferred standard diameter, **80 mm** (as per IS 1862).

> **Final Answer:** $d = 78.0 \text{ mm}$ (calculated) → adopt **80 mm standard.**

*Step-wise marks: 1.5 (torque) + 1 (bending given) + 1.5 (equivalent) + 1 (FoS) + 1.5 (diameter) + 0.5 (standard)*

---

**(b) Conceptual: Temperature, Fatigue, and FoS Selection [7 marks]**

**Influence of Operating Temperature:**

At elevated temperatures (typically > 300 °C for steels), the material experiences **time-dependent plastic deformation (creep)** even at stresses below the yield. The allowable stress must be reduced by a **creep factor**, and the FoS increased. Conversely, at cryogenic temperatures, the material becomes brittle; **notch toughness (impact strength)** becomes the governing property.

**Influence of Fatigue Loading:**

Under cyclic loading, the material can fail at stresses well below the static yield strength. The **endurance limit** $\sigma_e$ becomes the relevant failure criterion, and the FoS is computed against $\sigma_e$ rather than $\sigma_y$. Surface finish, size, reliability, temperature, and stress-concentration factors (the *Marin Factors*) further reduce the laboratory endurance limit in the actual design via:

$$
\sigma_{e,\text{actual}} = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, \sigma_{e,\text{lab}}
$$

**Why Soderberg over Goodman?**

| Criterion | Soderberg Line | Goodman Line |
|---|---|---|
| Failure locus | Yield on tension, endurance on compression | Ultimate on tension, endurance on compression |
| Safety level | **Most conservative** (lowest FoS region) | Less conservative |
| Best for | Critical, human-safety, unpredictable loads | Steady, well-characterised service |

The **Soderberg line** is the straight line from $\sigma_y$ on the mean-stress axis to $\sigma_e$ on the alternating-stress axis, and lies **entirely inside the Goodman and Gerber curves**, guaranteeing that the material neither yields (mean stress criterion) nor fatigues (alternating stress criterion). Hence KTU standard practice recommends Soderberg for conservative design.

*Marks: 2 (temperature) + 2 (fatigue) + 3 (Soderberg vs Goodman).*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Cumulative 8–10 mark losses reported in KTU valuation reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing FoS for brittle and ductile materials** — using $\sigma_u$ instead of $\sigma_y$ (or vice-versa) when computing design stress. *Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.*
> 2. **Forgetting the FoS in numericals** — many students compute the diameter using the permissible stress directly, skipping the FoS division. *Penalty: −2 marks.*
> 3. **Wrong equivalent-stress formula** — using Von Mises formula where Guest's (Max Shear Stress) is specified, or vice-versa. *Penalty: −1.5 marks.*
> 4. **Skipping unit consistency** — mixing mm and m, or MPa with N/m². *Penalty: −1 mark.*
> 5. **Not rounding to standard sizes** — KTU marks explicitly require selecting a **standard diameter** (e.g., 80 mm, not 78.0 mm). *Penalty: −0.5 mark.*
> 6. **Omitting the standard name in the theory question** — listing factors without naming "IS 2064" or "BIS" loses the **standards-and-codes** sub-mark.
> 7. **Writing only "dynamic load"** in the load classification — the examiner expects the four sub-types (fluctuating, shock, impact, reversed) for full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Factors influencing machine design = functional, material, manufacturing, environmental, economic, safety, and aesthetic constraints.
- [x] **Load types:** static, fluctuating, shock, impact, reversed — each with a distinct design treatment.
- [x] **Design stress:** $\sigma_d = \sigma_y / \text{FS}$ (ductile) or $\sigma_u / \text{FS}$ (brittle).
- [x] **Endurance limit:** $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$ (for steels below 1400 MPa).
- [x] **Equivalent shaft equations (canonical):**
   - $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ (Guest)
   - $\sigma_{e,b} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3} \left[ M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \right]$
- [x] **FoS hierarchy:** Static < Dynamic < Impact < Human-safety-critical.
- [x] **Soderberg line** is the safest failure criterion (use for unknown/variable loads).
- [x] **Standard sizes mandatory** — adopt IS 1862 / IS 2338 / IS 2102 series.
- [x] **Material** choice is governed by *strength, stiffness, ductility, hardness, fatigue, corrosion, cost, availability* — the "8-fold material test."
- [x] **Manufacturing constraints** (castability, machinability, weldability) can override pure strength arguments.
- [x] **Cost equation:** Optimise life-cycle cost, not first-cost.
- [x] **Temperature + Fatigue** require FoS to be increased by **Marin factors** $k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d, k_e$.
- [x] **Always draw** at least one supporting block diagram / classification tree in 14-mark answers.
- [x] **Mention the applicable IS/ISO standard** in long-answer questions for the standards-and-codes sub-mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Materials Based on Mechanical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Selection of Materials Based on Mechanical Properties # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Material Selection in Machine Design** is the systematic engineering process of identifying and choosing the most suitable engineering material for a machine component from a set of candidates, based on a weighted analysis of **mechanical properties**, functional requirements, manufacturing compatibility, service environment, economic viability, and safety factors mandated by design codes. In the context of KTU's Theory of Machines syllabus, this selection process is governed by the **performance indices** derived from mechanical properties such as: $$\text{Performance Index} = \frac{\text{Desired Functional Property}}{\text{Objectionable Property Constraint}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Material selection is NOT about choosing the "strongest" or "hardest" material. It is about choosing the material whose **property profile** best matches the **stress state, deformation tolerance, and failure mode** expected during service. ## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Material Selection For any machine element (shaft, gear, spring, bearing, cam, etc.), selection is driven by **four interacting criteria**: | Pillar | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Mechanical Fitness** | Ability to withstand applied loads without failure | Yield strength $> \sigma_{applied} \times FOS$ | | **Geometric Stability** | Resistance to undesirable deformation | Young's Modulus $E$ governs stiffness | | **Manufacturability** | Ease of casting, forging, machining, heat treatment | Castability, machinability rating | | **Economic Viability** | Total cost including raw material, processing, life | Cost per kg, life-cycle cost | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Prescription" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Doctor's Prescription Model** > Imagine a doctor treating a patient. The doctor does NOT prescribe the "strongest antibiotic." Instead, the doctor: > 1. **Diagnoses** the disease (Load type analysis → Static, Dynamic, Impact, Fatigue) > 2. **Considers allergies** (Environmental constraints → Corrosion, Temperature) > 3. **Checks the patient's age & weight** (Geometric constraints → Size, Weight, Stiffness) > 4. **Chooses the most effective, safe, affordable option** (Performance index vs. Cost) > > Material selection follows EXACTLY the same logic — you treat the machine component as a "patient," and the material as a "medicine" prescribed based on a precise engineering diagnosis. ## 1.4 Mechanical Properties — The Engineering "Vital Signs" Every mechanical property is a "vital sign" of the material, telling us how it will behave under a specific type of "stress illness": - **Strength (Yield, Ultimate, Fatigue):** Resistance to fracture (the material's "immune system" against breaking). - **Stiffness (Young's Modulus $E$):** Resistance to elastic deformation (the material's "skeletal rigidity"). - **Hardness:** Resistance to surface indentation/wear (the material's "skin toughness"). - **Toughness:** Ability to absorb energy before fracture (the material's "shock absorber capacity"). - **Ductility (Percentage Elongation %EL):** Ability to deform plastically before failure (the material's "flexibility warning"). - **Fatigue Strength ($\sigma_{e}$ or $S_{e}$):** Endurance under cyclic loading (the material's "stamina"). - **Creep Resistance:** Resistance to slow deformation at high temperatures. - **Wear Resistance:** Resistance to surface material loss due to relative motion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants to Remember:** > - **Modulus of Elasticity for Steel:** $E \approx 200$ GPa (typical range $190-210$ GPa) > - **Modulus of Elasticity for Aluminum Alloys:** $E \approx 70$ GPa > - **Modulus of Elasticity for Cast Iron:** $E \approx 100-170$ GPa > - **Poisson's Ratio for Most Metals:** $\nu \approx 0.3$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Material Selection Methodology (Ashby's Approach Simplified) The structured approach taught in KTU follows a hierarchical decision tree: ### **Step 1 — Functional Requirements Translation** Convert the design requirement into a property demand: - "Shaft must not bend excessively" $\rightarrow$ Demand: HIGH $E$ - "Spring must store energy" $\rightarrow$ Demand: HIGH resilience $U_{r}$ - "Gear tooth must not wear out" $\rightarrow$ Demand: HIGH surface hardness ### **Step 2 — Identify the Failure Mode** The dominant failure mode dictates the critical property: | Failure Mode | Critical Mechanical Property | |---|---| | Excessive elastic deflection | Stiffness $E$ | | Yielding (plastic deformation) | Yield Strength $\sigma_{y}$ | | Ductile fracture | Ultimate Tensile Strength $\sigma_{u}$ | | Brittle fracture | Fracture Toughness $K_{IC}$ | | Fatigue failure | Endurance Limit $\sigma_{e}$ | | Surface wear | Hardness $H$ | | Impact fracture | Toughness (Charpy V-notch energy) | | Buckling | $E$ and slenderness ratio | | Creep | Creep strength at service temperature | | Stress corrosion | Combination of $\sigma_{y}$ + corrosion resistance | ### **Step 3 — Apply the Performance Index (Material Index)** The **Material Index** $M$ is derived from the design requirement and is the key tool for ranking materials: $$M = \frac{(\text{Functional Property})^{a}}{(\text{Geometric/Cost Constraint})^{b}}$$ ### **Step 4 — Cross-Reference with Manufacturing & Environment** Finally, screen candidates for: - Manufacturability (casting, forging, welding, machining ease) - Corrosion resistance in service environment - Operating temperature range - Availability and cost ## 2.2 Mechanical Property Relationships and Engineering Utilities ### 2.2.1 Strength-Related Properties **Yield Strength ($\sigma_{y}$ or $S_{y}$):** The stress at which a material begins to deform plastically. Used to prevent permanent (unrecoverable) deformation. **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{u}$ or $S_{u}$):** Maximum stress a material can withstand before necking/fracture. **Factor of Safety (FOS):** $$\text{FOS} = n = \frac{\sigma_{y} \text{ (or } \sigma_{u} \text{)}}{\sigma_{working}}$$ > **KTU Rule of Thumb:** > - For **ductile materials** under static load: $n = 1.5$ to $2.0$ based on $\sigma_{y}$ > - For **brittle materials**: $n = 2.0$ to $4.0$ based on $\sigma_{u}$ > - For **fatigue loading**: $n = 2.0$ to $3.5$ based on $\sigma_{e}$ > - For **impact loading**: $n = 2.0$ to $3.0$ based on absorbed energy ### 2.2.2 Stiffness and Deformation **Hooke's Law (Uniaxial):** $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$ **Deflection of a beam under bending:** $$\delta_{max} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} \quad \text{(cantilever)}$$ $$\delta_{max} = \frac{F L^{3}}{48 E I} \quad \text{(simply supported, center load)}$$ Where: - $E$ = Young's Modulus (GPa) - $I$ = Second moment of area ($m^{4}$) - $L$ = Length (m) - $F$ = Applied force (N) **Specific Stiffness (used in weight-critical applications like aerospace):** $$\frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{where } \rho = \text{density (kg/m}^3\text{)}$$ ### 2.2.3 Hardness and Wear Resistance **Relationship between Hardness and Tensile Strength (Empirical, valid for steels):** $$\sigma_{u} \approx 3.45 \times \text{HB} \quad \text{(Brinell to UTS, in MPa)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This rule allows a quick **non-destructive** estimate of tensile strength using only a hardness indentation test on a finished component — extremely valuable in machine design and quality inspection. ### 2.2.4 Toughness and Resilience **Modulus of Resilience** (energy absorbed per unit volume in the elastic region): $$U_{r} = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E} \quad [\text{J/m}^3]$$ **Modulus of Toughness** (total energy absorbed up to fracture, approximated by area under $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve): $$U_{t} = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_{f}} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$ ### 2.2.5 Fatigue Endurance For steels under reversed loading (rotating beam, $R = -1$): $$\sigma_{e} \approx 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{u} \quad \text{(for } \sigma_{u} < 1400 \text{ MPa)}$$ **Modified Endurance Limit (accounting for real-world factors):** $$\sigma_{e}^{\prime} = k_{a} \cdot k_{b} \cdot k_{c} \cdot \sigma_{e}$$ Where: - $k_{a}$ = Surface finish factor - $k_{b}$ = Size factor - $k_{c}$ = Loading factor ### 2.2.6 Poisson's Ratio and Shear $$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$ $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$ Where $G$ = Shear modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $\gamma$ = shear strain. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law — elastic stress-strain relation | Pa | | 2 | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Shear Hooke's Law | Pa | | 3 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Elastic constant interrelation | Pa | | 4 | $\text{FOS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{w}}$ | Factor of Safety (ductile, static) | dimensionless | | 5 | $U_{r} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$ | Modulus of Resilience (spring design) | J/m³ | | 6 | $\sigma_{u} \approx 3.45 \cdot \text{HB}$ | Hardness to UTS conversion (steels) | MPa | | 7 | $\sigma_{e} \approx 0.5 \sigma_{u}$ | Endurance limit estimate (steels, $R=-1$) | MPa | | 8 | $\sigma_{e}^{\prime} = k_{a}k_{b}k_{c}\sigma_{e}$ | Modified endurance limit | MPa | | 9 | $\delta = \dfrac{FL^{3}}{3EI}$ | Cantilever deflection | m | | 10 | $M = \dfrac{P^{a}}{C^{b}}$ | Material Index (Ashby) | varies | | 11 | $\dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Specific Stiffness (lightweight design) | N·m/kg | | 12 | $\dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}$ | Specific Strength (lightweight design) | N·m/kg | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields 1. **Automotive Crankshaft Selection:** Forged alloy steel (e.g., 42CrMo4) chosen for HIGH $\sigma_{y}$, HIGH $\sigma_{e}$, and excellent fatigue life. Cast iron would be cheaper but cannot handle high cyclic bending. 2. **Aircraft Wing Spar:** Aluminum alloy (7075-T6) chosen for high $\sigma_{y}/\rho$ ratio. Steel would be stronger but too heavy. 3. **Gear Tooth (Surface):** Low-carbon steel **case-hardened** to produce a hard wear-resistant surface over a tough core. 4. **Springs (Helical Compression):** High-carbon spring steel (e.g., SUP9, EN47) chosen for high $U_{r} = \sigma_{y}^{2}/2E$. 5. **Lathe Bed (Machine Tool):** Cast iron (gray CI) chosen for HIGH damping capacity, good machinability, and compressive strength — not because it's the strongest. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Deflection-Based Material Index for a Lightweight Cantilever Beam **Engineering Problem:** Select a material for a cantilever beam of length $L$ that must not deflect more than $\delta_{max}$ at the free end under tip load $F$, while minimizing the **mass** of the beam. **Given Design Equations:** $$\delta_{max} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} \quad \text{and} \quad I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12}$$ For a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and height $h$: $$I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12}$$ **Step 1 — Express deflection constraint to find minimum height:** The stiffness requirement is $\delta_{max} \leq \delta_{allow}$. Rearranging: $$\frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} \leq \delta_{allow}$$ $$\frac{F L^{3}}{3 E \cdot \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}} \leq \delta_{allow}$$ $$\frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b h^{3}} \leq \delta_{allow}$$ Solving for $h^{3}$: $$h^{3} \geq \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b \cdot \delta_{allow}}$$ **Step 2 — Express the mass of the beam:** $$\text{Mass } m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot b h L$$ Substituting the minimum required $h$ (treating $h$ as fixed at the minimum value): $$m = \rho b L \left( \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b \cdot \delta_{allow}} \right)^{1/3}$$ **Step 3 — Group the constants (which are fixed by the design) together:** Let $K = \left( \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{\delta_{allow}} \right)^{1/3} \cdot \dfrac{L}{b^{2/3}}$ (a constant for a given design) $$\Rightarrow m = K \cdot \left( \frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Identify the Material Index:** To **minimize mass**, we must **minimize** the bracket term: $$\text{Material Index } M = \frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}} = \frac{\rho}{E^{1/3}}$$ Equivalently, to maximize the ratio (since indices are conventionally stated as "maximize this"): $$M = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$$ **Conclusion of Derivation:** > The optimal material for a **lightweight, stiff cantilever beam** is the material that **maximizes $\dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$** — a combination of stiffness and low density. This is why **carbon-fiber composites** dominate in aerospace structures (high $E$, very low $\rho$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Specific Strength for a Tie Rod Under Tension **Problem:** A tie rod must carry axial load $F$ without yielding. Minimize its weight. **Step 1 — Yield constraint:** $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \leq \frac{\sigma_{y}}{n}$$ Solving for minimum cross-sectional area: $$A_{min} = \frac{n F}{\sigma_{y}}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of the rod of length $L$:** $$m = \rho \cdot A_{min} \cdot L = \rho L \cdot \frac{n F}{\sigma_{y}}$$ **Step 3 — Group constants:** $$m = n F L \cdot \frac{\rho}{\sigma_{y}}$$ **Step 4 — Material Index:** To minimize mass, **minimize** $\dfrac{\rho}{\sigma_{y}}$, or equivalently, **maximize**: $$\boxed{M = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho} \quad \text{(Specific Strength)}}$$ > This is the index used to rank materials for **aircraft tension members**, **rocket motor casings**, and **suspension wishbones**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Selection Matrix — Common Machine Components | Component | Dominant Failure Mode | Critical Property | Recommended Material Family | Reason | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Transmission Shaft** | Fatigue, torsional yielding | $\sigma_{e}, \sigma_{y}$ | Medium-carbon alloy steel (e.g., 40C8, 4140) | High fatigue strength, good machinability | | **Helical Spring** | Permanent set, fatigue | $U_{r} = \sigma_{y}^{2}/2E$, $\sigma_{e}$ | Hardened spring steel (e.g., 51CrV4, SUP9) | High resilience, high elastic limit | | **Gear Tooth** | Surface wear, bending fatigue | Surface hardness, $\sigma_{e}$ | Case-hardened low-carbon steel (e.g., 16MnCr5, EN36) | Hard surface for wear + tough core | | **Flywheel** | Centrifugal bursting | $\sigma_{u}/\rho$ | Cast iron (CI grade FG260) | High damping, good compressive strength | | **Lathe Bed** | Vibration, wear, deflection | Damping capacity, $E$ | Gray cast iron (FG200-FG300) | Excellent damping, machinable, stable | | **Connecting Rod** | Fatigue, buckling | $\sigma_{e}, \sigma_{y}, E$ | Forged alloy steel (e.g., 42CrMo4) | High cyclic load capacity | | **Cam Follower** | Surface fatigue (pitting) | Surface hardness, fatigue | Through-hardened bearing steel (e.g., 100Cr6) | Hardness + dimensional stability | | **Bolts (High-strength)** | Fatigue, thread stripping | $\sigma_{y}, \sigma_{e}$ | Alloy steel (Grade 8.8, 10.9, 12.9) | High tensile + fatigue strength | | **Crankshaft (Automotive)** | Fatigue, wear at journals | $\sigma_{e}$, surface hardness | Ductile iron (SG iron) or forged steel | Balance of cost, fatigue, machinability | | **Wire Rope / Cable** | Tensile fatigue, wear | $\sigma_{u}$, fatigue | High-carbon steel wire (ropes) | Very high UTS, fatigue resistance | | **Piston (IC Engine)** | Thermal fatigue, wear | Strength at $T$, low thermal expansion | Cast aluminum alloy (e.g., Al-Si alloy) | Lightweight + good thermal conductivity | | **Cylinder Liner** | Wear, thermal fatigue | Hardness, thermal stability | Cast iron with graphite (bore) | Self-lubricating graphite, wear resistance | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern) **Problem (14-mark style):** A cantilever beam of length $L = 1.5$ m, rectangular cross-section $b = 50$ mm, must support a tip load of $F = 5$ kN with maximum deflection $\delta_{max} \leq 5$ mm. Compare the **mass** if the material is: - (i) Structural steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³) - (ii) Aluminum alloy 6061-T6 ($E = 70$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³) **Step 1 — Find the required minimum height for each material using deflection formula:** $$\delta = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b h^{3}}$$ Solving for $h$: $$h = \left( \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b \cdot \delta_{allow}} \right)^{1/3}$$ **Constant term (same for both materials):** $$\frac{4 F L^{3}}{b \cdot \delta_{allow}} = \frac{4 \times 5000 \times (1.5)^{3}}{0.050 \times 0.005}$$ Numerator: $4 \times 5000 \times 3.375 = 67{,}500$ Denominator: $0.050 \times 0.005 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ $$\text{Constant} = \frac{67{,}500}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.7 \times 10^{8} \text{ N·m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute $h$ for steel:** $$h_{steel} = \left( \frac{2.7 \times 10^{8}}{200 \times 10^{9}} \right)^{1/3} = \left( 1.35 \times 10^{-3} \right)^{1/3}$$ $$h_{steel} = 0.1106 \text{ m} = 110.6 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $h$ for aluminum:** $$h_{Al} = \left( \frac{2.7 \times 10^{8}}{70 \times 10^{9}} \right)^{1/3} = \left( 3.857 \times 10^{-3} \right)^{1/3}$$ $$h_{Al} = 0.1567 \text{ m} = 156.7 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the masses:** Volume: $V = b \cdot h \cdot L$ - Steel: $V_s = 0.050 \times 0.1106 \times 1.5 = 8.295 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ - Aluminum: $V_{Al} = 0.050 \times 0.1567 \times 1.5 = 1.175 \times 10^{-2}$ m³ Mass: $m = \rho V$ $$m_{steel} = 7850 \times 8.295 \times 10^{-3} = 65.12 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_{Al} = 2700 \times 1.175 \times 10^{-2} = 31.73 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** > The aluminum beam is **~51% lighter** despite needing a **deeper cross-section**, because of its much lower density. This is why aluminum is preferred for aerospace and vehicle structures where stiffness must be achieved with minimum weight. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Material Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["START: Component Design Requirement"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Identify Functional Demand
Load, Deflection, Life, Environment"] nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Determine Dominant Failure Mode
Yield, Fatigue, Wear, Fracture, Buckling"] nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Identify Critical Mechanical Property
sigma_y, sigma_e, E, H, K_IC"] nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Derive Material Index M
M = Functional Property / Constraint"] nodeE --> nodeF["Step 5: Screen Candidate Materials
Using Property Charts"] nodeF --> nodeG["Step 6: Apply Manufacturing Constraints
Casting, Forging, Machining, Welding"] nodeG --> nodeH["Step 7: Apply Environmental Constraints
Corrosion, Temperature, Wear"] nodeH --> nodeI["Step 8: Apply Economic Constraint
Cost per kg, Life-Cycle Cost"] nodeI --> nodeJ["FINAL: Selected Material"] nodeJ --> nodeK["Step 9: Verify by Detailed Design
Stress Analysis, FOS Check"] nodeK --> nodeL["Design Approved"] ``` ## 4.2 Classification of Engineering Materials with Key Properties ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeM["ENGINEERING MATERIALS"] --> nodeN["METALS"] nodeM --> nodeP["NON-METALS"] nodeN --> nodeQ["FERROUS"] nodeN --> nodeR["NON-FERROUS"] nodeQ --> nodeS["Steels
High sigma_y, sigma_e
E = 200 GPa"] nodeQ --> nodeT["Cast Irons
Good damping
E = 100 to 170 GPa"] nodeR --> nodeU["Aluminum Alloys
Low density 2700 kg per m3
E = 70 GPa"] nodeR --> nodeV["Copper Alloys
High conductivity
Good corrosion resistance"] nodeR --> nodeW["Titanium Alloys
High specific strength
E = 110 GPa"] nodeP --> nodeX["POLYMERS
Low E 1 to 5 GPa
Lightweight"] nodeP --> nodeY["CERAMICS
High hardness
Brittle low K_IC"] nodeP --> nodeZ["COMPOSITES
Tailored E and sigma_y
Low density"] ``` ## 4.3 Mechanical Property Decision Tree for Machine Components ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Component to be Designed"] --> startB{"Static or
Dynamic Load?"} startB -->|"Static"| startC{"Brittle or
Ductile Stress State?"} startB -->|"Dynamic / Cyclic"| startD["Demand HIGH Endurance Limit sigma_e
Demand HIGH Fatigue Strength"] startC -->|"Ductile"| startE["Demand HIGH Yield Strength sigma_y
Example: Low-carbon steel"] startC -->|"Brittle"| startF["Demand HIGH Fracture Toughness K_IC
Example: Ductile cast iron"] startD --> startG{"Surface Contact
Involved?"} startG -->|"Yes"| startH["Demand HIGH Surface Hardness
Case hardening, Induction hardening"] startG -->|"No"| startI["Demand HIGH Toughness
Example: Alloy steel 42CrMo4"] startE --> startJ["Material Selected"] startF --> startJ startH --> startJ startI --> startJ ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Property-Application Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR prop1["HIGH Stiffness E"] --> app1["Aircraft spars
Precision instrument frames"] prop2["HIGH Specific Strength sigma_y by rho"] --> app2["Rocket casings
Aircraft tension members"] prop3["HIGH Toughness U_t"] --> app3["Crankshafts
Connecting rods"] prop4["HIGH Hardness H"] --> app4["Gears
Cam followers
Cutting tools"] prop5["HIGH Resilience U_r"] --> app5["Springs
Antivibration mounts"] prop6["HIGH Damping Capacity"] --> app6["Lathe beds
Machine tool bases
Brake drums"] prop7["HIGH Corrosion Resistance"] --> app7["Marine components
Chemical pumps"] prop8["HIGH Wear Resistance"] --> app8["Bearings
Bushings
Piston rings"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *List any three mechanical properties that govern the selection of materials for machine components subjected to (i) static loading, (ii) cyclic loading, and (iii) impact loading.* **Model Answer:** - **(i) Static Loading:** Governed primarily by **Yield Strength ($\sigma_{y}$)** — to prevent plastic deformation — and **Young's Modulus ($E$)** — to control elastic deflection. Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{u}$) is used for brittle materials. - **(ii) Cyclic Loading:** Governed by **Endurance Limit / Fatigue Strength ($\sigma_{e}$)**. For steels, $\sigma_{e} \approx 0.5\sigma_{u}$. The modified endurance limit $\sigma_{e}^{\prime} = k_{a}k_{b}k_{c}\sigma_{e}$ accounts for surface, size, and loading effects. - **(iii) Impact Loading:** Governed by **Toughness** — the ability to absorb energy before fracture. Quantified by the **Charpy V-notch impact test** (energy in Joules). A ductile material like mild steel is preferred over a brittle one like gray cast iron. --- ### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *What is "specific strength" and why is it a critical index in aerospace material selection?* **Model Answer:** **Specific Strength** is defined as the ratio of a material's strength (typically yield strength $\sigma_{y}$ or ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{u}$) to its density $\rho$: $$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\sigma_{u}}{\rho} \quad [\text{N·m/kg}]$$ **Significance in Aerospace:** In aerospace applications, the **weight of every structural member directly affects fuel consumption, payload capacity, and flight range**. Therefore, designers do not just want the strongest material — they want the material that provides the **highest strength per unit weight**. For example: - **Aluminum alloy 7075-T6:** $\sigma_{y} \approx 503$ MPa, $\rho = 2810$ kg/m³ $\Rightarrow$ Specific Strength $\approx 179$ kN·m/kg - **Structural steel:** $\sigma_{y} \approx 250$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³ $\Rightarrow$ Specific Strength $\approx 32$ kN·m/kg - **Titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4V:** $\sigma_{y} \approx 880$ MPa, $\rho = 4430$ kg/m³ $\Rightarrow$ Specific Strength $\approx 199$ kN·m/kg Hence, titanium and aluminum alloys dominate aerospace structures despite steel having higher absolute strength. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the systematic procedure for selecting a material for a machine component. Use a **cantilever beam of length $L = 1$ m, width $b = 40$ mm, supporting a tip load of $F = 2$ kN with maximum allowable deflection of 3 mm** as an example. Determine the required minimum height $h$ for **structural steel** ($E = 200$ GPa) and **aluminum alloy** ($E = 70$ GPa).
