Discuss which is lighter per unit length. **(b) [7 Marks]** A helical compression spring is to be designed to store maximum elastic energy per unit volume. Define the **modulus of resilience** and identify which mechanical property combination governs its selection. Compare spring steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma_{y} = 1200$ MPa) with a phosphor bronze alloy ($E = 100$ GPa, $\sigma_{y} = 400$ MPa) for this application. --- #### **Model Solution for B1(a):** **Step 1 — Systematic Material Selection Procedure (General):** 1. Define the **functional requirement** (e.g., load support, energy storage, wear resistance). 2. Identify the **dominant failure mode** (yielding, fatigue, wear, fracture, buckling). 3. Identify the **critical mechanical property** that resists that failure. 4. Derive a **material index** combining property and constraint. 5. Screen candidate materials using property charts. 6. Apply **secondary constraints**: manufacturing, environment, cost, availability. **Step 2 — Apply to the given cantilever beam problem:** The deflection formula is: $$\delta_{max} = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b h^{3}}$$ Solving for $h$: $$h = \left( \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b \cdot \delta_{allow}} \right)^{1/3}$$ Substituting values: $F = 2000$ N, $L = 1$ m, $b = 0.040$ m, $\delta_{allow} = 0.003$ m. Constant numerator: $4 \times 2000 \times (1)^3 = 8000$ Constant denominator: $0.040 \times 0.003 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ $$\text{Constant} = \frac{8000}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.667 \times 10^{7}$$ **For Steel ($E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa):** $$h_{steel} = \left( \frac{6.667 \times 10^{7}}{200 \times 10^{9}} \right)^{1/3} = (3.333 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3}$$ $$h_{steel} \approx 0.0693 \text{ m} = 69.3 \text{ mm}$$ **For Aluminum ($E = 70 \times 10^9$ Pa):** $$h_{Al} = \left( \frac{6.667 \times 10^{7}}{70 \times 10^{9}} \right)^{1/3} = (9.524 \times 10^{-4})^{1/3}$$ $$h_{Al} \approx 0.0986 \text{ m} = 98.6 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Mass per unit length comparison:** Mass per unit length $m' = \rho \cdot b \cdot h$ - Steel ($\rho = 7850$ kg/m³): $m'_{steel} = 7850 \times 0.040 \times 0.0693 = 21.76$ kg/m - Aluminum ($\rho = 2700$ kg/m³): $m'_{Al} = 2700 \times 0.040 \times 0.0986 = 10.65$ kg/m **Conclusion:** The aluminum beam is **~51% lighter per meter** despite needing a taller cross-section, because of its lower density. --- #### **Model Solution for B1(b):** **Step 1 — Definition of Modulus of Resilience:** The **Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$)** is the energy stored per unit volume in a material when it is loaded elastically up to its yield point: $$U_r = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E} \quad [\text{J/m}^3]$$ It represents the **capacity of a material to absorb energy elastically** and release it on unloading — the fundamental requirement for any spring. **Step 2 — Governing Property Combination:** For a spring, the designer wants **high yield strength** (so the spring can be stressed to a high level without permanent set) **AND** **low Young's Modulus** (so a given stress produces large elastic strain, hence more stored energy). The combined criterion is: $$\text{Material Index } M = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{E} = 2 U_r$$ **Step 3 — Comparison:** **Spring Steel:** $$U_r = \frac{(1200 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{18}}{4 \times 10^{11}} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m}^3$$ **Phosphor Bronze:** $$U_r = \frac{(400 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 100 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{17}}{2 \times 10^{11}} = 8.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^3$$ **Conclusion:** Spring steel stores **4.5 times more elastic energy per unit volume** than phosphor bronze. Hence, spring steel is the unambiguous choice for **high-capacity helical springs** despite higher cost. Phosphor bronze is preferred for springs requiring **electrical conductivity** or **corrosion resistance** (e.g., electrical contact springs). --- ### **Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Alternative Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the relationship between **hardness and tensile strength** in steels. A hardened steel component has a Brinell Hardness Number of 450 HB. Estimate its ultimate tensile strength. If this component is subjected to a fully reversed cyclic load, estimate its endurance limit. What factor of safety would you recommend for fatigue loading, and what is the allowable working stress? **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the selection of materials for **gears** in a high-speed automobile transmission. Justify why case-hardened low-carbon alloy steel is preferred over through-hardened medium-carbon steel. List at least **four mechanical properties** the gear material must possess, and explain each. --- #### **Model Solution for B1B(a):** **Step 1 — Hardness-Tensile Strength Relationship:** For steels, an empirical correlation (derived from extensive experimental data) exists between Brinell Hardness Number (BHN or HB) and Ultimate Tensile Strength: $$\boxed{\sigma_{u} \approx 3.45 \times \text{HB} \quad [\text{MPa}]}$$ This holds reasonably well for steels with HB in the range 100–600. **Step 2 — Estimate UTS from HB = 450:** $$\sigma_{u} = 3.45 \times 450 = 1552.5 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.55 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 3 — Endurance Limit (Reversed Loading, $R = -1$):** For steels with $\sigma_{u} < 1400$ MPa, the rule is $\sigma_{e} = 0.5 \sigma_{u}$. However, here $\sigma_{u} = 1552.5$ MPa > 1400 MPa. For ultra-high-strength steels, the empirical formula is: $$\sigma_{e} \approx 0.4 \sigma_{u} = 0.4 \times 1552.5 = 621 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Recommended Factor of Safety:** For fatigue loading with uncertainty in loading and surface conditions, **FOS $n_f = 2.5$ to 3.5** is typical for steel machine components. We choose **$n_f = 3.0$**. **Step 5 — Allowable Working Stress:** $$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{e}}{n_f} = \frac{621}{3.0} = 207 \text{ MPa}$$ **Conclusion:** The component can safely carry a reversed stress of up to **207 MPa** without fatigue failure over its design life. --- #### **Model Solution for B1B(b):** **Material Selection for Automobile Transmission Gears:** The four essential mechanical properties are: | # | Property | Reason for Requirement | |---|---|---| | 1 | **High Surface Hardness (H)** | Gear teeth experience Hertzian contact stresses at the pitch circle. A hard surface resists **pitting, scuffing, and wear**. | | 2 | **High Core Toughness** | Teeth are subjected to **bending fatigue** at the root radius (fillet). The core must be tough to resist crack propagation. | | 3 | **High Fatigue Strength ($\sigma_e$)** | Each tooth is loaded and unloaded **twice per revolution**, leading to high-cycle fatigue. | | 4 | **Good Dimensional Stability** | Gears require precise tooth geometry. The material must resist **distortion** during heat treatment and service. | **Justification: Case-Hardened Low-Carbon Alloy Steel vs. Through-Hardened Medium-Carbon Steel:** - **Case-Hardened Low-Carbon Alloy Steel (e.g., 16MnCr5, 20MnCr5, EN36):** Through carburizing/nitriding, the surface acquires a hard case (**58–62 HRC**) while the core remains tough (~30–40 HRC). This produces an **ideal combination of hard surface + tough core** — exactly matching the gear's dual demand. - **Through-Hardened Medium-Carbon Steel (e.g., 40C8):** After quenching and tempering, the entire section has uniform hardness. While strong, it is **brittle at high hardness** and **lacks the gradient**. It is also **more prone to distortion** during quenching, ruining tooth geometry. It is suitable for **larger, slower gears** but not high-speed automobile gears. **Conclusion:** Case-hardened low-carbon alloy steel is the optimal choice for high-speed automobile transmission gears because it simultaneously satisfies the wear-resistance requirement (hard surface) and the fatigue-resistance requirement (tough core) — a property profile unattainable with through-hardened steels. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating "Hardness" with "Strength"** — Hardness is surface resistance to indentation; strength is bulk resistance to fracture. A material can be hard but brittle (e.g., ceramics) — never use hardness alone for design. > 2. **Forgetting to apply FOS** — The calculated allowable stress must always be divided by a factor of safety. KTU examiners heavily penalize designs with no FOS justification. > 3. **Using $\sigma_u$ instead of $\sigma_y$ for ductile materials** — For ductile materials under static load, design is based on **yield strength** to prevent permanent deformation, NOT ultimate strength. > 4. **Ignoring the Endurance Limit rule for high-strength steels** — The simple formula $\sigma_e = 0.5\sigma_u$ applies **only when $\sigma_u < 1400$ MPa**. For higher-strength steels, use $\sigma_e \approx 0.4\sigma_u$. Examiners specifically test this nuance. > 5. **Confusing Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$) with Modulus of Toughness ($U_t$)** — $U_r$ is the **elastic** energy storage (spring design); $U_t$ is the **total** energy to fracture (impact design). Mixing these up costs 3–5 marks easily. > 6. **Not mentioning manufacturing constraints** — A complete KTU answer must mention **castability, machinability, weldability, and availability**, not just mechanical properties. Omitting these is a common 2-mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Material Selection = Function + Property + Constraint + Cost.** A holistic decision, never based on a single property. - **Stiffness** ($E$) and **Strength** ($\sigma_y, \sigma_u$) are FUNDAMENTALLY DIFFERENT — a stiff material need not be strong (cast iron is stiff but weaker than alloy steel). - The **Material Index** $M = \dfrac{P^a}{C^b}$ is the formal KTU method to rank materials — derive it from the design equation before choosing. - **Specific Strength** ($\sigma_y/\rho$) and **Specific Stiffness** ($E/\rho$) are the indices for **weight-critical** designs (aerospace, automotive). - **Endurance Limit** for steels: $\sigma_e \approx 0.5\sigma_u$ (for $\sigma_u \leq 1400$ MPa); $\sigma_e \approx 0.4\sigma_u$ for higher-strength steels. - **Hardness–UTS relation** for steels: $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times$ HB (in MPa) — a quick non-destructive estimation tool. - **Case hardening** gives hard surface + tough core — ideal for gears, cams, and bearings. - **Gray cast iron** is selected for **machine tool beds** because of **high damping capacity**, NOT because of high strength. - **Aluminum alloys** ($E \approx 70$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³) are preferred where **lightweight + corrosion resistance** matter. - **Springs** demand **high modulus of resilience** $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / 2E$ — high yield, moderate modulus. - Always use **Yield Strength $\sigma_y$** as the design stress basis for ductile materials under static load; use **$\sigma_u$** for brittle materials; use **$\sigma_e$** for cyclic loading. - **Factor of Safety (FOS)** values: Static ductile $1.5$–$2.0$; Static brittle $2.0$–$4.0$; Fatigue $2.0$–$3.5$; Impact $2.0$–$3.0$. - The three pillars of a complete design answer: **(i) Functional demand, (ii) Failure mode analysis, (iii) Property–manufacturing–cost trade-off**.

topics.title: Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Considerations** in cam-and-follower systems refer to the systematic set of geometric, kinematic, dynamic, and material criteria that a machine designer must satisfy while synthesizing a cam profile. The aim is to ensure that the transmitted motion is *smooth*, the *contact stress* is within allowable limits, the *pressure angle* does not exceed permissible bounds, the *cam size* is compact, and the *follower motion* matches the prescribed displacement diagram without jamming or excessive vibration.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PCAGT403 – Theory of Machines), Module 3 (Cams) requires the student to translate the desired follower motion (function of angle θ) into a feasible cam geometry by accounting for:

- **Base circle radius** ($r_0$)
- **Eccentricity / offset** ($e$)
- **Roller radius** ($r_r$)
- **Pressure angle** ($\alpha$)
- **Radius of curvature** ($\rho$)
- **Operating speed** ($\omega$) and the resulting **contact stress**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Cams):**
> Design considerations for cams form the link between *kinematic synthesis* (drawing the displacement diagram) and *dynamic feasibility* (whether the cam can actually be manufactured and run safely at the given speed).

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Real-World Analogy

Think of a cam as a **valve-lifter in a car engine**. The rotating cam lobe (egg-shaped lump) pushes a valve spring-loaded follower up and down. The engine may spin at **3000 rpm** (i.e., $\omega = 314.16$ rad/s). If the cam lobe is shaped carelessly, the follower can:

1. **Jam** inside the guide (pressure angle too large → no normal force component to keep the follower seated).
2. **Smash** the cam surface at the nose (radius of curvature too small → Hertzian contact stress explodes).
3. **Skid or jump** at high speed (acceleration of the follower too high → follower separates from cam).

So before cutting the metal, the designer must check a *checklist* of geometric and dynamic limits — that checklist is what we call **Design Considerations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy in one line:** Just as a road curve must be banked to a safe angle so that a car doesn't skid off, a cam profile must be cut with a *safe pressure angle* so that the follower doesn't lock up.

### 1.3 Standard Quantitative Limits (commonly used in KTU textbooks)

| Parameter | Typical Permissible Value | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Max pressure angle ($\alpha_{max}$)** | **30° – 45°** | Translating followers |
| **Max pressure angle (oscillating)** | **30°** | Oscillating followers |
| **Eccentricity ratio ($e/r_0$)** | ≤ 0.25 – 0.30 | Compact cams |
| **Minimum radius of curvature** | ≥ 3 to 4 mm | Avoid undercutting |
| **Cam speed ($\omega$)** | 10 – 1000 rpm | Industrial applications |

These numbers are the *design guard-rails* every KTU paper expects you to recall and apply.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Cam Design

A cam is never designed in isolation. The designer must satisfy **six simultaneous constraints**:

1. **Kinematic Constraint — Displacement diagram:**
   The follower displacement $s(\theta)$ must be a smooth, continuous function. KTU textbooks allow three standard motions: *Uniform velocity*, *Simple harmonic motion (SHM)*, and *Uniform acceleration and retardation (Cycloidal)*.

2. **Geometric Constraint — Pressure angle ($\alpha$):**
   The pressure angle is the angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **common normal at the point of contact**. A large $\alpha$ means most of the cam force is wasted pushing the follower against its guides, and a small disturbance will cause jamming.

3. **Geometric Constraint — Base circle radius ($r_0$):**
   The smallest circle that can be drawn tangent to the cam profile. A larger $r_0$ gives a more compact pressure angle but a bigger cam.

4. **Manufacturing Constraint — Radius of curvature ($\rho$):**
   The cam profile itself must be curved enough to be machinable. If $\rho$ becomes negative or too small, the cam becomes a "cusp" or "undercut" — physically impossible to cut with a single-point tool.

5. **Dynamic Constraint — Contact stress:**
   At high $\omega$, the Hertzian contact stress between cam and roller must remain below the surface fatigue limit of the chosen material pair.

6. **Lubrication & Wear Constraint:**
   Sliding velocity at the contact point determines the PV (Pressure × Velocity) product — a key wear indicator.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pressure angle — Radial translating roller follower** | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp e}{s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}$ | $s$ = follower lift, $e$ = offset, $r_0$ = base radius |
| 2 | **Pressure angle — Offset translating roller follower** | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} - e}{s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}$ | For follower offset *in direction of rotation* (return stroke reverses sign) |
| 3 | **Pressure angle — Knife-edge follower** | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp e$ over $s$ | Limiting case as $r_0 \gg e$ and $r_r \to 0$ |
| 4 | **Pressure angle — Oscillating roller follower** | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{r_0 + r_r}{\sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}\cdot\dfrac{d\phi/d\theta}{1 + (d\phi/d\theta)\cdot e/\sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}$ | $\phi$ = angular displacement of follower |
| 5 | **Velocity of follower** | $v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$ | $\omega$ = cam angular velocity |
| 6 | **Acceleration of follower** | $a = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}$ | Determines inertia force |
| 7 | **Minimum cam size (no offset)** | $r_0 = \dfrac{v_s}{\omega \tan\alpha_p}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega \tan\alpha_p}{v_s}\right)^2 r_0^2} - 1\right]$ | Shukla's equation (iterative) |
| 8 | **Radius of curvature at any point** | $\rho = \dfrac{\left[r + \left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}{r + 2\left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)^2 - r\left(\dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}\right)}$ | $r$ = prime-circle radius at that instant |
| 9 | **Hertzian contact stress (line contact)** | $\sigma_c = 0.418\sqrt{\dfrac{PE}{b}\cdot\dfrac{(R_1 + R_2)}{R_1 R_2}}$ | $E$ = effective modulus, $P$ = normal load |
| 10 | **Sliding velocity at contact** | $v_s = \omega (r + r_r)\tan\alpha$ | Determines frictional heat |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for offset:**
> In KTU papers, *minus* corresponds to **outstroke with offset in direction of cam rotation**, and *plus* to the return stroke. Always draw the figure first and decide the sign — do not blindly copy the formula.

### 2.3 Why These Constraints Matter — Real-World Utility

- **Automotive camshafts:** High $\omega$ (up to 7000 rpm in racing engines) makes pressure angle and radius-of-curvature the *make-or-break* parameters.
- **Textile machinery cams:** Operate at very low $\omega$ but for millions of cycles, so **wear and lubrication** dominate the design.
- **Printing press cams:** Must be absolutely smooth, so the **acceleration curve** (jerk) is the key constraint.
- **Robotics and CNC pick-and-place:** Compact size is critical, driving the use of **offset followers** to shrink the base circle.

### 2.4 Logical Flow — From Motion to Metal

The design procedure taught in KTU follows this 4-step logical chain:

1. **Step 1 — Kinematic synthesis:** Choose $s(\theta)$ from the application (SHM, cycloidal, etc.). Compute $ds/d\theta$ and $d^2s/d\theta^2$.
2. **Step 2 — Geometric synthesis:** Using the pressure-angle formula, find the *minimum* $r_0$ that keeps $\alpha \le \alpha_{max}$. If offset is allowed, optimize $e$.
3. **Step 3 — Check radius of curvature:** Ensure $\rho > 0$ (no undercut) and $\rho \ge \rho_{min}$ (manufacturable).
4. **Step 4 — Dynamic check:** Compute Hertzian stress and PV product. Iterate step 2 if any check fails.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **exhaustive** — every algebraic transition is shown explicitly, in line with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE mandate.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure Angle for an Offset Translating Roller Follower

**Setting (as drawn in any standard KTU figure):**
- Cam rotates about center $O$ with angular velocity $\omega$.
- Follower axis is offset by a distance $e$ from $O$ (the "eccentricity" or "offset").
- The roller of radius $r_r$ rolls on the cam profile.
- $I$ is the instantaneous point of contact.
- $A$ is the center of the roller.
- $s$ is the displacement of the follower from its lowest position.
- $r_0$ is the radius of the base circle.

#### Step-by-step derivation:

**Step 1:** Resolve the geometry. The horizontal distance from the cam center $O$ to the roller center $A$ has two components:

- The vertical (or "lift") component is $s$.
- The horizontal offset is $\sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}$ (constant, since the follower moves parallel to its axis).

Therefore, the position vector of the roller center $A$ measured from $O$ has magnitude:

$$OA = \sqrt{\left(\sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}\right)^2 + s^2}$$

But it is more useful to express it in the cam-fixed frame. The roller center $A$ is at polar coordinates $(r, \beta)$ where $r = OA$ and $\beta$ is the angle that $OA$ makes with the cam-fixed reference axis.

**Step 2:** Differentiate the position. The follower is translating, so its velocity is purely along the follower axis. In cam coordinates, the velocity of $A$ has two components — a radial component $dr/dt$ and a tangential component $r \cdot d\beta/dt$. Resolving these:

$$\dfrac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = v \sin\alpha \mp \omega e$$

where the $\mp$ accounts for the component of cam rotation that contributes to the tangential velocity of $A$. The sign convention follows KTU textbooks:

- *Minus* sign: outstroke with offset in the direction of cam rotation.
- *Plus* sign: return stroke.

**Step 3:** The radial velocity component of $A$ is:

$$\dfrac{dr}{dt} = v \cos\alpha$$

**Step 4:** The vertical (or lift) component of the velocity of $A$ along the follower axis is:

$$v = \dfrac{ds}{dt} + \omega e \quad \text{(or } v = \dfrac{ds}{dt} - \omega e \text{)}$$

depending on direction. Combining with the geometry:

$$v \sin\alpha = \omega \dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp \omega e$$

**Step 5:** The radial distance from $O$ to $A$ is the "prime-circle radius" $r$ at that instant. By Pythagoras:

$$r^2 = s^2 + (r_0^2 - e^2) \cdot \left(\dfrac{?}{?}\right)$$

Wait — let us be careful. The roller center $A$ is at coordinates:

$$x_A = e, \quad y_A = s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}$$

when offset is along the $x$-axis. The radial distance is:

$$r^2 = e^2 + \left(s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}\right)^2$$

For small $s$ compared to $r_0$, $\sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2} \approx r_0$ and the radial direction is approximately perpendicular to the follower axis. So the **normal** to the cam profile is the line from $A$ to the contact point $I$, which is perpendicular to the cam surface. This is the **common normal** — and the **pressure angle** is the angle between this normal and the follower axis (direction of motion).

**Step 6:** From the velocity triangle:

$$v \cos\alpha = \omega r = \omega \left(s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}\right)$$

(the radial component of the velocity of $A$ equals the cam's rotation contribution along the line $OA$).

**Step 7:** Form the ratio $\tan\alpha$ by dividing the tangential component by the radial component:

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v \sin\alpha}{v \cos\alpha} = \dfrac{\omega \dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp \omega e}{\omega\left(s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}\right)}$$

Cancelling $\omega$:

$$\boxed{\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp e}{s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}}$$

This is the **master equation** used in every KTU Module-3 numerical problem on cam design.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Expression for Velocity and Acceleration of the Follower

**Given:** $s = s(\theta)$ — the displacement diagram.

**Step 1:** The follower is rigidly connected to a translating mass. Its instantaneous position is $s$, and we have:

$$s = f(\theta)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to time using the chain rule, $\theta = \omega t$:

$$\dfrac{ds}{dt} = \dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$$

Hence:

$$v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate again:

$$\dfrac{d^2 s}{dt^2} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\omega \dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right) = \omega \cdot \dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right) = \omega \cdot \dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2} \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$$

$$\boxed{a = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}}$$

These two results convert the **displacement diagram** (in terms of cam angle $\theta$) into **real physical velocity and acceleration** (in m/s and m/s²) once the operating speed $\omega$ is given.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Base-Circle Radius (No Offset)

When $e = 0$ and the maximum permissible pressure angle is $\alpha_p$, the condition for the worst case is:

$$\tan\alpha_p = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)_{max}}{s_{max} + r_0}$$

Solving for $r_0$:

$$r_0 = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)_{max}}{\tan\alpha_p} - s_{max}$$

For a simple harmonic outstroke with total lift $h$ over angle $\beta$:

$$s = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

$$\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta} \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)$$

The maximum slope is at $\theta = \beta/2$:

$$\left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)_{max} = \dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{r_0 = \dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta \tan\alpha_p} - \dfrac{h}{2}}$$

This is a *one-shot closed-form* for the minimum base-circle radius when using SHM outstroke. Similar closed-form expressions exist for cycloidal motion and uniform acceleration.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Radius of Curvature of the Cam Profile (Qualitative Outline)

For a radial translating follower (no offset), the cam profile in polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ is:

$$r = r_0 + s(\theta)$$

The radius of curvature $\rho$ of the cam profile at any point is:

$$\rho = \dfrac{\left[r^2 + \left(\dfrac{dr}{d\theta}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}{r^2 + 2\left(\dfrac{dr}{d\theta}\right)^2 - r\dfrac{d^2 r}{d\theta^2}}$$

Since $r = r_0 + s$, we have $dr/d\theta = ds/d\theta$ and $d^2r/d\theta^2 = d^2 s/d\theta^2$, giving the **KTU form**:

$$\boxed{\rho = \dfrac{\left[(r_0 + s)^2 + \left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}{(r_0 + s)^2 + 2\left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)^2 - (r_0 + s)\dfrac{d^2 s}{d\theta^2}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $\rho$ becomes **negative**, the cam has a "**cusp**" — physically it means the profile crosses itself and the cam cannot be manufactured. The KTU valuation strictly penalises a designer who ignores this.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Follower

| Follower Type | Best Use Case | Limitation | Design Hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knife-edge** | Theoretical analysis, simple SHM | Wear very high at tip, no roller means stress spikes | Never use in real machines |
| **Flat-faced** | High-speed, low noise, easy to manufacture | Pressure angle varies rapidly across face | Use for small lifts |
| **Roller (radial)** | General industrial cams | Larger cam size for same lift | Most common; default choice |
| **Roller (offset)** | Need to reduce pressure angle & cam size | Offset adds complexity to manufacture | Use when $ds/d\theta$ is large |
| **Spherical-faced** | Mating with curved surface, automotive rocker arms | High contact stress at high loads | Use with hardened cam |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Illustration

> **Problem (KTU-style, 14 marks):**
> A cam rotating at $\omega = 30$ rad/s gives a SHM outstroke of lift $h = 40$ mm in angle $\beta = 60°$ to a radial roller follower of roller radius $r_r = 10$ mm. Maximum permissible pressure angle is $\alpha_p = 30°$. Find the minimum base-circle radius.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute maximum velocity term.

For SHM outstroke:

$$\left(\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}\right)_{max} = \dfrac{\pi h}{2 \beta} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.040}{2 \times (\pi/3)} = \dfrac{0.040 \times 3}{2} = 0.060 \text{ m/rad}$$

Step 2 — Apply the minimum base-circle formula.

$$r_0 = \dfrac{(ds/d\theta)_{max}}{\tan\alpha_p} - s_{max} = \dfrac{0.060}{\tan 30°} - 0.040$$

$$\tan 30° = 0.5774$$

$$r_0 = \dfrac{0.060}{0.5774} - 0.040 = 0.1039 - 0.040 = 0.0639 \text{ m}$$

$$r_0 \approx \mathbf{64 \text{ mm}}$$

Step 3 — Convert to prime-circle (pitch) radius.

The **prime-circle radius** at the point of maximum velocity is:

$$r_p = r_0 + s_{max} = 64 + 40 = 104 \text{ mm}$$

Step 4 — Sanity check: At $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta$, the velocity is zero, so $\alpha = 0$ (purely radial force) — the design is *self-centering*. The answer passes the KTU valuation criterion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Because the cam-follower geometry is intrinsically a 2D plane figure, we present (a) a **Mermaid block-architecture diagram** capturing the logical flow of design, and (b) a **detailed Mermaid flow diagram** showing the geometric relationships. A physical 2D drawing is best sketched on graph paper during the exam.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cam Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["APPLICATION REQUIREMENT: lift, motion type, speed"]
    nodeB["STEP 1: Choose displacement function s of theta"]
    nodeC["STEP 2: Compute ds by dtheta and d2s by dtheta2"]
    nodeD["STEP 3: Assume r0 initial guess"]
    nodeE["STEP 4: Compute pressure angle alpha"]
    nodeF["STEP 5: Compare alpha with alpha_max"]
    nodeG["STEP 6: Check radius of curvature rho greater than zero"]
    nodeH["STEP 7: Check Hertzian contact stress"]
    nodeI["STEP 8: Check PV wear limit"]
    nodeJ["DESIGN ACCEPTED: r0 and e are final"]
    nodeK["ITERATE: increase r0 or adjust e and loop to step 4"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|"alpha exceeds limit"| nodeK
    nodeF -->|"alpha within limit"| nodeG
    nodeG -->|"rho less than zero or too small"| nodeK
    nodeG -->|"rho acceptable"| nodeH
    nodeH -->|"stress exceeds limit"| nodeK
    nodeH -->|"stress within limit"| nodeI
    nodeI -->|"PV exceeds limit"| nodeK
    nodeI -->|"PV within limit"| nodeJ
    nodeK --> nodeE
```

### 4.2 Geometric Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeO["POINT O: Cam center of rotation"]
    nodeE["ECCENTRICITY e: offset from O to follower axis"]
    nodeR0["BASE CIRCLE radius r0: smallest cam circle"]
    nodeS["FOLLOWER LIFT s: instantaneous vertical displacement"]
    nodeA["POINT A: Roller center"]
    nodeI["POINT I: Instantaneous cam-roller contact"]
    nodeAlpha["PRESSURE ANGLE alpha: between follower direction and common normal"]
    nodeRr["ROLLER radius rr"]

    nodeO ---|"horizontal offset e"| nodeE
    nodeE ---|"vertical leg sqrt r0 squared minus e squared"| nodeR0
    nodeR0 ---|"plus lift s gives prime circle"| nodeA
    nodeA ---|"common normal direction"| nodeI
    nodeI ---|"angle alpha with follower axis"| nodeAlpha
    nodeA ---|"offset by rr from cam profile"| nodeI
    nodeRr --- nodeA
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting Follower Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ["Is the operating speed high omega greater than 100 rpm"]
    nodeA1["Use FLAT-FACED follower"]
    nodeQ2["Is the lift small h less than 15 mm"]
    nodeA2["Use FLAT-FACED or small ROLLER"]
    nodeQ3["Is cam space constrained"]
    nodeA3["Use OFFSET roller follower to reduce r0"]
    nodeA4["Use RADIAL roller follower default choice"]

    nodeQ -->|"yes"| nodeA1
    nodeQ -->|"no"| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|"yes"| nodeA2
    nodeQ2 -->|"no"| nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 -->|"yes"| nodeA3
    nodeQ3 -->|"no"| nodeA4
```

### 4.4 Pressure-Angle vs Cam-Angle Profile (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["At theta equals zero: ds by dtheta equals zero, so alpha equals zero"]
    nodeTm["At theta equals beta by two: ds by dtheta is maximum, so alpha is maximum"]
    nodeTend["At theta equals beta: ds by dtheta equals zero again, alpha returns to zero"]
    nodePeak["PEAK ALPHA must be less than alpha_max for design to be valid"]

    nodeT0 --> nodeTm
    nodeTm --> nodePeak
    nodePeak --> nodeTend
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark distribution in KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation):**
> - Part A carries **5 questions × 3 marks = 15 marks** (answer any 3, full module).
> - Part B carries **one full-question of 14 marks per module** with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B.
> - The questions below are calibrated to this exact pattern.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (Module 3 — Cam Design Considerations) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "pressure angle" in a cam-follower pair. Why is its magnitude critical in cam design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **pressure angle** is the angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **common normal drawn at the point of contact** between the cam and the follower.

It is critical because:

1. **(1 mark)** The normal force $F_n$ transmitted from the cam to the follower can be resolved into a useful component $F_n \cos\alpha$ (along the follower axis) and a wasteful component $F_n \sin\alpha$ (pushing the follower against its guides).
2. **(1 mark)** A large $\alpha$ increases side-thrust on the follower, causing **binding, jamming, and rapid wear** of the guide.
3. **(1 mark)** For a translating follower, the maximum permissible $\alpha$ is **30° during return stroke** and **45° during outstroke** (in the absence of an offset). For an oscillating follower, the limit is **30°** for both strokes.

> **Examiner's tip:** A diagram with the normal drawn and the angle labelled earns **½ bonus mark** in KTU valuation.

#### Question 2 (Module 3 — Cam Design Considerations) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State any three design considerations that govern the size of the base circle of a cam.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** **Maximum permissible pressure angle:** The base circle must be large enough so that, at the position of maximum follower velocity, the pressure angle does not exceed the allowable limit (30°–45°).
2. **(1 mark)** **Radius of curvature of the cam profile:** The base circle radius must keep the cam profile curvature $\rho$ positive and above a minimum machinable value (typically 3–4 mm) throughout the lift, including the nose.
3. **(1 mark)** **Manufacturing and assembly constraints:** Larger base circle means a bigger, heavier, and more expensive cam. Designers seek the **minimum $r_0$** that satisfies (1) and (2) — a balance between performance and compactness.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between two independent questions.

---

#### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A cam rotating at a uniform speed of **300 rpm** imparts the following motion to a **radial translating roller follower** (roller radius = **15 mm**):

- **Outstroke** of **30 mm** during **120°** of cam rotation with **simple harmonic motion**.
- **Dwell** for the next **30°**.
- **Return stroke** of **30 mm** during **120°** of cam rotation with **uniform acceleration and retardation (cycloidal)**.
- **Dwell** for the remaining **90°**.

Find:

1. The maximum velocity and acceleration of the follower during the outstroke.
2. The maximum velocity and acceleration of the follower during the return stroke.
3. The minimum base-circle radius if the maximum permissible pressure angle is **30°**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the effect of **follower offset** on the pressure angle and the size of the cam. State the conditions under which an offset follower is preferred.

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

Outstroke angle: $\beta_o = 120° = \dfrac{2\pi}{3} \text{ rad} = 2.094 \text{ rad}$

Return stroke angle: $\beta_r = 120° = \dfrac{2\pi}{3} \text{ rad} = 2.094 \text{ rad}$

Lift: $h = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$

**Step 2 — Outstroke (SHM).**

For SHM outstroke, the displacement function is:

$$s = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta_o}\right)\right]$$

Velocity term:

$$v = \omega \cdot \dfrac{ds}{d\theta} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{\beta_o} \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

Maximum occurs at $\theta = \beta_o/2$:

$$v_{max}^{out} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{\pi h}{2 \beta_o} = 31.416 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 0.030}{2 \times 2.094}$$

$$v_{max}^{out} = 31.416 \times 0.02250 = \mathbf{0.707 \text{ m/s}}$$

Acceleration term:

$$a = -\omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi}{\beta_o}\right)^2 \cdot \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

Maximum magnitude occurs at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta_o$:

$$a_{max}^{out} = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi}{\beta_o}\right)^2 = (31.416)^2 \times 0.015 \times \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2.094}\right)^2$$

$$a_{max}^{out} = 987.0 \times 0.015 \times 2.250 = \mathbf{33.31 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Return stroke (Uniform acceleration and retardation).**

The motion profile is half accelerated, half retarded over the return angle $\beta_r$. The follower accelerates uniformly for the first half ($\beta_r/2$) and retards uniformly for the second half.

For uniform acceleration-retardation:

$$v_{max} = \omega \cdot \dfrac{2h}{\beta_r} = 31.416 \times \dfrac{2 \times 0.030}{2.094} = 31.416 \times 0.02865$$

$$v_{max}^{ret} = \mathbf{0.900 \text{ m/s}}$$

$$a_{max}^{ret} = \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{4h}{\beta_r^2} = (31.416)^2 \times \dfrac{4 \times 0.030}{(2.094)^2} = 987.0 \times 0.02736$$

$$a_{max}^{ret} = \mathbf{27.00 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum base-circle radius.**

The maximum value of $ds/d\theta$ during the outstroke is $\pi h / (2 \beta_o) = 0.02250$ m/rad. The peak pressure angle occurs when $s$ is also at its maximum value ($s = h = 0.030$ m) for SHM? — *No*, the maximum pressure angle is at the position where the ratio $(ds/d\theta)/(s + r_0)$ peaks. We use the standard result for SHM outstroke with the maximum permissible pressure angle:

$$r_0 = \dfrac{(ds/d\theta)_{max}}{\tan\alpha_p} - \dfrac{h}{2}$$

$$r_0 = \dfrac{0.02250}{\tan 30°} - \dfrac{0.030}{2} = \dfrac{0.02250}{0.5774} - 0.015$$

$$r_0 = 0.03897 - 0.015 = 0.02397 \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{24 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Cross-check the return stroke:** The maximum $ds/d\theta$ in the return stroke is $\dfrac{2h}{\beta_r} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.030}{2.094} = 0.02865$ m/rad, which is *larger* than the outstroke peak. Therefore the **return stroke governs the design** if SHM is used on the outstroke and uniform acceleration on the return. A careful designer should check **both** strokes.

Recomputing for the return stroke (uniform acceleration), $s_{max}$ at the peak is $h/2 = 0.015$ m. Using the formula for uniform acceleration:

$$r_0 = \dfrac{0.02865}{\tan 30°} - 0.015 = 0.04962 - 0.015 = 0.03462 \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{35 \text{ mm}}$$

So the design $r_0 = \mathbf{35 \text{ mm}}$ is governed by the return stroke.

**Part (b):**

An **offset** in the follower shifts the follower axis sideways from the cam rotation center by a distance $e$. The pressure-angle formula becomes:

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp e}{s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}$$

- **(2 marks)** During the outstroke (offset in direction of rotation): the term $(ds/d\theta - e)$ appears in the numerator, **reducing** the pressure angle.
- **(2 marks)** During the return stroke: $(ds/d\theta + e)$ appears, **increasing** the pressure angle. Hence the designer must check both strokes.
- **(2 marks)** The denominator also changes slightly — for small $e$ the effect is dominated by the numerator change.
- **(1 mark)** Conditions for using offset: when **cam space is constrained** and the outstroke has a high $ds/d\theta$ (i.e., high-speed cam with large lift), the offset reduces the required base circle and produces a more compact cam. The optimum offset is empirically $e \approx (0.25 \text{ to } 0.30) \cdot r_0$.

---

#### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **neat sketch**, derive the expression for the **pressure angle** of a **knife-edge follower** operating on a **circular cam** rotating about an offset center. Discuss the effect of the direction of offset on the pressure angle.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cam with a **flat-faced follower** is to lift a load of **500 N** at a cam speed of **600 rpm**. The cam and follower are made of **steel** ($E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$). If the radius of curvature of the cam at the contact is **50 mm** and the follower face is flat (radius of curvature $\to \infty$), determine the **Hertzian contact stress** and comment on whether the design is safe. (Use line-contact Hertz formula.)

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a):**

The knife-edge follower case is the **limit** of a roller follower as the roller radius $r_r \to 0$ and the offset $e$ remains finite. Starting from the general offset roller formula:

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp e}{s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}$$

and taking $r_r = 0$ (knife-edge, contact point = follower tip), with the additional simplification that the follower is a point sliding on the cam, the geometry reduces to:

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \mp e}{s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}$$

**Effect of direction of offset:**

- **(3 marks for derivation)** When the offset $e$ is in the **same direction** as cam rotation during the outstroke, the term $(ds/d\theta - e)$ reduces $\tan\alpha$, hence pressure angle decreases. During the return stroke, the same direction offset produces $(ds/d\theta + e)$, increasing the pressure angle.
- **(2 marks for sketch description)** A typical sketch shows: the cam rotation center $O$ at origin; follower axis offset to the right by $e$; the knife-edge point at coordinates $(e, s)$; the contact normal drawn at angle $\alpha$ to the vertical follower direction.
- **(2 marks for discussion)** The design lesson: an offset follower is **good for the outstroke** but **bad for the return stroke** — the designer chooses the offset direction to favour the more critical stroke.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Hertzian line-contact stress formula.**

For line contact (cylindrical or equivalent flat-flat geometry):

$$\sigma_c = 0.418 \sqrt{\dfrac{P \cdot E'}{b \cdot R_{eff}}}$$

where:

- $E' = \dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)}$ = effective modulus
- $R_{eff} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$
- $b$ = length of line contact (perpendicular to plane of figure)
- $P$ = normal load

**Step 2 — Compute effective modulus.**

$$E' = \dfrac{210 \times 10^9}{2(1 - 0.09)} = \dfrac{210 \times 10^9}{1.82} = 115.4 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective radius.**

$R_1 = 50$ mm (cam) and $R_2 = \infty$ (flat):

$$R_{eff} = \dfrac{50 \times \infty}{50 + \infty} = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Length of line contact $b$.**

Assume $b = 25$ mm = 0.025 m (typical width of a flat follower face).

**Step 5 — Compute stress.**

$$\sigma_c = 0.418 \sqrt{\dfrac{500 \times 115.4 \times 10^9}{0.025 \times 0.050}}$$

$$= 0.418 \sqrt{\dfrac{5.77 \times 10^{13}}{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$= 0.418 \sqrt{4.616 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$= 0.418 \times 2.149 \times 10^{8}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = 89.8 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on safety.**

For through-hardened steel cam and follower, the allowable Hertzian contact stress is in the range **800 – 1200 MPa** for static loading and **600 – 800 MPa** for fatigue (10⁸ cycles). The computed stress of **89.8 MPa** is well within the limit, so the design is **safe**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B-14 mark type):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 0.418** in the Hertz formula — full 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Mixing line-contact with point-contact** formulas — the coefficient changes to 0.591 for point contact.
> 3. **Ignoring $b$** (length of contact) — must be in the denominator for line contact.
> 4. **Not converting units** to SI (GPa, N, m) before plugging into the formula — common 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Using $E$ instead of $E'$** — the effective modulus $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$ is essential for contact mechanics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Read this section 5 minutes before the exam!**

- ✅ **Pressure angle $\alpha$** = angle between follower direction and common normal at contact. Limits: **30°–45°** for translating, **30°** for oscillating.
- ✅ **Master formula (offset roller):** $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{ds/d\theta \mp e}{s + \sqrt{r_0^2 - e^2}}$. Sign: **minus** if offset is in direction of rotation during outstroke.
- ✅ **Minimum base-circle (SHM, no offset):** $r_0 = \dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta \tan\alpha_p} - \dfrac{h}{2}$.
- ✅ **Minimum base-circle (uniform accel, no offset):** $r_0 = \dfrac{2h}{\beta \tan\alpha_p} - \dfrac{h}{2}$.
- ✅ **Velocity and acceleration:** $v = \omega \cdot ds/d\theta$; $a = \omega^2 \cdot d^2s/d\theta^2$.
- ✅ **Maximum $ds/d\theta$ values:**
  - SHM: $\pi h / (2\beta)$
  - Cycloidal: $2h / \beta$ (at mid-stroke)
  - Uniform accel/retard: $2h / \beta$
- ✅ **Radius of curvature** must be **positive** everywhere; a negative $\rho$ implies a cusp and is **physically unrealizable**.
- ✅ **Hertzian stress** for line contact: $\sigma_c = 0.418 \sqrt{P E' / (b R_{eff})}$; allowable for steel ≈ **800 MPa** static.
- ✅ **Sliding velocity at contact:** $v_s = \omega (r + r_r) \tan\alpha$ — high sliding velocity → high wear.
- ✅ **Eccentricity rule of thumb:** $e / r_0 \le 0.25$–$0.30$ for stable design.
- ✅ **Common student errors to avoid:**
  - Using $\alpha_{max}$ for *both* strokes regardless of direction.
  - Forgetting to check *both* outstroke and return stroke.
  - Confusing **prime-circle radius** $r_p = r_0 + s$ with **base-circle radius** $r_0$.
  - Plugging **roller radius** $r_r$ in the **denominator** instead of **adding it** to the cam radius when computing the roller-center path.
- ✅ **Engineering insight:** Reducing cam size saves material but increases $\alpha$ and contact stress — there is a *trade-off*, and the optimum is found by iteration.
- ✅ **Examination tip:** Always **draw a labelled sketch** showing $O$, $I$, $A$, $r_0$, $s$, $e$, and $\alpha$ — this alone is worth **1–2 marks** in KTU ESE.

> **End of Module 3 — Design Considerations Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes of Failure in Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Causes of Failure in Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **shaft** is a rotating machine element used to transmit power and torque between the prime mover and driven components (e.g., gears, pulleys, sprockets) in a power transmission system. Under service conditions, shafts are subjected to **bending moments** (due to transverse loads from pulleys, gears, etc.), **torsional moments** (due to transmitted power), **axial loads** (in some cases), and **fluctuating dynamic loads**.

**Failure of a shaft** is defined as the condition when the shaft can no longer perform its intended function safely or economically. In design parlance (KTU 2024 Scheme), failure is **not** synonymous with fracture alone — it includes any unacceptable state such as yielding, fatigue cracking, excessive deflection, or wear.

The principal causes of shaft failure are categorized under four canonical loading scenarios:

1. **Failure due to Pure Static Loading** — yielding governed by maximum shear stress or distortion energy theories.
2. **Failure due to Pure Fluctuating (Fatigue) Loading** — endurance governed by Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, or ASME criteria.
3. **Failure due to Combined Static and Fluctuating Loading** — shafts subject to steady torque plus fluctuating bending.
4. **Failure due to Combined Bending and Torsion** — principal stress approach for shafts under multiple simultaneous stresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PCAGT403 explicitly mandates that students must be able to identify the cause of failure, select the appropriate failure theory, and apply the corresponding design equation to compute the safe shaft diameter. This forms the **core assessment area** of the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bicycle axle** held between two wheels. When you pedal, the axle experiences **twisting** (torsion) along its length. When you ride over a bump, the axle experiences **bending** because your weight pushes down between the two wheels. The axle is therefore under **combined bending and torsion** — the most common real-world loading on shafts.

Now imagine a **paper clip**. If you bend it once, it deforms permanently (yielding = static failure). If you bend it back and forth many times, it eventually snaps (fatigue failure) — even though the force you applied each time was well below the yield force. **The number of cycles matters!** This is the heart of fatigue-based shaft design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics Used in Shaft Design:**
> - **Sut (Ultimate Tensile Strength)** — measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**
> - **Syt (Yield Tensile Strength)** — measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**
> - **See (Endurance Limit for completely reversed bending)** — measured in **MPa**
> - **Ses (Endurance Limit for completely reversed shear/torsion)** — measured in **MPa**
> - **Factor of Safety (N or FOS)** — dimensionless, typically **1.5 to 4** for general machinery shafts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Failure Modes — Structured Logic Breakdown

#### (A) Failure due to Pure Static Load
- **Why it happens:** A shaft is loaded once (or a few times) with a constant load exceeding the elastic limit. Relevant for **press-fit shafts, slowly loaded hoisting shafts, or shaft at start-up under a stall torque**.
- **How to handle:** Apply the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST)** for ductile materials or **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST)** for brittle materials. The von Mises / Distortion Energy Theory (DET) gives a more accurate (less conservative) result and is the **industry standard**.

#### (B) Failure due to Pure Fluctuating Load (Fatigue)
- **Why it happens:** Rotating shafts always undergo **stress reversal in bending** — every point on the surface moves from tension to compression every half revolution. Even if the bending moment is constant, the **bending stress is fluctuating**. Cyclic stress eventually nucleates a micro-crack that propagates to fracture.
- **How to handle:** Use the **Soderberg line** (most conservative), **Goodman line** (most commonly used in KTU problems), **Gerber parabola** (less conservative, valid for ductile materials), or the **ASME Elliptical** criterion.

#### (C) Combined Static and Fluctuating Loads
- **Why it happens:** Many shafts carry a **steady torque** (e.g., from a constant-load conveyor) superimposed on a **fluctuating bending moment** (e.g., from a cam-driven pump).
- **How to handle:** Split the stress into a **mean (static) component** and an **alternating (fluctuating) component** and apply the **Soderberg or Goodman line** to ensure neither component exceeds the safe limits.

#### (D) Failure due to Combined Bending and Torsion
- **Why it happens:** Almost every machine shaft experiences bending from overhung loads *and* torsion from power transmission simultaneously.
- **How to handle:** Compute the **equivalent torque (Te)** and **equivalent bending moment (Me)** using Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST) or Distortion Energy Theory (DET), then size the shaft using the bending/torsion formula.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are high-frequency KTU ESE questions. Memorize the formula, the constants, and the unit conventions.**

| # | Failure Theory | Design Equation (for solid circular shaft of diameter **d**) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MSST — Pure Torsion (Static)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^{3}} \le \dfrac{S_{sy}}{N}$ | Static torque on ductile shaft |
| 2 | **MSST — Pure Bending (Static)** | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{32\,M}{\pi\,d^{3}} \le \dfrac{S_{yt}}{N}$ | Static moment, ductile material |
| 3 | **Max Principal Stress Theory (MPST)** | $\sigma_{1} = \dfrac{16}{\pi\,d^{3}}\left(M + \sqrt{M^{2}+T^{2}}\right) \le \dfrac{S_{ut}}{N}$ | Brittle materials under combined loading |
| 4 | **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST) — Combined** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi\,d^{3}}\sqrt{M^{2}+T^{2}} \le \dfrac{S_{sy}}{N}$ | Ductile materials, conservative (most asked) |
| 5 | **Distortion Energy Theory (DET / von Mises) — Combined** | $\sigma_{VM} = \dfrac{16}{\pi\,d^{3}}\sqrt{M^{2} + 0.75\,T^{2}} \le \dfrac{S_{yt}}{N}$ | Ductile materials, less conservative (preferred) |
| 6 | **Soderberg Criterion** | $\dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{16}{\pi\,d^{3}}\left[\dfrac{1}{S_{yt}}\left(\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}}\right) + \dfrac{1}{S_{ee}}\left(\sqrt{M_{a}^{2}+T_{a}^{2}}\right)\right]$ | Combined steady + fluctuating, very safe |
| 7 | **Goodman Criterion** | $\dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{16}{\pi\,d^{3}}\left[\dfrac{1}{S_{ut}}\left(\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}}\right) + \dfrac{1}{S_{ee}}\left(\sqrt{M_{a}^{2}+T_{a}^{2}}\right)\right]$ | Most widely used in KTU problems |
| 8 | **Gerber Parabola** | $\left(\dfrac{T_{a}}{T_{e}}\right)^{2} - 2\left(\dfrac{T_{m}}{T_{e}}\right)\dfrac{S_{ut}}{S_{yt}} + 1 = 0$ (after substituting T) | Ductile, less conservative than Goodman |
| 9 | **ASME Elliptical** | $\left(\dfrac{T_{a}}{T_{e}}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{T_{m}}{S_{yt}}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{2}$ | Best for ductile, near accurate |
| 10 | **Endurance Limit Modifiers (Marin Equation)** | $S_{e} = k_{a}\,k_{b}\,k_{c}\,k_{d}\,k_{e}\,k_{f}\,S_{e}^{\prime}$ | Modify endurance for surface, size, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on Vertical Pipe Symbol:** In the markdown tables above, absolute value has been deliberately rendered as $\sqrt{(\cdots)}$ to avoid breaking the table parser. The expressions $\sqrt{M^{2}+T^{2}}$, $\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}}$, etc., represent the standard **resultant stress** form.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Drive Shafts:** Designed using **Goodman criterion** because they see fully reversed bending (rotation) + steady torsion.
- **Steam Turbine Shafts:** Use **DET with Marin factors** because of high temperature, surface finish, and reliability requirements.
- **Crane Hoist Shafts:** Use **MSST** because they are primarily under static loads (low speed, infrequent cycles).
- **Aircraft Propeller Shafts:** Use **Soderberg** because failure consequences are catastrophic — safety is paramount.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Bending Moment and Equivalent Torque (for MSST)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a circular shaft of diameter **d** subjected to a bending moment **M** and a torque **T** simultaneously. The material is ductile. We wish to replace this combined loading with an **equivalent pure bending (Me)** and an **equivalent pure torque (Te)** such that the maximum shear stress from the equivalent system equals the maximum shear stress from the original system.

**Step 1 — Write the actual stress components.**

The bending stress on the extreme fiber (tension/compression):

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{32\,M}{\pi\,d^{3}}$$

The shear stress due to torsion on the outer surface:

$$\tau = \frac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the principal stresses at the surface element.**

For a 2D stress state with $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$, $\sigma_y = 0$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_{b}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{b}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Maximum Shear Stress Theory.**

According to MSST, failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in the combined state equals the shear stress at yield in a simple tension test:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{1} - \sigma_{2}}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{b}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

Setting this equal to the allowable shear stress $S_{sy}/N$:

$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{b}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}} = \frac{S_{sy}}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

Plug $\sigma_b = 32M/(\pi d^3)$ and $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$:

$$\sqrt{\left(\frac{16\,M}{\pi\,d^{3}}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^{3}}\right)^{2}} = \frac{S_{sy}}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the common term.**

$$\frac{16}{\pi\,d^{3}} \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \frac{S_{sy}}{N}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Equivalent Torque.**

The shear stress in a shaft under pure torque $T_e$ is:

$$\tau = \frac{16\,T_{e}}{\pi\,d^{3}}$$

Equating to the LHS of Step 5:

$$\boxed{\,T_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the Equivalent Bending Moment.**

The bending stress in a shaft under pure moment $M_e$ is:

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{32\,M_{e}}{\pi\,d^{3}}$$

But the MSST equivalent requires the *shear* stress form. Since the original maximum shear stress in the MSST framework is $\sqrt{(\sigma_b/2)^2 + \tau^2}$, and for pure bending a normal stress $\sigma_b$ produces a maximum shear stress of $\sigma_b/2$, we set:

$$\frac{\sigma_b}{2} = \frac{16\,M_{e}}{\pi\,d^{3}} \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma_b = \frac{32\,M}{\pi\,d^{3}}$$

This gives $M_e = M$ for MSST, hence:

$$\boxed{\,M_{e} = M + \dfrac{T}{2} \text{ (MSST) or } M_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + 0.75\,T^{2}} \text{ (DET)}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Soderberg Line Equation

**Problem Setup:** A shaft carries a steady torque $T_m$ and steady moment $T_m$ (mean values) and a fully reversed bending moment $M_a$ and reversed torque $T_a$ (alternating values). We seek a linear failure envelope.

**Step 1 — Decompose the stress.**

For the mean and alternating components, the equivalent stresses are:

$$\sigma_{m} = \frac{32}{\pi\,d^{3}}\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}}, \quad \sigma_{a} = \frac{32}{\pi\,d^{3}}\sqrt{M_{a}^{2}+T_{a}^{2}}$$

Wait — the factor for the equivalent *shear* (which is what Soderberg originally used) is 16, not 32. The original Soderberg form uses:

$$\tau_{m} = \frac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}}, \quad \tau_{a} = \frac{16}{\pi d^{3}}\sqrt{M_{a}^{2}+T_{a}^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the failure criteria as a linear combination.**

Soderberg proposed that the sum of the *fraction* of each failure mode (static yielding + fatigue) must not exceed unity:

$$\frac{\tau_{m}}{S_{sy}/N} + \frac{\tau_{a}}{S_{se}/N} \le 1$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{N\,\tau_{m}}{S_{sy}} + \frac{N\,\tau_{a}}{S_{se}} \le 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions for $\tau_m$ and $\tau_a$.**

$$\frac{16\,N}{\pi\,d^{3}\,S_{sy}}\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}} + \frac{16\,N}{\pi\,d^{3}\,S_{se}}\sqrt{M_{a}^{2}+T_{a}^{2}} \le 1$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the design equation for diameter.**

$$d^{3} \ge \frac{16\,N}{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{S_{sy}}\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}} + \frac{1}{S_{se}}\sqrt{M_{a}^{2}+T_{a}^{2}}\right]$$

**Step 5 — For Goodman (most common in KTU), replace $S_{sy}$ with $S_{ut}$:**

$$\boxed{\,d^{3} \ge \frac{16\,N}{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{S_{ut}}\sqrt{M_{m}^{2}+T_{m}^{2}} + \frac{1}{S_{se}}\sqrt{M_{a}^{2}+T_{a}^{2}}\right]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where the lines come from (graphical view):** The Soderberg line connects $(S_{sy}, 0)$ and $(0, S_{se})$ on a $\sigma_m$ vs $\sigma_a$ plot. The Goodman line connects $(S_{ut}, 0)$ and $(0, S_{se})$. The Gerber parabola is the most accurate among these, and the ASME ellipse is a good engineering compromise.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Failure Theory

| Loading Condition | Material Type | Recommended Theory | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static moment only | Ductile | $\sigma = 32M/(\pi d^3) \le S_{yt}/N$ | High |
| Static torque only | Ductile | $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3) \le S_{sy}/N$ | High |
| Combined static (M, T) | Ductile | MSST: $T_e = \sqrt{M^2+T^2}$ | Very High |
| Combined static (M, T) | Ductile | DET: $M_e = \sqrt{M^2+0.75T^2}$ | High |
| Combined (M, T) | Brittle | MPST | Medium |
| Pure fluctuating bending | Any | Soderberg / Goodman | High |
| Steady torque + fluctuating bending | Ductile | Goodman + mean/alt split | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Shaft Failure Theory Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shaft Loading Identified] --> B{Type of Loading?}
    B -- Static Only --> C{Material Type?}
    C -- Ductile --> D[Use MSST: Te = sqrt M2 + T2]
    C -- Brittle --> E[Use MPST: principal stress]
    B -- Fluctuating / Cyclic --> F{Mean and Alternating Both Present?}
    F -- No, Pure Fluctuating --> G[Use Soderberg or Goodman]
    F -- Yes, Combined Static + Fluctuating --> H[Split into Mean and Alternating Components]
    H --> I[Apply Goodman Criterion: 1/N = sigma_m Sut + sigma_a See]
    I --> J[Solve for Shaft Diameter d]
    G --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K[Add Keyway and Stress Concentration Factor]
    K --> L[Final Shaft Design]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Marin Endurance Limit Modification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Uncorrected Endurance Se prime] --> B[Multiply by Surface Factor ka]
    B --> C[Multiply by Size Factor kb]
    C --> D[Multiply by Load Factor kc]
    D --> E[Multiply by Temperature Factor kd]
    E --> F[Multiply by Reliability Factor ke]
    F --> G[Multiply by Miscellaneous Factor kf]
    G --> H[Corrected Endurance Limit Se]
    H --> I[Use in Goodman or Soderberg Equation]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Physical Loading on a Shaft

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prime Mover: Motor or Engine] --> B[Coupling]
    B --> C[Shaft Under Torsion T]
    C --> D[Gear or Pulley Applying Bending Moment M]
    D --> E[Shaft Transmits Power to Driven Machine]
    C -- Rotates --> F[Stress Reversal in Bending Every Half Revolution]
    F --> G[Fluctuating Bending Stress]
    G --> H[Fatigue Failure Possibility]
    C -- Steady Torque --> I[Static Torsional Stress]
    I --> J[Static Yielding Possibility]
    H --> K[Use Combined Goodman Analysis]
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** The examiner often asks students to **identify the type of failure theory** to apply given a problem statement. The decision flow above is exactly the mental algorithm an examiner expects you to demonstrate in your answer script.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three causes of failure in machine shafts.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Static failure** due to excessive bending or torsional moment exceeding the yield strength of the shaft material.
2. **Fatigue failure** due to fluctuating stresses arising from the rotation of the shaft under a constant transverse load, leading to reversed bending stress at every point on the surface.
3. **Failure due to combined bending and torsion**, which is the most common condition in power transmission shafts and is analyzed using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST) or Distortion Energy Theory (DET).

*(A fourth point may be added: failure due to stress concentration at keyways, fillets, or steps.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define equivalent bending moment and equivalent torque as used in shaft design. State the relations used in the Maximum Shear Stress Theory.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equivalent bending moment (Me):** The fictitious pure bending moment which, when applied alone, would produce the same maximum shear stress as the combined bending and torsion.
- **Equivalent torque (Te):** The fictitious pure torque which, when applied alone, would produce the same maximum shear stress as the combined loading.
- According to **MSST**:

$$T_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}, \quad M_{e} = M + \frac{T}{2}$$

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** A solid circular shaft is subjected to a bending moment of **M = 1.2 kN·m** and a torque of **T = 0.9 kN·m**. The shaft is made of a ductile material with **Sut = 600 MPa** and **Syt = 380 MPa**. Using the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST)** with a factor of safety of **N = 2**, determine the **shaft diameter d**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- M = 1.2 kN·m = $1.2 \times 10^{6}$ N·mm
- T = 0.9 kN·m = $0.9 \times 10^{6}$ N·mm
- $S_{ut} = 600$ N/mm²
- $S_{yt} = 380$ N/mm²
- N = 2
- $S_{sy} \approx 0.577 \times S_{yt} = 0.577 \times 380 = 219.26$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Compute equivalent torque using MSST.**

$$T_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^{6})^{2} + (0.9 \times 10^{6})^{2}}$$

$$T_{e} = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{12} + 0.81 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$T_{e} = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the MSST design equation for diameter.**

$$d^{3} = \frac{16\,N\,T_{e}}{\pi\,S_{sy}} = \frac{16 \times 2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 219.26}$$

$$d^{3} = \frac{48 \times 10^{6}}{688.85} = 69{,}685 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for d.**

$$d = (69{,}685)^{1/3} = 41.18 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** **d ≈ 41.2 mm**, round up to standard size **d = 45 mm**.

---

**(b)** For the shaft in part (a), recompute the diameter using the **Distortion Energy Theory (DET)** and comment on which theory gives a more economical (smaller) shaft.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute equivalent bending moment using DET.**

$$M_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + 0.75\,T^{2}}$$

$$M_{e} = \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^{6})^{2} + 0.75 \times (0.9 \times 10^{6})^{2}}$$

$$M_{e} = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{12} + 0.75 \times 0.81 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$M_{e} = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{12} + 0.6075 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{2.0475 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$M_{e} = 1.4308 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DET design equation.**

$$\frac{32\,M_{e}}{\pi\,d^{3}} = \frac{S_{yt}}{N} \Rightarrow d^{3} = \frac{32\,N\,M_{e}}{\pi\,S_{yt}}$$

$$d^{3} = \frac{32 \times 2 \times 1.4308 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 380} = \frac{91.57 \times 10^{6}}{1193.81}$$

$$d^{3} = 76{,}705 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for d.**

$$d = (76{,}705)^{1/3} = 42.51 \text{ mm}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
The **DET (von Mises) gives a slightly larger diameter (42.5 mm) than the MSST (41.2 mm) in this case**, but DET is theoretically more accurate for ductile materials. MSST is the **more conservative (safer)** choice and is the most commonly used criterion in KTU examinations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert kN·m to N·mm is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. **Always convert: 1 kN·m = 10⁶ N·mm.**
> 2. **Forgetting to cube-root:** Many students write $d = \frac{16NT_e}{\pi S_{sy}}$ directly without solving for $d^3$ first. This is a fatal error. **Always write $d^3$ explicitly.**
> 3. **Using $S_{yt}$ instead of $S_{sy}$ in MSST:** MSST compares shear stresses. If you use $S_{yt}$, you will under-design the shaft. The correct allowable for MSST is $S_{sy} = 0.577 S_{yt}$ (or given in the problem).
> 4. **Mixing MSST formula with DET formula:** Students often use $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ (MSST) and then plug it into the DET equation. Pick one theory per part.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** A shaft is subjected to a **mean bending moment** $M_m = 600$ N·m, an **alternating bending moment** $M_a = 400$ N·m, a **mean torque** $T_m = 0$ N·m, and an **alternating torque** $T_a = 300$ N·m. The shaft material has **Sut = 560 MPa**, **Syt = 400 MPa**, and the corrected endurance limit is **See = 250 MPa**. Using the **Goodman criterion** with factor of safety **N = 2**, find the shaft diameter **d**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given:
- $M_m = 600 \times 10^{3}$ N·mm
- $M_a = 400 \times 10^{3}$ N·mm
- $T_m = 0$
- $T_a = 300 \times 10^{3}$ N·mm
- $S_{ut} = 560$ N/mm²
- $S_{ee} = 250$ N/mm²
- N = 2

**Step 1 — Compute the mean and alternating resultant moments.**

Mean resultant:

$$M_{m,res} = \sqrt{M_{m}^{2} + T_{m}^{2}} = \sqrt{(600 \times 10^{3})^{2} + 0} = 600 \times 10^{3} \text{ N·mm}$$

Alternating resultant:

$$M_{a,res} = \sqrt{M_{a}^{2} + T_{a}^{2}} = \sqrt{(400 \times 10^{3})^{2} + (300 \times 10^{3})^{2}}$$

$$M_{a,res} = \sqrt{160 \times 10^{6} + 90 \times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{250 \times 10^{6}} = 500 \times 10^{3} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Goodman design equation.**

$$d^{3} = \frac{16\,N}{\pi}\left[\frac{M_{m,res}}{S_{ut}} + \frac{M_{a,res}}{S_{ee}}\right]$$

$$d^{3} = \frac{16 \times 2}{\pi}\left[\frac{600 \times 10^{3}}{560} + \frac{500 \times 10^{3}}{250}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term inside the bracket.**

$$\frac{600 \times 10^{3}}{560} = 1071.43 \text{ N·mm/MPa}$$

$$\frac{500 \times 10^{3}}{250} = 2000 \text{ N·mm/MPa}$$

Sum = $3071.43$ N·mm/MPa

**Step 4 — Solve for $d^3$ and then d.**

$$d^{3} = \frac{32}{\pi} \times 3071.43 = 10.185 \times 3071.43 = 31{,}283 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$d = (31{,}283)^{1/3} = 31.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** **d ≈ 31.5 mm**, round up to **d = 32 mm** (standard size).

---

**(b)** If the same shaft is now designed using the **Soderberg criterion**, what diameter would you obtain? Compare with the Goodman result and state which criterion is more conservative.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Soderberg equation (replace $S_{ut}$ with $S_{yt} = 400$ N/mm²).**

$$d^{3} = \frac{16\,N}{\pi}\left[\frac{M_{m,res}}{S_{yt}} + \frac{M_{a,res}}{S_{ee}}\right]$$

$$d^{3} = \frac{32}{\pi}\left[\frac{600 \times 10^{3}}{400} + \frac{500 \times 10^{3}}{250}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term.**

$$\frac{600 \times 10^{3}}{400} = 1500 \text{ N·mm/MPa}$$

$$\frac{500 \times 10^{3}}{250} = 2000 \text{ N·mm/MPa}$$

Sum = 3500 N·mm/MPa

**Step 3 — Solve for d.**

$$d^{3} = \frac{32}{\pi} \times 3500 = 10.185 \times 3500 = 35{,}650 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$d = (35{,}650)^{1/3} = 32.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison (2 marks for the comment).**

| Criterion | Required Diameter |
|---|---|
| Goodman | 31.5 mm |
| Soderberg | 32.9 mm |

**Soderberg gives a larger diameter**, hence it is the **more conservative (safer)** criterion. Soderberg is preferred when **no plastic deformation is permitted at all**, even locally. For general machinery where minor local yielding is acceptable, **Goodman is the more economical** choice and is the most commonly applied criterion in KTU problems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Goodman/Soderberg Problems:**
> 1. **Using $S_{ut}$ for Soderberg:** Soderberg uses $S_{yt}$ in the mean term, not $S_{ut}$. This is the most common error.
> 2. **Forgetting to split M and T into mean and alternating components** before applying the resultant formula.
> 3. **Using the un-corrected endurance limit $S_e^{\prime} = 0.5 S_{ut}$ instead of the corrected $S_{ee}$** given in the problem. Always use the value specified in the problem statement.
> 4. **Plugging T into the bending stress formula or M into the torsion formula:** Remember the formula uses $M_{m,res} = \sqrt{M_m^2 + T_m^2}$ (combined), but the *resultant* is treated as a bending moment in the bending-stress equation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- [ ] **Shaft** = rotating machine element transmitting **torque**; it usually experiences **combined bending + torsion**.
- [ ] **Four causes of failure:** (i) static, (ii) fluctuating/fatigue, (iii) combined static + fluctuating, (iv) combined bending + torsion.
- [ ] **MSST equivalents:** $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ and $M_e = M + T/2$.
- [ ] **DET equivalents:** $M_e = \sqrt{M^2 + 0.75 T^2}$.
- [ ] **Static design equation:** $d^3 = 16 N T_e / (\pi S_{sy})$ for MSST, or $d^3 = 32 N M_e / (\pi S_{yt})$ for DET.
- [ ] **Goodman design equation:** $d^3 = (16 N / \pi) \left[ M_{m,res}/S_{ut} + M_{a,res}/S_{ee} \right]$.
- [ ] **Soderberg design equation:** $d^3 = (16 N / \pi) \left[ M_{m,res}/S_{yt} + M_{a,res}/S_{ee} \right]$.
- [ ] **Conservative ranking (most → least safe):** Soderberg > Goodman > ASME Elliptical > Gerber.
- [ ] **Soderberg is the safest** because it uses $S_{yt}$ (static yield) for the mean term, ensuring no yielding occurs.
- [ ] **Endurance limit correction:** $S_{ee} = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f \times S_e^{\prime}$, where $S_e^{\prime} = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for steels with $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa.
- [ ] **Unit conversions:** 1 kN·m = $10^6$ N·mm; 1 MPa = 1 N/mm²; 1 GPa = 1000 MPa.
- [ ] **For KTU, always round up** the final diameter to the next standard size and mention it explicitly in the answer.
- [ ] **Stress concentration** at keyways, shoulders, and fillets must be considered in the final design (use $K_t$ and $K_{fs}$ from charts).
- [ ] **Rotating shaft under constant transverse load → fully reversed bending** (this is the source of fatigue failure even at constant load).
- [ ] **Ductile materials** (steel, aluminium) → use MSST or DET. **Brittle materials** (cast iron) → use MPST.
- [ ] **Keyway allowance:** For a rough shaft diameter estimate, add 5–7% to the calculated diameter if a keyway is to be cut.
- [ ] **Standard diameters (KTU favourite):** 25, 30, 32, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80 mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:** Always **state the formula before substituting the values** and **show the unit conversion explicitly**. Examiners award method marks generously, but a missing formula or wrong units can cost you 2 marks per question — which adds up quickly in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Crank Method in Davis Steering Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Davis Steering Gear** is an approximate steering gear mechanism used in automobiles where the inner and outer wheels of the vehicle take up different angles while negotiating a turn, satisfying the condition of true rolling. The **Direct Crank Method** is the original classical configuration proposed by Davis in which the two steering arms (crank arms) are positioned on the **same side** of the live axle, projecting forward (or rearward) from the knuckle pins, and connected to a single track arm via a cross link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 3 covers *Steering Mechanisms – Davis Steering Gear (Direct Crank & Reverse Crank methods) and Ackermann Steering Gear*. The Direct Crank Method is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation question** in KTU University Examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are steering a four-wheeler (say a Maruti 800) while taking a U-turn. Have you noticed that the inner front wheel turns through a **larger angle** than the outer front wheel? This is because both wheels must roll without slipping on circles of different radii sharing a common instantaneous centre.

Now think of the front axle as a horizontal bar. The two stub axles (knuckle pins) stick upward from this bar. In the **Direct Crank Method**, Davis bent these stub axles so that the steering arms (short levers) project on **the same side** of the axle — like two parallel handles on the same side of a door. A horizontal rod called the **track link** (or cross link) connects both arms, and is itself connected to the steering lever operated by the driver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Ackermann Condition:**
> For perfect (true) rolling without tyre slip, the steering arms of the inner and outer wheels **must lie on the same straight line** that passes through the rear axle. This geometric condition is called the *Ackermann condition*.

### 1.3 Why "Direct" and Not "Reverse"?

In Davis's original (Direct) arrangement, both arms are on the same side → the geometry becomes simpler but the mechanism **does NOT exactly satisfy Ackermann condition** for all positions of the wheels. To improve this, Davis later proposed the **Reverse Crank Method** where arms project on opposite sides of the live axle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:**
> - Wheelbase of the vehicle = **c** (distance between front and rear axles) — typical passenger car: **2.5 m to 3.0 m**
> - Track width = **b** (distance between the two king pins) — typical: **1.4 m to 1.6 m**
