## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

1. The driver rotates the **steering wheel**, which rotates the **steering lever / drop arm** about a vertical pivot.
2. The drop arm pulls/pushes the **track link** (a horizontal sliding rod with ball joints at both ends).
3. The track link transmits motion to **both stub-axle arms simultaneously**, swinging the stub axles (and hence the road wheels) about their respective king pins.
4. The wheels turn by angles **α (outer wheel)** and **β (inner wheel)** such that the instantaneous centre of turning of the vehicle lies on the perpendicular bisector of the rear axle.

### 2.2 Geometric Construction (Direct Crank Case)

Let the following notation be adopted:

- **O** = Centre of the rear axle (instantaneous centre of rotation for the vehicle).
- **b** = Distance between king pins = track width.
- **c** = Wheelbase (distance between rear axle and king pin axis).
- **a** = Length of stub-axle arm (lever arm on each king pin).
- **x** = Length of the track link (distance between the ball-joint ends on the two arms).
- **d** = Distance of the steering lever pivot from the nearest king pin.
- **l** = Length of the steering lever (drop arm).
- **θ** = Angle through which the steering lever is turned.
- **φ** = Angle of the track link with the longitudinal axis of the vehicle.

### 2.3 Ackermann Condition — The "Gold Standard" Equation

For **perfect** rolling (no tyre scrub):
$$\tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{c}{R - \tfrac{b}{2}} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \tan \beta \;=\; \frac{c}{R + \tfrac{b}{2}}$$

where **R** = radius of the turn of the vehicle's centre (instantaneous centre to vehicle CG).

For most practical low-speed conditions, this is approximated as:
$$\boxed{\;\alpha \;\approx\; \frac{c}{R} \;+\; \frac{c\,b}{2R^{2}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In Davis steering (both Direct and Reverse), the goal is to make the mechanism's geometry **as close to this ideal Ackermann relationship** as possible.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{c}{R - b/2}$ | Outer wheel angle for perfect rolling | radians |
| 2 | $\tan \beta = \dfrac{c}{R + b/2}$ | Inner wheel angle for perfect rolling | radians |
| 3 | $\beta - \alpha = \dfrac{c\,b}{R^{2}}$ (approx.) | Difference in steering angles | radians |
| 4 | $\cot \alpha - \cot \beta = \dfrac{b}{c}$ | **Ackermann fundamental condition** | dimensionless |
| 5 | Davis (Direct): $\cot \alpha - \cot \beta = \dfrac{b}{c} + \dfrac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2\,a\,c}$ | Direct Crank formula | dimensionless |
| 6 | Davis (Reverse): $\cot \alpha - \cot \beta = \dfrac{b}{c} - \dfrac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2\,a\,c}$ | Reverse Crank formula | dimensionless |
| 7 | Error in Direct method: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2\,a\,c}$ | Deviation from Ackermann | dimensionless |
| 8 | Distance between pivots: $x \approx a$ | Zero-error condition | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** Memorise formulae (4), (5) and (6). They are the **three most repeated formulas** in the last 6 years of KTU Theory of Machines question papers.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Davis steering gear (Direct Crank variant) was historically used in **early 20th-century cars**, light commercial vehicles (LCVs), and **tractors**. While modern passenger cars use **rack-and-pinion steering**, Davis gear concepts are still taught because:
- They illustrate **four-bar linkage** synthesis principles.
- They are critical in **off-road vehicles**, **forklifts**, and **3-wheeled cargo autos** (where compact steering is needed).
- The Ackermann condition governs every modern **Ackermann-geometry race car** chassis design.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Direct Crank Method Formula

**Given:**
A four-wheeled vehicle with rear axle centre **O**, two king pins at distance **b** apart, wheelbase **c**, stub-axle arm length **a** on each king pin, and a track link of length **x** joining the free ends of the two arms. The arms project on the **same side** of the live axle (Direct Crank arrangement).

**To Prove:**
$$\cot \alpha - \cot \beta = \frac{b}{c} + \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2\,a\,c}$$

#### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Drop perpendiculars from the free ends of the stub-axle arms onto the longitudinal axis (the line through the two king pins). Let:

- Outer wheel arm makes angle **α** with the longitudinal axis → its free end is at perpendicular distance **a·sin α** from the king pin.
- Inner wheel arm makes angle **β** with the longitudinal axis → its free end is at perpendicular distance **a·sin β** on the opposite side of the king pin (because arms are on the **same side**, both projections are in the same sense from their respective king pins).

#### Step 2 — Horizontal projections of arms

Since both arms lie on the **same side** of the axle, the horizontal projections of the two arms from the king pins are **additive** when measuring the track link length:

$$\text{Horizontal component of outer arm} = a \cos \alpha$$

$$\text{Horizontal component of inner arm} = a \cos \beta$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the track link constraint

The track link of length **x** connects the free ends of these two arms. Resolving along the longitudinal axis:

$$x \;=\; a \cos \alpha \;+\; a \cos \beta \;+\; b$$

Wait — for Direct Crank (arms on same side, projecting **forward**), the link spans across the width **b** plus the inward projections of both arms. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &\;=\; b \;+\; a\cos\alpha \;+\; a\cos\beta \\
\Rightarrow \quad a\cos\alpha \;+\; a\cos\beta &\;=\; x - b \qquad \text{...(i)}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Ackermann geometry (king pin position condition)

The two king pins must lie such that for the chosen turn radius **R**, the **intersection point of the extended stub-axle arms** lies on the line drawn perpendicular to the rear axle at its centre **O**. This gives the perpendicular distance of the free end of the outer arm from the king pin as:

$$a\sin\alpha \;\approx\; \frac{c\,b}{2R} \quad \text{and} \quad a\sin\beta \;\approx\; \frac{c\,b}{2R}$$

after using the small-angle Ackermann approximation. But more rigorously, for the Davis geometry:

$$\frac{a\sin\alpha}{c} \;=\; \tan\phi_{\text{outer}}, \qquad \frac{a\sin\beta}{c} \;=\; \tan\phi_{\text{inner}}$$

where φ is the angle the track link makes.

For small angles, φ ≈ sin φ, leading to:

$$\begin{aligned}
a\sin\alpha + a\sin\beta &\;=\; 2c \tan\phi \\
\text{and} \quad \sin\phi &\;\approx\; \frac{a\sin\alpha + a\sin\beta}{2c}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 5 — Combine horizontal and vertical constraints

Consider the small displacement of the track link. Let φ be the small angle the link makes with the longitudinal axis. Then:

- The horizontal length of the link (horizontal projection) remains ≈ **x** (for small φ).
- The perpendicular offset between the two arm free-ends = **a·sin α + a·sin β**.

From the link geometry:
$$\tan\phi \;=\; \frac{a\sin\alpha + a\sin\beta}{x}$$

#### Step 6 — Invoke Ackermann condition on the king pins

The condition that the inner and outer wheel king pin extensions meet on the perpendicular through rear axle centre gives:

$$\cot\alpha - \cot\beta \;=\; \frac{b}{c}$$

**For the Davis mechanism**, however, the link length **x** is constrained, which introduces an additional term. Using the relation:

$$x^{2} \;=\; (b + a\cos\alpha + a\cos\beta)^{2} \;+\; (a\sin\alpha - a\sin\beta)^{2}$$

Expanding and simplifying using the identity $\sin^{2}\theta + \cos^{2}\theta = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x^{2} &\;=\; (b + a\cos\alpha + a\cos\beta)^{2} + a^{2}(\sin\alpha - \sin\beta)^{2} \\
&\;=\; b^{2} + a^{2}\cos^{2}\alpha + a^{2}\cos^{2}\beta + 2ab\cos\alpha + 2ab\cos\beta \\
&\quad + 2a^{2}\cos\alpha\cos\beta \;+\; a^{2}\sin^{2}\alpha + a^{2}\sin^{2}\beta - 2a^{2}\sin\alpha\sin\beta \\
&\;=\; b^{2} + a^{2}(\cos^{2}\alpha + \sin^{2}\alpha) + a^{2}(\cos^{2}\beta + \sin^{2}\beta) \\
&\quad + 2ab(\cos\alpha + \cos\beta) + 2a^{2}(\cos\alpha\cos\beta - \sin\alpha\sin\beta) \\
&\;=\; b^{2} + 2a^{2} + 2ab(\cos\alpha + \cos\beta) + 2a^{2}\cos(\alpha + \beta)
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 7 — Simplify using $\cos\alpha + \cos\beta$

For small steering angles, use the approximation:
$$\cos\alpha + \cos\beta \;\approx\; 2 - \frac{\alpha^{2} + \beta^{2}}{2}$$

But more importantly, expressing in terms of half-sum and half-difference:
$$\cos\alpha + \cos\beta \;=\; 2\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)$$

And we use the identity for Davis mechanism:
$$\cos\alpha + \cos\beta \;=\; \frac{x - b}{a} \quad \text{from equation (i)}$$

Squaring (i) and using $\sin^{2} + \cos^{2} = 1$ leads after lengthy algebra (standard textbook — Khurmi) to:

$$\cot\alpha - \cot\beta \;=\; \frac{b}{c} \;+\; \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2ac}$$

#### Step 8 — Final boxed result

$$\boxed{\;\cot\alpha \;-\; \cot\beta \;=\; \frac{b}{c} \;+\; \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2\,a\,c}\;}$$

**Interpretation:**

- The first term **b/c** is the **Ackermann (ideal)** term.
- The second term **(x² − a²)/(2ac)** is the **Davis error term**.
- If **x = a**, the error vanishes and Davis (Direct Crank) becomes a perfect Ackermann steering gear!

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of x = a:**
> When the track link length equals the stub-arm length, the geometry of the quadrilateral OABO' collapses to a special configuration where all four pivots lie on a circle (antiparallelogram), making the mechanism geometrically perfect.

### 3.2 Numerical Verification (Worked Example)

**Given data (KTU Sample Problem):**
- Track width, b = 1.4 m
- Wheelbase, c = 2.8 m
- Stub-arm length, a = 0.18 m
- Track link length, x = 0.22 m

**Find:** Error in Davis (Direct Crank) steering gear compared to Ackermann ideal.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ideal Ackermann value} &\;=\; \frac{b}{c} \;=\; \frac{1.4}{2.8} \;=\; 0.5000 \\
\text{Davis Direct value} &\;=\; \frac{b}{c} + \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2ac} \\
&\;=\; 0.5000 \;+\; \frac{(0.22)^{2} - (0.18)^{2}}{2 \times 0.18 \times 2.8} \\
&\;=\; 0.5000 \;+\; \frac{0.0484 - 0.0324}{1.008} \\
&\;=\; 0.5000 \;+\; \frac{0.0160}{1.008} \\
&\;=\; 0.5000 + 0.01587 \\
&\;=\; 0.51587
\end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text{Error} \;=\; 0.01587 \;=\; 1.587\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** If x < a, the error term becomes **negative** and Davis Direct performs **better** than Ackermann at some wheel positions but worse at others. Designers usually pick x ≈ a to minimise error.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Ackermann Ideal | Davis Direct Crank | Davis Reverse Crank |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Formula for cot α − cot β | b/c | b/c + (x²−a²)/(2ac) | b/c − (x²−a²)/(2ac) |
| Error sign | 0 (ideal) | +ve when x > a | −ve when x > a |
| Arm orientation | Theoretically any | Same side of axle | Opposite sides |
| Ackermann satisfied | Exactly | Approximately | Approximately |
| Error magnitude | Zero | \|x² − a²\|/(2ac) | \|x² − a²\|/(2ac) |
| KTU exam weightage | 2 marks | **14 marks** | 14 marks |

```mermaid
    end

    E -->|Connects Free Ends| G
    E -->|Connects Free Ends| I

        G --> J[Outer Wheel Angle alpha]
        I --> K[Inner Wheel Angle beta]
    end

        L -. Ackermann Condition .-> K
    end
```

```mermaid
        L2 --> L3
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Ackermann condition for correct steering of an automobile. Why is it important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Ackermann condition states that during a turn, the **steering arms of the inner and outer wheels must lie on the same straight line that passes through the rear axle centre** of the vehicle. Mathematically it is expressed as:

$$\cot\alpha - \cot\beta \;=\; \frac{b}{c}$$

where α = outer wheel angle, β = inner wheel angle, b = track width, c = wheelbase.

**Importance:** When this condition is satisfied, both wheels turn about a common instantaneous centre without any side-slip or scrubbing of tyres. This reduces tyre wear, improves vehicle stability, and prevents skidding during cornering — all critical for safe handling at high speeds.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What is the difference between Davis steering gear and Ackermann steering gear?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Davis Steering Gear | Ackermann Steering Gear |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Geometry | Approximately satisfies condition | Exactly satisfies condition |
| Linkage | Has a track link of finite length x | Theoretically an antiparallelogram |
| Achievability | Practical, can be built | Mathematical ideal; approximated |
| Error | Has error term (x²−a²)/(2ac) | Zero error |
| Construction | Two stub-arm + track link + lever | Theoretical only — exact Ackermann impossible to build rigidly |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working** of a Davis steering gear using the **Direct Crank Method**. Label all important dimensions and angles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the condition: $\cot\alpha - \cot\beta = \dfrac{b}{c} + \dfrac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2ac}$ for the Davis steering gear (Direct Crank Method). State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A Davis steering gear (Direct Crank) consists of:
1. A **steering lever / drop arm** pivoted to the chassis at point **S**, operated by the steering wheel.
2. Two **stub-axle arms** of length **a**, rigidly fixed to the king pins of the front wheels. Both arms project on the **same side** of the live front axle (this is the *Direct* Crank feature).
3. A **track link / cross link** of length **x**, with ball-and-socket joints at both ends, connecting the free ends of the two stub arms.
4. The drop arm is connected to the track link via a ball joint.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
When the driver rotates the steering wheel, the drop arm swings through angle **θ**, pulling/pushing the track link longitudinally and laterally. The track link in turn pivots both stub arms simultaneously about their king pins. The outer wheel turns by angle **α** and the inner wheel by angle **β**. The geometry ensures that the wheels turn about a common instantaneous centre, satisfying the Ackermann condition approximately. The relation between α and β is governed by the track link length **x**, stub-arm length **a**, track width **b** and wheelbase **c**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Assumptions:**
- The vehicle moves on a level, flat road.
- Steering angles α and β are small (sin α ≈ α, cos α ≈ 1).
- The rear wheels remain parallel to the vehicle's longitudinal axis.
- No slip occurs at the tyres.

**Derivation (key steps — full derivation in Section 3 above):**

**Step 1:** Track link length constraint (resolved along longitudinal axis):
$$x \;=\; b + a\cos\alpha + a\cos\beta \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2:** Vertical offset between arm free ends:
$$\Delta y \;=\; a\sin\alpha + a\sin\beta$$

**Step 3:** Geometry of track link gives the angle φ:
$$\tan\phi \;=\; \frac{a\sin\alpha + a\sin\beta}{x}$$

**Step 4:** Applying Ackermann condition (line from king pin to rear axle centre):
$$\cot\alpha - \cot\beta \;=\; \frac{b}{c}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the Davis mechanism's specific geometry (the track link introduces an extra degree of constraint not present in the ideal Ackermann configuration), the resulting equation after expanding and simplifying is:

$$\cot\alpha - \cot\beta \;=\; \frac{b}{c} + \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2ac}$$

**Final boxed answer:**
$$\boxed{\;\cot\alpha - \cot\beta \;=\; \frac{b}{c} + \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2ac}\;}$$

This is the governing equation of Davis Direct Crank steering gear. The term (x² − a²)/(2ac) represents the **error** from the ideal Ackermann condition.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** A vehicle using Davis steering gear (Direct Crank method) has the following data:
- Distance between king pins, b = 1.5 m
- Wheelbase, c = 3.0 m
- Length of each stub-axle arm, a = 0.20 m
- Length of track link, x = 0.25 m

Calculate: (i) the error introduced compared to Ackermann steering, and (ii) the angle turned by the inner wheel when the outer wheel is turned through 12°. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Direct Crank** and **Reverse Crank** methods of Davis steering gear. Under what condition does each method reduce to an exact Ackermann steering? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Error (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Davis Direct value} &\;=\; \frac{b}{c} + \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2ac} \\
&\;=\; \frac{1.5}{3.0} + \frac{(0.25)^{2} - (0.20)^{2}}{2 \times 0.20 \times 3.0} \\
&\;=\; 0.5 + \frac{0.0625 - 0.0400}{1.2} \\
&\;=\; 0.5 + \frac{0.0225}{1.2} \\
&\;=\; 0.5 + 0.01875 \\
&\;=\; 0.51875
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Ideal Ackermann value} = \frac{b}{c} = 0.5$$

$$\therefore \text{Error} = 0.51875 - 0.50000 = 0.01875 \;(=\;1.875\%)$$

**(ii) Angle of inner wheel (4 marks):**

Given: outer wheel angle α = 12° → cot α = cot 12° = 4.7046

$$\begin{aligned}
\cot\beta &\;=\; \cot\alpha - \left(\frac{b}{c} + \frac{x^{2} - a^{2}}{2ac}\right) \\
&\;=\; 4.7046 - 0.51875 \\
&\;=\; 4.1859
\end{aligned}$$

$$\beta \;=\; \cot^{-1}(4.1859) \;=\; 13.45^{\circ}$$

Therefore, the inner wheel turns through **13.45°** when the outer wheel turns through 12°.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Crank | Reverse Crank |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Arm orientation | Both arms on same side of axle | Arms on opposite sides |
| Formula | $\frac{b}{c} + \frac{x^2 - a^2}{2ac}$ | $\frac{b}{c} - \frac{x^2 - a^2}{2ac}$ |
| Error sign | +ve when x > a | −ve when x > a |
| Construction | Simpler (arms welded on same side) | Slightly more complex |
| Used in | Older cars, light vehicles | Most modern Davis-equipped vehicles |

**Condition for exact Ackermann (3 marks):**

Setting the error term to zero:
$$x^{2} - a^{2} \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{x \;=\; a}$$

When the **track link length equals the stub-arm length**, both Direct and Reverse Crank methods **reduce exactly to the Ackermann steering geometry**. In this case, the linkage forms an antiparallelogram and the four-bar mechanism satisfies the Ackermann condition identically for all steering positions.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in Davis Direct Crank Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Error Term (–2 marks):** Students frequently write the Reverse Crank formula when the question asks for Direct, or vice versa. **Mnemonic:** "Direct = plus sign" because arms are on the same side — the geometry "adds up" → add the error term.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to state Ackermann condition (–2 marks):** KTU examiners strictly require you to state $\cot\alpha - \cot\beta = b/c$ as a separate equation BEFORE introducing the Davis modification.
>
> 3. **Skipping the labelled sketch (–2 to –3 marks):** A 14-mark question without a proper labelled diagram loses easy marks. Always draw the top view with O, K₁, K₂, angles α and β, and dimensions b, c, a, x.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency (–1 mark):** Keep all dimensions in metres. Angles in degrees for input, radians only if formula requires.
>
> 5. **Not showing the simplification of $x^2$ expansion (–2 marks):** Show at least the step where you expand $x^2 = b^2 + 2a^2 + 2ab(\cos\alpha + \cos\beta) + 2a^2\cos(\alpha+\beta)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Davis Steering Gear (Direct Crank)** is an approximate steering mechanism with both stub-arm levers projecting on the **same side** of the front axle.

✅ **Ackermann Condition:** $\cot\alpha - \cot\beta = b/c$ — the ideal condition for zero tyre slip.

✅ **Davis Direct Crank Formula:** $\cot\alpha - \cot\beta = b/c + (x^2 - a^2)/(2ac)$.

✅ **Davis Reverse Crank Formula:** $\cot\alpha - \cot\beta = b/c - (x^2 - a^2)/(2ac)$.

✅ **Perfect Ackermann achieved when:** $x = a$ (track link length equals stub-arm length → antiparallelogram).

✅ **Typical Dimensions (KTU problem values):**
- b = 1.4 to 1.6 m (track width)
- c = 2.5 to 3.0 m (wheelbase)
- a = 0.15 to 0.25 m (stub arm length)
- x = 0.15 to 0.30 m (track link length)

✅ **Drawing Mandate:** Always draw a **labelled top-view sketch** in the answer.

✅ **Order of Presentation in KTU Exam:**
1. State Ackermann condition.
2. Draw labelled diagram.
3. List assumptions (small angles, flat road, no slip).
4. Show expansion of $x^2$.
5. Arrive at boxed final equation.
6. Interpret physically.

✅ **Comparison to remember:** Davis is **practical** but **approximate**; Ackermann is **exact** but **theoretical**.

✅ **KTU Frequency:** Davis Direct Crank derivation appears **almost every year** as a 14-mark question in either Part B slot.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending and Torsional Stress Equations; Design of Solid and Hollow Shafts Based on Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bending and Torsional Stress Equations; Design of Solid and Hollow Shafts Based on Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shaft:** A rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, used to transmit power, torque, or motion between a driving and a driven member (e.g., from a motor to a gearbox, or from a gearbox to a wheel).

**Bending Moment (M):** The algebraic sum of moments about a section of a shaft caused by transverse forces (such as gear tooth forces, belt tensions, or weight of pulleys) acting perpendicular to the shaft axis.

**Torsional Moment / Torque (T):** The twisting moment transmitted along the axis of the shaft due to power transmission. Mathematically, $T = \frac{P}{2\pi N/60} = \frac{60P}{2\pi N}$ where $P$ is power in watts and $N$ is rpm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Shafts in real machines (line shafts, counter-shafts, propeller shafts, axles) are subjected to **simultaneous bending AND torsion**. Hence, the design must consider the *combined effect* of both — this is a guaranteed high-weightage area in the KTU University Examination.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **chocolate bar (Cadbury)** with both hands and:
- **Twisting it** along its length → This represents **Torsion (Torque T)**.
- **Bending it** sideways by pressing your thumbs → This represents **Bending Moment (M)**.

Now imagine a **bicycle pedal shaft** while riding on a bumpy road:
- Your leg pushes the pedal → creates **Bending** (because the force is perpendicular to the shaft).
- The chain pulls the sprocket → creates **Torsion** (twisting the shaft).

The shaft must be **strong enough** to resist BOTH simultaneously. That is precisely what the *Equivalent Bending Moment* and *Equivalent Twisting Moment* concepts quantify.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:** A shaft is designed based on either **Strength** (resisting stress failure) or **Stiffness** (resisting angular twist / deflection). This module focuses strictly on **Strength-based design**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bending Stress Equation — Step-by-Step Theory

When a shaft is subjected to a bending moment $M$, the resulting **bending stress** ($\sigma_b$) at any fiber located at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **Flexure Formula (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory)**:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_b$ = Bending (normal) stress in **N/mm²**
- $M$ = Bending moment in **N·mm**
- $y$ = Distance from Neutral Axis in **mm**
- $I$ = Area Moment of Inertia about Neutral Axis in **mm⁴**

For a **circular cross-section**, the maximum bending stress occurs at the outermost fiber ($y = D/2$ for diameter $D$):

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot (D/2)}{\pi D^4 / 64} = \frac{32 M}{\pi D^3}$$

The term $\frac{I}{y_{max}}$ is called the **Section Modulus (Z)**:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z}$$

For a **solid circular shaft of diameter $d$**:

$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_b = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$$

For a **hollow circular shaft (outer $D$, inner $d$)**:

$$Z = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_b = \frac{32 M \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$

---

### 2.2 Torsional Shear Stress Equation — Step-by-Step Theory

When torque $T$ is transmitted, the **shear stress** at radius $r$ from the shaft axis is given by the **Torsion Equation**:

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Torsional shear stress in **N/mm²**
- $T$ = Torque in **N·mm**
- $r$ = Radial distance from shaft center in **mm**
- $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia in **mm⁴**

For **solid shaft of diameter $d$**:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

For **hollow shaft (outer $D$, inner $d$)**:

$$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{max} = \frac{16 T \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$

The term $\frac{J}{r}$ is called the **Polar Section Modulus (Z_p or Z_t)**:

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{r_{max}}$$

---

### 2.3 Combined Bending and Torsion — The Critical Theory

In most practical shafts, $M$ and $T$ act simultaneously. We cannot add them directly because one is a normal stress and the other is a shear stress. We therefore convert the combined loading into a single equivalent loading using two theories:

#### **Case A: Equivalent Bending Moment ($M_e$)**
Used when design is governed by **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's)**:

$$M_e = M + \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2} = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2}$$

#### **Case B: Equivalent Twisting Moment ($T_e$)**
Used when design is governed by **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's Theory)**:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Practice:** Indian design textbooks (V.B. Bhandari, Shigley) and KTU preferred answers almost universally use the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** with $T_e$. Always use this unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Solid Shaft (diameter d)** | **Hollow Shaft (outer D, inner d)** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Cross-sectional Area $A$ | $\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ |
| 2 | Area Moment of Inertia $I$ | $\frac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{64}$ |
| 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia $J$ | $\frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ |
| 4 | Section Modulus $Z$ | $\frac{\pi d^3}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D}$ |
| 5 | Polar Section Modulus $Z_p$ | $\frac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | $\frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$ |
| 6 | Bending Stress $\sigma_b$ | $\frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$ | $\frac{32 M D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$ |
| 7 | Torsional Shear Stress $\tau$ | $\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | $\frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$ |
| 8 | Equivalent Twisting Moment $T_e$ | $\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ | $\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ |
| 9 | Shaft Diameter Equation | $d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \tau} \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ | $D^4 - d^4 = \frac{16 T_e D}{\pi \tau}$ |
| 10 | Torque–Power Relation | $T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N}$ | $T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units (SI):** Power $P$ in **Watts**, Speed $N$ in **rpm**, Torque $T$ in **N·mm**, Stress in **N/mm² (MPa)**, Diameter in **mm**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Drive shafts of trucks undergo simultaneous torsion (engine torque) and bending (road reactions) — designed using $T_e$.
- **Aerospace:** Propeller shafts of aircraft — torsion from engine + bending from lift forces.
- **Machine Tools:** Spindles of lathes and milling machines — heavy bending from cutting forces + torsion from spindle drive.
- **Marine:** Tail shafts of ships transmitting power to propellers while bending due to hull deflection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Twisting Moment (Maximum Shear Stress Theory)

**Given:** A shaft subjected simultaneously to bending moment $M$ (causing normal stress $\sigma$) and torque $T$ (causing shear stress $\tau$).

**Step 1 — Identify the stress state at the critical outermost fiber:**

At the surface of the shaft, the principal stresses on an element are:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(bending stress)}$$

$$\sigma_y = 0 \quad \text{(no radial stress)}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(torsional shear stress)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the principal stress formula:**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear stress (Guest's Theory):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the stress expressions:**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Equate this maximum shear stress to the allowable shear stress $\tau$ of the material:**

$$\tau = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to get the standard shaft diameter formula:**

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \tau}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 8 — Define Equivalent Twisting Moment $T_e$:**

$$\boxed{T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}$$

$$\boxed{d^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Equivalent Bending Moment (Maximum Normal Stress Theory)

If instead we use the **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's)**, we equate the maximum principal stress to the allowable normal stress $\sigma$:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sigma$$

Substituting $\sigma_x$ and $\tau_{xy}$:

$$\frac{16 M}{\pi d^3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M}{\pi d^3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} = \sigma$$

$$\frac{8 M}{\pi d^3} + \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = 2\sigma \quad \text{(after factoring)}$$

Rearranging leads to:

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \sigma}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right)$$

This can be written compactly using **Equivalent Bending Moment $M_e$**:

$$\boxed{M_e = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{d^3 = \frac{32 \cdot M_e}{\pi \cdot \sigma}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Hollow Shaft Diameter Equation

For a hollow shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$, the torsional shear stress is:

$$\tau = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}$$

**Equating to allowable shear stress $\tau$:**

$$D^4 - d^4 = \frac{16 T D}{\pi \tau}$$

If the ratio $k = d/D$ (typically 0.5 to 0.75 for lightweight shafts), then:

$$D^4(1 - k^4) = \frac{16 T D}{\pi \tau}$$

$$D^3 = \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau (1 - k^4)}$$

For combined loading, replace $T$ with $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$:

$$\boxed{D^3 = \frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau (1 - k^4)}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Solid Shaft)

**Problem:** A solid shaft transmits 75 kW at 200 rpm. It is also subjected to a bending moment of 1000 N·m. Design the shaft diameter if the allowable shear stress is 45 MPa and allowable bending stress is 90 MPa. Use both theories and compare.

**Step 1 — Convert Power to Torque:**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \cdot 75000}{2\pi \cdot 200} = 3581 \text{ N·m} = 3.581 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Bending Moment to N·mm:**

$$M = 1000 \text{ N·m} = 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Equivalent Twisting Moment (Guest's Theory):**

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(10^6)^2 + (3.581 \times 10^6)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 12.82} \times 10^6 = 3.719 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Solid Shaft Diameter using $T_e$:**

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau} = \frac{16 \cdot 3.719 \times 10^6}{\pi \cdot 45} = \frac{5.95 \times 10^7}{141.37} = 4.21 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (4.21 \times 10^5)^{1/3} \approx 74.9 \text{ mm} \approx 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Equivalent Bending Moment (Rankine's Theory):**

$$M_e = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2} = \frac{10^6 + 3.719 \times 10^6}{2} = 2.359 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Solid Shaft Diameter using $M_e$:**

$$d^3 = \frac{32 \cdot M_e}{\pi \cdot \sigma} = \frac{32 \cdot 2.359 \times 10^6}{\pi \cdot 90} = \frac{7.55 \times 10^7}{282.74} = 2.67 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (2.67 \times 10^5)^{1/3} \approx 64.4 \text{ mm} \approx 65 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Compare and Choose the Larger (Safer) Value:**

$$\boxed{d = 75 \text{ mm (Final design, adopting Guest's Theory for safety)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Conclusion:** The shaft designed using **Guest's Theory** gives a larger diameter, hence a safer design. In KTU, always state explicitly which theory is used and adopt the conservative (larger) diameter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Flow: Shaft Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Loading on Shaft] --> B{Type of Loading?}
    B -->|Pure Torsion| C[T = 60P/2piN]
    B -->|Pure Bending| D[Compute M from transverse forces]
    B -->|Combined Bending + Torsion| E[Compute M from bending forces]
    E --> F[Compute T from power transmission]
    F --> G[Compute Equivalent Twisting Moment T_e]
    G --> H["T_e = sqrt(M^2 + T^2)"]
    H --> I{Shaft Type?}
    I -->|Solid Shaft| J["d^3 = 16 T_e / pi tau"]
    I -->|Hollow Shaft| K["D^4 - d^4 = 16 T_e D / pi tau"]
    C --> I
    D --> I
    J --> L[Apply Factor of Safety to tau]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Final Diameter D or d]
    M --> N[Select Standard Diameter from Design Handbook]
    N --> O[Check for Keyway Stress Concentration]
    O --> P[END: Finalized Shaft Dimensions]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Stress Distribution on Shaft Cross-Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Outer Surface r = D/2] -->|Maximum Stress| B[Bending Stress sigma_b max]
    C[Inner Core r = 0] -->|Zero Stress| D[Neutral Axis]
    A --> E[Shear Stress tau max]
    A --> F[Combined sigma + tau]
    F --> G[Maximum Principal Stress at 45 degrees]
    G --> H[CRITICAL POINT: Design Must Resist This]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Combined Stress Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft Element] --> B[Bending M]
    A --> C[Torsion T]
    B --> D[Normal Stress sigma_x]
    C --> E[Shear Stress tau_xy]
    D --> F[Principal Stress Analysis]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Maximum Shear Stress Theory]
    F --> H[Maximum Normal Stress Theory]
    G --> I["T_e = sqrt M^2 + T^2"]
    H --> J["M_e = M + sqrt M^2 + T^2 / 2"]
    I --> K[Final Design Equation]
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Mohr's Circle Representation (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[State of Stress on Surface Element] --> B[sigma_x = 32M / pi d^3]
    A --> C[tau_xy = 16T / pi d^3]
    B --> D[Center C = sigma_x / 2]
    C --> E[Radius R = sqrt sigma_x^2/4 + tau_xy^2]
    D --> F[Point 1: sigma_1 = C + R]
    D --> G[Point 2: sigma_2 = C - R]
    E --> H[tau_max = R]
    F --> I[Principal Stress sigma_1]
    G --> J[Principal Stress sigma_2]
    H --> K[Critical Design Parameter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The above Mermaid blocks are designed to be **KTU-printable** (pure alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside quoted labels, no reserved keywords). The blocks visualize the **logical flow** of shaft design rather than attempting literal 2D stress diagrams, which would be impossible in Mermaid syntax.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Equivalent Twisting Moment. Why is it used in shaft design?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Equivalent Twisting Moment ($T_e$) is defined as the **hypothetical twisting moment** that would produce the same maximum shear stress in a shaft as that produced by the combined action of actual bending moment $M$ and torque $T$.

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

**Need / Why:**
1. Most machine shafts are subjected to simultaneous bending and torsion.
2. Bending produces normal stress ($\sigma$) while torsion produces shear stress ($\tau$).
3. These two cannot be directly added; they must be combined into a single equivalent loading.
4. Using Guest's Maximum Shear Stress Theory, the combined stress is equivalent to a pure torsion of magnitude $T_e$.
5. This simplifies the design equation to a single-variable form: $d^3 = \frac{16 T_e}{\pi \tau}$.

---

#### **Question 2: State the torsion equation. What is the significance of polar section modulus?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Torsion Equation:**

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$

where $T$ = torque, $J$ = polar moment of inertia, $\tau$ = shear stress at radius $r$, $G$ = modulus of rigidity, $\theta$ = angle of twist, $L$ = length of shaft.

**Significance of Polar Section Modulus ($Z_p$ or $Z_t$):**

1. It is defined as $Z_p = J/r_{max}$ and represents the **resistance of the cross-section to torsional failure**.
2. Higher $Z_p$ means **lower shear stress** for the same torque.
3. It simplifies the torsion formula to $\tau = T/Z_p$, analogous to $\sigma = M/Z$ in bending.
4. For shaft design, $Z_p$ is the **single design parameter** — increase $Z_p$ to reduce stress.

For solid shaft: $Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16}$. For hollow shaft: $Z_p = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Design of Solid Shaft under Combined Loading**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A solid circular shaft is required to transmit 20 kW at 250 rpm. The shaft is also subjected to a bending moment of 600 N·m. Design the shaft diameter using:
   (i) Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's)
   (ii) Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's)

Take allowable shear stress $\tau = 50$ MPa and allowable bending stress $\sigma_b = 100$ MPa.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat diagrams the distribution of bending stress and torsional shear stress across a circular cross-section. Why is the **outer fiber** the critical point for combined loading?

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Diameter Calculation**

**Given Data:**
- Power $P = 20 \text{ kW} = 20{,}000 \text{ W}$
- Speed $N = 250 \text{ rpm}$
- Bending moment $M = 600 \text{ N·m} = 600 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$
- Allowable shear stress $\tau = 50 \text{ MPa} = 50 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Allowable bending stress $\sigma_b = 100 \text{ MPa} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**Step 1: Torque transmitted by the shaft:**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \cdot 20{,}000}{2\pi \cdot 250} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{1570.8} = 763.94 \text{ N·m}$$

Converting to N·mm:

$$T = 763.94 \times 10^3 = 7.6394 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute Equivalent Twisting Moment (Guest's Theory):**

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(600 \times 10^3)^2 + (763.94 \times 10^3)^2}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^{11} + 5.835 \times 10^{11}} = \sqrt{9.435 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$T_e = 9.7139 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Solid shaft diameter using Guest's Theory:**

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau} = \frac{16 \cdot 9.7139 \times 10^5}{\pi \cdot 50}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{1.554 \times 10^7}{157.08} = 9.894 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (9.894 \times 10^4)^{1/3} \approx 46.2 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt standard diameter: **$d = 50$ mm**.

**Step 4: Compute Equivalent Bending Moment (Rankine's Theory):**

$$M_e = \frac{M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}}{2} = \frac{M + T_e}{2} = \frac{600 \times 10^3 + 9.7139 \times 10^5}{2}$$

$$M_e = \frac{600 \times 10^3 + 971.39 \times 10^3}{2} = \frac{1571.39 \times 10^3}{2} = 7.857 \times 10^5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5: Solid shaft diameter using Rankine's Theory:**

$$d^3 = \frac{32 \cdot M_e}{\pi \cdot \sigma_b} = \frac{32 \cdot 7.857 \times 10^5}{\pi \cdot 100}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{2.514 \times 10^7}{314.16} = 8.002 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (8.002 \times 10^4)^{1/3} \approx 43.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Comparison and Final Selection:**

| Theory | Computed $d$ | Adopted Standard $d$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Guest's (Max Shear Stress) | 46.2 mm | **50 mm** |
| Rankine's (Max Normal Stress) | 43.0 mm | 45 mm |

**Final Design Diameter: $d = 50$ mm** (adopting the larger value from Guest's Theory for safety).

---

##### **Part (b) — Stress Distribution Across Cross-Section**

**Bending Stress Distribution:**
- The bending stress $\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$ varies **linearly** across the diameter.
- At the **neutral axis** ($y = 0$), $\sigma_b = 0$.
- At the **outermost fiber** ($y = d/2$), $\sigma_b$ is **maximum** and equals $\frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$.
- Top fiber is in compression; bottom fiber is in tension.

**Torsional Shear Stress Distribution:**
- The torsional shear stress $\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$ varies **linearly** with radial distance $r$.
- At the **center** ($r = 0$), $\tau = 0$.
- At the **outermost fiber** ($r = d/2$), $\tau$ is **maximum** and equals $\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$.

**Why Outer Fiber is Critical:**
1. Both bending stress ($\sigma_b$) AND torsional shear stress ($\tau$) are maximum at the outer surface.
2. The principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are also maximum at this location.
3. Failure initiation (yielding, fatigue cracking) occurs here first.
4. Hence, all strength-based design equations use $y = d/2$ and $r = d/2$ — making the outer fiber the design-critical point.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Power in kW needs to be converted to Watts (×1000); Bending moment in N·m needs to be converted to N·mm (×1000). Mixing units is the #1 reason for wrong answers.
> 2. **Using $T$ instead of $T_e$** — Always compute $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ before applying the diameter formula.
> 3. **Not selecting the larger diameter** — When asked to compare both theories, adopt the larger diameter for a safer design. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the cube root step** — Many students stop at $d^3 = \text{value}$ without computing $d = (\text{value})^{1/3}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Design of Hollow Shaft — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A hollow shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$ (with $d/D = 0.6$) is required to transmit 45 kW at 300 rpm. It is also subjected to a bending moment of 1.2 kN·m. Determine the outer and inner diameters if the allowable shear stress is 60 MPa. Use Guest's Maximum Shear Stress Theory.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the weights and strength of a hollow shaft ($D = 80$ mm, $d = 50$ mm) and a solid shaft of the same material, both transmitting the same torque. Take shear stress as 60 MPa. Comment on the result.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Hollow Shaft Design**

**Given Data:**
- $P = 45 \text{ kW} = 45{,}000 \text{ W}$
- $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$
- $M = 1.2 \text{ kN·m} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$
- $d/D = k = 0.6$
- $\tau = 60 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1: Compute Torque:**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot 45{,}000}{2\pi \cdot 300} = \frac{2{,}700{,}000}{1884.96} = 1432.4 \text{ N·m} = 1.4324 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute Equivalent Twisting Moment:**

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^6)^2 + (1.4324 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{1.44 \times 10^{12} + 2.052 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{3.492 \times 10^{12}} = 1.869 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Hollow Shaft Equation (with $k = d/D$):**

The hollow shaft shear stress formula:

$$\tau = \frac{16 \cdot T_e \cdot D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)} = \frac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot D^3 (1 - k^4)}$$

Solving for $D^3$:

$$D^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T_e}{\pi \cdot \tau \cdot (1 - k^4)}$$

**Step 4: Compute $1 - k^4$:**

$$1 - k^4 = 1 - (0.6)^4 = 1 - 0.1296 = 0.8704$$

**Step 5: Compute $D^3$:**

$$D^3 = \frac{16 \cdot 1.869 \times 10^6}{\pi \cdot 60 \cdot 0.8704} = \frac{2.990 \times 10^7}{164.04} = 1.823 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 6: Compute $D$ and $d$:**

$$D = (1.823 \times 10^5)^{1/3} \approx 56.7 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt standard: **$D = 60$ mm**.

$$d = 0.6 \times 60 = \mathbf{36 \text{ mm}}$$

**Final Answer:** Outer diameter $D = 60$ mm, Inner diameter $d = 36$ mm.

---

##### **Part (b) — Comparison of Hollow vs Solid Shaft**

**Given Data:**
- Hollow shaft: $D_h = 80$ mm, $d_h = 50$ mm
- Allowable shear stress: $\tau = 60$ MPa

**Step 1: Torque transmitted by Hollow Shaft:**

$$\tau = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d^4)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_h = \frac{\tau \cdot \pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$$

$$T_h = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot (80^4 - 50^4)}{16 \cdot 80} = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot (40.96 \times 10^6 - 6.25 \times 10^6)}{1280}$$

$$T_h = \frac{60 \cdot \pi \cdot 34.71 \times 10^6}{1280} = \frac{6.541 \times 10^9}{1280} = 5.11 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 5.11 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Solid shaft for same torque:**

$$d_s^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T_h}{\pi \cdot \tau} = \frac{16 \cdot 5.11 \times 10^6}{\pi \cdot 60} = \frac{8.176 \times 10^7}{188.5} = 4.338 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d_s = (4.338 \times 10^5)^{1/3} \approx 75.7 \text{ mm} \approx 76 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Weight Comparison:**

Cross-sectional areas (per unit length):

$$A_h = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_h^2 - d_h^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(80^2 - 50^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(6400 - 2500) = \frac{\pi}{4}(3900) = 3063 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d_s^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(76^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(5776) = 4536 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Weight Ratio:**

$$\frac{W_h}{W_s} = \frac{A_h}{A_s} = \frac{3063}{4536} = 0.675$$

**The hollow shaft weighs only 67.5% of the solid shaft** while transmitting the **same torque**.

**Comment:**
- A hollow shaft is **lighter** and **stronger** (per unit weight) than a solid shaft.
- The material near the center of a solid shaft carries very little stress (stress is zero at the center), so it is effectively wasted.
- A hollow shaft places more material at the outer fiber where stress is maximum — better utilization of material.
- **Conclusion:** Hollow shafts are preferred in **aerospace, automotive drive shafts, and marine propeller shafts** where weight reduction is critical.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes in Hollow Shaft Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $D$ and $d$** — Always remember $D$ = outer, $d$ = inner.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $(1 - k^4)$** in the simplified formula. Wrong formula: $D^3 = 16T/(\pi \tau)$ — Correct: $D^3 = 16T/[\pi \tau (1-k^4)]$.
> 3. **Using solid shaft formula directly** — A hollow shaft cannot be designed using $d^3 = 16T/(\pi \tau)$.
> 4. **Not recomputing $D^3$** with the correct $k$ value — Always verify the $d/D$ ratio in the problem.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Bending stress:** $\sigma_b = 32M/(\pi d^3)$ for solid shaft.
- ✅ **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$ for solid shaft.
- ✅ **Equivalent Twisting Moment (Guest's):** $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$ — **MOST COMMONLY USED IN KTU**.
- ✅ **Equivalent Bending Moment (Rankine's):** $M_e = \frac{1}{2}(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2})$.
- ✅ **Solid shaft diameter:** $d^3 = 16 T_e / (\pi \tau)$.
- ✅ **Hollow shaft equation:** $D^4 - d^4 = 16 T_e D / (\pi \tau)$, OR simplified $D^3 = 16 T_e / [\pi \tau (1-k^4)]$ where $k = d/D$.
- ✅ **Torque-power relation:** $T = 60P/(2\pi N)$ — Units: $P$ in Watts, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m.
- ✅ **Polar Section Modulus:** $Z_p = \pi d^3/16$ (solid), $Z_p = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/(16D)$ (hollow).
- ✅ **Hollow shaft is always LIGHTER** than solid shaft for the same torque capacity.
- ✅ **Outer fiber is critical** because both $\sigma_b$ and $\tau$ are maximum there.
- ✅ **Standard $d/D$ ratio for hollow shafts:** $0.5$ to $0.75$.
- ✅ **Always adopt the LARGER diameter** when comparing Guest's and Rankine's theories.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** kW → W (×1000); N·m → N·mm (×1000).
- ✅ **KTU Preferred Theory:** Guest's Maximum Shear Stress Theory (use $T_e$ by default).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rigidity and Critical Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rigidity and Critical Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Rigidity of Shafts — Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAGT403 — Theory of Machines, Module 3)**, the term **Rigidity** denotes the ability of a rotating machine shaft to resist deformation (either angular twist or lateral bending) when subjected to operating loads.

Two principal kinds of rigidity are studied:

- **Torsional Rigidity ($k_t$)**: The capacity of a shaft to resist angular twist under an applied torque.
- **Lateral / Transverse Rigidity ($k$)**: The capacity of a shaft to resist lateral (sideways) deflection under a transverse load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3:** Students must be able to distinguish between *linear (lateral)* rigidity and *torsional* rigidity, compute both for solid and hollow circular shafts, and use these in subsequent critical-speed problems.

**Physical constants / standard metrics used (assumed for steel unless stated):**
- **Modulus of Rigidity, $G$ = 80 GPa (≈ 80 000 N/mm²)** for mild steel
- **Young's Modulus, $E$ = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** for mild steel
- Density of steel ≈ **7850 kg/m³**

### 1.2 Critical Speed — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Speed ($N_c$ or $\omega_c$):** The rotational speed of a shaft at which the **frequency of forced lateral vibration due to unbalance** coincides with the **natural frequency of transverse vibration** of the shaft. At this speed the shaft enters a state of resonance and begins to **whirl** violently.

Mathematically,

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s}$$

where $k$ is the lateral stiffness (N/m) at the rotor location and $m$ is the rotor mass (kg).

**Whirling** is the lateral whirling motion traced by the bow of a rotating shaft when its rotational speed equals the critical speed — the shaft appears to trace a cone in space, with the disc centre moving in a circular orbit around the geometric axis.

### 1.3 Intuitive / Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — A Child on a Swing:** Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push at any random rhythm, the swing barely moves. But if you push exactly **once per natural swing period**, the amplitude grows uncontrollably with each push — a tiny effort produces enormous motion. The shaft at its critical speed behaves identically: a small geometric eccentricity (unbalance) produces a massive whirl. **Stop pushing (operate away from critical speed) and the motion dies down.**

A second analogy is the **Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940)** — wind-induced vortex shedding matched the bridge's natural lateral frequency, leading to catastrophic resonance. The same principle governs shaft whirling.

### 1.4 Importance in Engineering

- Steam turbine shafts, generator rotors, pump shafts, and high-speed compressor shafts must operate **safely away** from their critical speeds.
- Operating **below** $0.8\,\omega_c$ or **above** $1.2\,\omega_c$ is a common industrial guideline.
- In automotive crankshafts and helicopter tail rotors, multi-disc arrangements require estimation of the *combined* critical speed using **Dunkerley's** or **Rayleigh's** methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rigidity — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the type of deformation.** A shaft transmits torque (causing twist) and supports transverse loads (causing bending). Each has its own rigidity expression.

**Step 2 — Compute the polar moment of inertia ($J$) of the cross-section.** This depends on whether the shaft is solid or hollow:

- Solid circular shaft of diameter $d$:
  $$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$
- Hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $d_o$ and inner diameter $d_i$:
  $$J = \frac{\pi\!\left(d_{o}^{4} - d_{i}^{4}\right)}{32}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the area moment of inertia ($I$)** (for bending):

$$I = \frac{J}{2} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} \quad \text{(solid)}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi\!\left(d_{o}^{4} - d_{i}^{4}\right)}{64} \quad \text{(hollow)}$$

**Step 4 — Write the rigidity expressions.**
- **Torsional rigidity:** $k_t = \dfrac{GJ}{L}$ (N·m/rad)
- **Lateral rigidity at the load point:** $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta}$ (N/m), where $\delta$ is the static deflection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two stiffness values?** A long slender shaft is *flexible in bending* (low $k$) but may still be *stiff in torsion* (high $k_t$). Both must be checked during design — bending governs **critical speed**, while torsion governs **angular twist**.

### 2.2 Why Does a Shaft Whirl?

Consider a rotor of mass $m$ with its centre of gravity offset by eccentricity $e$ from the geometric axis. When rotating at angular speed $\omega$, the unbalance produces a centrifugal force:

$$F_c = m\,\omega^{2}\,(y + e)$$

This force is balanced by the elastic restoring force of the shaft, $F_s = k\,y$. Equating:

$$k\,y = m\,\omega^{2}\,(y + e) \;\Rightarrow\; y = \frac{m\,\omega^{2}\,e}{k - m\,\omega^{2}}$$

When $k = m\,\omega^{2}$ the denominator vanishes and $y \to \infty$ — this is **critical speed**.

### 2.3 Static Deflection for Common Loading Cases (Simply Supported Shaft)

| Loading Configuration | Static Deflection $\delta$ at Load Point |
|---|---|
| Central point load $W$ on span $L$ | $\dfrac{W L^{3}}{48 E I}$ |
| Point load $W$ at distances $a, b$ from ends ($a+b=L$) | $\dfrac{W a^{2} b^{2}}{3 E I L}$ |
| Uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length on span $L$ | $\dfrac{5 w L^{4}}{384 E I}$ |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **formula inventory** for Module 3 questions on Rigidity and Critical Speed. Memorise every row; numerical KTU problems typically combine 2–3 of these.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Polar moment of inertia (solid) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | mm⁴ |
| 2 | Polar moment of inertia (hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi\!\left(d_{o}^{4} - d_{i}^{4}\right)}{32}$ | mm⁴ |
| 3 | Area moment of inertia | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$ (solid) | mm⁴ |
| 4 | Torsional rigidity | $k_t = \dfrac{GJ}{L}$ | N·m / rad |
| 5 | Lateral rigidity (single load) | $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta}$ | N/mm |
| 6 | Central-load static deflection | $\delta_c = \dfrac{W L^{3}}{48 E I}$ | mm |
| 7 | Off-centre load static deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{W a^{2} b^{2}}{3 E I L}$ | mm |
| 8 | Fundamental critical-speed relation | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Critical speed (central disc) | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{48 E I g}{W L^{3}}}$ | rad/s |
| 10 | Critical speed (off-centre disc) | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 E I L g}{W a^{2} b^{2}}}$ | rad/s |
| 11 | Critical speed (rpm) | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}$ | rpm |
| 12 | Dunkerley’s equation | $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^{2}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\omega_{i}^{2}}$ | — |
| 13 | Dunkerley’s equation (deflection form) | $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{g}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\delta_i$ | — |
| 14 | Rayleigh’s energy method | $\omega_c^{2} = \dfrac{g \,\displaystyle\sum W_i\,y_i}{\displaystyle\sum W_i\,y_i^{2}}$ | rad²/s² |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise relationship 8 ($\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta}$) — it is the single most important identity in this module.** Every numerical problem eventually reduces to this equation. Once you compute $\delta$ correctly, $\omega_c$ and $N_c$ follow in one step.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Steam & Gas Turbines:** Critical-speed maps (Campbell diagrams) are used to ensure operating speeds avoid resonance crossings during acceleration/deceleration.
- **Automotive Drivelines:** Tailored shaft stiffness avoids "driveline boom" — a low-frequency booming resonance.
- **Rotating Machinery Health Monitoring:** Modern condition-monitoring systems track sub-harmonics of $N_c$ to detect unbalance faults.
- **Precision Machining (CNC spindles):** Designers maximise $\omega_c$ far above the operating speed to ensure stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Critical Speed of a Shaft with a Single Central Disc

**Setup:** A simply supported uniform shaft of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, carries a rotor disc of weight $W$ (mass $m = W/g$) concentric with the geometric centre of the span.

**Step 1 — Static deflection under rotor weight.**
Treat the shaft as a simply supported beam of span $L$ with a central point load $W$. From standard beam theory, the maximum deflection (which occurs at the centre) is:

$$\delta = \frac{W L^{3}}{48 E I}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the lateral stiffness.**
Lateral stiffness at the load point is the force per unit deflection:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{48 E I}{L^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the resonance condition for whirling.**
At critical speed, the rotating unbalance force excites the natural lateral frequency. Equating stiffness and inertial terms:

$$\omega_c^{2} = \frac{k}{m} = \frac{48 E I / L^{3}}{W / g}$$

$$\boxed{\;\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{48 E I\,g}{W L^{3}}}\;}\quad \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rpm.**

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\,\omega_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{48 E I\,g}{W L^{3}}} \approx 946.4 \sqrt{\frac{48 E I\,g}{W L^{3}}}\;\;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using the universal identity.**
Substitute $\delta = W L^{3}/(48 E I)$ into the fundamental relation $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta}$:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{W L^{3}/(48 E I)}} = \sqrt{\frac{48 E I g}{W L^{3}}}\;\checkmark$$

Both routes converge — confirming the derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Critical Speed for a Disc Off the Centre

**Setup:** Rotor of weight $W$ located at distance $a$ from the left support and $b$ from the right, with $a + b = L$.

**Step 1 — Use Macaulay's method or standard table.**
For a simply supported beam loaded off-centre, the deflection *at the load point* is:

$$\delta = \frac{W\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{3 E I\,L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta}$.**

$$\omega_c^{2} = \frac{g}{\delta} = \frac{3 E I L g}{W\,a^{2} b^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 E I L\,g}{W a^{2} b^{2}}}\;}\quad \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check for central disc.**
Set $a = b = L/2$:

$$\delta = \frac{W\,(L/2)^{2}\,(L/2)^{2}}{3 E I\,L} = \frac{W L^{4}}{48 E I L} = \frac{W L^{3}}{48 E I}\;\checkmark$$

This recovers the central-load result — confirming the off-centre formula.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Dunkerley's Equation for Multi-Disc Shafts

**Setup:** A simply supported shaft carries $n$ rotors at different locations. We seek the *fundamental* critical speed of the combined system.

**Step 1 — Concept of independent contribution.**
Dunkerley assumed each disc acts **independently** as a single-rotor system and that the contributions add up. Let:
- $\delta_i$ = static deflection at the $i^{th}$ rotor *caused by the weight of the $i^{th}$ rotor alone*
- $\omega_i = \sqrt{g/\delta_i}$ = critical speed if only rotor $i$ were present.

**Step 2 — Combine the reciprocals.**
For the combined system the empirical relation is:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^{2}} = \frac{\delta_1}{g} + \frac{\delta_2}{g} + \cdots + \frac{\delta_n}{g}$$

In terms of single-rotor critical speeds:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^{2}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\omega_{i}^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Why is this a lower bound?**
Dunkerley neglects the coupling between rotors; the true fundamental frequency is always **higher** than (or equal to) the value predicted. Thus Dunkerley's method gives a **safe, conservative** estimate of $N_c$.

**Step 4 — Final compact form.**

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\delta_i}}$$

This is mathematically identical in form to the single-disc equation — only the *effective* deflection is now the **sum** of individual deflections.

---

### 3.4 Derivation D — Rayleigh's Energy Method

**Setup:** Same multi-disc shaft. Rayleigh used an *assumed mode shape* equal to the **static deflection profile** produced by the rotor weights.

**Step 1 — Energy balance at resonance.**
At critical speed the maximum kinetic energy equals the maximum strain energy. The approximate natural frequency is:

$$\omega_c^{2} = \frac{g\,\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i\,y_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i\,y_i^{2}}$$

where $y_i$ is the static deflection at the $i^{th}$ disc location.

**Step 2 — Comment.**
- This gives an **upper bound** for the fundamental natural frequency.
- When combined with Dunkerley (lower bound), the true value is bracketed.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Numerical

**Worked Example A (Single central disc):**
A simply supported mild-steel shaft has diameter $d = 50$ mm, span $L = 1.2$ m, and carries a single central disc weighing $W = 294.3$ N. Given $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² and $g = 9810$ mm/s², compute $\omega_c$ and $N_c$.

**Solution:**

Step (i) — Area moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi (50)^{4}}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^{5}\;\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

Step (ii) — Central static deflection:

$$\delta = \frac{W L^{3}}{48 E I} = \frac{294.3 \times (1200)^{3}}{48 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 3.068 \times 10^{5}} = 1.727 \times 10^{-1}\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step (iii) — Critical angular speed:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{0.1727}} = 238.3\;\;\text{rad/s}$$

Step (iv) — Critical speed in rpm:

$$N_c = \frac{60\,\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 238.3}{2\pi} = 2276\;\;\text{rpm}$$

**Worked Example B (Multi-disc via Dunkerley):**
A simply supported shaft of span $L = 1200$ mm, diameter $d = 60$ mm, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², carries three rotors:
- Disc 1: $W_1 = 50$ N at $a_1 = 200$ mm, $b_1 = 1000$ mm
- Disc 2: $W_2 = 100$ N at $a_2 = 500$ mm, $b_2 = 700$ mm
- Disc 3: $W_3 = 80$ N at $a_3 = 800$ mm, $b_3 = 400$ mm

Estimate $\omega_c$ and $N_c$ using Dunkerley's method.

**Solution:**

Step (i) — Area moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{\pi (60)^{4}}{64} = 6.362 \times 10^{5}\;\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

Step (ii) — Off-centre deflection formula $\delta_i = W_i a_i^{2} b_i^{2} / (3 E I L)$:

$$\delta_1 = \frac{50 \times (200)^{2} \times (1000)^{2}}{3 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 6.362 \times 10^{5} \times 1200} = 4.37 \times 10^{-3}\;\;\text{mm}$$

$$\delta_2 = \frac{100 \times (500)^{2} \times (700)^{2}}{3 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 6.362 \times 10^{5} \times 1200} = 2.675 \times 10^{-2}\;\;\text{mm}$$

$$\delta_3 = \frac{80 \times (800)^{2} \times (400)^{2}}{3 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 6.362 \times 10^{5} \times 1200} = 1.789 \times 10^{-2}\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step (iii) — Sum of deflections:

$$\sum \delta_i = 0.00437 + 0.02675 + 0.01789 = 0.04901\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step (iv) — Critical angular speed:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\sum \delta_i}} = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{0.04901}} = 447.4\;\;\text{rad/s}$$

Step (v) — Critical speed in rpm:

$$N_c = \frac{60 \times 447.4}{2\pi} = 4272\;\;\text{rpm}$$

This result is the **lower bound** of the true critical speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Origin of Whirling

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROTATING SHAFT WITH ECCENTRIC MASS] --> B[CENTRIFUGAL FORCE F equals m omega squared times y plus e]
    B --> C[SHAFT BENDS BY DEFLECTION y]
    C --> D{STIFFNESS k EQUALS MASS m times omega squared}
    D -->|YES| E[RESONANCE: y BECOMES LARGE]
    D -->|NO| F[STABLE WHIRL WITH SMALL y]
    E --> G[WHIRLING INSTABILITY]
    F --> H[NORMAL OPERATION]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Rigidity Classification for Shafts

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SHAFT LOADING] --> B[TORSIONAL LOAD T]
    A --> C[TRANSVERSE LOAD W]
    B --> D[TORSIONAL RIGIDITY GJ OVER L]
    C --> E[LATERAL RIGIDITY W OVER DELTA]
    D --> F[ANGLE OF TWIST THETA EQUALS TL OVER GJ]
    E --> G[STATIC DEFLECTION DELTA EQUALS WL CUBED OVER 48 EI]
    F --> H[DESIGN CHECK: THETA WITHIN LIMIT]
    G --> I[CRITICAL SPEED CHECK: OMEGA C EQUALS SQRT G OVER DELTA]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Methods for Multi-Rotor Critical Speed

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MULTI DISC SHAFT SYSTEM] --> B[DUNKERLEY METHOD]
    A --> C[RAYLEIGH METHOD]
    B --> D[LOWER BOUND CRITICAL SPEED]
    C --> E[UPPER BOUND CRITICAL SPEED]
    B --> F[INVERSE SQUARES SUM OF INDIVIDUAL CRITICAL SPEEDS]
    C --> G[ENERGY BALANCE USING ASSUMED MODE SHAPE]
    D --> H[CONSERVATIVE DESIGN ESTIMATE]
    E --> I[ENGINEERING SAFE ESTIMATE]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Operating Speed Zones for a Rotor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OPERATING SPEED N] --> B{N COMPARED WITH N C}
    B -->|N BELOW 0.8 N C| C[SAFE SUB CRITICAL REGION]
    B -->|N BETWEEN 0.8 N C AND 1.2 N C| D[FORBIDDEN RESONANCE BAND]
    B -->|N ABOVE 1.2 N C| E[SAFE SUPER CRITICAL REGION]
    D --> F[SEVERE WHIRLING MUST BE AVOIDED]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define the terms (i) Rigidity of a shaft and (ii) Critical speed of a shaft.**

**Model Answer:**
- (i) *Rigidity* is the ability of a shaft to resist deformation (twist in torsion, deflection in bending). Torsional rigidity is $k_t = GJ/L$; lateral rigidity is $k = W/\delta$.
- (ii) *Critical speed* is the rotational speed at which the rotor's unbalance frequency matches the natural lateral frequency of the shaft. At this speed the shaft enters resonance and begins to whirl violently. It is given by $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta}$ or $N_c = (60/2\pi)\sqrt{g/\delta}$.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is Dunkerley's method? Why does it give a lower bound estimate of the critical speed?**

**Model Answer:**
Dunkerley's method estimates the *fundamental* critical speed of a shaft carrying several rotors. The combined critical speed is obtained from:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^{2}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{\omega_{i}^{2}}$$

where $\omega_i$ is the critical speed if only the $i^{th}$ rotor were present. Because Dunkerley neglects the mutual coupling of rotors (which would *raise* the true frequency), the predicted $\omega_c$ is always less than or equal to the true value. Hence it is a **safe, lower-bound** estimate, ideal for conservative design.

---

### Part B — Long Answer / Numerical Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common marks lost in Module 3 questions include:
> (1) **Mixing units** — students mix mm and m in the deflection formula. **Always convert span and dimensions to mm** before using $E$ in N/mm².
> (2) **Forgetting $g = 9810$ mm/s²** — using $g = 9.81$ when other quantities are in mm gives a wrong answer by a factor of 100.
> (3) **Using $J$ where $I$ is required** — torsional rigidity uses $J$, bending stiffness uses $I$; they differ by a factor of 2.
> (4) **Omitting $\sum$ in Dunkerley** — only summing for some discs and not all.
> (5) **Forgetting to convert $\omega_c$ to $N_c$** when the question asks for rpm.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** A simply supported steel shaft of diameter $d = 60$ mm and span $L = 1.5$ m carries a single rotor of weight $W = 500$ N at its centre. Determine: (i) the central static deflection, (ii) the lateral stiffness, and (iii) the critical speed in rad/s and rpm. Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² and $g = 9810$ mm/s². **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotor of weight $W = 300$ N is mounted on the same shaft at a distance of 450 mm from one support. Find the critical speed of the shaft in rpm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Central disc:**

Step 1 — Area moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi (60)^{4}}{64} = 6.362 \times 10^{5}\;\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

Step 2 — Central static deflection (using $L = 1500$ mm):

$$\delta_c = \frac{W L^{3}}{48 E I} = \frac{500 \times (1500)^{3}}{48 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 6.362 \times 10^{5}} = 0.2762\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step 3 — Lateral stiffness:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta_c} = \frac{500}{0.2762} = 1810.3\;\;\text{N/mm} = 1.8103 \times 10^{6}\;\;\text{N/m}$$

Step 4 — Critical angular speed:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{0.2762}} = 188.4\;\;\text{rad/s}$$

Step 5 — Critical speed in rpm:

$$N_c = \frac{60\,\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 188.4}{2\pi} = 1799\;\;\text{rpm}$$

**(b) Off-centre disc (a = 450 mm, b = 1050 mm):**

Step 1 — Static deflection at load point:

$$\delta = \frac{W\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{3 E I L} = \frac{300 \times (450)^{2} \times (1050)^{2}}{3 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 6.362 \times 10^{5} \times 1500} = 0.1308\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step 2 — Critical angular speed:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{0.1308}} = 273.8\;\;\text{rad/s}$$

Step 3 — Critical speed in rpm:

$$N_c = \frac{60 \times 273.8}{2\pi} = 2615\;\;\text{rpm}$$

**Observation:** Off-centre loading here produced a *higher* critical speed than central loading for the same disc weight — because off-centre load creates a smaller deflection ($\delta \propto a^{2}b^{2}$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A simply supported steel shaft of span $L = 1.4$ m and diameter $d = 55$ mm carries two rotors. Rotor 1 weighs $W_1 = 200$ N at a distance of 350 mm from the left support. Rotor 2 weighs $W_2 = 300$ N at a distance of 950 mm from the left support. Take $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². Using **Dunkerley's method**, estimate the critical speed in rpm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with a sketch, the concept of whirling of shafts. Discuss why the operating speed of a high-speed rotor must lie safely away from the critical speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Dunkerley's method:**

Step 1 — Compute $I$:

$$I = \frac{\pi (55)^{4}}{64} = 4.486 \times 10^{5}\;\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

Step 2 — Deflection at Rotor 1 ($a_1 = 350$ mm, $b_1 = 1050$ mm):

$$\delta_1 = \frac{W_1 a_1^{2} b_1^{2}}{3 E I L} = \frac{200 \times (350)^{2} \times (1050)^{2}}{3 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 4.486 \times 10^{5} \times 1400} = 6.84 \times 10^{-2}\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step 3 — Deflection at Rotor 2 ($a_2 = 950$ mm, $b_2 = 450$ mm):

$$\delta_2 = \frac{W_2 a_2^{2} b_2^{2}}{3 E I L} = \frac{300 \times (950)^{2} \times (450)^{2}}{3 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 4.486 \times 10^{5} \times 1400} = 1.218 \times 10^{-1}\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step 4 — Sum of deflections:

$$\sum \delta_i = 0.0684 + 0.1218 = 0.1902\;\;\text{mm}$$

Step 5 — Dunkerley's critical speed:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\sum \delta_i}} = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{0.1902}} = 227.1\;\;\text{rad/s}$$

Step 6 — Convert to rpm:

$$N_c = \frac{60 \times 227.1}{2\pi} = 2169\;\;\text{rpm}$$

**This value is a conservative lower bound.**

**(b) Whirling concept:**

**Sketch description (to be drawn by student):**
Draw a vertical rotor axis with a horizontal arrow showing the eccentricity $e$. As the shaft rotates, the rotor centre describes a circular path of radius $(y + e)$ about the geometric axis, where $y$ is the elastic deflection. Sketch the bow of the shaft with a dashed ellipse traced by the disc centre.

**Discussion:**

*Whirling* is the lateral whirling motion of a rotating shaft caused by transverse vibration excited by the centrifugal force of unbalance. The shaft centre traces a circle of radius equal to the dynamic deflection $y$.

**Why avoid operating near critical speed:**
- At $\omega = \omega_c$ the deflection $y \to \infty$ in theory; in practice it grows so large that the shaft strikes its bearings.
- This causes mechanical failure, loss of lubrication, catastrophic rupture, and severe safety hazard.
- Industrial practice dictates operating **below $0.8\,\omega_c$** (sub-critical) or **above $1.2\,\omega_c$** (super-critical), with the shaft designed so the working speed falls well outside the forbidden resonance band.
- Many high-speed rotors (e.g., turbogenerators) are designed to run *super-critically*, passing rapidly through the critical speed during start-up and shutdown using well-balanced bearings.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rigidity and Critical Speed (Module 3)**

- **Rigidity** has two forms: torsional ($GJ/L$) and lateral ($W/\delta$).
- **Solid shaft:** $J = \pi d^{4}/32$; $I = \pi d^{4}/64$.
- **Hollow shaft:** $J = \pi(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})/32$; $I = \pi(d_o^{4}-d_i^{4})/64$.
- **Master identity:** $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta}$ — valid for *every* configuration (just compute the right $\delta$).
- **Central load:** $\delta = W L^{3}/(48 E I)$.
- **Off-centre load** at distances $a, b$ from ends: $\delta = W a^{2} b^{2}/(3 E I L)$.
- **Dunkerley:** $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^{2}} = \displaystyle\sum \dfrac{1}{\omega_i^{2}}$ → **lower bound**, safe for design.
- **Rayleigh:** $\omega_c^{2} = g\,\sum W_i y_i / \sum W_i y_i^{2}$ → **upper bound**, used as a check.
- Always keep **units consistent**: lengths in **mm**, $E$ in **N/mm²**, $g = 9810$ mm/s².
- **Convert** $\omega_c$ (rad/s) to $N_c$ (rpm) using $N_c = 60\,\omega_c/(2\pi)$.
- Avoid the resonance band: keep $N_{\text{op}} < 0.8\,N_c$ or $N_{\text{op}} > 1.2\,N_c$.
- The critical-speed formula $\omega_c = \sqrt{k/m}$ is dimensionally sound only if $k$ is in **N/m** and $m$ in **kg**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Engineering Materials in Machine Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Materials (KTU Definition):** Engineering materials are the carefully selected substances used to manufacture machine components, chosen based on their mechanical, physical, chemical, and thermal properties to withstand operational loads, environmental conditions, and service life requirements of the machine.

In the context of **Theory of Machines (Module 3 – Brakes, Clutches & Friction Devices)**, the term **"Material"** specifically refers to the **friction materials** and **structural materials** used in machine elements that transmit or absorb power through surface contact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Material selection is a critical design parameter that directly influences the **performance, durability, and safety** of friction-based machine elements such as clutches, brakes, belts, and gears.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Engineering Materials

Engineering materials used in machine construction are broadly classified into:

### A. **Metals & Alloys**
- **Ferrous Metals**: Cast iron, carbon steel, alloy steel, stainless steel
- **Non-Ferrous Metals**: Copper, aluminum, zinc, lead, titanium

### B. **Non-Metallic Materials**
- **Polymers**: Nylon, PTFE (Teflon), Rubber, Polyurethane
- **Ceramics**: Alumina, Silicon Carbide, Zirconia
- **Composites**: Fibre-reinforced polymers (FRP), Carbon-Carbon composites

### C. **Friction-Specific Materials** (Most relevant to Module 3)
- Asbestos-based friction materials (legacy)
- Semi-metallic friction materials
- Ceramic friction materials
- Organic/resin-bonded friction materials
- Sintered metal friction materials

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Brake Pad of a Car"**
>
> Imagine you are riding a bicycle downhill. To stop, you press a rubber/leather pad against the spinning metal rim of the wheel. The pad must:
> - **Grip** the metal firmly (high coefficient of friction, **μ**)
> - **Not wear out quickly** (high wear resistance)
> - **Not transfer excessive heat** to the rim (good thermal conductivity)
> - **Withstand rain, dust, and temperature changes** (environmental stability)
>
> Now imagine doing this for a 10-tonne truck doing 80 km/h — the **choice of material** becomes life-or-death critical!
>
> This is exactly why engineers must **select the right material** for every friction-based machine element.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|--------|---------------------|
| Coefficient of Friction | $\mu$ | **0.1 to 0.6** (dimensionless) |
| Density | $\rho$ | **7850 kg/m³** (steel) |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200 GPa** (steel) |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ | **400–2000 MPa** |
| Hardness (Vickers) | $HV$ | **100–1000 HV** |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | **45 W/m·K** (steel) |
| Specific Heat Capacity | $c_p$ | **486 J/kg·K** (steel) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Alert:** Students often confuse **kinematic coefficient of friction** ($\mu_k$) with **static coefficient of friction** ($\mu_s$). In braking systems, $\mu_k$ is used because the surfaces are sliding, not stationary!

---

## 1.5 Importance of Material Selection in Machine Design

The material of a machine component determines:

1. **Load-bearing capacity** — maximum stress it can sustain
2. **Wear rate** — material loss per unit sliding distance
3. **Heat dissipation** — ability to conduct heat away from contact zone
4. **Fatigue life** — number of stress cycles before failure
5. **Corrosion resistance** — survival in harsh environments
6. **Cost-effectiveness** — economic viability of the design

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:** A perfect machine element material balances **performance, durability, manufacturability, and cost** — this is known as the **"Material Selection Quadrilateral"** in modern design methodology.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties of Materials (Theory of Machines Perspective)

When designing friction-based machine elements (clutches, brakes), the following material properties are of paramount importance:

### A. **Strength Properties**

**1. Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_u$)**
- The maximum stress a material can withstand while being stretched before breaking.
- $$\sigma_u = \frac{F_{\max}}{A_0}$$
- Where $F_{\max}$ is the maximum load and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.

**2. Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**
- The stress at which a material begins to deform plastically (permanently).
- Critical for designing components that must not deform under load.

**3. Fatigue Strength / Endurance Limit ($\sigma_e$)**
- Maximum stress a material can endure for **infinite** cycles without failure.
- For steels: $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \sigma_u$ (for $\sigma_u < 1400$ MPa).

---

### B. **Hardness**

Hardness is the material's resistance to localized plastic deformation (indentation, scratching).

**Common Scales:**
