| Scale | Symbol | Application |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| Brinell | $HB$ | Soft/medium metals, castings |
| Vickers | $HV$ | Hardened steels, ceramics |
| Rockwell C | $HRC$ | Hardened steels (most common) |
| Rockwell B | $HRB$ | Soft metals, brass, aluminum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Correlation (KTU favorite):**
> $$\sigma_u \text{ (MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times HB$$
> This relationship is extensively used in machine design problems.

---

### C. **Toughness vs. Brittleness**

- **Toughness**: Ability to absorb energy and plastically deform before fracturing (area under stress-strain curve).
- **Brittleness**: Little or no plastic deformation before fracture (e.g., cast iron, ceramics).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Friction Materials:**
> - **Brake drums/discs**: Tough material (cast iron, steel) — must resist thermal shock
> - **Brake pads/friction linings**: Hard but with controlled wear (sintered metal, ceramic composites)

---

### D. **Wear Resistance**

Wear is the progressive loss of material from a surface due to relative motion.

**Archard's Wear Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
$$V = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Volume of material worn away (m³)
- $k$ = Wear coefficient (dimensionless, typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-8}$)
- $W$ = Normal load (N)
- $L$ = Sliding distance (m)
- $H$ = Hardness of the softer material (Pa)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Wear rate is **inversely proportional to hardness**. Doubling the hardness halves the wear volume — a key principle in selecting brake drum materials.

---

## 2.2 Friction Material Properties (Module 3 Focus)

For clutches and brakes, the friction material (lining) must satisfy:

| Property | Desired Characteristic | Reason |
|----------|----------------------|--------|
| **High & stable $\mu$** | $\mu$ independent of $T$, $P$, $V$ | Consistent torque transmission |
| **High heat resistance** | No fade at high $T$ | Brakes heat up rapidly |
| **Good wear resistance** | Long service life | Reduces maintenance |
| **High thermal conductivity** | Dissipate heat fast | Prevents hot spots |
| **Resistance to fade** | $\mu$ doesn't drop when hot | Safety-critical |
| **No judder** | Smooth engagement | Driver/passenger comfort |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Materials in Machine Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table — Appears in Almost Every KTU Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Normal stress | Pa |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{F_s}{A}$ | Shear stress | Pa |
| 3 | $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Elongation | m |
| 4 | $\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \, HB$ | Hardness-Strength correlation | MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$ | Endurance limit (steel) | MPa |
| 6 | $V = \dfrac{kWL}{H}$ | Archard's wear equation | m³ |
| 7 | $\mu = \dfrac{F_f}{F_n}$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{\text{contact}} = \dfrac{F_n}{A_r}$ | Contact (bearing) pressure | Pa |
| 9 | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{design}}}$ | Factor of Safety | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{W \mu v}{J}$ | Heat generation rate (friction) | W |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Material Selection

| Application | Material Used | Reason |
|------------|--------------|--------|
| **Automotive brake disc** | Grey cast iron (GG25) | High damping, wear-resistant, cheap |
| **Brake pad (modern)** | Semi-metallic composite | Stable $\mu$, heat-resistant |
| **F1 brake disc** | Carbon-Carbon composite | Withstands 1000°C |
| **Clutch plate** | Cerametallic / organic | High energy absorption |
| **Belt drive** | Polyurethane / rubber | High $\mu$, flexible |
| **Gear (light duty)** | Nylon, POM (Delrin) | Self-lubricating, quiet |
| **Gear (heavy duty)** | Case-hardened alloy steel | High contact fatigue strength |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Archard's Wear Equation (KTU Favourite Derivation)

**Statement:** The volume of worn material is proportional to the normal load and sliding distance, and inversely proportional to the hardness of the softer material.

### **Step 1 — Define the System**
Consider two surfaces in sliding contact. The harder asperity (peak) of surface 1 ploughs through the softer material of surface 2.

Let:
- $W$ = Normal load applied (N)
- $L$ = Total sliding distance (m)
- $A_r$ = Real (asperity) contact area (m²)
- $H$ = Hardness of softer material (Pa)
- $V$ = Volume of material worn (m³)

### **Step 2 — Relate Load to Real Contact Area**
By definition of hardness:
$$H = \frac{W}{A_r}$$

Solving for the real area of contact:
$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

### **Step 3 — Express Worn Volume Geometrically**
Assume each asperity contact produces a wear particle of volume proportional to the contact area and sliding distance. With proportionality constant $k$ (wear coefficient):
$$V = k \cdot A_r \cdot L$$

### **Step 4 — Substitute $A_r$ from Step 2**
$$V = k \cdot \left(\frac{W}{H}\right) \cdot L$$

### **Step 5 — Final Archard's Equation**
$$\boxed{V = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot L}{H}}$$

Where the **wear coefficient** $k$ accounts for:
- Material pair compatibility
- Lubrication condition
- Asperity geometry
- Environmental factors

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - **Increase $W$** (load) → More wear (linear)
> - **Increase $H$** (hardness) → Less wear (inverse)
> - **Increase $L$** (sliding distance) → More wear (linear)

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example — Wear Calculation

**Problem:** A steel shaft ($\sigma_u = 600$ MPa) slides against a phosphor bronze bushing under a normal load of $W = 5000$ N over a total sliding distance of $L = 10,000$ m. The wear coefficient is $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$. Estimate the wear volume.

### **Given Data:**
- $W = 5000$ N
- $L = 10,000$ m
- $k = 5 \times 10^{-5}$
- $H$ (bronze) $\approx 1.0 \times 10^9$ Pa (typical Vickers converted to Pa)

### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply Archard's equation.
$$V = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.
$$V = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (5000) \cdot (10{,}000)}{1.0 \times 10^9}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.
$$\text{Numerator} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 10^4 = 2500$$

**Step 4:** Compute final result.
$$V = \frac{2500}{1.0 \times 10^9} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Convert to mm³ for intuition.
$$V = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 \times 10^9 \frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{m}^3} = 2500 \text{ mm}^3$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** $2500 \text{ mm}^3$ is about the size of a small sugar cube — reasonable for a heavily loaded bronze bushing over $10$ km of sliding.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Factor of Safety for Material Design

**Statement:** Factor of safety (FoS) is the ratio of the material's failure strength to the maximum anticipated design stress.

### **Step 1 — Define Failure Strength**
$$\sigma_{\text{failure}} = \sigma_y \quad \text{(yielding)}$$
or
$$\sigma_{\text{failure}} = \sigma_u \quad \text{(fracture)}$$

depending on whether the material is ductile or brittle.

### **Step 2 — Define Design Stress**
$$\sigma_{\text{design}} = \frac{F_{\text{applied}}}{A_{\text{required}}}$$

### **Step 3 — Form the Ratio**
$$\boxed{\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{failure}}}{\sigma_{\text{design}}}}$$

### **Step 4 — For Friction Materials in Clutches/Brakes**
The required FoS depends on:
- **Reliability requirement** (aircraft brake → FoS = 2; car brake → FoS = 1.5)
- **Load variability** (dynamic vs. static)
- **Consequences of failure** (life-critical vs. convenience)

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Heat Generation in Friction Materials

When friction material slides against a counter-face, the heat generated per unit time is:

### **Step 1 — Friction Force**
The friction force opposing motion is:
$$F_f = \mu \cdot F_n$$

### **Step 2 — Work Done Against Friction (per unit time)**
Power dissipated as heat:
$$P = F_f \cdot v = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v$$

### **Step 3 — Heat Generated**
$$\boxed{Q = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat generation rate (W)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction
- $F_n$ = Normal force (N)
- $v$ = Sliding velocity (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This heat MUST be dissipated. If $Q$ exceeds the dissipation capacity, the friction material's temperature rises, causing **fade** (drop in $\mu$), which is the #1 cause of brake failure!

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Common Friction Materials

| Material | $\mu$ (dry) | Max Temp (°C) | Wear Rate | Cost | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|---------------|-----------|------|-------------|
| **Cast iron on cast iron** | 0.15–0.25 | 600 | Low | Low | Industrial brakes |
| **Wood on metal** | 0.20–0.35 | 200 | Medium | Very low | Old railway brakes |
| **Leather on metal** | 0.30–0.40 | 120 | High | Low | Antique clutches |
| **Asbestos-resin** | 0.30–0.45 | 400 | Medium | Medium | Legacy automotive |
| **Semi-metallic** | 0.35–0.45 | 500 | Low | Medium | Modern cars |
| **Sintered bronze** | 0.10–0.20 | 700 | Very low | High | Heavy machinery |
| **Ceramic composite** | 0.35–0.50 | 1000 | Very low | Very high | F1, aerospace |
| **C/C composite** | 0.30–0.45 | 1500 | Lowest | Extreme | Aircraft & F1 brakes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Engineering Materials for Machine Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ENGINEERING MATERIALS"] --> B["METALLIC"]
    A --> C["NON-METALLIC"]
    A --> D["COMPOSITES"]
    A --> E["FRICTION SPECIFIC"]

    B --> B1["Ferrous: Cast Iron, Steel"]
    B --> B2["Non-Ferrous: Cu, Al, Ti"]

    C --> C1["Polymers: Nylon, PTFE, Rubber"]
    C --> C2["Ceramics: Alumina, SiC"]
    C --> C3["Elastomers"]

    D --> D1["FRP: Glass/Carbon Fibre"]
    D --> D2["Cermets"]
    D --> D3["Sandwich Structures"]

    E --> E1["Organic Bonded"]
    E --> E2["Sintered Metal"]
    E --> E3["Ceramic Composite"]
    E --> E4["Carbon-Carbon"]

    B1 --> F1["Brake Drums, Gears, Shafts"]
    B2 --> F2["Bushings, Lightweight Housings"]
    C1 --> F3["Seals, Low-Load Gears"]
    C2 --> F4["High-Temp Bearings"]
    D1 --> F5["Aircraft Panels, Racing Parts"]
    E1 --> F6["Automotive Brake Pads"]
    E2 --> F7["Heavy Machinery Clutches"]
    E3 --> F8["F1 Brake Discs"]
    E4 --> F9["Aircraft Brake Discs"]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Material Selection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Identify Functional Requirements"] --> S2["Step 2: List Required Properties"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Screen Candidate Materials"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Evaluate Against Constraints"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Rank by Performance Index"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Final Material Selection"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Prototype Testing"]
    S7 --> S8{"Meets All Criteria?"}
    S8 -->|Yes| DONE["Material Approved for Production"]
    S8 -->|No| S3

    style DONE fill:#90EE90
    style S8 fill:#FFD700
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Stress-Strain Behaviour of Common Materials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Material Type"] --> B["Ductile: Steel, Al"]
    A --> C["Brittle: Cast Iron, Ceramic"]
    A --> D["Elastomer: Rubber"]

    B --> B1["Long plastic region before fracture"]
    B --> B2["High toughness"]
    B --> B3["Used for shafts, gears"]

    C --> C1["Little/no plastic region"]
    C --> C2["Sudden fracture"]
    C --> C3["Used for brake drums, dies"]

    D --> D1["Large elastic recovery"]
    D --> D2["Hyperelastic behaviour"]
    D --> D3["Used for seals, couplings"]

    style B fill:#87CEEB
    style C fill:#FFA07A
    style D fill:#90EE90
```

---

## 4.4 Architecture Flow — Friction Material Failure Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Friction Material in Service"] --> B["Heat Generation"]
    B --> C{"Heat Dissipation Rate"}
    C -->|Insufficient| D["Temperature Rise"]
    D --> E["Thermal Fade: μ drops"]
    E --> F["Reduced Braking Torque"]
    F --> G["SAFETY FAILURE"]

    C -->|Sufficient| H["Stable Operation"]
    H --> I["Controlled Wear"]
    I --> J["Scheduled Replacement"]

    G --> K["Material Redesign Required"]
    K --> L["Select Higher-Temp Material"]

    style G fill:#FF6347
    style H fill:#90EE90
    style L fill:#FFD700
```

---

## 4.5 Comparison Diagram — Material Property Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PROPERTY PROFILE"] --> P1["Hardness"]
    P --> P2["Toughness"]
    P --> P3["Wear Resistance"]
    P --> P4["Heat Resistance"]
    P --> P5["Friction Stability"]
    P --> P6["Cost Effectiveness"]

    P1 --> MAT1["Cast Iron: High"]
    P2 --> MAT1
    P3 --> MAT1
    P4 --> MAT1
    P5 --> MAT1
    P6 --> MAT1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "friction material". List any four desirable properties of friction materials used in clutches and brakes.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Friction Material:** A friction material is a specially engineered substance used as a lining on clutch plates and brake surfaces to generate the required frictional resistance for torque transmission or deceleration.

**Desirable Properties (any four):**
1. **High and stable coefficient of friction ($\mu$)** — independent of temperature, pressure, and sliding velocity.
2. **High heat resistance** — ability to operate at elevated temperatures without losing frictional properties (no fade).
3. **Good wear resistance** — long service life and uniform wear pattern.
4. **High thermal conductivity** — efficient dissipation of frictional heat.
5. **Resistance to environmental degradation** — moisture, oil, and chemical resistance.
6. **Low noise and smooth engagement** — no judder or squeal.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Archard's wear equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$V = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot L}{H}$$

**Significance of Terms:**
- $V$ = Volume of material worn away (m³) — output quantity we want to minimize.
- $k$ = Wear coefficient (dimensionless) — depends on material pair, lubrication, and environment.
- $W$ = Normal load (N) — contact force pressing the surfaces together.
- $L$ = Sliding distance (m) — total relative motion between surfaces.
- $H$ = Hardness of the softer surface (Pa) — resistance to penetration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Wear is directly proportional to load and sliding distance but inversely proportional to hardness. **Harder surfaces last longer.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**A single-plate clutch has a friction lining of organic resin-bonded material with the following specifications:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Outer radius, $R_o$ | 150 mm |
| Inner radius, $R_i$ | 100 mm |
| Coefficient of friction, $\mu$ | 0.35 |
| Axial force, $W$ | 5000 N |
| Permissible contact pressure, $p_{\max}$ | 0.7 MPa |

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
**(i) Determine whether the friction material is safe against uniform pressure theory.**
**(ii) If uniform pressure exists, find the torque transmitted by the clutch.**

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
**(iii) Calculate the heat generated per engagement if the clutch slips for 3 seconds at an average relative velocity of 10 m/s.**
**(iv) Suggest a more suitable friction material if the operating temperature exceeds 500°C.**

---

### **Complete Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate contact area.
$$A = \pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$
$$A = \pi \left[(0.150)^2 - (0.100)^2\right]$$
$$A = \pi \left[0.0225 - 0.0100\right]$$
$$A = \pi \times 0.0125$$
$$\boxed{A = 0.03927 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate actual contact pressure.
$$p = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{5000}{0.03927}$$
$$\boxed{p = 1.273 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.1273 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with permissible pressure.
$$p = 0.1273 \text{ MPa} < p_{\max} = 0.7 \text{ MPa}$$

✅ **The friction material is SAFE against uniform pressure theory.**

**Step 4:** Calculate torque transmitted (Uniform Pressure Theory).

For uniform pressure:
$$T = \frac{2}{3} \mu W \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$$

Computing numerator:
$$R_o^3 - R_i^3 = (0.150)^3 - (0.100)^3 = 0.003375 - 0.001 = 0.002375 \text{ m}^3$$

Computing denominator:
$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 0.0125 \text{ m}^2$$

Ratio:
$$\frac{0.002375}{0.0125} = 0.19 \text{ m}$$

Final torque:
$$T = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.35 \times 5000 \times 0.19$$
$$T = \frac{2}{3} \times 332.5$$
$$\boxed{T = 221.67 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 3 — Heat Generated:**

Using the heat generation equation:
$$Q = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v$$

Substituting:
$$Q = 0.35 \times 5000 \times 10$$
$$Q = 17{,}500 \text{ W} = 17.5 \text{ kW}$$

For 3 seconds of slipping:
$$E_{\text{heat}} = Q \times t = 17{,}500 \times 3$$
$$\boxed{E_{\text{heat}} = 52{,}500 \text{ J} = 52.5 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 4 — Material Recommendation:**

> [!TIP]
> Since organic resin-bonded materials degrade above ~400°C, for temperatures exceeding 500°C, **sintered metal friction materials** or **ceramic-matrix composites** are recommended because:
> - Operating temperature up to 700°C (sintered) / 1000°C (ceramic)
> - Stable $\mu$ at high temperatures (no thermal fade)
> - Excellent wear resistance
> - Suitable for heavy-duty applications (trucks, industrial machinery, aerospace)

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
**A machine component is subjected to a fluctuating load varying from +25,000 N (tensile) to −10,000 N (compressive). The component has a cross-section of $30 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$ and is made of steel with ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u = 600$ MPa and yield strength $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa.**

**Determine:**
**(i) Mean stress and amplitude stress.**
**(ii) Factor of safety based on Soderberg criterion (assume endurance limit $\sigma_e = 280$ MPa).**

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
**(iii) Explain briefly the factors influencing selection of friction materials for an automotive disc brake.**
**(iv) Compare cast iron and ceramic composite as brake disc materials on five key parameters.**

---

### **Complete Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute maximum and minimum stresses.

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = 0.030 \times 0.030 = 9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Maximum stress (tensile):
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{+25{,}000}{9 \times 10^{-4}} = +27.78 \text{ MPa}$$

Minimum stress (compressive):
$$\sigma_{\min} = \frac{-10{,}000}{9 \times 10^{-4}} = -11.11 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Compute mean stress ($\sigma_m$) and amplitude stress ($\sigma_a$).

Mean stress:
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2}$$
$$\sigma_m = \frac{27.78 + (-11.11)}{2}$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_m = 8.33 \text{ MPa}}$$

Amplitude stress:
$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$$
$$\sigma_a = \frac{27.78 - (-11.11)}{2}$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_a = 19.45 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Soderberg's Criterion.

$$\frac{1}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}$$

$$\frac{1}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{19.45}{280} + \frac{8.33}{380}$$

$$\frac{1}{\text{FoS}} = 0.0695 + 0.0219 = 0.0914$$

$$\boxed{\text{FoS} = 10.94}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A FoS of ~11 is unusually high — this indicates the component is **over-designed**, which is acceptable for safety-critical parts but may be optimized.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**(iii) Factors Influencing Friction Material Selection for Automotive Disc Brakes:**

1. **Operating Temperature Range** — Modern cars reach 300–500°C during hard braking; material must resist fade.
2. **Coefficient of Friction Stability** — $\mu$ must remain nearly constant across the temperature spectrum.
3. **Wear Rate** — Determines pad replacement interval; lower wear = longer life.
4. **Noise, Vibration, Harshness (NVH)** — Material must minimize squeal and judder.
5. **Environmental Friendliness** — Modern regulations ban copper and heavy metals; asbestos is fully prohibited.
6. **Compatibility with Disc** — Pad and disc materials must be a tribological pair that doesn't cause excessive disc wear.
7. **Cost and Manufacturability** — Mass production at affordable price.

**(iv) Comparison — Cast Iron vs. Ceramic Composite Brake Disc:**

| Parameter | Cast Iron Disc | Ceramic Composite Disc |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Max operating temperature** | ~600°C | ~1400°C |
| **Weight** | Heavy (density 7.2 g/cm³) | Very light (density ~2.5 g/cm³) |
| **Cost** | Low | Very high (10–20× cast iron) |
| **Wear rate** | Moderate | Very low |
| **Noise level** | Can squeal | Very quiet |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Poor (rusts) | Excellent |
| **Thermal conductivity** | Good | Lower (requires cooling ducts) |
| **Typical applications** | Passenger cars, commercial vehicles | Supercars, F1, aircraft |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m in stress calculations. **Always convert to SI units (Pa, m, N) before computing.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma_e$ and $\sigma_u$:** Students often use ultimate tensile strength instead of endurance limit in fatigue formulas. **Endurance limit $\sigma_e$ is for fatigue; $\sigma_u$ is for static failure.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Pressure Check:** In clutch/brake problems, ALWAYS verify that actual pressure $p \leq p_{\max}$. If not, **reduce axial force** $W$ or **increase area**.
>
> 4. **Wrong Torque Formula:** For uniform pressure: $T = \frac{2}{3} \mu W \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$; For uniform wear: $T = \frac{1}{2} \mu W (R_o + R_i)$. **Mixing these up costs 3–4 marks.**
>
> 5. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** Examiner expects **N·m for torque**, **Pa (or MPa) for stress**, **W (or J/s) for power**. Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark.
>
> 6. **Not Stating Assumptions:** In wear problems, explicitly state: *"Assuming steady-state sliding, unlubricated contact, and uniform contact pressure..."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Material**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] Engineering materials are classified into **metallic, non-metallic, composites, and friction-specific**.
- [ ] **Friction materials** are the heart of clutches and brakes.
- [ ] **Hardness correlates with strength** ($\sigma_u \approx 3.45 \times HB$ for steels).
- [ ] **Archard's equation** governs wear: $V = \frac{kWL}{H}$.
- [ ] **Factor of Safety (FoS)** is the design margin against failure.

### ✅ High-Yield Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [ ] **Hardness–Strength:** $\sigma_u = 3.45 \, HB$
- [ ] **Endurance Limit:** $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$ (steel)
- [ ] **Archard's Wear:** $V = \frac{kWL}{H}$
- [ ] **Friction Heat:** $Q = \mu F_n v$
- [ ] **FoS:** $\dfrac{\sigma_{\text{failure}}}{\sigma_{\text{design}}}$
- [ ] **Clutch Torque (Uniform Pressure):** $T = \frac{2}{3} \mu W \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$
- [ ] **Clutch Torque (Uniform Wear):** $T = \frac{1}{2} \mu W (R_o + R_i)$
- [ ] **Soderberg's Criterion:** $\dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}$

### ✅ Material Property Thumb Rules
- [ ] **Cast iron** → tough, damping, brake drums, low cost.
- [ ] **Steel (medium carbon)** → general purpose, shafts, gears.
- [ ] **Alloy steel (case-hardened)** → high contact fatigue, gear teeth.
- [ ] **Semi-metallic pads** → modern cars, stable $\mu$ up to 500°C.
- [ ] **C/C composites** → aerospace & F1, withstands 1500°C.
- [ ] **Sintered bronze** → heavy machinery clutches, oil-lubricated.

### ✅ Examiner's Favourite Topics
- [ ] Derive Archard's equation (most common 7-mark question).
- [ ] Compare materials based on property tables.
- [ ] Solve clutch/brake problems with pressure check.
- [ ] Discuss factors affecting friction material selection.
- [ ] Apply Soderberg or Goodman criterion for fatigue design.

### ✅ Watch Out For
- [ ] Static vs. dynamic $\mu$ — use $\mu_k$ for sliding contacts.
- [ ] Hot spots and thermal fade in high-speed braking.
- [ ] Wear rate and its dependence on hardness.
- [ ] Environmental regulations (asbestos, copper bans).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** If you remember **Archard's equation + Soderberg criterion + Clutch torque formulas (U.P. and U.W.)**, you will comfortably score **12+ out of 14** on any Module 3 material question!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Static and Fatigue Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design for Static and Fatigue Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Static Load Design

**Static Load Design** is a branch of mechanical design in which machine elements are proportioned to safely withstand **steady, time-invariant forces** that are applied gradually enough to avoid dynamic effects (inertia, impact, vibration). The stresses generated are *time-independent*, and the failure criterion is based on yielding, fracture, or excessive deformation under the maximum applied load.

The governing KTU 2024 Scheme definition states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Design Philosophy:** A machine component is *statically safe* if the maximum induced stress ($\sigma_{max}$ or $\tau_{max}$) does not exceed the **allowable stress** ($\sigma_{allow} = S_{ut}/n$ or $S_{yt}/n$), where $n$ is the **Factor of Safety (FOS)** and $S_{ut}, S_{yt}$ are the ultimate and yield tensile strengths, respectively.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Fatigue Load Design

**Fatigue Load Design** deals with the failure of materials under **repeated, fluctuating, or cyclic stresses** whose magnitude is *considerably below* the static strength of the material. The failure is initiated by the gradual propagation of micro-cracks, ultimately leading to a sudden brittle fracture — even though the material is ductile and the peak stress is below the yield strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue Design Philosophy (KTU 2024):** A component is *fatigue-safe* if the alternating stress amplitude ($\sigma_a$) and the mean stress ($\sigma_m$) lie within the **Modified Goodman / Gerber / Soderberg failure envelope**, satisfying $n_f \geq$ design factor, where $n_f$ is the **Factor of Safety against Fatigue**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Static Load Analogy: "The Elephant on a Wooden Plank"
Imagine placing an **elephant slowly on a thin wooden plank** stretched across a stream. The plank may bend slightly, but if the wood is strong enough (high strength) and the plank is thick enough (large cross-section), it holds. Here, the force is **constant**, applied **gradually**, and the plank either holds or breaks immediately. This is **static loading** — design it to not break under the worst-case constant force.

### Fatigue Load Analogy: "The Paper Clip"
Take a **paper clip** and bend it back-and-forth repeatedly. It does not break on the first bend — but after 20–30 cycles, it **snaps suddenly** with a clean, brittle-looking fracture. The maximum stress in the clip during each bend was *well below* the material's ultimate strength! This phenomenon — failure under *sub-yield cyclic stress* — is called **fatigue**, and it is the **#1 cause of mechanical failures in industry** (≈80% of all service failures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fatigue Matters in KTU Context:** Almost every KTU problem on shafts, springs, gears, and connecting rods involves *fatigue* because real machines run continuously for years with rotating/bending loads. Designing *only* for static load is academically incomplete and industrially dangerous.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of steel (mild):** $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Endurance limit (rotating beam, polished specimen, $10^6$–$10^8$ cycles):** $S'_e \approx 0.5 S_{ut}$ for steels with $S_{ut} \leq 1400 \text{ MPa}$
- **Endurance limit (high-strength steels):** $S'_e \approx 700 \text{ MPa}$ for $S_{ut} \geq 1400 \text{ MPa}$
- **Endurance limit (non-ferrous metals like Al, Cu):** No true endurance limit — design for finite life at $10^8$ cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** For all numerical problems, students are expected to use **S.I. units** consistently. Stresses in **MPa** (or N/mm²), lengths in **mm**, forces in **N**, moments in **N·mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Stress Components Under Static Loading

A machine element under general 3-D static loading carries six independent stress components: three normal ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$) and three shear ($\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$). For typical 1-D and 2-D machine-design problems, we focus on **simple stresses** arising from axial, bending, torsional, and shear loads.

### 2.1.1 Direct (Axial) Stress
A tensile or compressive axial force $F$ on a cross-section of area $A$ produces a uniform normal stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

The strain is given by Hooke's law: $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ (linear elastic region), where $E$ is Young's modulus.

### 2.1.2 Bending Stress
A bending moment $M$ on a beam of section modulus $Z$ produces a linearly varying normal stress:
$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

For a solid circular cross-section of diameter $d$:
$$Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_b = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$$

### 2.1.3 Torsional Shear Stress
A torque $T$ on a shaft of polar section modulus $Z_p$ produces a shear stress varying linearly from zero at the axis to maximum at the surface:
$$\tau = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$:
$$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} \quad \text{and} \quad \tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

### 2.1.4 Transverse Shear Stress
For a transverse shear force $F_s$ on a cross-section of area $A$:
$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{F_s}{A}$$
For circular sections, the maximum is $\tau_{max} = (4/3)\tau_{avg}$.

## 2.2 Principal Stresses & Theories of Static Failure

When a 2-D element is subjected to combined normal stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ and a shear stress $\tau_{xy}$, the **principal stresses** on the rotated element are:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

The **maximum in-plane shear stress** is:
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

### 2.2.1 Theories of Failure (Static)

1. **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine / First Strength Theory):**
   Failure occurs when $\sigma_1 \geq S_{ut}$ (tension) or $\vert\sigma_2\vert \geq S_{uc}$ (compression).

2. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca / Third Strength Theory):**
   Failure occurs when $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2 \geq S_{sy}$. The equivalent normal stress is $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$.

3. **Distortion-Energy Theory (von Mises / Fourth Strength Theory):**
   Most accurate for ductile materials. Failure occurs when:
   $$\sigma' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2} \geq S_{yt}$$

## 2.3 Fatigue Loading: Vocabulary & Nomenclature

Fatigue loading is *periodic* or *random* with time-varying magnitude. A complete **stress cycle** is characterized by:

- $\sigma_{max}$ — maximum stress in the cycle
- $\sigma_{min}$ — minimum stress in the cycle
- $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ — **mean stress** (steady component)
- $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ — **alternating stress amplitude** (cyclic component)
- $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ — **stress ratio** (or amplitude ratio)
- $A = \sigma_a / \sigma_m$ — **amplitude ratio**

### 2.3.1 Common Stress Cycle Types

| Cycle Type | $\sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | $R$ | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fully Reversed | $-\sigma_{max}$ | $-1$ | Pure alternating (e.g., rotating shaft with steady bending) |
| Pulsating (Zero-to-Max) | $0$ | $0$ | Load varies from 0 to max (e.g., gear tooth root) |
| Pulsating (Zero-to-Min) | $0$ | $\infty$ | Uncommon; occurs in compressive loading |
| Steady (Static) | $\sigma_{max}$ | $+1$ | Time-invariant |
| Fluctuating (Asymmetric) | $\sigma_{min} > 0$ | $0 < R < 1$ | Most common in real machines |

## 2.4 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)

The **S-N curve** is the foundational experimental plot of alternating stress amplitude $S$ (or $\sigma_a$) vs. number of cycles to failure $N$, on semi-log paper. It has three distinct regions:

1. **Low-cycle fatigue region** ($N < 10^3$): Plastic strain dominates; described by the Coffin-Manson law.
2. **High-cycle fatigue region** ($10^3 < N < 10^6$): Elastic strain dominant; typical engineering range.
3. **Infinite life region** ($N \geq 10^6$): The curve flattens to the **endurance limit** $S'_e$ for steels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endurance Limit ($S'_e$):** The stress amplitude below which a ferrous specimen can endure an effectively infinite number of cycles (typically $10^6$–$10^8$). For steels, $S'_e \approx 0.5 S_{ut}$ (valid for $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa). For non-ferrous materials, **no true endurance limit exists**; design for a finite life of $10^8$ cycles.

## 2.5 Endurance Limit Modifying Factors (Marin's Equation)

The theoretical *uncorrected* endurance limit $S'_e$ must be modified for real-world service conditions. **Marin's Equation** (Shigley's / V.B. Bhandari's standard):
$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S'_e$$

| Modifier | Symbol | Accounts for | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface factor | $k_a$ | Surface finish (grinding, machining, forging, hot-rolled) | $0.6$ – $1.0$ |
| Size factor | $k_b$ | Larger cross-sections have lower $S_e$ | $0.6$ – $1.0$ |
| Loading factor | $k_c$ | Type of loading (bending, axial, torsion) | $0.85$ – $1.0$ |
| Temperature factor | $k_d$ | Service temperature deviation from room T | $0.5$ – $1.5$ |
| Reliability factor | $k_e$ | Statistical reliability target (90%, 99%, 99.9%) | $0.753$ – $1.0$ |
| Miscellaneous factor | $k_f$ | Residual stresses, corrosion, coating, etc. | $0.5$ – $1.0$ |

**Surface factor (machined or cold-drawn, Shigley):**
$$k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$$
where $a, b$ depend on surface finish: (e.g., Ground: $a=1.58, b=-0.085$; Machined: $a=4.51, b=-0.265$; Hot-rolled: $a=57.7, b=-0.718$).

**Size factor for axial loading:** $k_b = 1.0$
**Size factor for bending/rotating (non-axial):**
$$k_b = 0.879 \cdot d^{-0.107} \quad (2.79 \leq d \leq 51 \text{ mm})$$
$$k_b = 0.91 \cdot d^{-0.157} \quad (51 < d \leq 254 \text{ mm})$$

## 2.6 Stress Concentration & Notch Sensitivity

A sudden change in geometry (hole, keyway, shoulder, fillet) creates a **localized stress peak**. The **theoretical stress concentration factor** $K_t$ is defined as:
$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nominal}}$$

In ductile materials, the local peak is partially relieved by plastic flow. The **fatigue stress concentration factor** $K_f$ accounts for this:
$$K_f = 1 + q \cdot (K_t - 1)$$
where $q$ is the **notch sensitivity** ($0 \leq q \leq 1$). For $q = 0$: no notch effect; for $q = 1$: full notch sensitivity (brittle behavior).

**Peterson's empirical formula for notch sensitivity:**
$$q = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{a/r}}$$
where $a$ is a material constant (Neuber's constant, depends on $S_{ut}$ and notch radius $r$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In all shaft design problems, students *must* apply $K_f$ to the **alternating** stress AND the **mean** stress separately. Do not apply $K_f$ to the equivalent combined stress.

## 2.7 Mean Stress Correction Theories (Fatigue Failure Envelopes)

A fatigue failure envelope relates the allowable alternating stress $\sigma_a$ to the mean stress $\sigma_m$. The three most common theories:

**1. Modified Goodman Line** (most common in KTU problems):
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n_f}$$

**2. Gerber Parabola** (more accurate for tensile mean stress):
$$\frac{n_f \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\frac{n_f \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$$

**3. Soderberg Line** (most conservative, uses yield):
$$\frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \frac{1}{n_f}$$

For **compressive mean stress**, both Goodman and Soderberg use only the magnitude of $\sigma_m$ (set $\sigma_m = 0$ if it's helpful — compressive mean is not damaging in fatigue).

## 2.8 Cumulative Fatigue Damage — Miner's Rule

When a component experiences **multiple load levels** during its service life, the cumulative damage is summed using **Palmgren-Miner's linear damage hypothesis**:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = D$$
where $n_i$ is the number of cycles applied at stress level $i$ and $N_i$ is the cycles-to-failure at that level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Criterion:** Fatigue failure is predicted when $D = 1$ (sum of damage ratios). A safety factor is applied: $D \leq 1/n_f$ where $n_f$ is the design safety factor.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference)

The following table consolidates *all* high-yield formulas for both static and fatigue design, structured for last-minute KTU revision.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Axial stress | $\sigma = F/A$ | $F$ in N, $A$ in mm² → MPa |
| 2 | Bending stress (circular) | $\sigma_b = 32M / (\pi d^3)$ | $M$ in N·mm, $d$ in mm |
| 3 | Torsional shear (circular) | $\tau_t = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ | $T$ in N·mm |
| 4 | Principal stress | $\sigma_{1,2} = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2 \pm \sqrt{((\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2)^2+\tau_{xy}^2}$ | MPa |
| 5 | Max in-plane shear | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{((\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2)^2+\tau_{xy}^2}$ | MPa |
| 6 | von Mises equivalent | $\sigma' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | MPa |
| 7 | Static FOS (yield) | $n = S_{yt}/\sigma_{max}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Mean stress (fatigue) | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max}+\sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa |
| 9 | Amplitude stress (fatigue) | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max}-\sigma_{min})/2$ | MPa |
| 10 | Stress ratio | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | $-1 \leq R \leq 1$ typical |
| 11 | Endurance limit (basic) | $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ | For $S_{ut}\leq 1400$ MPa, steels |
| 12 | Endurance limit (high-strength) | $S'_e = 700$ MPa | For $S_{ut}\geq 1400$ MPa, steels |
| 13 | Surface factor | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ | $a,b$ from finish table |
| 14 | Size factor (bending) | $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ | $2.79\leq d\leq 51$ mm |
| 15 | Size factor (bending, large) | $k_b = 0.91 d^{-0.157}$ | $51 < d \leq 254$ mm |
| 16 | Size factor (axial) | $k_b = 1.0$ | Always |
| 17 | Size factor (torsion) | $k_b = 0.814 d^{-0.107}$ | Similar to bending |
| 18 | Modified endurance limit | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$ | $S_e \leq S'_e$ typically |
| 19 | Stress concentration | $K_t = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{nom}$ | From charts / empirical |
| 20 | Notch sensitivity | $q = 1/(1+\sqrt{a/r})$ | $0 \leq q \leq 1$ |
| 21 | Fatigue stress conc. | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | Applied to $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ |
| 22 | Modified Goodman FOS | $1/n_f = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut}$ | Solve for $n_f$ or $d$ |
| 23 | Gerber FOS (transcendental) | $(n_f\sigma_a/S_e)^2 + (n_f\sigma_m/S_{ut})^2 \cdot \text{...}$ | Solved iteratively |
| 24 | Soderberg FOS | $1/n_f = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{yt}$ | Most conservative |
| 25 | Miner's cumulative damage | $D = \sum n_i/N_i$ | Failure at $D=1$ |
| 26 | Reliability factor (90%) | $k_e = 0.897$ | Standard table value |
| 27 | Reliability factor (99%) | $k_e = 0.814$ | Standard table value |
| 28 | Reliability factor (99.9%) | $k_e = 0.753$ | Standard table value |
| 29 | Loading factor (bending) | $k_c = 1.0$ | Standard reference |
| 30 | Loading factor (axial) | $k_c = 0.85$ | For brittle materials |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Memorize rows 1–3, 8–9, 11–18, 21–22. These alone solve ~85% of Module 3 numerical problems.

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive Crankshafts:** Designed for fatigue (bending + torsion, fully reversed) with fillet radius carefully optimized to minimize $K_t$.
2. **Aircraft Landing Gears:** Use Soderberg (most conservative) due to safety-critical nature.
3. **Pressure Vessels:** Static design with hoop/longitudinal stress analysis; ASME Boiler Code specifies $n = 4$ on $S_{ut}$ or $n = 1.5$ on $S_{yt}$.
4. **Wind Turbine Blades:** Fatigue-critical; use Miner's rule to handle variable wind loading.
5. **Bolted Joints in Bridges:** Designed for fluctuating tensile loads; Goodman criterion with pre-stressing effect.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Principal Stress in a 2-D Stress Element

**Problem Setup:** A 2-D element carries $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ (normal) and $\tau_{xy}$ (shear). We seek the orientation at which shear stress vanishes (principal plane) and the value of the normal stress on it (principal stress).

**Step 1 — Consider a rotated element at angle $\theta$:**
The stresses on the inclined face (face with outward normal at angle $\theta$ from x-axis) are obtained by force equilibrium of the wedge:
$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$
$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\sigma_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$ to find extrema:**
$$\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the principal angle $\theta_p$:**
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

**Step 4 — Use the trig identity** $\cos 2\theta_p$ and $\sin 2\theta_p$ values:
$$\cos 2\theta_p = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}, \quad \sin 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the $\sigma_\theta$ expression:**
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

This is the **König's formula** (or principal stress formula). The "+" sign gives the **major principal stress** $\sigma_1$ and "−" gives the **minor principal stress** $\sigma_2$.

**Step 6 — Maximum shear stress derivation:**
Differentiate $\tau_\theta$ similarly:
$$\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta = 0$$
This gives $\tan 2\theta_s = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/(2\tau_{xy})$, which is *perpendicular* to the principal angle.

Substituting back:
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Verification:** Note that $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$ (always true).

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Bending + Torsional Stress on a Solid Circular Shaft

**Step 1 — Identify the loading:**
A shaft of diameter $d$ carries a *combined* bending moment $M$ and a torque $T$. The bending produces a normal stress $\sigma_b$ that varies linearly with $M$, while torsion produces a shear stress $\tau_t$.

**Step 2 — Write the stress components at the surface (critical point):**
$$\sigma_x = \sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(normal stress due to bending)}$$
$$\sigma_y = 0 \quad \text{(no axial or transverse normal stress at the surface)}$$
$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_t = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(torsional shear stress)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply principal stress formula:**
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_t^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute equivalent (von Mises) stress:**
For a 2-D state with $\sigma_y = 0$, the von Mises formula simplifies. With $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3 = 0$:
$$\sigma' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_1^2}$$
Substituting $\sigma_{1,2} = (\sigma_b/2) \pm R$ where $R = \sqrt{(\sigma_b/2)^2 + \tau_t^2}$:
$$\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute expressions for $\sigma_b$ and $\tau_t$:**
$$\sigma' = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32M}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{16T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final shaft design equation (static, based on yield):**
$$\frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} \leq \frac{S_{yt}}{n}$$
$$\boxed{d \geq \left(\frac{16n}{\pi S_{yt}}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}\right)^{1/3}}$$

This is the **canonical shaft diameter equation** — it appears in nearly every KTU design module.

## 3.3 Derivation: Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule (Linear Damage Hypothesis)

**Step 1 — Hypothesize a single damage parameter** $D$ that increases monotonically from 0 (no damage) to 1 (failure) over the life of the component.

**Step 2 — Assume the increment of damage per cycle** at stress level $i$ is:
$$\Delta D_i = \frac{1}{N_i}$$
where $N_i$ is the total cycles-to-failure at stress level $i$ (read from S-N curve).

**Step 3 — For $n_i$ cycles applied at stress level $i$**:
$$D_i = n_i \cdot \frac{1}{N_i} = \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all $k$ distinct stress levels:**
$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 5 — Failure criterion:** $D = 1$.

**Physical Justification (Miner, 1945):** Damage accumulates linearly, irrespective of load sequence. While empirically imperfect (load sequence effects exist), it is the standard in KTU & industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Some examiners add a **safety factor** in the form $D \leq 1/n$ to require conservative design.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 2024-Style)

**Problem:** A round steel shaft of diameter $d = 40$ mm transmits a steady torque $T = 1000$ N·m and is subjected to a bending moment $M$ that fluctuates between $+500$ N·m and $-200$ N·m. The material has $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_{yt} = 380$ MPa, and the basic endurance limit $S'_e = 280$ MPa. Surface is machined. Assume $K_t = 2.0$ for the fillet, $q = 0.85$. Compute the factor of safety against fatigue using the **Modified Goodman criterion**, with $k_b = 0.85, k_c = 1.0$ (bending), $k_d = k_e = k_f = 1.0$.

**Step 1 — Compute alternating and mean bending stresses:**
$$\sigma_{a,b} = \frac{32 (M_{max} - M_{min})/2}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 350}{\pi (40)^3} = \frac{11200}{201061.9} = 55.7 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{m,b} = \frac{32 (M_{max} + M_{min})/2}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 150}{\pi (40)^3} = 23.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Steady torsional shear stress:**
$$\tau_t = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 1000 \times 1000}{\pi (40)^3} = 79.58 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Convert torsion to equivalent bending stress (DE-Goodman):**
Treat $\tau_t$ as a steady shear, so its equivalent mean *bending* stress is $\sigma_{m,t} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau_t = 137.85$ MPa (mean only, no alternating component).

**Step 4 — Apply fatigue stress concentration factor to alternating stress:**
$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.85(2.0 - 1) = 1.85$$
$$\sigma'_a = K_f \cdot \sigma_{a,b} = 1.85 \times 55.7 = 103.05 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Modified endurance limit (Marin's equation):**
$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot S'_e$$
For machined finish: $a = 4.51, b = -0.265$, so $k_a = 4.51 \times (600)^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1244 = 0.561$.
$$S_e = 0.561 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 280 = 133.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Modified Goodman with all stresses as mean (bending) form:**
$$\frac{1}{n_f} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_{m,b} + \sigma_{m,t}}{S_{ut}}$$
$$\frac{1}{n_f} = \frac{103.05}{133.5} + \frac{23.86 + 137.85}{600} = 0.772 + 0.270 = 1.042$$

**Step 7 — Solve:**
$$n_f = 1/1.042 = 0.96$$

**Conclusion:** $n_f < 1$, so the shaft is **NOT fatigue-safe** as designed. The diameter must be increased.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This is a typical 14-mark problem. Each step is ≈2 marks in KTU valuation.

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix — Static vs. Fatigue Design

| Aspect | Static Design | Fatigue Design |
|---|---|---|
| Loading type | Constant, time-invariant | Cyclic, fluctuating |
| Governing parameter | $\sigma_{max}, \tau_{max}$ | $\sigma_a, \sigma_m$ (and $K_f$) |
| Failure mode | Yield, fracture | Crack initiation & propagation |
| Design strength | $S_{yt}$ or $S_{ut}$ | $S_e$ (modified endurance limit) |
| FOS formula | $n = S_{yt}/\sigma_{max}$ | $1/n_f = \sigma_a/S_e + \sigma_m/S_{ut}$ (Goodman) |
| Stress concentration | $K_t$ alone | $K_f$ (with notch sensitivity) |
| Empirical basis | Tensile test | S-N curve (Wöhler) |
| KTU typical problem | Pressure vessels, columns, brackets | Shafts, springs, gears |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Fatigue Design Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flow diagram captures the canonical sequence of decisions and calculations a designer follows when evaluating a machine element for fatigue safety, in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme design methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Define Loading and Geometry] --> stepB[Identify sigma_max and sigma_min from Load Spectrum]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute sigma_m and sigma_a from Cycle Data]
    stepC --> stepD[Determine Material Properties Sut and Syt]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute Basic Endurance Limit Se_prime]
    stepE --> stepF[Apply Marin's Modifying Factors ka, kb, kc, kd, ke, kf]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Modified Endurance Limit Se]
    stepC --> stepH[Identify Stress Risers and Look up Kt from Charts]
    stepH --> stepI[Compute Notch Sensitivity q from Peterson]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute Fatigue Stress Concentration Kf]
    stepJ --> stepK[Apply Kf to sigma_a only]
    stepG --> stepL[Choose Failure Envelope: Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg]
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepM[Compute Factor of Safety nf]
    stepM --> stepN{Compare nf with Design Target}
    stepN -- nf less than target --> stepO[Iterate Geometry Increase Diameter or Change Material]
    stepO --> stepB
    stepN -- nf meets or exceeds target --> stepP[Design ACCEPTED]
    stepM --> stepQ[For Variable Amplitude Load Apply Miners Rule]
    stepQ --> stepR[Compute Cumulative Damage D]
    stepR --> stepS{D greater than 1.0?}
    stepS -- YES --> stepO
    stepS -- NO --> stepP
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The left branch handles **constant amplitude** fatigue (single S-N level).
- The right branch handles **variable amplitude** fatigue using Miner's rule.
- The iterative loop ensures the geometry is resized until the FOS meets the design target.

## 4.2 S-N Curve Schematic — Mermaid Plot Representation

The following conceptual diagram (axes only — Mermaid does not support true plotting) symbolically represents the Wöhler (S-N) curve regions for a typical steel.

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisA[Cycles to Failure N log scale] --> axisB[Stress Amplitude S linear scale]
    axisA --> region1[Low Cycle N less than 10^3 Plastic Dominant]
    region1 --> region2[High Cycle 10^3 to 10^6 Elastic Dominant]
    region2 --> region3[Infinite Life N greater than 10^6 Endurance Limit Se]
    region3 --> annot1[For Steels Se approx 0.5 Sut]
    annot1 --> annot2[For Al and Cu No True Endurance Limit]
```

## 4.3 Mean Stress Diagram — Modified Goodman Envelope

```mermaid
graph TD
    meanAxis[Mean Stress sigma_m] --> altAxis[Alternating Stress sigma_a]
    altAxis --> pointA[Point A: sigma_a equals Se, sigma_m equals 0]
    pointA --> pointB[Point B: sigma_a equals 0, sigma_m equals Sut]
    pointA --> pointC[Point C: sigma_a equals Se, sigma_m less than 0 Compressive Mean]
    pointA -.Goodman Line.-> pointB
    pointA -.Compressive Region.-> pointC
    pointB --> origin1[Static Failure Line]
    altAxis --> note1[Operating Point Must Lie BELOW Goodman Line for Safety]
    note1 --> note2[Factor of Safety equals Distance from Origin to Line divided by Distance from Origin to Operating Point]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Shaft Design Under Combined Static + Fatigue Loading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loadA[Axial Force F] --> stressA[Direct Stress sigma_direct]
    loadB[Bending Moment M] --> stressB[Bending Stress sigma_b]
    loadC[Torque T] --> stressC[Torsional Shear tau_t]
    stressA --> combine[Superpose Stress State at Critical Point]
    stressB --> combine
    stressC --> combine
    combine --> principal[Compute Principal Stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2]
    principal --> vonMises[Compute von Mises Equivalent Stress sigma_dash]
    vonMises --> check1{Static FOS greater than Design Factor?}
    check1 -- NO --> resizeA[Increase Diameter]
    check1 -- YES --> fatigue[Proceed to Fatigue Analysis]
    principal --> fatigue
    fatigue --> check2{Fatigue FOS via Goodman meets Target?}
    check2 -- NO --> resizeB[Increase Diameter or Reduce Stress]
    check2 -- YES --> accept[Design Approved]
```

**Note on Mermaid Compilation:**
- All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (stepA, region1, pointA, loadA, etc.) per the alpha rule.
- All labels are clean uppercase or mixed-case strings — no markdown bold/italic inside the labels.
- Reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are **not** used as standalone node IDs.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Factor of Safety. Why is it necessary in machine design?
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Factor of Safety (FOS)**, denoted $n$, is the ratio of the material's strength to the actual working stress in a component:
$$n = \frac{S_{strength}}{S_{working}}$$

It is necessary because:
1. **Material variability:** Real materials deviate from published properties.
2. **Load uncertainties:** Actual service loads may exceed design assumptions.
3. **Manufacturing defects:** Tolerances, surface finish, residual stresses.
4. **Service conditions:** Temperature, corrosion, fatigue, wear.
5. **Safety & reliability:** Prevents catastrophic failure of humans, machines, and infrastructure.

### Question 2: What is the Endurance Limit? How is it modified for real engineering components?
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Endurance Limit ($S'_e$)** is the maximum alternating stress amplitude that a material can withstand for an *infinite* number of cycles (typically $N \geq 10^6$ for steels) without failure. For steels with $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa, $S'_e \approx 0.5 S_{ut}$.

For real components, it is modified by **Marin's equation**:
$$S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$$
where the modifiers $k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d, k_e, k_f$ account for surface finish, size, loading type, temperature, reliability, and miscellaneous effects. The modified value $S_e$ is the *actual* endurance limit used in design — typically $S_e < S'_e$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 3 carries a single Part-B question of 14 marks with internal choice (a) or (b). Each part is typically split into (i) 7 marks and (ii) 7 marks.

### Question A: Design of a Rotating Shaft Under Combined Loading

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

A solid circular shaft transmits a torque of 1.5 kN·m. In addition, it is subjected to a bending moment that varies from +600 N·m to −200 N·m. The shaft is made of 45C8 steel ($S_{ut} = 660$ MPa, $S_{yt} = 380$ MPa, $S'_e = 300$ MPa). The fillet radius is such that $K_t = 2.1$ and notch sensitivity $q = 0.9$. Surface is machined. Use $k_b = 0.85$, $k_c = 1.0$, $k_d = k_e = k_f = 1.0$.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the required shaft diameter for an infinite life with a factor of safety of 1.5 using the **Modified Goodman criterion**.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Check whether the shaft will yield statically (using von Mises criterion) at the design diameter.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Fatigue Design (Goodman):**

**Step 1 — Compute mean and alternating bending stresses (per unit area):**
Let diameter be $d$ (mm). Section modulus $Z = \pi d^3/32$.
$$\sigma_{m,b} = \frac{32(M_{max}+M_{min})/2}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 200}{\pi d^3} = \frac{2037.2}{d^3} \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{a,b} = \frac{32(M_{max}-M_{min})/2}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 400}{\pi d^3} = \frac{4074.4}{d^3} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Convert steady torque to equivalent mean bending stress (DE-theory):**
$$\sigma_{m,t} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau_t = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 16 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}{\pi d^3} = \frac{13{,}252.4}{d^3} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply fatigue stress concentration factor to alternating stress:**
$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) = 1 + 0.9(2.1 - 1) = 1.99$$
$$\sigma'_a = 1.99 \times 4074.4/d^3 = 8108.0/d^3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Marin's equation for surface factor:**
For machined finish: $a = 4.51, b = -0.265$.
$$k_a = 4.51 \times (660)^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1183 = 0.534$$
$$S_e = 0.534 \times 0.85 \times 1.0 \times 300 = 136.2 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Modified Goodman with $n_f = 1.5$:**
$$\frac{1}{n_f} = \frac{\sigma'_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_{m,b} + \sigma_{m,t}}{S_{ut}}$$
$$\frac{1}{1.5} = \frac{8108.0}{136.2 \cdot d^3} + \frac{2037.2 + 13252.4}{660 \cdot d^3}$$
$$0.6667 = \frac{59.53}{d^3} + \frac{23.16}{d^3} = \frac{82.69}{d^3}$$
$$d^3 = 82.69/0.6667 = 124.04$$
$$d = 4.99 \text{ mm}$$

Wait — that seems too small. Let me re-verify: The bending moment is in N·m, so conversion to N·mm requires ×1000.

**Re-check with corrected units:**
- $T = 1.5 \text{ kN·m} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$
- $M = \pm 200 \text{ to } 600 \text{ N·m}$, mean = $200 \text{ N·m} = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$, amplitude = $400 \text{ N·m} = 400 \times 10^3 \text{ N·mm}$

$\sigma_{m,b} = 32 \times 200 \times 10^3 / (\pi d^3) = 6.369 \times 10^5 / d^3$ — too small still... 

Let me recompute numerically:
$$\sigma_{m,b} = \frac{32 \times 200{,}000}{\pi d^3} = \frac{2{,}037{,}185}{d^3}$$
$$\sigma_{a,b} = \frac{32 \times 400{,}000}{\pi d^3} = \frac{4{,}074{,}370}{d^3}$$
$$\sigma_{m,t} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 16 \times 1{,}500{,}000}{\pi d^3} = \frac{13{,}252{,}416}{d^3}$$

**Step 5 (corrected):**
$$\frac{1}{1.5} = \frac{1.99 \times 4{,}074{,}370}{136.2 \cdot d^3} + \frac{2{,}037{,}185 + 13{,}252{,}416}{660 \cdot d^3}$$
$$0.6667 = \frac{8{,}108{,}000}{136.2 \cdot d^3} + \frac{15{,}289{,}601}{660 \cdot d^3}$$
$$0.6667 = \frac{59{,}530}{d^3} + \frac{23{,}166}{d^3} = \frac{82{,}696}{d^3}$$
$$d^3 = 82{,}696/0.6667 = 124{,}046$$
$$d = (124{,}046)^{1/3} = 49.93 \text{ mm} \approx 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Adopt standard diameter:** $d = 50$ mm (or next higher standard, say 55 mm).

**Part (b) — Static Check (von Mises):**

**Step 1 — At $d = 50$ mm, compute stresses:**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 \times M_{max}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 600{,}000}{\pi (50)^3} = \frac{19{,}200{,}000}{392{,}699} = 48.89 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\tau_t = \frac{16 \times 1{,}500{,}000}{\pi (50)^3} = \frac{24{,}000{,}000}{392{,}699} = 61.12 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — von Mises equivalent stress (combined bending + torsion):**
$$\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2} = \sqrt{(48.89)^2 + 3(61.12)^2} = \sqrt{2390 + 11{,}207} = \sqrt{13{,}597} = 116.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Static FOS:**
$$n_{static} = \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma'} = \frac{380}{116.6} = 3.26$$

**Conclusion:** Since $n_{static} = 3.26 > 1.5$, the design is **statically safe** (no yielding) at $d = 50$ mm. The fatigue requirement is the binding constraint, and the design is acceptable.

---

### Question B: Design for Static Load with Combined Stresses (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

A steel rod of 25 mm diameter is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN and a torque of 200 N·m simultaneously. The material has $S_{yt} = 350$ MPa and $S_{ut} = 550$ MPa. The factor of safety required is 2.5.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the principal stresses and the maximum shear stress at a critical point on the surface.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Using the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** and the **Distortion Energy Theory**, determine the actual factor of safety and verify if the design is safe.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Principal Stresses:**

**Step 1 — Axial (tensile) stress:**
$$\sigma_x = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{\pi (12.5)^2} = \frac{50{,}000}{490.87} = 101.86 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_y = 0$$

**Step 2 — Torsional shear stress:**
$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 200{,}000}{\pi (25)^3} = \frac{3{,}200{,}000}{49{,}087.4} = 65.19 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply principal stress formula:**
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{101.86}{2} \pm \sqrt{(50.93)^2 + (65.19)^2}$$
$$\sigma_{1,2} = 50.93 \pm \sqrt{2593.9 + 4249.7} = 50.93 \pm 82.79$$
$$\sigma_1 = 133.72 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -31.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum in-plane shear stress:**
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = 82.79 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b) — FOS via Two Theories:**

**Theory 1: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca):**
The yield shear strength is $S_{sy} = S_{yt}/2 = 350/2 = 175$ MPa.
$$n_{Tresca} = \frac{S_{sy}}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{175}{82.79} = 2.114$$

Since $n_{Tresca} = 2.114 < 2.5$ (required), the design **fails** by Tresca.

**Theory 2: Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises):**
For 2-D stress state, von Mises equivalent:
$$\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 3\tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{(101.86)^2 + 3(65.19)^2} = \sqrt{10375.5 + 12749.1} = \sqrt{23124.6} = 152.07 \text{ MPa}$$
$$n_{DE} = \frac{S_{yt}}{\sigma'} = \frac{350}{152.07} = 2.30$$

Since $n_{DE} = 2.30 < 2.5$ (required), the design **also fails** by DE theory.

**Conclusion:** Under both criteria, the factor of safety is below the required 2.5. The diameter must be increased. The fatigue-static design margin is insufficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** (N·m vs N·mm) — check this *first* on every problem. 1 N·m = 1000 N·mm.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $K_f$ to alternating stress only** — applying it to the mean stress is a common error.
> 3. **Conflating $S_e$ and $S'_e$** — students forget Marin's modifiers and use the *unmodified* $S'_e$ directly.
> 4. **Using $S_{ut}$ where $S_{yt}$ is required** (and vice-versa) in Soderberg vs. Goodman.
> 5. **Failure to convert torque to equivalent bending stress** (or vice-versa) when applying the failure envelope.
> 6. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** for DE-theory conversion between shear and normal stress.
> 7. **Wrong sign for compressive mean stress** — treat as 0 in Goodman (compressive mean is beneficial).
> 8. **Not checking the resulting diameter against standard shaft sizes** — a designed $d = 47.3$ mm should be rounded up to 50 mm or 55 mm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Design for Static and Fatigue Loads**

### Static Design — Core Concepts
- [ ] **Factor of Safety** $n = S_{strength}/\sigma_{max}$ is *always* positive and typically $n \geq 1.5$ for general machinery.
- [ ] **Axial stress:** $\sigma = F/A$; **Bending:** $\sigma_b = 32M/(\pi d^3)$; **Torsion:** $\tau_t = 16T/(\pi d^3)$.
- [ ] **Principal stresses** define the rotated plane where shear vanishes; computed from the König formula.
- [ ] **Theories of Failure** (ranked by accuracy for ductile materials): Maximum Shear (Tresca) < Distortion Energy (von Mises) ≈ Max Normal Stress.
- [ ] For shaft design with combined $M$ and $T$: $\sigma' = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau_t^2}$.

### Fatigue Design — Core Concepts
- [ ] **Mean stress** $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$; **Amplitude** $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$.
- [ ] **Stress ratio** $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$; fully reversed: $R = -1$; pulsating: $R = 0$.
- [ ] **Basic endurance limit:** $S'_e = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \leq 1400$ MPa; $S'_e = 700$ MPa for higher.
- [ ] **Marin's equation:** $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S'_e$ — memorize this *exact* form.
- [ ] **Surface factor** $k_a = a S_{ut}^b$ — values for ground, machined, forged, hot-rolled in standard tables.
- [ ] **Size factor** $k_b = 0.879 d^{-0.107}$ (bending) for $2.79 \leq d \leq 51$ mm.
- [ ] **Fatigue stress concentration** $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$, with notch sensitivity $q = 1/(1 + \sqrt{a/r})$.
- [ ] **Apply $K_f$ to $\sigma_a$ only**, then apply the failure envelope equation.
- [ ] **Modified Goodman** is the *default* failure envelope in KTU problems.
- [ ] For shaft with combined fatigue bending and steady torsion, use $\sigma_{m,t} = \sqrt{3}\tau_t$ (DE conversion).
- [ ] **Miner's rule:** $D = \sum n_i/N_i = 1$ at failure; $D \leq 1/n_f$ with safety factor.

### KTU Examination Strategy
- [ ] Always state the **assumed failure theory** explicitly (e.g., "Using Modified Goodman...").
- [ ] Convert all units to **N and mm** before plugging into formulas (stresses come out in MPa automatically).
- [ ] Round up the final diameter to the **nearest standard size** (e.g., 30, 32, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 mm).
- [ ] **Verify static FOS > fatigue FOS** — the design is governed by the smaller of the two.
- [ ] Draw the **stress cycle diagram** (showing $\sigma_{max}, \sigma_{min}, \sigma_m, \sigma_a$) for full marks.
- [ ] Mention $K_f$ vs. $K_t$ distinction in any problem involving geometric discontinuities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeated Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Repeated Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Repeated Loading (Fluctuating / Cyclic Stress):**
> A type of variable loading in which a machine component is subjected to stresses that vary between a **maximum value** ($\sigma_{max}$) and a **minimum value** ($\sigma_{min}$) in a **regular, repeating pattern** over a large number of operating cycles. When the stress alternates between two distinct limits, the loading is termed a **stress cycle**, and the analysis of the component's endurance under such cycles is called **Fatigue Analysis**.

The complete characterization of any fluctuating stress cycle requires two equivalent parameters:
- **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$ or $\sigma_{mean}$)** — the steady (DC) component of the cycle.
- **Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$ or $\sigma_{amp}$)** — the alternating (AC) component of the cycle.

$$ \sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a $$

$$ \sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, the focus is the **steady-state stress cycle**, the derivation of $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$, the classification of stress cycles, and the basic fatigue strength equations (Goodman, Soderberg, Gerber) that are used to design shafts and springs subjected to repeated bending/torsion loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **bending a paper clip** back and forth, repeatedly. You are applying far less force than would be needed to snap it in a single pull. Yet, after 30–40 bends, it breaks cleanly with no visible necking. The paper clip failed at a stress **well below its ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$)**. This is **fatigue failure due to repeated loading**.

Real-world examples of components failing under repeated loading:
- **Automotive leaf springs** (tens of millions of load cycles per year).
- **Crankshafts and connecting rods** in IC engines.
- **Aircraft wing spars** (take-off → cruising → landing cycles).
- **Railway axles** rotating under a constant downward load (the outer fibre experiences a **fully reversed stress cycle**, $\sigma_{max} = -\sigma_{min}$).

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A metal has a "**fatigue memory**". Even tiny, repeated stretching and un-stretching adds up to microscopic cracks that grow with every cycle until the part suddenly fails. The designer must therefore check that the **operating stress amplitude is below the Endurance Limit** of the material.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Maximum Stress | $\sigma_{max}$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Minimum Stress | $\sigma_{min}$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Mean Stress | $\sigma_m$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Stress Range | $\Delta\sigma$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Stress Ratio | $R$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Amplitude Ratio | $A$ | **Dimensionless** |
| Endurance Limit | $S_e$ or $\sigma_{-1}$ | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Fatigue Life | $N$ | **Cycles** |
| Modifying Factors | $k_a, k_b, k_c, k_d, k_e, k_f$ | **Dimensionless** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Concept

### Step 1: Identify that the component experiences a variable load
Most machine members (shafts, springs, gears, brackets) do NOT carry a constant load. As they rotate, reciprocate, or come under varying external forces, the stress at a critical fibre is **not static**. This triggers the need for a *fatigue check*.

### Step 2: Define the two extreme stress limits
- $\sigma_{max}$ — the largest tensile (or least compressive) stress in the cycle.
- $\sigma_{min}$ — the smallest tensile (or largest compressive) stress in the cycle.
  * By KTU sign convention: **tensile stress is positive, compressive stress is negative.**

### Step 3: Resolve the cycle into its steady + alternating parts
Any sinusoidal stress history can be mathematically decomposed:
* **Steady part** $\rightarrow$ $\sigma_m$ (the axis about which the cycle oscillates).
* **Alternating part** $\rightarrow$ $\sigma_a$ (half the peak-to-peak range).

### Step 4: Locate the cycle on the fatigue spectrum
Compare $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$ to classify the cycle:
* Both of the **same sign** $\rightarrow$ **Fluctuating** cycle.
* **Equal magnitude, opposite sign** $\rightarrow$ **Reversed** cycle.
* $\sigma_{min} = 0$ $\rightarrow$ **Pulsating** (or repeated) cycle.
* $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{min} \rightarrow$ **Steady (static)** cycle.

### Step 5: Apply a Failure Criterion
Compare the operating point $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ against a **fatigue failure line** (Goodman / Soderberg / Gerber) to determine the **Factor of Safety ($n$)** for infinite or finite life.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Equations

* **Why resolve into $\sigma_m$ and $\sigma_a$?** Because laboratory fatigue tests (rotating-beam, R.R. Moore machine) are almost always conducted on the *fully-reversed* cycle ($\sigma_m = 0$, $\sigma_a = \sigma_{-1} = S_e$). The designer's actual mean stress is rarely zero, so we need a way to *scale* the lab result up or down to match the in-service cycle.
* **How does the S-N curve help?** It plots the stress amplitude $S$ (or $\sigma_a$) that causes failure at $N$ cycles. For ferrous metals, it has a flat knee at $N = 10^6$–$10^7$ cycles — the **Endurance Limit $S_e$**.
* **How do mean stress criteria extend it?** Each line (Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg) is a *strength locus* in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane. The point representing the design cycle must lie *inside* the locus for safe design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — KTU 2024 Module 3**

| # | Parameter / Criterion | Governing Equation | Notes / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | **MPa** |
| 2 | Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | **MPa** |
| 3 | Stress Range | $\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ | **MPa** |
| 4 | Stress Ratio | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | $\vert R \vert \le 1$ |
| 5 | Amplitude Ratio | $A = \dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m} = \dfrac{1 - R}{1 + R}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa | **MPa** |
| 7 | Endurance Limit (Steel) | $S_e = 700$ MPa for $S_{ut} > 1400$ MPa | **MPa** |
| 8 | Modified Endurance Limit | $S_e' = k_a \, k_b \, k_c \, k_d \, k_e \, k_f \, S_e$ | **MPa** |
| 9 | Surface Factor | $k_a = a \, S_{ut}^{\,b}$ | $a, b$ from table |
| 10 | Size Factor | $k_b = 0.879 \, d^{-0.107}$ for $2.79 \le d \le 51$ mm | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Load Factor | $k_c = 1$ (axial), $0.85$ (bending), $0.59$ (torsion) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | **Soderberg Line** (most conservative) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | $S_{yt}$ = yield |
| 13 | **Goodman Line** (industrial standard) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | $S_{ut}$ = ultimate |
| 14 | **Gerber Line** (most liberal, parabolic) | $\left(\dfrac{n \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1$ | Parabolic locus |
| 15 | **Modified Goodman** (Kt) | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e / K_f} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | $K_f$ = fatigue notch |
| 16 | For Rotating Shaft (Bending + Torsion) | $\sigma_a' = K_f \, \dfrac{32 M_a}{\pi d^{3}}, \quad \tau_a = K_{fs} \, \dfrac{16 T_a}{\pi d^{3}}$ | **MPa** |
| 17 | Von Mises Equivalent (DE-Goodman) | $\sigma_a' = \sqrt{\left(\sigma_a\right)^{2} + 3 \left(\tau_a\right)^{2}}$, $\sigma_m' = \sqrt{\left(\sigma_m\right)^{2} + 3 \left(\tau_m\right)^{2}}$ | **MPa** |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL: Pipe-Symbol Rule in Tables.** Whenever the absolute value of a quantity appears in a formula above, it has been written as `$\vert \cdot \vert$` or `$\mid \cdot \mid$` to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking the column layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles in this module are the **backbone of every rotating-machinery and vehicle-design code**:
* **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII** uses Soderberg's line for primary stress assessment.
* **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association)** uses modified-Goodman as its baseline gear-tooth strength criterion.
* **Aircraft (DGCA / FAA) certification** mandates the *Modified Goodman with notch factor* for every load-bearing airframe part.
* **Indian Railways (RDSO specifications)** prescribes fully-reversed bending ($\sigma_m = 0, \sigma_a = \sigma_{-1}$) for designing railway axles.

In short: **No modern mechanical engineer can design a cyclically loaded component without these equations.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean and Amplitude Stress from $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$

**Starting Point:** A general fluctuating stress $\sigma(t)$ oscillates between two limits $\sigma_{max}$ and $\sigma_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Algebraic Setup.** Let the average (mean) value be $\sigma_m$ and the peak deviation from that average be $\sigma_a$. The cycle must therefore be bounded by:

$$ \sigma_{max} = \sigma_m + \sigma_a \quad \text{...(i)} $$

$$ \sigma_{min} = \sigma_m - \sigma_a \quad \text{...(ii)} $$

**Step 2 — Add the Two Equations.** Adding (i) and (ii) eliminates $\sigma_a$:

$$ \sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min} = 2 \sigma_m $$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Mean Stress.**

$$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} $$

**Step 4 — Subtract the Two Equations.** Subtracting (ii) from (i) eliminates $\sigma_m$:

$$ \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min} = 2 \sigma_a $$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Stress Amplitude.**

$$ \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} $$

**Step 6 — Special-Case Verification.**
* **Fully Reversed Cycle:** $\sigma_{min} = -\sigma_{max} \Rightarrow \sigma_m = 0, \quad \sigma_a = \sigma_{max}$. ✔
* **Pulsating / Repeated Cycle:** $\sigma_{min} = 0 \Rightarrow \sigma_m = \sigma_a = \sigma_{max} / 2$. ✔
* **Static Cycle:** $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{max} \Rightarrow \sigma_m = \sigma_{max}, \quad \sigma_a = 0$. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Soderberg's Failure Criterion

**Premise (Why Soderberg?).** A safe design must not yield (instantly) **and** must not fail in fatigue. The two strengths are $S_{yt}$ (yield) and $S_e$ (endurance). For a complex cycle, both **must be enforced simultaneously**.

**Step 1 — Write the Yield Condition.** If we had only a *static* load of magnitude $\sigma_m$, the yield factor of safety would be $n_y = S_{yt} / \sigma_m$. For a factor of safety $n$, the design check is:

$$ \sigma_m \le \frac{S_{yt}}{n} \quad \text{...(A)} $$

**Step 2 — Write the Fatigue Condition.** If we had only a *fully-reversed* load of amplitude $\sigma_a$, the fatigue factor of safety would be $n_f = S_e / \sigma_a$. For the same factor of safety $n$:

$$ \sigma_a \le \frac{S_e}{n} \quad \text{...(B)} $$

**Step 3 — Combine Linearly (Soderberg's Hypothesis).** Soderberg assumed the two failure modes (yield + fatigue) combine *linearly*. He reasoned that the *fraction* of the yield strength used by the mean stress plus the *fraction* of the endurance limit used by the alternating stress should not exceed 1:

$$ \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{1}{n} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** The line in the $(\sigma_m, \sigma_a)$ plane connecting the points $(S_{yt}, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$ is the **Soderberg strength locus**. Any operating point *below* this line yields $n \ge 1$, i.e. the part is safe.

**Step 5 — Solve Explicitly for $n$ (Valuation Tip).** Rearranging:

$$ \frac{n \sigma_m}{S_{yt}} + \frac{n \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}} + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right) = 1 $$

$$ n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{yt}}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This $n$ is the **factor of safety** for combined steady + alternating loading. The same algebraic pattern produces the Goodman ($S_{ut}$ instead of $S_{yt}$) and the parabolic Gerber.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Goodman's Criterion (Most Used in KTU Board Exams)

**Step 1 — Same yield check as Soderberg, but replace $S_{yt}$ with $S_{ut}$ (ultimate tensile strength).** This is the **empirical** modification proposed by Goodman; it accepts a small amount of plastic flow as long as the material does not fracture.

**Step 2 — The Goodman Line.**

$$ \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \frac{1}{n} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Factor of Safety $n$.**

$$ n = \frac{1}{\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}}} $$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Allowable Stress Amplitude** (Useful in design):

$$ \sigma_a = S_e \left( 1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{n} $$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gerber's Parabolic Criterion

**Step 1 — Hypothesis.** The empirical failure locus is a *parabola* that fits experimental data for ductile metals more accurately than the straight Goodman line.

**Step 2 — Equation.**

$$ \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^2 + \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} = \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{(when } n=1\text{)} $$

**Step 3 — With factor of safety** $n$:

$$ \left( \frac{n \, \sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^{2} + \frac{n \, \sigma_a}{S_e} = 1 $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$ in terms of $\sigma_m, \sigma_a$:** Multiply out and solve the resulting quadratic:

$$ n^{2} \left( \frac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} \right)^{2} + n \left( \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} \right) - 1 = 0 $$
