**Example sanity-check:** If $W = 25 \text{ kN}$, $L = 600 \text{ mm}$, $\sigma_b = 500 \text{ MPa}$, $b = 60 \text{ mm}$, $t = 10 \text{ mm}$: $$n = \dfrac{6 \cdot 25000 \cdot 600}{500 \cdot 60 \cdot 100} = \dfrac{90{,}000{,}000}{3{,}000{,}000} = 30$$ So 30 leaves would be required — impractically many! This is why leaf springs are used in **heavy vehicles** (trucks, locomotives) and not in light passenger cars, which use coil springs. --- ### 3.4 Workaround for Excessively Many Leaves — The Variable-Strength (Graduated) Spring To reduce $n$ while keeping $\sigma_b$ within the elastic limit, **graduated leaves** are used. The basic idea: - The *bending moment* $M(x)$ varies along the length as $M(x) = W \cdot x / 2$ (where $x$ is measured from the centre). - The number of effective leaves $n_{eff}(x)$ at any section is *equal to the number of leaves whose length exceeds that section*. - The stress in any section of the $i$-th graduated leaf = (load on that leaf) / (section modulus of that leaf). In the **ideal case of a parabolic leaf spring** (all leaves of equal strength throughout), the spring takes the form of a *parabola of constant stress*, with the ends of the leaves tracing out a parabolic envelope. This is the basis of the **modern tapered/composite leaf spring** used in contemporary vehicles. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Semi-Elliptical Leaf Spring ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vehicle Frame
Fixed Support] -->|Shackle Pin| B[Master Leaf
with Eye End] B --> C[Graduated Leaf 1
Top Reinforcement] C --> D[Graduated Leaf 2
Mid Layer] D --> E[Graduated Leaf 3
Bottom Layer] E --> F[Centre Bolt Zone
Load Application Point] F --> G[U-Bolt Clamp
Connection to Axle] G --> H[Axle Hub
Wheel Mount] H --> I[Wheel & Tyre
Ground Contact] J[Vertical Road Load W] --> F K[Spring Steel
E = 210 GPa] -.-> B K -.-> C K -.-> D K -.-> E L[Inter-Leaf Lubricant
Graphite Grease] -.-> B L -.-> C L -.-> D L -.-> E style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style G fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Interpretation of the Diagram:** - The **vehicle frame** (left) anchors one end of the spring via a *shackle pin* that allows longitudinal flexing. - The **master leaf** is the longest and strongest; it carries the eye connection. - The **graduated leaves** (Layers 1, 2, 3) are progressively shorter, stacked beneath the master leaf, sharing the load. - All leaves converge at the **centre bolt zone**, which is the point of *load application* $W$ (the road force is transmitted through the wheel → axle → U-bolts → centre bolt). - The **U-bolt clamp** rigidly connects the spring stack to the *axle hub*. - The **dashed arrows** indicate *shared* attributes (material, lubrication) that apply to all leaves. --- ### 4.2 Force & Stress Flow Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Load W] --> B[Centre Bolt
Distribution Point] B --> C[Load Splitting
W -> n equal shares] C --> D1[Leaf 1
W/n] C --> D2[Leaf 2
W/n] C --> D3[Leaf n
W/n] D1 --> E1[Bending Stress
6WL / nbt^2] D2 --> E2[Bending Stress
6WL / nbt^2] D3 --> E3[Bending Stress
6WL / nbt^2] E1 --> F[Maximum Stress at Centre] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G{sigma_b less than sigma_allow} G -->|Yes| H[SPRING SAFE
Design Acceptable] G -->|No| I[REDESIGN
Increase n, b, or t] style A fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px ``` This flowchart encapsulates the **KTU design methodology:** 1. Estimate $W$ (worst-case load from vehicle weight + dynamic factor). 2. Assume a trial $n$ and $t$ (often $L/t \approx 10$). 3. Compute $\sigma_b$ using $\sigma_b = 6WL / (nbt^2)$. 4. Check $\sigma_b \leq \sigma_{allowable}$ (≈ $500$ MPa for spring steel). 5. If not, increase $n$, $b$, or $t$, and iterate. --- ### 4.3 Nomenclature Map (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SpringAssembly[LEAF SPRING ASSEMBLY] M[Master Leaf] F1[Full-Length Leaf 1] F2[Full-Length Leaf 2] G1[Graduated Leaf 1] G2[Graduated Leaf 2] end M -->|Eye End| SH[Shackle] M -->|Eye End| PI[Pin] F1 --> CB[Centre Bolt] F2 --> CB G1 --> CB G2 --> CB CB --> UB[U-Bolt] UB --> AX[Axle] M -.->|Camber| CA[Initial Curvature] F1 -.->|Lubricant| LB[Graphite] G1 -.->|Lubricant| LB style M fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px style CB fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style AX fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1.** *What is a leaf spring? List the various types of leaf springs used in engineering practice.* **Model Answer:** A leaf spring is a flat, laminated spring made of one or more rectangular plates (leaves) clamped together, loaded primarily in bending. The various types are: 1. **Full-length (constant-section) spring** – all leaves of equal length. 2. **Semi-elliptical spring** – both ends rest on supports, load at the centre; most widely used in automobiles. 3. **Quarter-elliptical spring** – one end fixed, the other carries the load. 4. **Three-quarter elliptical spring** – hybrid of quarter- and half-elliptical forms. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correct listing of types.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2.** *State the standard formula for maximum bending stress and deflection of a leaf spring and identify the materials commonly used.* **Model Answer:** $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2} \quad \text{and} \quad \delta = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{E \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^3}$$ The standard material is **silicon-manganese spring steel** (e.g., 55Si7, 60Si7, EN 45) with $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$, an ultimate tensile strength of $1200\text{–}1600 \text{ MPa}$, and a working bending stress of $400\text{–}600 \text{ MPa}$. *(3 marks: 1 each for the two formulas + 1 for material and properties.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** *A semi-elliptical laminated leaf spring has 10 leaves, each of width 50 mm and thickness 8 mm. The length of the spring is 1000 mm. If the spring carries a load of 12 kN at the centre, find:* *(i) the maximum bending stress developed in the leaves,* *(ii) the maximum deflection of the spring.* *Take $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a semi-elliptical leaf spring. List any four materials used for leaf springs.* **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer — Q.A (a)** **Given:** - $n = 10$ leaves - $b = 50 \text{ mm}$ - $t = 8 \text{ mm}$ - $L = 1000 \text{ mm}$ (effective length) - $W = 12 \text{ kN} = 12{,}000 \text{ N}$ - $E = 210 \text{ GPa} = 210 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$ **(i) Maximum bending stress:** $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2} = \dfrac{6 \times 12{,}000 \times 1000}{10 \times 50 \times 8^2} = \dfrac{72{,}000{,}000}{32{,}000} = 2250 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ Hmm — this exceeds the elastic limit. *See warning callout below for an important correction.* **Corrected (with $L$ taken as the half-length, i.e., $L = 500 \text{ mm}$):** $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \times 12{,}000 \times 500}{10 \times 50 \times 64} = \dfrac{36{,}000{,}000}{32{,}000} = 1125 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Disambiguation for KTU Examiners:** The factor of 6 in $\sigma_b$ and $\delta$ already accounts for the *cantilever half-length* model. Therefore, $L$ in the KTU formula is **the half-length of the spring (centre bolt to eye)** = $1000/2 = 500$ mm in this problem. The final answer should be: $$\boxed{\sigma_b = 1125 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1125 \text{ MPa}}$$ This is unacceptably high — the design would require fewer leaves, a shorter length, or a wider section. **(ii) Maximum deflection:** $$\delta = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{E \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^3} = \dfrac{6 \times 12{,}000 \times 500^3}{210 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 50 \times 8^3}$$ Compute numerator: $$6 \times 12{,}000 \times 1.25 \times 10^8 = 9 \times 10^{12}$$ Compute denominator: $$210 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 50 \times 512 = 5.376 \times 10^{10}$$ $$\delta = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{12}}{5.376 \times 10^{10}} \approx 167.4 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{\delta \approx 167.4 \text{ mm}}$$ --- #### **Model Answer — Q.A (b)** **Construction:** A semi-elliptical leaf spring consists of: - A **master leaf** with an *eye* at each end. One end is fixed to the *frame* of the vehicle; the other end is connected via a **shackle** that allows longitudinal movement. - Several **graduated leaves** placed below the master leaf, each successively shorter. - A **centre bolt** passing through the centre hole of all leaves, fixing them to the axle bracket. - **U-bolts** that clamp the entire assembly to the axle. - **Rebound clips** (2 or 3) that bind the leaves together along the length, preventing individual leaves from slip. **Working:** When the wheel hits a bump, an upward force $W$ is transmitted from the axle → U-bolts → centre bolt → all $n$ leaves. Each leaf flexes (bends) and stores strain energy. The leaves are pre-curved (camber) so that they straighten (but do not reverse) at maximum deflection. Upon release of the load, the leaves recoil, returning the wheel to its normal position — this is the *spring action*. **Materials (any four):** 1. **Silicon-manganese spring steel** (e.g., 55Si7, 60Si7) — most common. 2. **Chromium-vanadium steel** (e.g., 50CrV4) — for high-stress applications. 3. **Oil-hardened and tempered carbon-steel spring wire** (e.g., EN 45). 4. **Stainless steel** (e.g., AISI 301, 304) — for corrosion-resistant applications. 5. **Composite materials (e.g., glass-fibre/epoxy)** — modern lightweight alternative. *(7 marks: 3 for sketch + 2 for construction + 2 for materials.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** *Design a leaf spring for a road vehicle with the following data:* - *Maximum load on the spring = 24 kN* - *Length of the spring (eye-to-eye) = 1200 mm* - *Allowable bending stress = 450 MPa* - *Modulus of elasticity = 210 GPa* - *Width-to-thickness ratio of leaf = 8* *Find: (i) the number of leaves, (ii) thickness of each leaf, (iii) width of each leaf, and (iv) the maximum deflection under full load.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Compare a leaf spring with a helical spring. State any three advantages and three disadvantages of leaf springs.* **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Answer — Q.B (a)** **Given:** - $W = 24{,}000 \text{ N}$ - Total length = $1200 \text{ mm}$, so effective half-length $L = 600 \text{ mm}$ - $\sigma_b = 450 \text{ N/mm}^2$ - $E = 210 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$ - $b / t = 8 \Rightarrow b = 8t$ **Step 1 — Assume trial $n$ and find $t$:** We use the bending stress equation rearranged for $t$ (with $b = 8t$): $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^2} = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot 8t \cdot t^2} = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{8 \cdot n \cdot t^3}$$ **Trial 1: $n = 8$:** $$t^3 = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{8 \cdot n \cdot \sigma_b} = \dfrac{6 \times 24{,}000 \times 600}{8 \times 8 \times 450} = \dfrac{86{,}400{,}000}{28{,}800} = 3000$$ $$t = \sqrt[3]{3000} \approx 14.42 \text{ mm}$$ Round to $t = 15 \text{ mm}$ (next standard size). **Re-check $\sigma_b$ with $t = 15$, $b = 8t = 120$ mm:** $$\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 \times 24{,}000 \times 600}{8 \times 120 \times 15^2} = \dfrac{86{,}400{,}000}{216{,}000} = 400 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 450 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\; \checkmark$$ **Design is safe.** **(i) Number of leaves:** $$\boxed{n = 8}$$ **(ii) Thickness of each leaf:** $$\boxed{t = 15 \text{ mm}}$$ **(iii) Width of each leaf:** $$\boxed{b = 120 \text{ mm}}$$ **(iv) Maximum deflection:** $$\delta = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L^3}{E \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^3} = \dfrac{6 \times 24{,}000 \times 600^3}{210 \times 10^3 \times 8 \times 120 \times 15^3}$$ Numerator: $6 \times 24{,}000 \times 2.16 \times 10^8 = 3.1104 \times 10^{13}$ Denominator: $210{,}000 \times 8 \times 120 \times 3375 = 6.804 \times 10^{11}$ $$\delta = \dfrac{3.1104 \times 10^{13}}{6.804 \times 10^{11}} \approx 45.7 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{\delta \approx 45.7 \text{ mm}}$$ --- #### **Model Answer — Q.B (b)** | Feature | Leaf Spring | Helical Spring | |:--------|:-----------|:---------------| | Shape | Flat laminated plates | Coiled wire | | Load direction | Primarily vertical (bending) | Primarily axial (torsion of wire) | | Energy storage | Bending strain energy | Torsional strain energy | | Load capacity | **High** (suitable for heavy vehicles) | **Moderate** (light/medium vehicles) | | Structural role | Acts as a *locating link* (positions the axle) | Purely a *cushioning* element | | Damping | **High** (inter-leaf friction) | **Low** (needs external damper) | | Maintenance | Needs periodic lubrication | Almost maintenance-free | | Manufacturing cost | Higher | Lower | | Compactness | Bulky | Compact | **Three advantages of leaf springs:** 1. Can carry very large loads with good fatigue life. 2. Provide *inherent damping* due to inter-leaf friction. 3. Serve a **dual role**: suspension *and* structural link. **Three disadvantages of leaf springs:** 1. Heavier than equivalent coil springs. 2. Require periodic lubrication and maintenance. 3. More complex and expensive to manufacture and replace. *(7 marks: 3 for comparison + 2 for advantages + 2 for disadvantages.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes & Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 6:** Students often write $\sigma_b = WL / (nbt^2)$, missing the factor of 6. This results in an **under-estimation of stress by a factor of 6** — a *fatal design error* and an immediate loss of 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing $L$ (effective half-length) with total spring length:** $L$ in the formula is the **half-length (centre bolt to eye)**, not the total eye-to-eye span. Always divide the total length by 2 before substituting. > 3. **Not rounding up $n$ to an integer:** If your calculation gives $n = 7.3$, the answer is **$n = 8$** (round up). A partial leaf is impossible in practice. > 4. **Ignoring units consistency:** Mixing mm and m, or N and kN, is the most common *silent* mistake. Always convert to a single unit system *before* plugging into the formula. > 5. **Forgetting the width-to-thickness ratio constraint ($b/t \approx 6$ to $9$):** A leaf that is too narrow will shear off at the centre; a leaf too wide will not deflect uniformly. > 6. **Neglecting the camber:** Always specify that the master leaf has a camber of ~50–100 mm so the spring is never fully straightened under load (which would cause stress reversal and fatigue failure). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Leaf Spring = Flat laminated spring** loaded in *bending*; most common type is the **semi-elliptical spring**. - **Material of choice:** Silicon-manganese spring steel (e.g., 55Si7); $E = 210$ GPa; allowable $\sigma_b \approx 450$ MPa. - **Master leaf** = longest leaf with eyes; **graduated leaves** = successively shorter, share load. - **Two governing equations** (KTU 2024 formula sheet): - Bending stress: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 W L}{n b t^2}$ - Deflection: $\delta = \dfrac{6 W L^3}{E n b t^3}$ - **Length $L$** in the formulas = *effective half-length* (centre bolt to eye) — not the full eye-to-eye span. - **Number of leaves $n$** = must be an integer, **rounded up**; design constraint: $L/t \approx 10$ to $12$. - **Width $b$** = typically $6t$ to $9t$. - **Camber** of the master leaf is essential to prevent stress reversal under full load. - **Maximum shear stress** in a leaf spring = $3W / (2nbt)$ — small and usually **neglected**. - **Stiffness** $k = W/\delta = E n b t^3 / (6L^3)$. - **Strain energy** stored $U = \tfrac{1}{2} W \delta$ — useful for designing impact-absorbing systems. - **Advantages of leaf spring:** high load capacity, inbuilt damping, structural link role. - **Disadvantages:** heavy, needs lubrication, bulky, expensive. - **Inter-leaf lubrication** (graphite) is critical to avoid premature fatigue failure. - **Shot peening** is the standard surface treatment to extend fatigue life. - **Applications:** heavy-vehicle suspensions, railway wagons, agricultural equipment, industrial buffers. - **Always cross-check** that your computed $\sigma_b \leq \sigma_{allowable}$ and $\delta$ is within the vehicle's permissible suspension travel (~100–150 mm for cars, ~50–80 mm for trucks).

topics.title: Disc and Torsional Springs Under Constant Loads; Sliding Contact Bearing: Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disc and Torsional Springs Under Constant Loads; Sliding Contact Bearing: Lubrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Disc Springs (Belleville Springs)

A **Disc Spring** is a conical, washer-shaped mechanical spring, typically loaded along its axis. It is manufactured from a single disc of spring steel, hardened and tempered, with the shape of a shallow truncated cone. The most common type is the **Belleville Spring**, named after its inventor Louis Belleville.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCAGT403 - Module 4):**
> A disc spring is a thin, annular, conically shaped plate whose load is applied uniformly along the outer edge in the axial direction. When loaded, the cone is flattened, converting axial force into elastic bending strain energy stored in the disc.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine crushing a small paper cup (cone shape) along its axis. The paper cup resists the crushing force. As you push down, the cone tries to flatten out — and the resistance you feel *is* the spring force. A Belleville spring behaves identically, but it's made of hardened steel and engineered so the bending is perfectly elastic (recoverable). The **steeper the cone angle**, the stiffer the spring. Stack multiple cups in series, and the deflection adds up; stack them in parallel (face-to-face), and the force capacity multiplies.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Material:** Typically **Chromium-Vanadium Steel (51CrV4)** or **Stainless Steel (X10CrNi18-8)**.
> - **Standard:** Manufactured as per **DIN 6796 / DIN 2093**.
> - **Characteristic:** Nonlinear load-deflection curve (progressive, degressive, or linear depending on cone height ratio $h_0/t$).

### 1.2 Torsional Springs Under Constant Loads

A **Torsional Spring** is a mechanical spring that resists twisting (rotational) motion rather than axial compression/extension. When subjected to a constant torque, it stores strain energy in the form of torsional shear strain.

**Types of Torsional Springs:**
- **Helical Torsion Spring** (coiled wire wound in a helix, with axial arms for applying torque)
- **Spiral Torsion Spring** (wound in flat spirals)
- **Torsion Bar Spring** (straight bar loaded in torsion)

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **clothespin spring** or the spring inside a **mouse-trap**. The wire coils you see wrapped around the pivot pin is a helical torsion spring. When you open the clothespin, you're applying torque to the arms — the wire twists along its axis, and it tries to snap back. For engineering analysis, "constant load" means the applied torque does not change appreciably during the angular deflection — useful when designing door hinges, balances, and counterbalance mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> Unlike a helical compression spring (loaded axially), a helical **torsion spring is loaded perpendicularly to the wire axis**, producing **bending stress** in the wire (NOT torsional shear stress in the conventional sense). However, when analyzing the **shaft/torque** transmission case (where the spring itself is the shaft transmitting torque), the stress is pure torsion.

### 1.3 Sliding Contact Bearing: Lubrication

A **Sliding Contact Bearing** (also called a **Plain Bearing** or **Journal Bearing**) is a bearing in which two surfaces slide against each other in relative motion, with a lubricant film separating them to reduce friction and wear. The effectiveness of this bearing is governed by the **Lubrication Regime** operating in the contact zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU):**
> Lubrication in sliding bearings refers to the process of introducing a viscous fluid (typically oil) between the journal and the bearing surface to support the load, reduce friction, dissipate heat, and minimize wear. The governing theoretical framework is **Hydrodynamic Lubrication** developed by Osborne Reynolds (1886).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pushing a flat wooden plank across a thin film of honey on a table. The honey resists the motion — the plank "floats" on the honey. The faster you push, the more the honey gets dragged under, building up pressure that lifts the plank higher. This pressure wedge is exactly what happens in a journal bearing: the **shaft drags oil into the converging clearance**, generating a pressure distribution that supports the shaft and prevents metal-to-metal contact.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Petroff's Friction** (zero-load, lightly loaded regime): Friction is purely viscous, no asperity contact.
> - **Viscosity (μ):** Measured in **Pa·s** or **reyn** (1 reyn = 6895 Pa·s in old units). Typical values: SAE 30 oil at 100°C ≈ **0.0097 Pa·s**.
> - **Reynolds Number** for bearing flow: extremely low, so flow is laminar (creeping flow).

### 1.4 Lubrication Regimes — The Stribeck Curve

The Stribeck Curve classifies the friction coefficient $\mu$ as a function of the **dimensionless lubrication parameter** $\dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ (viscosity × speed ÷ load):

| Regime | Boundary Condition | Friction Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Lubrication** | $\mu N / P \to 0$ | High friction, asperity contact, governed by surface chemistry |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | $\mu N / P$ moderate | Partial film, some asperity contact |
| **Hydrodynamic (Full-Film) Lubrication** | $\mu N / P$ large | Low friction, complete film separation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Disc Spring — Geometry and Load-Deflection Theory

A disc spring is characterized by the following geometric parameters:

| Symbol | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $D_e$ | Outer diameter | 8 mm – 1000 mm |
| $D_i$ | Inner diameter | $D_e / 2$ typically |
| $t$ | Disc thickness | 0.3 mm – 25 mm |
| $h_0$ | Cone height (unloaded) | 0.2 mm – 12 mm |
| $h$ | Cone height under load | $h_0 - \delta$ |
| $D$ | Mean diameter | $(D_e + D_i)/2$ |
| $\delta$ | Axial deflection | $0$ to $h_0$ |
| $F$ | Axial load | N |

**Load-Deflection Relationship (Almen-Laszlo Equation):**

The load-deflection relation is derived from the bending of an annular plate:

$$F = \dfrac{4 \cdot E \cdot \delta}{K_1 \cdot D^2} \left[ (h_0 - \delta) \cdot (h_0 - \delta/2) \cdot t + t^3 \right]$$

where $K_1$ is a constant:

$$K_1 = \dfrac{6}{\pi \cdot \ln \alpha} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{(\alpha - 1)^2}{\alpha^2} \right] \quad ; \quad \alpha = D_e / D_i$$

**Maximum Bending Stress (at inner edge, point I):**

$$\sigma_I = \dfrac{4 \cdot E \cdot \delta}{(1 - \nu^2) \cdot D^2} \cdot K_4 \cdot \left[ h_0 - \dfrac{\delta}{2} + K_3 \cdot t \right]$$

where $K_2$, $K_3$, $K_4$ are dimensionless constants depending only on $\alpha$.

**Simplified Linear Form (when $\delta \ll h_0$):**

$$F \approx \dfrac{4 \cdot E \cdot \delta \cdot h_0 \cdot t}{K_1 \cdot D^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A disc spring exhibits a **nonlinear** load-deflection characteristic. If $h_0/t < 0.4$, behavior is **regressive** (stiffens as loaded). If $h_0/t > 0.4$, behavior is **progressive** (softens). When $h_0/t = \sqrt{2}$, characteristic is approximately **linear** — useful in precision instruments.

### 2.2 Torsional Spring — Theory of Operation

For a **helical torsion spring** with axial loading on the arms (perpendicular to wire axis):

**Bending Stress in the Wire:**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{32 \cdot M}{\pi \cdot d^3}$$

where $M = F \cdot r_{arm}$ is the moment on the wire due to the arm force $F$ acting at radius $r_{arm}$.

**Angular Deflection of the Wire (in radians):**

$$\phi = \dfrac{32 \cdot M \cdot n \cdot D_m}{\pi \cdot d^4 \cdot E}$$

**Strain Energy Stored:**

$$U = \dfrac{M^2 \cdot L}{2 \cdot E \cdot I} = \dfrac{M^2 \cdot L \cdot \pi \cdot d^4}{2 \cdot E \cdot 32} = \dfrac{M^2 \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot n \cdot D_m}{8 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

where $L = \pi \cdot D_m \cdot n$ is the active wire length.

**For a Torsion Bar (solid shaft loaded in pure torsion):**

$$\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \quad ; \quad \tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3} \quad ; \quad U = \dfrac{T^2 \cdot L}{2 \cdot G \cdot J}$$

where $J = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Load Condition:** For a torsion spring under constant load $T$, the maximum stress is reached immediately. There is no fatigue amplification. Hence design is based on **static yield criterion** (not endurance limit). This is a key KTU exam point.

### 2.3 Sliding Contact Bearing — Petroff's Equation

Petroff (1883) derived the friction torque for a **lightly loaded, concentric journal** with complete film lubrication, no side leakage:

$$T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \cdot \mu \cdot N \cdot r^3 \cdot \omega}{c}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of lubricant (Pa·s)
- $N$ = bearing load (N)
- $r$ = journal radius (m)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $c$ = radial clearance (m)

**Friction Coefficient (Petroff):**

$$f = \dfrac{T_f}{N \cdot r} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \cdot \mu \cdot r^2 \cdot \omega}{c}$$

Or in terms of $PV$:

$$f = 2 \pi^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{r}{c} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \right) \quad \text{where } P = N/(2 r l)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Petroff's equation predicts friction **increases with speed** — but in real bearings, beyond a certain speed, friction **decreases** due to eccentric loading (Reynolds regime). Petroff is valid only at very low $\mu N / P$.

### 2.4 Reynolds Equation and Sommerfeld Number

For a **partial arc bearing** with film thickness $h(\theta) = c (1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$ (where $\varepsilon$ is eccentricity ratio):

**Reynolds Equation (1-D, steady, laminar):**

$$\dfrac{d}{d\theta} \left( h^3 \cdot \dfrac{dp}{d\theta} \right) = 6 \mu U r \cdot \dfrac{dh}{d\theta}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $p = p_a$ (atmospheric) at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = 2\pi - \psi$
- $\dfrac{dp}{d\theta} = 0$ at $\theta = \pi$ (point of maximum pressure)

After integration and applying the condition that the net vertical force from pressure equals the load $W$:

$$\dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi \mu U}{W} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{\varepsilon \sqrt{(1 + \varepsilon) \cdot \text{arccosh}(...)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> The **Sommerfeld Number** is a dimensionless group used to characterize bearing performance:
> $$S_o = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$$
> Lower $S_o$ = higher friction, higher wear, closer to boundary regime.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the formulas examiners expect you to reproduce from memory.**

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Disc spring load (general) | $F = \dfrac{4 E \delta}{K_1 D^2} \left[ (h_0 - \delta)(h_0 - \delta/2) t + t^3 \right]$ | $E$ in Pa, $D$ in m, $\delta$ in m, $F$ in N |
| 2 | Disc spring stress (inner) | $\sigma_I = \dfrac{4 E \delta}{(1 - \nu^2) D^2} \cdot K_4 \cdot \left[ h_0 - \delta/2 + K_3 t \right]$ | $\nu \approx 0.3$ for steel |
| 3 | Disc spring constant $K_1$ | $K_1 = \dfrac{6(\alpha-1)^2}{\pi \alpha^2 \ln \alpha}$ | $\alpha = D_e / D_i$ |
| 4 | Torsion bar stress | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | $T$ in N·m, $d$ in m, $\tau$ in Pa |
| 5 | Torsion bar twist | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | $G$ in Pa, $J$ in m⁴ |
| 6 | Torsion bar strain energy | $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$ | $U$ in J |
| 7 | Helical torsion spring deflection | $\phi = \dfrac{32 M n D_m}{\pi d^4 E}$ | $M$ in N·m, $D_m$ mean coil dia |
| 8 | Petroff friction coefficient | $f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^2 \omega}{c}$ | $\mu$ in Pa·s, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 9 | Petroff friction torque | $T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 \omega}{c}$ | $T_f$ in N·m |
| 10 | Reynolds equation | $\dfrac{d}{d\theta}\left(h^3 \dfrac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = 6 \mu U r \dfrac{dh}{d\theta}$ | $U = \omega r$ |
| 11 | Sommerfeld number | $S_o = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Eccentricity ratio | $\varepsilon = e / c$ | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |
| 13 | Minimum film thickness | $h_{min} = c (1 - \varepsilon)$ | At angle $\theta = \pi$ |
| 14 | Coefficient of friction (Reynolds) | $f \approx \dfrac{2 \pi^2 (r/c)}{\sqrt{\varepsilon}}$ | Approximate |
| 15 | Unit bearing load | $P = W / (l \cdot d)$ | $W$ = load, $l \cdot d$ = projected area |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** Disc springs are used in **circuit breakers** (large force in small space), **ball valves** (high loads), and **die sets** (where exact preload is needed). Torsion bars are critical in **automobile suspensions** (e.g., Chevrolet Corvette and many trucks). Petroff's analysis directly informs **bearing selection** in turbochargers, electric motors, and turbines where $\mu N / P$ must be above a threshold to avoid boundary lubrication.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Disc Spring Load-Deflection Equation

We consider a thin conical disc spring fixed at the outer edge and loaded at the inner edge by axial force $F$. The classical approach (Almen-László) treats the disc as a series of annular elements, each undergoing flexure.

**Step 1: Establish the geometric parameters**

For a point on the disc at radius $r$ and meridional distance $s$ along the cone surface:

$$z(r) = h_0 \left( \dfrac{D_e/2 - r}{D_e/2 - D_i/2} \right)$$

so that the slope of the meridian:

$$\dfrac{dz}{dr} = -\dfrac{2 h_0}{D_e - D_i} = \text{const}$$

**Step 2: Apply plate bending theory**

For a conical shell loaded by axial force $F$, the bending moment per unit length along the meridian (from shell theory, **Donnell's equations**):

$$M_s = -D_f \left( \dfrac{d^2 w}{dr^2} + \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{dw}{dr} \right) + \dfrac{F \cdot z}{2\pi r}$$

where $D_f = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate, and $w(r)$ is the additional deflection due to bending.

**Step 3: Integrate over the disc**

Total axial deflection $\delta$ comes from integrating the radial strain (due to membrane action) plus bending contribution. After integration over the radius from $D_i/2$ to $D_e/2$:

$$\delta = \int_{D_i/2}^{D_e/2} \left( \dfrac{F \cdot z}{2\pi r \cdot E t} \right) dr + \int_{D_i/2}^{D_e/2} \left( \dfrac{M_s \cdot \Delta h}{...} \right) dr$$

**Step 4: Combine and simplify (Almen-László result)**

Combining membrane and bending contributions, after introducing the dimensionless parameter $\alpha = D_e/D_i$ and evaluating the integrals:

$$F = \dfrac{4 E \delta}{K_1 D^2} \left[ (h_0 - \delta)(h_0 - \delta/2) t + t^3 \right]$$

with:

$$K_1 = \dfrac{6}{\pi \ln \alpha} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{(\alpha - 1)^2}{\alpha^2} \right] \cdot \left[ \dfrac{\alpha + 1}{\alpha - 1} - \dfrac{2}{\ln \alpha} \right]^{-1}$$

For $\alpha = 2$ (typical), $K_1 \approx 0.681$.

**Step 5: Special case — linear range (small deflection, $\delta \ll h_0$, $t \ll h_0$)**

$$\delta \to 0, \quad (h_0 - \delta)(h_0 - \delta/2) \to h_0^2$$

$$F \approx \dfrac{4 E \delta h_0^2 t}{K_1 D^2}$$

So the **linear spring rate** is:

$$k = \dfrac{F}{\delta} = \dfrac{4 E h_0^2 t}{K_1 D^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This linearization explains why disc springs with $h_0/t = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ give the most "linear" characteristic over the working range.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Petroff's Friction Equation

**Assumptions (Petroff, 1883):**
1. The journal is perfectly concentric in the bearing ($\varepsilon = 0$).
2. The film is continuous, laminar, and fully developed.
3. No side leakage — the bearing is treated as if it were infinitely long.
4. The lubricant is a Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity $\mu$.
5. Inertia forces are negligible (creeping flow).

**Step 1: Velocity profile in the gap**

The journal surface moves with velocity $U = \omega r$ relative to the stationary bearing surface. For Couette flow in a clearance of height $c$:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \dfrac{y}{c}$$

where $y$ is the radial coordinate measured from the bearing surface inward.

**Step 2: Shear stress in the fluid**

For a Newtonian fluid:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \dfrac{U}{c} = \mu \cdot \dfrac{\omega r}{c}$$

**Step 3: Friction force per unit area**

This shear acts on the entire journal surface area $A = 2 \pi r l$:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot A = \dfrac{\mu \cdot \omega r}{c} \cdot 2 \pi r l = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu \omega r^2 l}{c}$$

**Step 4: Friction torque**

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu \omega r^3 l}{c}$$

**Step 5: Introduce the bearing load**

For a lightly loaded concentric journal, the load is supported hydrostatically by the fluid film pressure. For a vertical load $N$ on a bearing of length $l$ and diameter $2r$, the projected area is $2 r l$, so the unit load:

$$P = \dfrac{N}{2 r l} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = 2 P r l$$

The coefficient of friction:

$$f = \dfrac{F_f}{N} = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu \omega r^2 l / c}{2 P r l} = \dfrac{\pi \mu \omega r}{P c}$$

Substituting $\omega = 2 \pi N_s$ (where $N_s$ is in rev/s):

$$f = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \mu r N_s}{P c} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 (r/c) (\mu N_s / P)}{1}$$

Or with $\omega$ in rad/s:

$$f = \dfrac{\pi \mu \omega r}{P c} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^2 \omega}{c \cdot 2 r l \cdot P / 2 r l}$$

The textbook standard form is:

$$f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^2 \omega}{c \cdot (N/l)} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 (r/c)(\mu N)}{P} \quad \text{(see Shigley, Norton, etc.)}$$

Final unified form:

$$f = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 (r/c)(\mu \omega / P)}{...} \rightarrow \boxed{f = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \mu \omega r^2}{P c}}$$

**Step 6: Friction torque final form**

$$T_f = f \cdot N \cdot r = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^3 l \omega}{c} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N r^3 \omega}{P \cdot 2 r l} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \mu N r^2 \omega}{P c}$$

Equivalent textbook form (using $N = W$):

$$T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu W r^3 \omega}{c \cdot 2 r l} \quad \text{or} \quad T_f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu W r^3 \omega}{P c} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2 r l}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petroff's critical insight:** Since $f \propto \omega$, friction *increases* with speed in a perfectly concentric journal. In practice, journals become eccentric under load, and friction peaks then drops — this is the **minimum-friction point** on the Stribeck curve, often at $\mu N / P \approx 10^{-6}$ for typical oils.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Reynolds Equation (Sliding Bearing)

**Step 1: Geometry of the film**

Let the journal of radius $r$ rotate at $\omega$, with center $O_j$ offset from bearing center $O_b$ by eccentricity $e$. The clearance at angle $\theta$ (measured from line of centers):

$$h(\theta) = c + e \cos\theta = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

where $\varepsilon = e/c$ is the eccentricity ratio, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$.

**Step 2: Continuity and Navier-Stokes in thin film**

In the thin film ($h \ll r$), pressure variation across the film is negligible, and inertia terms drop. The 2D thin-film Navier-Stokes reduces to:

$$\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \quad ; \quad \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} = \mu \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial y^2}$$

where $u(x,y,z)$ is the velocity along the slider motion direction, $w$ is the transverse velocity.

**Step 3: Integrate across the film**

For unidirectional motion with no transverse flow (long bearing assumption, $w = 0$), integrating twice in $y$:

$$u(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu} \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} (y^2 - hy) + U \dfrac{y}{h}$$

**Step 4: Volume flow rate per unit width**

$$q = \int_0^h u \, dy = -\dfrac{h^3}{12 \mu} \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \dfrac{U h}{2}$$

**Step 5: Mass conservation (continuity in the control volume)**

For steady-state, the net flow into a small element of width $dx$ and length $dz$ equals flow out:

$$\dfrac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial q_z}{\partial z} = -\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

For steady state, $\partial h/\partial t = 0$. For 1-D flow (long bearing), $q_z = 0$:

$$\dfrac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[ \dfrac{Uh}{2} - \dfrac{h^3}{12\mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} \right] = 0$$

Expanding:

$$\dfrac{U}{2} \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x} - \dfrac{h^2}{4\mu} \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x} \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \dfrac{h^3}{12\mu} \dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

**Step 6: Substitute $x = r\theta$ and $U = \omega r$:**

Using $\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x} = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \theta}$ and $\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial \theta^2}$:

$$\dfrac{\omega r}{2} \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \theta} - \dfrac{h^2}{4\mu} \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \theta} \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial \theta} - \dfrac{h^3}{12\mu r^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial \theta^2} = 0$$

Multiplying by $12 \mu r^2 / h^3$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d}{d\theta}\left( h^3 \dfrac{dp}{d\theta} \right) = 6 \mu \omega r^2 \dfrac{dh}{d\theta}}$$

**Step 7: Apply film geometry**

With $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$, $\dfrac{dh}{d\theta} = -c \varepsilon \sin\theta$:

$$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}\left[ c^3 (1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^3 \dfrac{dp}{d\theta} \right] = -6 \mu \omega r^2 c \varepsilon \sin\theta$$

This is the **Reynolds Equation for a short journal bearing**, integrated numerically or via Sommerfeld substitution to find the load-carrying capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> The **physical meaning**: The right-hand side $\dfrac{dh}{d\theta} \ne 0$ (converging wedge) is what generates pressure. Where $h$ is increasing ($\sin\theta < 0$), the converging wedge drags oil in and pressurizes it. This is the **hydrodynamic wedge effect**.

---

### 3.4 Strain Energy in a Torsion Bar Under Constant Load

A torsion bar is a uniform shaft of length $L$, diameter $d$, polar moment of inertia $J = \pi d^4 / 32$, and shear modulus $G$.

**Step 1: Stress and strain under torque $T$**

$$\tau = \dfrac{T r}{J} \quad ; \quad \gamma = \dfrac{\tau}{G} = \dfrac{T r}{G J}$$

**Step 2: Strain energy density**

$$u = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G} = \dfrac{T^2 r^2}{2 G J^2}$$

**Step 3: Total strain energy**

$$U = \int_V u \, dV = \int_0^L \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{d/2} \dfrac{T^2 r^2}{2 G J^2} \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta \, dx$$

$$U = \dfrac{T^2}{2 G J^2} \cdot L \cdot 2\pi \cdot \int_0^{d/2} r^3 \, dr = \dfrac{T^2 L \pi}{G J^2} \cdot \dfrac{(d/2)^4}{2} = \dfrac{T^2 L \pi d^4}{32 G J^2}$$

Substituting $J = \pi d^4 / 32$, so $J^2 = \pi^2 d^8 / 1024$:

$$U = \dfrac{T^2 L \pi d^4}{32 G} \cdot \dfrac{1024}{\pi^2 d^8} = \dfrac{32 T^2 L}{\pi G d^4}$$

Or, more compactly, the **standard textbook result**:

$$\boxed{U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}}$$

**Step 4: Equate to work done (constant load)**

For a constant torque $T$ applied gradually from 0 to $T$, the work done equals the strain energy:

$$W = \dfrac{1}{2} T \theta = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$$

This confirms the formula. For **constant load** (i.e., $T$ applied instantly and held), the strain energy is the same — what changes is the **strain energy density** which is uniform along the bar.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** What is a disc spring? List any two advantages of disc springs over helical compression springs.

**Model Answer:**
A disc spring (or Belleville spring) is a **conical, washer-shaped spring** that is loaded axially. It is manufactured from a single disc of spring steel hardened and tempered, with the shape of a shallow truncated cone. When loaded axially, the cone tends to flatten, storing strain energy through elastic bending of the disc.

**Advantages over helical compression springs:**
1. **High load-to-size ratio** — delivers very large forces in a compact space (up to 100 times the load of a helical spring of equivalent dimensions).
2. **No tendency to buckle** — the disc geometry is self-centering, eliminating lateral instability seen in long helical springs.
3. **Long service life** — under static or low-cycle loading, disc springs last for millions of cycles with minimal relaxation.
4. **Stacking flexibility** — series stacking increases deflection; parallel stacking increases load capacity.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** State Petroff's equation for friction in a journal bearing. Under what conditions is it valid?

**Model Answer:**
**Petroff's equation** for the coefficient of friction in a lightly loaded journal bearing is:

$$f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^2 \omega}{P c} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 (r/c)(\mu N_s)}{P}$$

where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity, $r$ is the journal radius, $\omega$ is the angular velocity, $P$ is the unit load, $c$ is the radial clearance, and $N_s$ is the speed in rev/s.

**Conditions of validity:**
1. The journal is **perfectly concentric** with the bearing (no eccentricity).
2. The film is **continuous, laminar, and complete** (no asperity contact).
3. **No side leakage** (infinitely long bearing assumption).
4. Lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** with constant viscosity.
5. **Inertia forces are negligible**.
6. The bearing is **lightly loaded** so that the journal remains concentric.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Note for students:** In KTU ESE, Module 4 of Theory of Machines is a **14-mark question** with **internal choice** (either-or). The question has two parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, S8 B.Tech Mechanical]**

**(a)** A disc spring has the following specifications: outer diameter $D_e = 100$ mm, inner diameter $D_i = 50$ mm, thickness $t = 5$ mm, cone height $h_0 = 1.5$ mm. The spring is made of steel with $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Calculate: (i) the spring constant $K_1$ (assume $\alpha = D_e/D_i$), (ii) the load corresponding to a deflection of 0.6 mm, and (iii) the maximum stress at the inner edge. Assume $K_3 = 1.05$ and $K_4 = 1.30$ for $\alpha = 2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $K_1$**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{D_e}{D_i} = \dfrac{100}{50} = 2$$

$$K_1 = \dfrac{6}{\pi \ln \alpha} \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha-1)^2}{\alpha^2} = \dfrac{6}{\pi \cdot \ln 2} \cdot \dfrac{(2-1)^2}{2^2}$$

$$K_1 = \dfrac{6}{\pi \cdot 0.6931} \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{6}{0.8712} \cdot 0.25 = 6.886 \cdot 0.25 = 0.6884 \cdot \text{(standard value for }\alpha=2\text{)} \approx 0.689$$

**Step 2: Mean diameter**

$$D = \dfrac{D_e + D_i}{2} = \dfrac{100 + 50}{2} = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Load at $\delta = 0.6$ mm**

$$F = \dfrac{4 E \delta}{K_1 D^2} \left[ (h_0 - \delta)(h_0 - \delta/2) t + t^3 \right]$$

Compute each term (all in mm, then convert at the end):

$$h_0 - \delta = 1.5 - 0.6 = 0.9 \text{ mm}$$

$$h_0 - \delta/2 = 1.5 - 0.3 = 1.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$(h_0 - \delta)(h_0 - \delta/2) t = 0.9 \times 1.2 \times 5 = 5.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$t^3 = 5^3 = 125 \text{ mm}^3$$

Sum: $5.4 + 125 = 130.4 \text{ mm}^3$

Now substitute with proper units. Converting to meters: $D^2 = 0.075^2 = 5.625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$, $\delta = 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $E = 2.1 \times 10^{11}$ Pa.

$$F = \dfrac{4 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.689 \times 5.625 \times 10^{-3}} \times (130.4 \times 10^{-9})$$

$$F = \dfrac{5.04 \times 10^{8}}{3.876 \times 10^{-3}} \times 130.4 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$F = 1.3003 \times 10^{11} \times 130.4 \times 10^{-9} = 16,956 \text{ N} \approx 16.96 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Maximum stress at inner edge**

$$\sigma_I = \dfrac{4 E \delta}{(1 - \nu^2) D^2} \cdot K_4 \cdot \left[ h_0 - \delta/2 + K_3 t \right]$$

Convert to consistent units: $h_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $t = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$h_0 - \delta/2 + K_3 t = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} - 0.3 \times 10^{-3} + 1.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= (1.2 + 5.25) \times 10^{-3} = 6.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91$$

$$\sigma_I = \dfrac{4 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.91 \times 5.625 \times 10^{-3}} \times 1.30 \times 6.45 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_I = \dfrac{5.04 \times 10^{8}}{5.119 \times 10^{-3}} \times 1.30 \times 6.45 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_I = 9.846 \times 10^{10} \times 8.385 \times 10^{-3} = 8.255 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 825.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** $K_1 = 0.689$, $F \approx 17$ kN, $\sigma_I \approx 825$ MPa.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert all dimensions to **meters** before plugging into $E$ in Pa. A common error is using mixed units (mm and MPa) leading to off-by-10⁶ factors.
> 2. Using $\ln \alpha$ vs $\log_{10} \alpha$ — KTU strictly uses **natural logarithm (ln)** in the disc spring formula.
> 3. Forgetting the Poisson's ratio term $(1 - \nu^2)$ in the stress equation.

---

**(b)** A full journal bearing has the following data: diameter $D = 60$ mm, length $L = 30$ mm, radial clearance $c = 0.06$ mm, viscosity $\mu = 0.06$ Pa·s, speed $N = 1440$ rpm, load $W = 5000$ N. Calculate: (i) the Petroff coefficient of friction, (ii) the Petroff friction torque, and (iii) the Sommerfeld number. Assume a long bearing approximation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert all values to SI units**

- $r = D/2 = 0.03$ m
- $L = 0.03$ m
- $c = 0.06 \times 10^{-3} = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $\omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi \times 1440 / 60 = 48\pi = 150.796$ rad/s
- $\mu = 0.06$ Pa·s
- $W = 5000$ N

**Step 2: Petroff coefficient of friction**

$$f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^2 \omega}{W \cdot c / (2 r L)} \cdot \text{(rearranged)} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 (r/c)(\mu \omega r L)}{W}$$

Standard form:

$$f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu r^2 \omega}{P c} \quad \text{where } P = W/(2rL) = 5000/(0.06 \times 0.03) = 5000/0.0018 = 2.778 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$f = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \times 0.06 \times (0.03)^2 \times 150.796}{2.778 \times 10^6 \times 6 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator: $4 \pi^2 \times 0.06 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 150.796$

$$= 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.06 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 150.796$$

$$= 39.478 \times 0.06 \times 0.1357 = 0.3214 \text{ N}$$

Denominator: $2.778 \times 10^6 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = 166.67$ N

$$f = \dfrac{0.3214}{166.67} = 1.928 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00193$$

**Step 3: Petroff friction torque**

$$T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r = 1.928 \times 10^{-3} \times 5000 \times 0.03$$

$$T_f = 0.2892 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Sommerfeld number**

$$S_o = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N_s}{P}$$

With $N_s = 1440/60 = 24$ rev/s:

$$S_o = \dfrac{0.03}{6 \times 10^{-5}} \cdot \dfrac{0.06 \times 24}{2.778 \times 10^6}$$

$$S_o = 500 \cdot \dfrac{1.44}{2.778 \times 10^6} = 500 \times 5.184 \times 10^{-7} = 2.592 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Result:** $f \approx 0.00193$, $T_f \approx 0.289$ N·m, $S_o \approx 2.59 \times 10^{-4}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to multiply by $2\pi$ when converting RPM to rad/s, or use the bearing's circumference ($2\pi r$) where journal radius $r$ is required. Always draw the bearing schematic first and label $r$, $c$, $L$, $W$ clearly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S8 B.Tech Mechanical]**

**(a)** A torsion bar of circular cross-section is used as a suspension spring in an automobile. The bar has length $L = 1.2$ m, diameter $d = 25$ mm, and is made of spring steel with $G = 80$ GPa. Under a constant torque of $T = 1800$ N·m, calculate: (i) the angle of twist, (ii) the maximum shear stress, and (iii) the strain energy stored. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Polar moment of inertia**

$$J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.025)^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3.906 \times 10^{-7}}{32} = 3.835 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2: Angle of twist**

$$\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J} = \dfrac{1800 \times 1.2}{80 \times 10^9 \times 3.835 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$= \dfrac{2160}{3068} = 0.7040 \text{ rad} = 40.34°$$

**Step 3: Maximum shear stress**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 \times 1800}{\pi \times (0.025)^3}$$

$$= \dfrac{28,800}{\pi \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{28,800}{4.909 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.867 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 586.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Strain energy**

$$U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J} = \dfrac{(1800)^2 \times 1.2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9 \times 3.835 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$= \dfrac{3.888 \times 10^6}{6135.6} = 633.7 \text{ J}$$

**Result:** $\theta = 0.704$ rad, $\tau_{max} = 586.7$ MPa, $U = 633.7$ J.

---

**(b)** A journal bearing is to be designed for a load of $W = 25$ kN at a speed of $N = 1500$ rpm. The radial clearance ratio is $c/r = 0.001$ and the projected area pressure should not exceed $P = 1.5$ MPa. The lubricant has viscosity $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s. Using Petroff's equation, determine: (i) the bearing dimensions $r$ and $L$ assuming $L/D = 1$, (ii) Petroff's friction coefficient, and (iii) power loss in friction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Bearing dimensions**

Projected area $A_p = 2 r L = 2 r^2$ (since $L = D = 2r$, so $L = 2r$, $A_p = 2r \cdot 2r = 4r^2$). Wait, if $L/D = 1$, then $L = 2r$, so $A_p = 2r \cdot L = 2r \cdot 2r = 4r^2$.

Setting $P = W/A_p$:

$$1.5 \times 10^6 = \dfrac{25,000}{4 r^2} \Rightarrow r^2 = \dfrac{25,000}{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{25,000}{6 \times 10^6} = 4.167 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$r = 0.0645 \text{ m} = 64.5 \text{ mm}, \quad D = 129 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 129 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute clearance**

$$c = 0.001 \times r = 0.001 \times 0.0645 = 6.45 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Angular velocity**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Petroff's coefficient of friction**

$$f = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 (r/c) \mu \omega}{P} = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \times (1/0.001) \times 0.025 \times 157.08}{1.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 1000 \times 0.025 \times 157.08}{1.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$= \dfrac{77,510}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 0.0517$$

**Step 5: Friction force and power loss**

$$F_f = f W = 0.0517 \times 25,000 = 1292 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{Power loss} = F_f \cdot v = F_f \cdot \omega r = 1292 \times 157.08 \times 0.0645$$

$$= 13,090 \text{ W} \approx 13.09 \text{ kW}$$

**Result:** $r \approx 64.5$ mm, $D = L = 129$ mm, $f \approx 0.0517$, Power loss $\approx 13.1$ kW.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Disc Spring Specifically:**
> 1. **Stress check:** Always verify that the calculated $\sigma_I$ does not exceed the **allowable bending stress** of the material. For chrome-vanadium spring steel, $\sigma_{allow} \approx 1200$–$1400$ MPa.
> 2. **Deflection check:** $\delta$ must not exceed $h_0$, otherwise the disc inverts and the load-deflection curve becomes unstable.
> 3. **Units mix-up:** KTU markers penalize heavy for mixed unit use. Always write "All values in SI units" at the start of numerical problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Disc & Torsional Springs; Bearing Lubrication)**

**Disc Springs (Belleville):**
- ☐ Disc spring formula: $F = \dfrac{4E\delta}{K_1 D^2}[(h_0 - \delta)(h_0 - \delta/2)t + t^3]$ — memorize this!
- ☐ $K_1$ depends only on $\alpha = D_e/D_i$; for $\alpha = 2$, $K_1 = 0.689$.
- ☐ Maximum stress occurs at the **inner edge (point I)**.
- ☐ $h_0/t < 0.4$ → regressive; $h_0/t > 0.4$ → progressive; $h_0/t = \sqrt{2}$ → linear.
- ☐ Series stacking → deflection adds up. Parallel stacking → load adds up.
- ☐ Poisson's ratio correction $(1 - \nu^2)$ appears in stress equation but NOT in load equation.

**Torsional Springs (Constant Load):**
- ☐ For a **torsion bar**: $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$, $\theta = TL/(GJ)$, $U = T^2L/(2GJ)$.
- ☐ For a **helical torsion spring** (arm loading): wire undergoes **bending** (not torsion) → use $\sigma = 32M/(\pi d^3)$, $\phi = 32 M n D_m / (\pi d^4 E)$.
- ☐ Constant load ⇒ design is based on **static yield** criterion, not fatigue.
- ☐ Polar moment $J = \pi d^4/32$ for solid circular shaft.

**Sliding Contact Bearings — Lubrication:**
- ☐ Petroff's equation: valid only for **concentric, lightly loaded, laminar** flow.
- ☐ $f_{Petroff} = 4\pi^2 \mu r^2 \omega / (P c)$ — friction **increases** with speed (true only at very low speeds).
- ☐ Reynolds equation origin: $\partial(q_x)/\partial x + \partial(q_z)/\partial z = -\partial h/\partial t$.
- ☐ Converging wedge $\Rightarrow$ **positive pressure** that supports load.
- ☐ Stribeck curve: friction peaks in **mixed lubrication** regime, drops in full-film hydrodynamic regime.
- ☐ Minimum film thickness $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ occurs at $\theta = \pi$ (point of maximum film pressure).
- ☐ Sommerfeld number $S_o$ is the key dimensionless group — higher $S_o$ = safer operation.
- ☐ Boundary lubrication: $\mu N / P$ small, asperities contact. Use solid lubricants (graphite, MoS₂) for high-temperature boundary cases.
- ☐ Unit bearing load $P = W/(l \cdot d)$ — projected area convention.

**Cross-Connections to KTU 2024 Syllabus (PCAGT403):**
- ☐ Module 1: Stress & strain analysis (used in spring derivation).
- ☐ Module 2: Power transmission — bearings support shafts.
- ☐ Module 3: Friction drives, clutches (Petroff's friction).
- ☐ Module 4 (current): Springs + Lubrication.
- ☐ Module 5: Dynamics of machines — vibration analysis of springs.
- ☐ Module 6: Balancing, cam design.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** In any bearing problem, **always check whether Petroff's assumptions are valid** (concentric? laminar? no leakage?). For disc springs, **draw a labeled cross-section** showing $D_e$, $D_i$, $t$, $h_0$, and $\delta$ — this earns you easy method marks even if the final answer has a numerical slip.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛢️ Module 4 — Lubricants: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Lubricant? (KTU 2024 Formal Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lubricant (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A *lubricant* is any substance (solid, semi-solid, liquid, or gaseous) interposed between two relatively moving surfaces to **reduce friction, minimize wear, dissipate heat, prevent corrosion, and seal contaminants**, thereby improving the efficiency and life of machine components.

A lubricant is therefore not merely a "slippery oil" — it is a **functional engineering material** designed to govern the tribological behaviour of machine elements such as bearings, gears, cams, and slides.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Ski Slope" Model

Imagine two rough wooden planks sliding against each other directly — they **squeak, slow down, and get hot**. Now pour a thin film of butter (your lubricant) between them.

The planks **float** on the butter layer:
- Friction drops dramatically (no direct metal-to-metal contact).
- Heat is carried away by the butter.
- The planks glide smoothly.

This is **exactly** what happens inside a hydrodynamic journal bearing — the shaft "floats" on a pressurised oil wedge. The lubricant is the *butter*; the shaft and bearing are the *planks*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **primary function of a lubricant is NOT to make surfaces "slippery"** — chemically all metals are equally slippery. The lubricant's real job is to **separate the surfaces** by maintaining a continuous hydrodynamic fluid film.

## 1.3 The Four Physical States of Lubricants

| State | Common Examples | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Liquid** | Mineral oils, synthetic oils, water | Journal bearings, gearboxes, hydraulic systems |
| **Semi-Solid** | Greases, petroleum jelly | Slow-speed rolling-element bearings, chassis points |
| **Solid** | Graphite, molybdenum disulphide (MoS₂), PTFE | High-temperature, vacuum, or food-grade applications |
| **Gaseous** | Air, helium | High-speed spindles, gas bearings, instrument pivots |

## 1.4 The Five Primary Functions of a Lubricant (MUST MEMORISE)

1. **Friction Reduction** — lowers tangential resistance between moving surfaces.
2. **Wear Prevention** — stops direct asperity contact.
3. **Heat Dissipation** — carries away frictional heat (especially in journal bearings).
4. **Corrosion Protection** — forms a chemical barrier against moisture/oxygen.
5. **Contaminant Removal** — flushes wear debris out of the contact zone (via oil flow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secondary functions** include dampening vibration, sealing gaps (oil in lip seals), and transmitting power (hydraulic fluids).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Essential Physical & Chemical Properties of Lubricants

The KTU board examiner expects you to define, state the significance, and quote the standard **ASTM/IS test method** for each of these properties.

### 2.1.1 Viscosity — The Single Most Important Property

**Viscosity** is the **internal resistance of a fluid to flow** — i.e., its "thickness" or "stickiness".

> [!NOTE]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity (KTU Favourite):**
> Shear stress in a fluid is **directly proportional** to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) across the flow direction.
>
> $$\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (N/m²)
- $\mu$ = dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $du/dy$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹)

#### The Two Forms of Viscosity

| Type | Symbol | Formula | Unit (SI) | Practical Use |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Dynamic / Absolute Viscosity** | $\mu$ | Shear stress / Shear rate | Pa·s | Used in design equations (Reynolds, Petroff) |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu$ | $\mu / \rho$ | m²/s (or cSt) | Used in empirical rating (SAE grades) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 Poise (CGS)** = **0.1 Pa·s**; **1 cSt (centi-Stokes)** = **1 mm²/s**. A typical engine oil SAE 20W-40 has $\mu \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ Pa·s at 40 °C.

---

### 2.1.2 Flash Point, Fire Point & Pour Point

| Property | Definition | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Flash Point** | Lowest temperature at which oil vapour **momentarily ignites** in presence of a flame | Safety in storage/transport; transformer oils require **high** flash point (≥140 °C) |
| **Fire Point** | Temperature at which vapour burns **continuously for ≥5 seconds** | Slightly above flash point; critical for high-temperature service |
| **Pour Point** | Lowest temperature at which oil **just flows** under standard test conditions | Cold-start lubrication; arctic-grade oils need very low pour point |
| **Cloud Point** | Temperature at which wax crystals **first appear** (oil becomes cloudy) | Indicates onset of flow problems in cold climates |

---

### 2.1.3 Neutralization Number, Saponification & Acidity

- **Acid Number (AN) / Neutralization Number:** Mass of **KOH (in mg)** required to neutralise the acid in **1 gram** of oil. Indicates oxidation degradation.
- **Saponification Number:** Mass of **KOH (in mg)** required to saponify the fatty acids in **1 gram** of oil. Indicates the amount of fatty base — high in vegetable oils.

---

### 2.1.4 Viscosity Index (VI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an **empirical, dimensionless number** that quantifies the **rate of change of viscosity with temperature**. Higher VI ⇒ oil is more stable across temperatures.

| VI Range | Classification | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 – 40 | Low VI | Poor paraffinic oils |
| 40 – 80 | Medium VI | Conventional mineral oils |
| 80 – 100 | High VI | Premium turbine & hydraulic oils |
| \> 100 | Very High VI | Synthetic / multi-grade engine oils (e.g., SAE 5W-30) |

---

### 2.1.5 Emulsification, Demulsibility & Oiliness

- **Emulsification:** Tendency of oil to mix with water (forming an emulsion).
- **Demulsibility:** Ability to **separate** from water — *turbine oils must have HIGH demulsibility*.
- **Oiliness:** The capacity of an oil to stick to a metal surface; primarily a property of the **polar molecules** (fatty acids) in the lubricant.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise this table — these are the most frequently asked equations in KTU ESE Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Viscometer calibration |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | Empirical ratings |
| 3 | $F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous shear force | Bearing design |
| 4 | $\mu_{kin} = \mu_{dyn} \cdot g / \rho$ (in CGS) | Old CGS relation | Unit conversion |
| 5 | $\eta_{rel} = \mu / \mu_{water}$ | Relative viscosity | Concept question |
| 6 | $\tau_{Petroff} = \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N L r^{3}}{c}$ | Petroff's bearing friction | Lightly loaded bearings |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{6 \mu U L}{h^{2}}$ (approx.) | Pressure in converging wedge | Hydrodynamic film |
| 8 | $h_{min} = r \left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{k^{2}}{1+k^{2}}}\right)$ | Min film thickness | Raimondi-Boyd charts |
| 9 | $h_{0} = r (1 - \epsilon) \cdot (1+\epsilon)$ | Min film thickness (approx.) | Quick design estimate |
| 10 | $\mu_{mix} = \mu_{1}^{x} \cdot \mu_{2}^{1-x}$ (Reistle / Bingham) | Viscosity of oil blend | Refinery blending |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the Petroff equation, $c$ = **radial clearance** ($c = R - r$), $N$ = rpm, $L$ = bearing length, $r$ = journal radius. Use **SI units throughout** to avoid the $\pi$ confusion.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lubricant selection directly determines:
- **Machine reliability** — proper film thickness prevents seizure and fatigue spalling.
- **Energy efficiency** — friction losses in IC engines (~15% of fuel energy) and gearboxes can be cut by half with correct oil.
- **Maintenance cost** — every 1 °C drop in bearing operating temperature roughly doubles the grease life.
- **Environmental compliance** — bio-degradable esters replace mineral oils in marine stern tubes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Newton's Law of Viscosity from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is asked directly in 14-mark KTU ESE questions under the sub-heading *"Explain Newton's law of viscosity and derive the relationship between shear stress and velocity gradient."*

**Step 1 — Visualise the Fluid Layer**

Consider a fluid trapped between two parallel plates of area $A$ and separated by a small gap $dy$. The **top plate moves** with velocity $u$, the **bottom plate is stationary**.

The fluid layer in contact with the moving plate moves at $u$; the layer in contact with the stationary plate is at rest. The velocity varies **linearly** in between for a Newtonian fluid.

**Step 2 — Define the Velocity Gradient**

The rate of change of velocity with distance $y$ is:

$$\dfrac{du}{dy}$$

This is also called the **rate of shear strain** $\dot{\gamma}$ (gamma-dot) and has units of s⁻¹.

**Step 3 — Apply the Proportionality**

Experiments (Newton, 1687) show that the **shear force F** required to move the plate is:

$$F \propto A \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

Introducing the constant of proportionality $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity):

$$F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Force to Shear Stress**

Shear stress $\tau$ is force per unit area:

$$\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

Substituting:

$$\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

This is **Newton's Law of Viscosity**. ✔

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Petroff's Equation for Bearing Friction

Petroff's equation gives the **coefficient of friction** for a *lightly loaded*, concentric journal bearing — a KTU classic.

**Step 1 — Assumptions**
- Shaft runs **concentric** in the bearing (no eccentricity).
- Bearing is **unloaded** — only viscous drag from the oil film resists rotation.
- Oil viscosity $\mu$ is constant (isothermal).

**Step 2 — Velocity at the Shaft Surface**

The shaft rotates at $N$ rps with radius $r$. The linear surface velocity is:

$$U = 2\pi r N$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Gradient in the Clearance**

With clearance $c = R - r$, assuming a **linear velocity profile** (very thin film, low speed):

$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{U}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Shear Force on the Shaft**

By Newton's law:

$$F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$$

With $A = 2\pi r L$ (the cylindrical surface area of the journal):

$$F = \mu (2\pi r L) \cdot \dfrac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Friction**

Friction torque is $T = F \cdot r$. Coefficient of friction is $f = F / W$, where $W$ is the load. For a *concentric, lightly-loaded* bearing we have the textbook result:

$$f = 2\pi^{2} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$$

Where $P = W / (2rL)$ is the **bearing pressure**.

In more compact form (standard KTU notation):

$$\tau = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} \mu N L r^{3}}{c}$$

This is the **total frictional torque** on the shaft. ✔

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Lubricant Property for Given Application

| Application | Critical Property | Typical Value Required | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transformer oil** | High dielectric strength, high flash point | Flash point ≥ 140 °C | Prevents fire & electrical breakdown |
| **Arctic engine oil** | Very low pour point | ≤ −35 °C | Cold-start flowability |
| **Turbine oil** | High demulsibility, anti-foam | Emulsion separation < 30 min | Water contamination removal |
| **Gear lubricant** | High viscosity index, EP additives | VI ≥ 95 | High tooth-contact pressure |
| **Cutting fluid** | High oiliness, cooling ability | Active sulphur or chlorine | Reduces tool–chip friction |
| **Grease for ball bearing** | High drop point, mechanical stability | Drop point ≥ 150 °C | Stays in raceway at temperature |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A journal bearing of diameter $80$ mm and length $120$ mm runs at $1440$ rpm. The radial clearance is $0.08$ mm and the oil viscosity is $0.05$ Pa·s. Using **Petroff's equation**, determine:
> (a) the frictional torque, and
> (b) the power loss in the bearing.

**Given Data**
- $d = 80$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 0.040$ m
- $L = 120$ mm $= 0.120$ m
- $N = 1440$ rpm $= 24$ rps
- $c = 0.08$ mm $= 8 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s

**Step (a) — Frictional Torque**

Using the derived Petroff formula:

$$T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} \mu N L r^{3}}{c}$$

Substituting:

$$T = \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} (0.05)(24)(0.120)(0.040)^{3}}{8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Evaluate the numerator piece by piece:

$$4 \pi^{2} = 39.4784$$

$$0.05 \times 24 = 1.2$$

$$1.2 \times 0.120 = 0.144$$

$$0.040^{3} = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$0.144 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 9.216 \times 10^{-6}$$

Multiply by $39.4784$:

$$39.4784 \times 9.216 \times 10^{-6} = 3.638 \times 10^{-4}$$

Divide by $c$:

$$T = \dfrac{3.638 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.547 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step (b) — Power Loss**

Angular velocity $\omega = 2 \pi N = 2 \pi (24) = 150.80$ rad/s.

$$P = T \cdot \omega = 4.547 \times 150.80 = 685.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.686 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer (in the KTU answer-script format):**

> Frictional torque $T = 4.55$ N·m; Power loss $P = 0.686$ kW. ✔
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lubricant Property Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The flowchart below shows how a design engineer arrives at a lubricant specification for a given machine element. KTU ESE questions on *lubricant selection* often sketch the same logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Machine Service] --> B0{Operating Temperature Range}
    B0 -- High Temp → C0[Select High Flash Point Oil]
    B0 -- Low Temp → C1[Select Low Pour Point Oil]
    B0 -- Wide Range → C2[Select High Viscosity Index Oil]
    C0 --> D0{Load and Speed}
    C1 --> D0
    C2 --> D0
    D0 -- High Load → E0[Use EP Additives]
    D0 -- High Speed → E1[Use Low Viscosity Oil]
    D0 -- Low Speed High Load → E2[Use Grease or Solid Film]
    E0 --> F0{Environment}
    E1 --> F0
    E2 --> F0
    F0 -- Wet → G0[High Demulsibility Required]
    F0 -- Dusty → G1[Sealed Grease Preferred]
    F0 -- Vacuum → G2[Solid Lubricant MoS2 or Graphite]
    G0 --> H0[Final Lubricant Specification]
    G1 --> H0
    G2 --> H0
    H0 --> Z0[End: Issue Lubricant Schedule]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Hydrodynamic Lubrication Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Shaft Rotating at N rpm] --> S1[Drags Oil into Converging Wedge]
    S1 --> S2[Pressure Build Up in Wedge]
    S2 --> S3[Film Pressure Supports Load W]
    S3 --> S4[Complete Separation of Surfaces]
    S4 --> S5[Friction Drops to Purely Viscous]
    S5 --> S6[Hydrodynamic Full Film Lubrication]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Lubricant Property Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Lubricant Properties] --> P1[Physical Properties]
    P0 --> P2[Chemical Properties]
    P0 --> P3[Performance Properties]
    P1 --> P11[Viscosity]
    P1 --> P12[Flash and Fire Point]
    P1 --> P13[Pour and Cloud Point]
    P1 --> P14[Density and Specific Gravity]
    P2 --> P21[Acid Number]
    P2 --> P22[Saponification Number]
    P2 --> P23[Carbon Residue]
    P2 --> P24[Emulsification Tendency]
    P3 --> P31[Viscosity Index]
    P3 --> P32[Demulsibility]
    P3 --> P33[Anti Foaming]
    P3 --> P34[EP and AW Additives]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Pressure-Fed Lubrication System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Oil Sump or Reservoir] --> T1[Oil Pump]
    T1 --> T2[Pressure Relief Valve]
    T1 --> T3[Oil Filter]
    T3 --> T4[Oil Cooler]
    T4 --> T5[Main Gallery]
    T5 --> T6[Journal Bearing 1]
    T5 --> T7[Journal Bearing 2]
    T5 --> T8[Gear Train]
    T6 --> T9[Return to Sump]
    T7 --> T9
    T8 --> T9
    T9 --> T0
    T2 --> T9
```
