# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** Define the term **"Viscosity Index"** of a lubricant and state its significance. What is the typical VI range for a multi-grade engine oil?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Viscosity Index (VI) is an empirical, dimensionless number which indicates the **rate at which the viscosity of an oil changes with temperature**. A high VI means viscosity varies only slightly with temperature, ensuring adequate lubrication in both cold-start and high-temperature running conditions. Standard paraffinic oils are assigned VI = 100 (Pennsylvania crude) and VI = 0 (Texas Gulf crude); other oils are interpolated. A multi-grade engine oil such as SAE 20W-40 has a VI typically in the range of **120 – 150**, indicating excellent thermal stability.* ✔

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Flash Point** and **Fire Point** of a lubricating oil. Why are both properties critical in transformer oil specifications?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Flash Point | Fire Point |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Definition | Lowest temperature at which oil vapour **momentarily ignites** (flame dies out) when a test flame is brought near | Lowest temperature at which oil vapour **burns continuously** for at least 5 seconds |
> | Typical value (engine oil) | 200 – 230 °C | 220 – 260 °C |
> | Relation | Fire Point is **always higher** by 5 – 30 °C | — |
>
> *In a transformer, the oil acts as both coolant and dielectric. A **low flash point** poses a serious fire hazard in the confined substation environment. Hence IS 335 specifies a minimum flash point of **140 °C** (closed cup) for transformer oils.* ✔

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU Module-Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1 — Lubricant Property Questions**
> Common mark-loss areas:
> 1. **Confusing cloud point and pour point** — cloud point is the *appearance* of wax, pour point is the *cessation of flow*. KTU evaluators award **½ mark deduction** per such error.
> 2. **Omitting ASTM/IS test method** — for every property, name the standard test (e.g., *Flash Point by Cleveland Open Cup — ASTM D92*). Missing this loses 1 mark per property.
> 3. **Wrong units in Petroff's equation** — mixing CGS Poise and SI Pa·s is the #1 numerical error. Always convert to SI first.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Lubricant Selection + Petroff Derivation**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of lubricants** based on physical state. State **two applications** of each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A hydrodynamic journal bearing has the following data:
> Diameter $d = 100$ mm, length $L = 150$ mm, speed $N = 900$ rpm, radial clearance $c = 0.10$ mm, oil viscosity $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s. Using **Petroff's equation**, determine:
> (i) the frictional torque, and
> (ii) the power loss in the bearing. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Lubricants are classified into four physical states:

1. **Liquid Lubricants** — Most common. Mineral oils, synthetic esters, vegetable oils. *Applications: IC engine crankcase, hydraulic systems, gearboxes.*

2. **Semi-Solid Lubricants (Greases)** — Oil + thickener (lithium/calcium soap). *Applications: Wheel bearings of automobiles, rolling-element bearings in low-speed machinery.*

3. **Solid Lubricants** — Graphite, MoS₂, PTFE, mica. *Applications: High-temperature oven chains, food-grade machinery, vacuum environment bearings.*

4. **Gaseous Lubricants** — Air, helium. *Applications: High-speed instrument bearings, gyroscopes, dental drills.*

(Allocate ~1.75 marks per type; include neat diagram, two applications per type.) ✔

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $d = 0.100$ m $\Rightarrow r = 0.050$ m; $L = 0.150$ m; $N = 900/60 = 15$ rps; $c = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m; $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Petroff's Friction Torque Formula**

$$T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} \mu N L r^{3}}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

Numerator evaluation:

$$4 \pi^{2} = 39.4784$$

$$\mu N L r^{3} = (0.04)(15)(0.150)(0.050)^{3} = (0.04)(15)(0.150)(1.25 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= (0.04)(15)(1.875 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.125 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$T_{num} = 39.4784 \times 1.125 \times 10^{-5} = 4.441 \times 10^{-4}$$

Divide by clearance:

$$T = \dfrac{4.441 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.44 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Power Loss**

$$\omega = 2 \pi N = 2 \pi (15) = 94.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P = T \cdot \omega = 4.44 \times 94.25 = 418.4 \text{ W} \approx 0.418 \text{ kW}$$

> **Final Answer (Part b):** Frictional torque $T = 4.44$ N·m; Power loss $P = 0.418$ kW. ✔

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Lubricant Properties + Viscosity Blending**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the working of a **Redwood Viscometer No. 1**. Derive the relation between **kinematic and dynamic viscosity**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Two oils of viscosities $\mu_1 = 0.080$ Pa·s and $\mu_2 = 0.020$ Pa·s are blended in equal volumes. Using the **Reistle equation**, determine the **viscosity of the blend**. State one limitation of the Reistle equation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Redwood Viscometer No. 1 (Diagram Marks — 2)**

Components: oil cup with orifice, water bath for temperature control, 50 mL kohlrausch flask, leveling screw, thermometer, ball valve closing the orifice.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Fill the oil cup up to the tip of the gauge point.
2. Heat the surrounding water bath to desired test temperature (e.g., 60 °C).
3. Open the ball valve; oil flows through the standard orifice.
4. Measure time in **seconds** for **50 mL** of oil to collect in the kohlrausch flask — this is the **Redwood Seconds (R)** at that temperature.

**Kinematic Viscosity Relation (2 marks):**

By the empirical Redwood formula:

$$\nu = (0.0026 t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}) \text{ cSt}$$

where $t$ is time in seconds. Once kinematic viscosity is known, dynamic viscosity follows from:

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$$

where $\rho$ is oil density. This completes the derivation $\nu = \mu / \rho$. ✔

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $\mu_1 = 0.080$ Pa·s, $\mu_2 = 0.020$ Pa·s, equal-volume blend ⇒ $x = 0.5$.

**Reistle Equation:**

$$\mu_{mix} = \mu_{1}^{x} \cdot \mu_{2}^{1-x}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\mu_{mix} = (0.080)^{0.5} \cdot (0.020)^{0.5}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $(0.080)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.080} = 0.2828$

**Step 2:** Compute $(0.020)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.020} = 0.1414$

**Step 3:** Multiply:

$$\mu_{mix} = 0.2828 \times 0.1414 = 0.0400 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Final Answer (Part b):** $\mu_{mix} = 0.040$ Pa·s. ✔

**Limitation of Reistle Equation (1 mark):**
*It is purely empirical and assumes ideal blending behaviour. It does not account for the chemical interaction between the two oil bases (e.g., additive precipitation, wax crystallisation) and is unreliable when the viscosity ratio $\mu_1 / \mu_2$ exceeds 20.* ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2 — Petroff's Equation Numerical Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm → rps** before substituting in $T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\mu N L r^{3}}{c}$. Always divide by 60. Common error: -2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $c$ and $r$** — Petroff's equation uses **radial clearance** $c$, not the journal radius $r$. Examiner allots **½ mark** for this unit/dimensional mix-up.
> 3. **Petroff's equation is valid only for concentric, lightly-loaded bearings** — students who apply it to a high-load hydrodynamic bearing lose **1 mark** for wrong assumption.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!)**

- ⭐ **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$ — pure memorise.
- ⭐ **Petroff's torque equation:** $T = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}\mu N L r^{3}}{c}$ — memorise **and** know the assumptions.
- ⭐ **Viscosity Index (VI)** = empirical, dimensionless, **higher is better**.
- ⭐ **Flash point** (momentary ignition) < **Fire point** (continuous burn). Difference: 5 – 30 °C.
- ⭐ **Pour point** = lowest flow temperature; **Cloud point** = first wax crystal appearance.
- ⭐ **Transformer oil** ⇒ high flash point (≥ 140 °C) and high dielectric strength.
- ⭐ **Turbine oil** ⇒ high **demulsibility** (quick water separation).
- ⭐ **Arctic oil** ⇒ low **pour point** (≤ −35 °C).
- ⭐ **Viscosity mixing** ⇒ use **Reistle equation** when blend ratio by volume is known.
- ⭐ **1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s**; **1 cSt = 1 mm²/s**.
- ⭐ **Dynamic vs Kinematic:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — keep SI units consistent.
- ⭐ **Viscometers to remember:** **Redwood** (UK), **Saybolt** (US), **Engler** (Europe) — all measure kinematic viscosity indirectly.
- ⭐ **Acidity / Neutralization Number** = mg KOH / g oil — indicates oxidation.
- ⭐ **Saponification Number** = mg KOH to saponify 1 g oil — high in vegetable oils.
- ⭐ **Solid lubricants** (graphite, MoS₂) are used in high-temp, vacuum, or food-grade environments.
- ⭐ **Grease selection:** match **drop point** to operating temperature, and **consistency (NLGI grade)** to bearing type.
- ⭐ **Emulsification** is good for **cutting fluids**; **demulsibility** is good for **turbine/hydraulic oils**.

> 🏁 **End of Module 4 — Lubricants Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme | PCAGT403)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Viscosity?

**Viscosity** is the fundamental internal property of a fluid (liquid or gas) that quantifies its **resistance to gradual deformation by shear stress or tensile stress**. In simpler terms, it is the measure of a fluid's "thickness" or its internal friction, which determines how easily it flows.

In the context of **Theory of Machines**, viscosity is of paramount importance in **tribology**, specifically in the analysis and design of **bearings, lubrication systems, and friction drives**. The performance of hydrodynamic and boundary-lubricated bearings is governed almost entirely by the viscous behavior of the lubricant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Viscosity is treated as a foundational concept supporting bearing friction analysis, Petroff's equation, and the design of journal bearings. Mastery of this topic is essential for solving numerical problems on friction torque in bearings.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Newton's Law of Viscosity

According to **Sir Isaac Newton's Law of Viscosity (1687)**, the shear stress ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of change of velocity with respect to the perpendicular distance from the fixed surface (velocity gradient, $\frac{du}{dy}$).

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress developed in the fluid, in **N/m²** or **Pa**
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of dynamic (absolute) viscosity, in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain), in **s⁻¹**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fluids that obey this linear relationship ($\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$) are called **Newtonian Fluids** (e.g., water, air, mineral oils at moderate conditions). Fluids that do not follow this law are called **Non-Newtonian Fluids** (e.g., blood, paints, greases, polymer solutions).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Honey vs. Water Intuition

Imagine pouring **honey** and **water** down the same inclined plane.

- The **water** flows rapidly and freely. Its internal layers slide past each other with minimal resistance. This indicates **low viscosity**.
- The **honey** flows slowly, almost reluctantly, because the internal molecular layers resist sliding past one another. This indicates **high viscosity**.

Now apply this to a **shaft rotating inside a journal bearing**. The lubricant (say, SAE 30 oil) sits between the rotating journal and the stationary bearing surface. The layers of oil closest to the rotating shaft are dragged along with it, while the layers nearest the stationary bearing remain still. Viscosity is what creates the **shear stress** that resists this relative motion — and it is precisely this viscous shear stress that supports the load on the bearing.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Connection:** Without viscosity, there would be no load-carrying capacity in hydrodynamic bearings. The viscous oil wedge forms a pressure distribution that keeps the shaft "floating," preventing metal-to-metal contact. This is why high-viscosity oils are chosen for slow, heavily loaded bearings, and low-viscosity oils are chosen for high-speed bearings.

## 1.4 Two Key Viscosity Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Standard Unit |
|-----------|--------|------------|----------------|
| **Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity** | $\mu$ | Measure of internal fluid friction resisting shear | **Pa·s** (or N·s/m²) |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu$ | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density | **m²/s** |
| **Fluidity** | $\phi$ | Reciprocal of dynamic viscosity: $\phi = \frac{1}{\mu}$ | **m²/(N·s)** |

The mathematical relationship between them is:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Where $\rho$ is the **mass density of the fluid** in kg/m³.

## 1.5 Legacy and Modern Units of Viscosity

Since this topic appears frequently in KTU numerical problems, you must master all unit conversions.

| Quantity | CGS Unit | SI Unit | Conversion |
|----------|----------|---------|------------|
| Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | **Poise (P)** or **Centipoise (cP)** | **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²** | 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s; 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s |
| Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | **Stoke (St)** or **Centistoke (cSt)** | **m²/s** | 1 Stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s; 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** 1 cP of water at 20.2°C = 0.001 Pa·s. The viscosity of water at room temperature is a commonly used benchmark reference in fluid mechanics problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Shear in a Fluid Film

Consider a fluid confined between **two parallel plates** of area $A$, separated by a small distance $h$. The lower plate is stationary, and the upper plate is moving with a velocity $U$.

Under steady-state, laminar flow conditions, a **linear velocity profile** develops across the fluid film:

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h}$$

The velocity gradient (rate of shear) is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

By Newton's law, the shear stress at any horizontal plane in the fluid is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

The **viscous force** resisting the motion of the upper plate is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \frac{A \cdot U}{h}$$

This force opposes the relative motion and represents the frictional resistance in a lubricated contact.

## 2.2 Three Critical Viscosity Concepts for KTU

### A. Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$)
- Represents the **true internal friction** of the fluid.
- Depends on both the fluid's molecular structure and temperature.
- Used directly in **Petroff's equation** for bearing friction torque.

### B. Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)
- Combines the effect of viscosity and density.
- Used in **Reynolds number** calculations: $Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{U L}{\nu}$
- Measured practically using **Saybolt, Redwood, or Engler viscometers**, which report kinematic viscosity in **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)**, **Redwood Seconds (RWs)**, or **Engler Degrees (°E)**.

### C. Viscosity Index (VI)
- An **empirical, dimensionless number** that indicates the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature.
- Introduced by **Dean and Davis in 1929**.
- **High VI oils** (e.g., VI = 100 for paraffinic base oils) change viscosity slowly with temperature.
- **Low VI oils** (e.g., VI = 0 for naphthenic base oils) change viscosity rapidly with temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For bearing design, **high VI lubricants** are preferred because they maintain adequate viscosity across the operating temperature range, ensuring the hydrodynamic film is not destroyed at high temperatures.

## 2.3 Effect of Temperature on Viscosity

Viscosity of liquids **decreases sharply** with increasing temperature, while viscosity of gases **increases** with temperature. The relationship is not linear.

### Empirical Equations (High-Yield for KTU):

**1. Vogel's Equation (or ASTM D341 standard equation):**

$$\log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log_{10}(T)$$

Where:
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity in cSt
- $T$ = Temperature in **Kelvin**
- $A, B$ = Fluid-specific constants

**2. Walther Equation (ASTM standard):**

$$\log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log_{10}(T)$$

(Same form, but used for petroleum oils specifically)

**3. Simple Exponential Approximation:**

$$\mu = \mu_0 \cdot e^{-k(T - T_0)}$$

Where $k$ is a fluid-specific constant and $\mu_0$ is the viscosity at reference temperature $T_0$.

## 2.4 Effect of Pressure on Viscosity

For most engineering calculations, viscosity is assumed **independent of pressure**. However, at very high pressures (above ~100 MPa), viscosity increases significantly.

The **Barus equation** (pressure-viscosity relationship):

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \cdot e^{\alpha p}$$

Where:
- $\mu_p$ = Viscosity at pressure $p$
- $\mu_0$ = Viscosity at atmospheric pressure
- $\alpha$ = Pressure-viscosity coefficient (typically $1 \times 10^{-8}$ to $3 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹ for mineral oils)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau$ in Pa, $\mu$ in Pa·s, gradient in s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic-Viscosity Relation | $\nu$ in m²/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 3 | $F = \mu \cdot \frac{A \cdot U}{h}$ | Viscous Shear Force | $F$ in N |
| 4 | $T_{friction} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N^2 r^3 L}{h}$ | **Petroff's Friction Torque** | $T$ in N·m |
| 5 | $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{L \cdot D}$ | Petroff's Bearing Load | $P$ in N/m² |
| 6 | $f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{h}$ | Coefficient of Friction (Petroff) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\mu_p = \mu_0 \cdot e^{\alpha p}$ | Barus Pressure-Viscosity | $\mu_p$ in Pa·s |
| 8 | $\log_{10}\log_{10}(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log_{10}(T)$ | Walther / ASTM Equation | $\nu$ in cSt, $T$ in K |
| 9 | $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$ | Unit Conversion | — |
| 10 | $1 \text{ Stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ | Unit Conversion | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Petroff's Equation (Row 4 & 5)** is the most heavily tested viscosity-related concept in KTU Theory of Machines. The friction torque of a journal bearing under laminar, thin-film, no-load conditions is derived directly from the viscous shear assumption.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Viscosity Analysis

1. **Bearing Design:** Petroff's equation uses $\mu$ to predict friction torque and power loss in journal bearings.
2. **Lubricant Selection:** SAE viscosity grades (e.g., SAE 10W-30) are based on viscosity-temperature behavior.
3. **Hydraulic Systems:** Viscosity dictates pressure drop, pumping power, and system efficiency.
4. **Friction Drives:** Traction drives rely on the shear stress in a lubricant film to transmit torque.
5. **Boundary Lubrication:** The ZN/ZTP anti-wear additives function by forming a low-shear-strength film, but the bulk viscosity of the oil still carries away heat.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Petroff's Equation for Friction Torque in a Journal Bearing

Petroff's equation gives the **friction torque** of a **lightly loaded, fully lubricated journal bearing** assuming a concentric journal, laminar flow, and no side leakage.

### Given Conditions:
- Journal radius: $r$ (m)
- Bearing length: $L$ (m)
- Radial clearance: $h$ (m)
- Dynamic viscosity of lubricant: $\mu$ (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- Journal speed: $N$ (rev/s) — Angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N$ (rad/s)
- Load on bearing: $W$ (N)

### Step 1 — Velocity of the Journal Surface
The journal rotates at angular velocity $\omega$. The surface velocity at radius $r$ is:

$$v = \omega \cdot r = 2\pi N r$$

### Step 2 — Velocity Gradient in the Concentric Film
Because the journal is concentric with the bearing, the lubricant film thickness $h$ is constant. The velocity varies **linearly** from 0 (at the stationary bearing surface) to $v$ (at the moving journal surface).

Therefore, the velocity gradient is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{v - 0}{h} = \frac{2\pi N r}{h}$$

### Step 3 — Shear Stress in the Film
Applying Newton's Law of Viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi N r}{h}$$

### Step 4 — Friction Force on a Differential Area
Consider a small elemental ring of the journal surface at radius $r$, subtending an angle $d\theta$ at the center. The area of this element is:

$$dA = r \cdot d\theta \cdot L$$

The differential friction force on this element is:

$$dF = \tau \cdot dA = \mu \cdot \frac{2\pi N r}{h} \cdot (r \, d\theta \, L) = \frac{2\pi N \mu L r^2}{h} \, d\theta$$

### Step 5 — Friction Torque from a Single Element
The torque produced by the force $dF$ acting at radius $r$ is:

$$dT = dF \cdot r = \frac{2\pi N \mu L r^3}{h} \, d\theta$$

### Step 6 — Integrate Around the Full Journal
Integrate $dT$ from $\theta = 0$ to $\theta = 2\pi$ (full circumference):

$$T = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{2\pi N \mu L r^3}{h} \, d\theta$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi N \mu L r^3}{h} \cdot [\,\theta\,]_0^{2\pi}$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi N \mu L r^3}{h} \cdot 2\pi$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{h}}$$

This is **Petroff's Equation** for the friction torque of a journal bearing.

### Step 7 — Coefficient of Friction
The frictional force on the journal is obtained by dividing the torque by the radius:

$$F = \frac{T}{r} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{h}$$

The bearing pressure is:

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{W}{2 r L}$$

The coefficient of friction is:

$$f = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{F}{2 r L P} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / h}{2 r L P} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P h}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Petroff's coefficient of friction is **inversely proportional to bearing pressure $P$** and **directly proportional to viscosity $\mu$ and speed $N$**. This is why heavily loaded bearings experience lower friction coefficients than lightly loaded ones at the same speed.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity from Dynamic Viscosity and Density

Starting from the defining relation:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Dimensional analysis confirms:

$$[\nu] = \frac{[\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}]}{[\text{kg/m}^3]} = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s}/\text{m}^2}{\text{kg}/\text{m}^3}$$

Substituting $\text{N} = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$:

$$[\nu] = \frac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2 \cdot \text{s}/\text{m}^2}{\text{kg}/\text{m}^3} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

Hence, kinematic viscosity has the unit **m²/s** in SI and **Stokes (St)** in CGS, where 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Converting Viscosity Units

**Problem:** A lubricant has a dynamic viscosity of $0.085$ Pa·s and a density of $880$ kg/m³. Calculate:
(a) The kinematic viscosity in m²/s, cm²/s, and Stokes.
(b) The viscosity in Poise and Centipoise.

### Part (a): Kinematic Viscosity

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.085 \text{ Pa·s}}{880 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 9.659 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Converting to cm²/s (1 m² = 10⁴ cm²):

$$\nu = 9.659 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^4 = 0.9659 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

Converting to Stokes (1 St = 1 cm²/s):

$$\nu = 0.9659 \text{ St} = 96.59 \text{ cSt}$$

### Part (b): Dynamic Viscosity in CGS

$$\mu = 0.085 \text{ Pa·s} \times \frac{1 \text{ Poise}}{0.1 \text{ Pa·s}} = 0.85 \text{ Poise}$$

$$\mu = 0.85 \times 100 = 85 \text{ cP}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Petroff's Bearing Friction Torque

**Problem:** A journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Radius: $r = 50$ mm = $0.05$ m
- Length: $L = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m
- Radial clearance: $h = 0.05$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- Speed: $N = 1500$ RPM = $25$ rev/s
- Lubricant dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s

Calculate the **friction torque** and the **power loss** in the bearing.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Petroff's equation:

$$T = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{h}$$

$$T = \frac{4 \pi^2 \times 0.05 \times 25 \times (0.05)^3 \times 0.1}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Calculate the numerator:

$$4 \times \pi^2 \approx 39.478$$

$$39.478 \times 0.05 \times 25 = 49.348$$

$$(0.05)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$49.348 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1 = 6.1685 \times 10^{-4}$$

Divide by $h = 5 \times 10^{-5}$:

$$T = \frac{6.1685 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 12.337 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2:** Power loss:

$$P_{loss} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot (2\pi N)$$

$$P_{loss} = 12.337 \times 2\pi \times 25 = 12.337 \times 157.08 = 1937.9 \text{ W} \approx 1.94 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Significance:** Even a single lightly loaded bearing can dissipate nearly 2 kW. In a machine with 10 such bearings, that's ~20 kW of wasted power — directly impacting energy efficiency.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Parameters on Viscosity-Related Bearing Performance

| Parameter | Effect on Viscosity $\mu$ | Effect on Friction Torque $T$ | Effect on Coefficient of Friction $f$ | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Increase Temperature | **Decreases** (liquids) | **Decreases** | **Decreases** | Less friction, but thinner film — risk of metal contact |
| Increase Pressure | Slightly increases (Barus) | Slightly increases | Complex (denominator dominates) | EP additives become critical at high contact pressures |
| Increase Speed $N$ | No direct effect (assuming isothermal) | **Increases linearly** | **Increases** | Higher power loss, more cooling required |
| Increase Load $W$ | No direct effect | No direct effect on $T$, but $P$ increases | **Decreases** (denominator effect) | Heavily loaded bearings run with lower $f$ |
| Increase Clearance $h$ | No direct effect | **Decreases** (inverse) | **Decreases** | More clearance = less viscous shear, but more vibration risk |
| Increase Viscosity Index (VI) | Stabilizes $\mu$ vs. T | More stable $T$ over temperature range | More stable $f$ | Preferred for variable-temperature service |
