# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the *attitude angle* of a journal bearing and explain how it varies with the eccentricity ratio.

**Model Answer:**
The **attitude angle** $\phi$ is the angle measured from the line of action of the applied load to the line joining the bearing centre to the journal centre, in the direction of rotation.

- At $\varepsilon \to 0$ (concentric), $\phi \to 0^\circ$.
- At $\varepsilon \to 1$ (nearly touching), $\phi \to 90^\circ$.
- It *monotonically* increases with $\varepsilon$, typically ranging from $\sim 6^\circ$ at $\varepsilon = 0.2$ to $\sim 80^\circ$ at $\varepsilon = 0.9$.

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State the **assumptions** made in deriving the Reynolds equation for hydrodynamic lubrication.

**Model Answer:**
1. Lubricant is a Newtonian fluid (viscosity constant).
2. Flow is laminar.
3. The fluid film is *thin* compared with bearing dimensions; body forces and inertia are neglected.
4. No-slip at the bearing and journal surfaces.
5. Pressure is constant across the film thickness.
6. The bearing is infinitely long in the axial direction (no side leakage).
7. Lubricant obeys the continuity equation (steady, incompressible).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> (a) **Derive Petroff's equation** for the friction torque in a journal bearing, clearly stating the assumptions. **[7 marks]**
>
> (b) A 60 mm diameter journal runs at 1200 rpm in a 60.10 mm diameter bearing of length 80 mm. The lubricant viscosity is 0.025 Pa·s. Find (i) the coefficient of friction, (ii) the friction torque, and (iii) the power loss. **[7 marks]**

#### (a) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Define geometry.** $r = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m, $c = (60.10 - 60)/2 = 0.05$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $L = 0.08$ m.

**Step 2 — Velocity at journal surface.**
$$U = \omega r = 2\pi N r = 2\pi \cdot 20 \cdot 0.03 = 3.77 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Shear stress (Petroff's assumption: uniform film $h = c$).**
$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{c} = 0.025 \cdot \frac{3.77}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1885 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Friction force.**
$$F_f = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L = 1885 \cdot 2\pi (0.03)(0.08) \approx 28.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Friction torque.**
$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = 28.4 \times 0.03 = 0.853 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Power loss.**
$$P = T_f \cdot \omega = 0.853 \times 2\pi(20) \approx 107 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient of friction.**
$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} \quad \text{but Petroff is for } W \to 0$$
For Petroff's formula, $f$ expressed as $f = 2\pi^2 \mu N r / (P c)$. With no load given, $f$ cannot be evaluated directly — only the absolute friction force/torque are valid.

#### (b) Model Solution

Given: $\mu = 0.025$ Pa·s, $N = 1200$ rpm $= 20$ rev/s, $r = 0.03$ m, $c = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $L = 0.08$ m.

(i) **Coefficient of friction** (Petroff's formula):
$$f = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

Since $W$ is not specified, Petroff's coefficient of friction cannot be directly evaluated — instead compute the friction force and torque.

(ii) **Friction torque:**
$$T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c} = \frac{4\pi^2 (0.025)(20)(0.03)^3 (0.08)}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{4\pi^2 (0.025)(20)(2.7 \times 10^{-5})(0.08)}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{4\pi^2 (0.025)(20)(0.08)(2.7 \times 10^{-5})}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{0.00426}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \\[4pt]
    &\approx 0.853 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

(iii) **Power loss:**
$$P = \frac{8\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L}{c} \approx 107 \text{ W}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> (a) **Derive the Reynolds equation** for pressure distribution in the lubricant film of a journal bearing, listing the assumptions used. **[7 marks]**
>
> (b) Define the **Sommerfeld number** and derive its relation to the eccentricity ratio for an infinitely-long journal bearing. A bearing has $L/D = 0.8$, $r/c = 1000$, $\varepsilon = 0.6$. Compute the Sommerfeld number and the load capacity if $\mu = 0.02$ Pa·s and $N = 2400$ rpm, $r = 50$ mm. **[7 marks]**

#### (a) Model Solution

(See **Derivation II in Section 3.2** for the full step-by-step derivation. Brief outline:)

1. Set up Navier–Stokes for thin-film flow: $\partial p/\partial x = \mu \partial^2 u/\partial y^2$.
2. Integrate twice with no-slip at $y = 0$ (journal) and $y = h$ (bearing).
3. Find velocity profile:
$$u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(y^2 - yh) + U\left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)$$
4. Compute volume flow $q = \int_0^h u \, dy$.
5. Apply continuity $dq/dx = 0$.
6. Convert $x \to r\theta$ to get:
$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = 6\mu \omega r^2 \frac{dh}{d\theta}$$

#### (b) Model Solution

**Sommerfeld Number Definition:**
$$S_o = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2, \quad \text{where } P = \frac{W}{2rL}$$

**Relation to eccentricity (from Section 3.3):**
$$S_o = \frac{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{12\pi^2 \varepsilon}$$

**Given:** $L/D = 0.8$, $r/c = 1000$, $\varepsilon = 0.6$, $\mu = 0.02$ Pa·s, $N = 2400/60 = 40$ rev/s, $r = 0.05$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute $S_o$ from $\varepsilon$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
S_o &= \frac{(2 + 0.36)\sqrt{1 - 0.36}}{12\pi^2 (0.6)} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{(2.36)(0.8)}{12\pi^2 (0.6)} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{1.888}{71.0} \approx 0.0266
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bearing pressure $P$:**
$$P = \frac{\mu N}{S_o}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{(0.02)(40)}{0.0266}(1000)^2 \approx 3.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the load capacity $W$:**
Bearing length $L = 0.8 \times D = 0.8 \times 0.1 = 0.08$ m.
$$W = P \cdot 2rL = (3.0 \times 10^7)(2)(0.05)(0.08) \approx 2.4 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 240 \text{ kN}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Hydrodynamic Theory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s** when substituting into Petroff's formulas. A factor-of-$2\pi$ slip-up instantly loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up Petroff's and Reynolds' domains**: Petroff assumes $e = 0$ (no load support); using Petroff to "calculate load" is a fundamental conceptual error.
> 3. **Confusing $r/c$ with $c/r$** in the Sommerfeld number — the squared term is *radius over clearance*, not the inverse.
> 4. **Wrong sign for $\cos\theta$ in film thickness** depending on whether $\theta$ is measured from the load line or from the line of centres.
> 5. **Neglecting cavitation boundary condition** ($p = p_a$ at $\theta = 0$ AND at the divergent side): the oil cannot sustain tension, so the integrand must be truncated where $p < p_a$.
> 6. **Ignoring units** of viscosity: 1 Pa·s $= 1$ N·s/m², not cP or poise.
> 7. **Drawing the pressure curve full circle**: in reality, the curve must end where pressure returns to atmospheric in the divergent region — full marks require showing this clearly in the diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hydrodynamic Theory**

- [x] **Petroff** = concentric, *only friction*, no load support. Use formulas:
  - $F_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c}$
  - $T_f = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$
  - $P = \frac{8\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L}{c}$
- [x] **Reynolds** = eccentric, *full pressure distribution*, leads to load support.
- [x] Final **Reynolds ODE** (1-D): $\dfrac{d}{d\theta}\!\left(h^3 \dfrac{dp}{d\theta}\right) = 6\mu\omega r^2 \dfrac{dh}{d\theta}$
- [x] **Film profile**: $h = c(1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta)$.
- [x] **Sommerfeld Number**: $S_o = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \dfrac{(2 + \varepsilon^2)\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^2}}{12\pi^2 \varepsilon}$.
- [x] Higher $S_o$ ⇒ thicker film ⇒ *safer* operation.
- [x] Typical safe $S_o > 0.05$ for moderate-load machinery.
- [x] **Attitude angle** $\phi$ increases from $0$ to $90^\circ$ as $\varepsilon$ increases from $0$ to $1$.
- [x] Minimum film thickness $h_{min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$.
- [x] Friction variable $\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)f$ is read from the **Sommerfeld chart** vs. $\varepsilon$ and $L/D$.
- [x] Cavitation in the divergent region ⇒ pressure returns to ambient (boundary condition).
- [x] Viscosity $\mu$ drops with temperature ⇒ $S_o$ drops ⇒ risk of metal-to-metal contact.
- [x] Side leakage reduces both load capacity and friction compared to infinite-length analysis.
- [x] Petroff's equations are *upper bounds* on friction — actual friction is *less* due to load-induced eccentricity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroff's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Petroff's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Petroff's Equation** is a classical hydrodynamic lubrication theory developed by the Russian engineer **Nikolai Petrov** in **1883**. It provides a theoretical relationship for the **coefficient of friction** in a **fully lubricated journal bearing** operating under conditions of **perfect concentricity** (zero eccentricity), where the bearing load is entirely supported by the **viscous shear resistance** of a thick, continuous lubricant film.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> Petroff's equation is a foundational analytical model in *Tribology* used to predict the minimum possible friction in a journal bearing. It assumes an idealized, hydrodynamically lubricated, concentric bearing and is the theoretical lower bound against which all real (eccentric) bearings are benchmarked.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey Between Two Glass Plates"

Imagine pressing two circular glass plates together with a thin film of **honey (lubricant)** trapped between them. If you slide the top plate sideways, the plate does **not** rub against the lower one — instead, the honey resists the motion through its **viscosity (stickiness)**. The faster you slide, the more resistance you feel. The thicker the honey layer, the easier the slide.

Petroff's equation works on the **exact same principle** for a shaft (journal) rotating inside a bearing. The lubricant film acts like the honey — resisting rotation purely through **viscous shear**, not through metal-to-metal contact. This is the dream state of lubrication: **zero wear, friction purely from fluid viscosity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Real-World Context:**
> Petroff's equation predicts the *minimum* coefficient of friction ($\mu_f$) in a hydrodynamic journal bearing. In practice, real bearings are **eccentric** (the shaft sits slightly off-center), and the actual friction is **higher** than Petroff's prediction. However, the equation remains indispensable for *design baseline calculations* and understanding the physics of fluid friction in bearings.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

The equation uses the following standard engineering metrics:

- **Absolute Viscosity (μ)** → measured in **N·s/m²** (or **Pa·s** or **reyn**)
- **Dynamic Viscosity (μ)** → SI unit: **Pa·s = kg/(m·s)**
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν)** → SI unit: **m²/s** (where $ν = μ/ρ$, with ρ being density)
- **Speed (N)** → measured in **rev/s** (revolutions per second)
- **Radius (r) and Clearance (c)** → measured in **meters (m)**
- **Bearing Pressure (P)** → measured in **N/m² (Pa)**

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Conversion Tip:** $1 \text{ centipoise (cP)} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$. Always convert viscosity to **SI units** before substituting into the equation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Petroff's Theory

Petroff's equation is built on a set of idealized assumptions. Understanding these assumptions is **critical** for both exams and engineering judgment, as the equation is **only valid** when these conditions are approximately met:

1. **Concentric Bearing Geometry:** The journal is **perfectly aligned** with the bearing — eccentricity ratio ($\epsilon$) = 0. The journal axis coincides with the bearing axis.
2. **Thick Film Lubrication:** A **continuous, unbroken film** of lubricant completely separates the journal from the bearing. There is no asperity (metal-to-metal) contact.
3. **Viscous (Laminar) Flow:** The lubricant flows in smooth, parallel streamlines with no turbulence. Reynolds number is low.
4. **Newtonian Fluid:** The lubricant obeys **Newton's law of viscosity** — shear stress is linearly proportional to the velocity gradient.
5. **Incompressible Flow:** Lubricant density remains constant.
6. **Constant Viscosity:** Viscosity (μ) does **not change** with pressure or temperature across the film (no thermal effects considered).
7. **Constant Film Thickness:** The radial clearance is uniform all around the journal.
8. **No Side Leakage:** Lubricant does not leak from the sides of the bearing; the flow is purely circumferential.
9. **Negligible Inertia:** Fluid inertia forces are negligible compared to viscous forces.
10. **Uniform Pressure:** The pressure variation in the film is negligible; only velocity gradient contributes to friction.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Theory

- **Why concentric?** Petroff wanted to isolate the *viscous friction* component, which exists even when there is no load-induced eccentricity. This isolates the baseline friction of a *frictionless* bearing.
- **How does friction arise?** When the journal rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, each particle of the lubricant film moves with a velocity that increases **linearly** from the bearing surface (stationary) to the journal surface (rotating at $\omega r$). The shear rate across this thin film of thickness $c$ is $\omega r / c$. The shear stress is $\tau = \mu \cdot (\omega r / c)$. Integrating this over the bearing surface gives the total friction force.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every Petroff's equation problem on KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Shear Stress** | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{\omega \cdot r}{c}$ | N/m² | Viscous shear in lubricant film |
| 2 | **Friction Force** | $F_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c}$ | N (Newtons) | Total tangential friction on journal |
| 3 | **Friction Torque** | $T_f = F_f \cdot r = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$ | N·m | Moment resisting rotation |
| 4 | **Coefficient of Friction** | $f = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ | dimensionless | Petroff's friction coefficient |
| 5 | **Power Loss** | $P_{\text{loss}} = 2\pi N T_f = \dfrac{8\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L}{c}$ | W (Watts) | Power dissipated as heat in oil |
| 6 | **Bearing Pressure** | $P = \dfrac{W}{2rL}$ | N/m² (Pa) | Projected load on bearing |
| 7 | **Angular Velocity Relation** | $\omega = 2\pi N$ | rad/s | Converts rev/s to rad/s |
| 8 | **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s | Ratio of dynamic to density |
| 9 | **Bearing Characteristic Number** | $\dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | dimensionless | The "bearing modulus" |
| 10 | **Specific Friction (variable form)** | $f \cdot \left(\dfrac{c}{r}\right) = 2\pi^2 \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | dimensionless | Dimensionless grouping |

> **Notation Key:**
> - $N$ = Speed in **rev/s** (NOT rpm; convert rpm to rev/s by dividing by 60)
> - $\mu$ = Absolute (dynamic) viscosity
> - $r$ = Journal radius
> - $c$ = Radial clearance (diametral clearance $c_d = 2c$)
> - $L$ = Axial length of bearing
> - $W$ = Applied load on bearing
> - $P$ = Bearing pressure

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Petroff's equation, despite its simplifying assumptions, is used in practice for:

- **Sizing preliminary journal bearings** in turbines, generators, and heavy rotating machinery.
- **Estimating minimum power loss** to size lubrication systems and coolers.
- **Benchmarking** real, eccentric bearings against the theoretical minimum friction.
- **Teaching the fundamental physics** of hydrodynamic lubrication before introducing more complex (Reynolds, Sommerfeld) models.
- **Quality control** in bearing testing — comparing measured friction to Petroff's prediction to detect wear, misalignment, or contamination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Petroff's Equation

We will derive Petroff's equation from the **fundamental Newtonian shear principle**, step by step, with every algebraic and physical transition explicitly shown.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry

Consider a journal of radius $r$ rotating inside a bearing of radius $R = r + c$, where $c$ is the radial clearance. The journal rotates at an angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s (or $N$ rev/s). The lubricant fills the cylindrical gap of thickness $c$ between them.

For a concentric bearing, the gap thickness is **uniform** all around the journal: $h(\theta) = c$ for all angles $\theta$.

### Step 2 — Velocity Distribution in the Film

Due to the **no-slip boundary condition**:
- At the **bearing surface** (radius $R$): fluid velocity = 0 (stationary).
- At the **journal surface** (radius $r$): fluid velocity = $U = \omega \cdot r$ (rotating with the journal).

Since the film is very thin ($c \ll r$) and the bearing is concentric, the velocity in the gap varies **linearly** from 0 at the outer wall to $\omega r$ at the journal surface.

The **velocity gradient** (shear rate) is:

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{c} = \frac{\omega \cdot r}{c}
$$

### Step 3 — Shear Stress from Newton's Law of Viscosity

By Newton's law of viscosity:

$$
\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \mu \cdot \frac{\omega \cdot r}{c}
$$

This is the shear stress acting on the journal surface, **tangential to the rotation direction** (opposing the motion).

### Step 4 — Differential Friction Force on an Element

Consider a small elemental area on the journal surface: $dA = r \, d\theta \, L$, where $L$ is the axial length of the bearing.

The differential friction force on this element is:

$$
dF = \tau \cdot dA = \tau \cdot r \, d\theta \, L
$$

Substituting $\tau$:

$$
dF = \mu \cdot \frac{\omega \cdot r}{c} \cdot r \, d\theta \, L = \frac{\mu \omega r^2 L}{c} \, d\theta
$$

### Step 5 — Integrate Around the Journal

Integrate $dF$ around the full circumference ($\theta = 0$ to $2\pi$):

$$
F_f = \int_{0}^{2\pi} dF = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{\mu \omega r^2 L}{c} \, d\theta
$$

Since all terms are constants (concentric case):

$$
F_f = \frac{\mu \omega r^2 L}{c} \cdot \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = \frac{\mu \omega r^2 L}{c} \cdot 2\pi
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_f = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega r^2 L}{c}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert Angular Velocity to Revolutions per Second

Recall: $\omega = 2\pi N$, where $N$ is in rev/s.

Substituting:

$$
F_f = \frac{2\pi \mu (2\pi N) r^2 L}{c} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the Petroff friction force** — the total tangential force resisting rotation.

$$
F_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{2} L}{c}
$$

### Step 7 — Friction Torque

The torque is the friction force times the moment arm (the journal radius $r$):

$$
T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c} \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}
$$

$$
T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{3} L}{c}
$$

### Step 8 — Coefficient of Friction

The coefficient of friction is defined as:

$$
f = \frac{\text{Friction Force}}{\text{Normal Load}} = \frac{F_f}{W}
$$

The normal load $W$ on the bearing is related to the bearing pressure $P$ by:

$$
W = P \cdot (2rL)
$$

This is because the **projected area** of the bearing (load-carrying area) is $2r \cdot L$ (diameter × length), which is the standard bearing design convention.

Therefore:

$$
f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / c}{2rL \cdot P} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
f = \frac{2 \pi^{2}}{P} \cdot \frac{\mu N r}{c}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Petroff Coefficient of Friction Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}\,}
$$

This is the most-cited form of Petroff's equation in KTU examinations.

### Step 9 — Power Loss

The power dissipated as heat in overcoming viscous friction is:

$$
\text{Power} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot (2\pi N)
$$

Substituting $T_f$:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c} \cdot 2\pi N = \frac{8\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L}{c}
$$

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{8 \pi^{3} \mu N^{2} r^{3} L}{c}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Variable Affects Friction

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners love asking "what happens to friction if we double the speed?"** — use this table.

| Parameter | Effect on Friction Coefficient $f$ | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| $\uparrow$ Viscosity ($\mu$) | $\uparrow$ Increases linearly | Thicker oil → more friction |
| $\uparrow$ Speed ($N$) | $\uparrow$ Increases linearly | Faster rotation → more friction |
| $\uparrow$ Bearing Pressure ($P$) | $\downarrow$ Decreases (inversely) | Heavier load → *relatively* less friction |
| $\uparrow$ Radius ($r$) | $\uparrow$ Increases linearly | Bigger journals → more friction |
| $\uparrow$ Clearance ($c$) | $\downarrow$ Decreases (inversely) | More clearance → less friction |
| $\uparrow$ Length ($L$) | $\uparrow$ Increases (in $F_f$) | Longer bearings → more friction force |

> **Key Insight:** $f$ is **inversely proportional** to bearing pressure $P$. This is a counterintuitive but classical result of hydrodynamic theory — heavier loads, by squeezing the film thinner, can actually *reduce* the relative friction ratio (though absolute friction force $F_f$ increases).

## 3.3 Limitation Insight — Why Petroff is a "Lower Bound"

In real bearings, the journal sits **eccentrically** below the bearing center due to the load. This causes:
- **Non-uniform film thickness** (wedge effect → pressure build-up).
- **Higher shear rates** in the thin region.
- **Result:** Actual friction $\geq$ Petroff's predicted friction.

Petroff's equation is therefore the **theoretical minimum** for the *frictionless* hydrodynamic regime.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Journal Bearing Physical Model (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **physical-functional architecture** of the Petroff lubrication model, showing how the journal, bearing, and lubricant film interact to produce the friction output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["JOURNAL RADIUS r"] --> B["ANGULAR VELOCITY omega = 2pi N"]
    B --> C["JOURNAL SURFACE VELOCITY U = omega r"]
    D["BEARING SURFACE STATIONARY V = 0"] --> E["LUBRICANT FILM THICKNESS c"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["LINEAR VELOCITY GRADIENT du/dy = U / c"]
    F --> G["SHEAR STRESS tau = mu x du/dy"]
    G --> H["FRICTION FORCE Ff = integral tau dA"]
    H --> I["FRICTION TORQUE Tf = Ff x r"]
    I --> J["POWER LOSS Ploss = Tf x omega"]
    H --> K["COEFFICIENT OF FRICTION f = Ff / W"]
    L["BEARING PRESSURE P = W / 2rL"] --> K
    M["PROJECTED AREA 2rL"] --> L
    A --> I
    N["LENGTH L"] --> H
    O["ABSOLUTE VISCOSITY mu"] --> G
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Derivation Chain

This flowchart shows the **logical chain of derivation** from Newton's law of viscosity to Petroff's final coefficient of friction formula.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Newton's Law of Viscosity"] --> P2["tau = mu x du/dy"]
    P3["Velocity Gradient in Film"] --> P2
    P2 --> P4["Friction Force Ff"]
    P4 --> P5["Friction Torque Tf"]
    P5 --> P6["Power Loss"]
    P4 --> P7["Coefficient of Friction f = Ff / W"]
    P8["Bearing Pressure P"] --> P7
    P9["Projected Area 2rL"] --> P8
    P7 --> P10["Final Petroff Equation"]
    P10 --> P11["f = 2pi^2 x muN/P x r/c"]
```

## 4.3 Assumption Hierarchy (Concentric Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["PETROFF'S IDEALIZED BEARING MODEL"] --> A1["Concentric Journal e = 0"]
    A0 --> A2["Thick Continuous Oil Film"]
    A0 --> A3["Newtonian Fluid"]
    A0 --> A4["Laminar Viscous Flow"]
    A0 --> A5["No Side Leakage"]
    A0 --> A6["Constant mu and rho"]
    A1 --> B1["Uniform Gap c everywhere"]
    B1 --> C1["Linear Velocity Profile"]
    C1 --> D1["Constant Shear Stress"]
    D1 --> E1["Closed-Form Friction Equation"]
    A2 --> E1
    A3 --> E1
    A4 --> E1
    A5 --> E1
    A6 --> E1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: State Petroff's equation for the coefficient of friction in a journal bearing and list any four assumptions used in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

Petroff's equation for the coefficient of friction in a journal bearing is:

$$
f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}
$$

Where $\mu$ = absolute viscosity of lubricant, $N$ = journal speed in rev/s, $P$ = bearing pressure, $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance.

**Four Key Assumptions:**

1. The journal and bearing are **perfectly concentric** (eccentricity = 0).
2. The lubricant forms a **continuous, thick hydrodynamic film** completely separating the journal and bearing surfaces.
3. The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** with constant viscosity and obeys Newton's law of viscosity.
4. The flow is **viscous (laminar)** with **no side leakage** of the lubricant from the bearing ends.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Why is Petroff's equation considered to give the "minimum" coefficient of friction in a journal bearing?**

**Model Answer:**

Petroff's equation assumes a **perfectly concentric** journal, where the journal axis coincides with the bearing axis. In this idealized case, the lubricant film thickness is **uniform** all around the journal, and friction arises purely from the **viscous shear** of the lubricant.

In a **real bearing**, the journal is displaced **eccentrically** due to the applied load, causing a **wedge-shaped film** with non-uniform thickness. This eccentricity increases the **local shear rate** in the thinner regions of the film, raising the friction above Petroff's theoretical value.

Therefore, Petroff's equation gives the **theoretical minimum** coefficient of friction — the actual friction in a real bearing is always **greater** than or equal to Petroff's prediction. It serves as a **lower bound** for design calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model ESE Pattern, Module 4]**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter: $D = 100$ mm
- Radial clearance: $c = 0.2$ mm
- Length of bearing: $L = 200$ mm
- Speed of journal: $N = 1500$ rpm
- Load on bearing: $W = 50$ kN
- Viscosity of lubricant: $\mu = 0.05$ N·s/m²

**Using Petroff's equation, calculate:**
1. The coefficient of friction $f$.
2. The friction torque $T_f$.
3. The power loss in the bearing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the physical significance of the term $\left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)$ in Petroff's equation. How does this term influence the design selection of a journal bearing?

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI units:**

- $D = 100$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 0.05$ m
- $c = 0.2$ mm $= 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $L = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- $N = 1500$ rpm $= \dfrac{1500}{60} = 25$ rev/s
- $W = 50$ kN $= 50{,}000$ N
- $\mu = 0.05$ N·s/m²

**Step 2 — Calculate the bearing pressure $P$:**

$$
P = \frac{W}{2 r L} = \frac{50{,}000}{2 \times 0.05 \times 0.2}
$$

$$
P = \frac{50{,}000}{0.02} = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2 = 2.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the coefficient of friction $f$ using Petroff's equation:**

$$
f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
f = 2 \times (3.1416)^2 \times \frac{0.05 \times 25}{2{,}500{,}000} \times \frac{0.05}{2 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
f = 19.7392 \times \frac{1.25}{2{,}500{,}000} \times 250
$$

$$
f = 19.7392 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 250
$$

$$
f = 19.7392 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{f \approx 2.467 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00247}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the friction force $F_f$:**

$$
F_f = f \cdot W = 0.00247 \times 50{,}000 = 123.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the friction torque $T_f$:**

$$
T_f = F_f \cdot r = 123.5 \times 0.05 = 6.175 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the power loss:**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 2\pi N T_f = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 25 \times 6.175
$$

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 154.375 = 970.1 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{loss}} \approx 970 \text{ W} \approx 0.97 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

The term $\left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)$ is known as the **Bearing Characteristic Number** or **Bearing Modulus**. It is a **dimensionless group** that captures the interplay between the **lubricant property (μ)**, the **operating speed (N)**, and the **load intensity (P)**.

**Physical Significance:**

- It represents the **ratio of viscous forces (friction-generating)** to the **load-carrying capacity** of the bearing.
- A **high** value indicates a **thick, sticky lubricant** at high speed and/or a lightly loaded bearing — friction will be high.
- A **low** value indicates a **light lubricant**, low speed, or a **heavily loaded** bearing — friction will be relatively low (but film breakdown risk increases).

**Design Influence:**

1. **Lubricant Selection:** For high-speed bearings, a **lower-viscosity oil** is chosen to keep $\left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)$ small and reduce friction. For slow, heavily loaded bearings, a **higher-viscosity oil** is used to maintain a thick film.
2. **Operating Speed:** Petroff's equation shows $f \propto N$, so designers balance speed against lubricant grade.
3. **Load Considerations:** Since $f \propto 1/P$, increasing the load-bearing area (larger $2rL$) reduces $P$ and therefore reduces $f$.
4. **Bearing Geometry:** The ratio $\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)$ is a **design parameter** — a larger ratio (smaller clearance) increases friction but improves load capacity.

In essence, the bearing modulus is the **key design knob** that machine designers tune to balance friction, load capacity, and wear life.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Petroff's equation for the coefficient of friction in a journal bearing, clearly stating all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 60 mm diameter journal runs at 1200 rpm in a 60 mm long bearing with a radial clearance of 0.15 mm. The bearing is lubricated with an oil of viscosity 0.03 N·s/m² and carries a load of 30 kN. Using Petroff's equation, find:
1. The coefficient of friction.
2. The friction torque.
3. The power loss.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

*(Full derivation as presented in Section 3.1 of these notes, with all 9 steps reproduced in the answer script. Students are expected to draw a clear labeled diagram of the journal, bearing, lubricant film, and indicate velocity profile, then proceed step-by-step.)*

**Key Assumptions** (state at the top of the answer):
1. Concentric journal and bearing (no eccentricity).
2. Thick, continuous lubricant film.
3. Newtonian fluid, constant viscosity.
4. Viscous (laminar) flow.
5. No side leakage of oil.
6. Negligible pressure variation across the film.
7. Incompressible lubricant.

**Final Derived Equation:**

$$
f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units:**

- $D = 60$ mm $\Rightarrow r = 0.03$ m
- $c = 0.15$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $L = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m
- $N = 1200$ rpm $= 20$ rev/s
- $W = 30$ kN $= 30{,}000$ N
- $\mu = 0.03$ N·s/m²

**Step 2 — Bearing Pressure:**

$$
P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{30{,}000}{2 \times 0.03 \times 0.06} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.0036} = 8{,}333{,}333 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Friction:**

$$
f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}
$$

$$
f = 2 \times (3.1416)^2 \times \frac{0.03 \times 20}{8{,}333{,}333} \times \frac{0.03}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
f = 19.7392 \times \frac{0.6}{8{,}333{,}333} \times 200
$$

$$
f = 19.7392 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-8} \times 200
$$

$$
f = 19.7392 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{f \approx 2.842 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

**Step 4 — Friction Torque:**

$$
T_f = F_f \cdot r = f \cdot W \cdot r = 2.842 \times 10^{-4} \times 30{,}000 \times 0.03
$$

$$
T_f = 2.842 \times 10^{-4} \times 900 = 0.2558 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_f \approx 0.256 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Power Loss:**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 2\pi N T_f = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 20 \times 0.256
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{loss}} \approx 32.17 \text{ W}}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Petroff's Equation Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Blunder in Speed:** Students write $N = 1500$ (rpm) directly into the formula. Petroff's equation **strictly** requires $N$ in **rev/s**. Forgetting to divide by 60 gives a friction value 60× too high — and full mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert c, r, L to meters:** Mixing mm and m is the #1 source of numerical errors. Always convert **all** lengths to meters **first**.
>
> 3. **Wrong Bearing Area Formula:** Petroff uses **projected area = 2rL** (diameter × length), **not** the cylindrical area $2\pi r L$. Using $2\pi rL$ makes $P$ smaller by a factor of $\pi$, inflating the friction result.
>
> 4. **Confusing μ and ν:** Dynamic viscosity (μ) and kinematic viscosity (ν) are **not the same**. Petroff's equation uses **μ** in N·s/m². If the problem gives ν, you must multiply by the oil density: $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$.
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** In derivation questions (7 marks), if assumptions are not listed, expect a 2–3 mark deduction. **Always begin with a numbered list of assumptions.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Petroff's Equation (Module 4)**

- 🔹 Petroff's equation was developed by **Nikolai Petrov (1883)** for the **minimum (concentric) friction** in a hydrodynamic journal bearing.
- 🔹 The defining equation is: $f = 2\pi^2 \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$
- 🔹 Friction force: $F_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L}{c}$
- 🔹 Friction torque: $T_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L}{c}$
- 🔹 Power loss: $P_{\text{loss}} = \dfrac{8\pi^3 \mu N^2 r^3 L}{c}$
- 🔹 Bearing pressure (projected): $P = \dfrac{W}{2rL}$
- 🔹 The **Bearing Characteristic Number** $\left(\dfrac{\mu N}{P}\right)$ is the key design parameter for lubricant and speed selection.
- 🔹 Petroff gives the **minimum** coefficient of friction — real (eccentric) bearings have higher friction.
- 🔹 $N$ must be in **rev/s** (not rpm); all lengths in **meters**; $\mu$ in **N·s/m²** (or Pa·s).
- 🔹 Key assumptions: concentric, thick film, Newtonian, laminar, no leakage, constant μ, no pressure variation.
- 🔹 **Limitation:** The equation does **not** account for eccentricity, side leakage, or variable viscosity — these are introduced in more advanced models (Reynolds, Sommerfeld, Raimondi-Boyd).
- 🔹 For KTU exam: **always state assumptions first**, then derive, then substitute numerically with units shown.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bearing Characteristic Number (Sommerfeld Number)**
> The **Bearing Characteristic Number**, universally designated as **S** (or Z in some textbooks), is a fundamental *dimensionless group* that governs the hydrodynamic performance of a dynamically loaded full journal bearing. It represents the ratio of the viscous forces developed in the oil film to the applied bearing load per unit projected area.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$S = \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^2 \cdot \frac{\mu \, N}{P}$$

where every term is a measured physical property of the bearing assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** This is a *core examinable definition* in Module 4 (Friction and Lubrication of Bearings). The expression **S = (μN/P)(r/c)²** is the single most tested formula in journal bearing problems. Memorize it with units.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **spinning a wet ball inside a slightly larger spherical socket** that has a thin film of honey between them.

- If the **honey is very thick (high μ)** and the **spin is very fast (high N)**, the ball tends to "float" more centrally because the viscous drag lifts the journal. This corresponds to a **HIGH Bearing Characteristic Number**.
- Conversely, if the **load is heavy (high P)** or the **clearance is very small (low c)**, the ball is forced closer to the bearing wall, increasing the contact risk. This corresponds to a **LOW Bearing Characteristic Number**.

**Physical Meaning:** The number tells the designer *how robustly* the oil wedge can support the applied load. A high S value ⇒ thick film ⇒ safe operation. A low S value ⇒ thin film ⇒ risk of metal-to-metal contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** A healthy, well-designed journal bearing operates with **S between 0.001 and 1.0** (dimensionless). Modern high-speed machinery tends to operate near the lower end (around 0.001) due to high rotational speeds.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | Bearing Characteristic Number | Dimensionless | 0.001 – 1.0 |
| **r** | Journal radius | **m (meter)** | 0.01 – 0.5 |
| **c** | Radial clearance (R − r) | **m (meter)** | 0.0001 – 0.005 |
| **μ** | Dynamic viscosity of lubricant | **N·s/m² (Pa·s)** | 0.01 – 0.5 |
| **N** | Journal speed | **rev/s** or **rev/min** | 1 – 5000 |
| **P** | Load intensity (W/(2rl)) | **N/m² (Pa)** | 0.5 × 10⁶ – 5 × 10⁶ |
| **W** | Total load on bearing | **N (Newton)** | 1000 – 500000 |
| **l** | Bearing length | **m (meter)** | 0.05 – 1.0 |
| **e** | Eccentricity | **m (meter)** | 0 – c |
| **ε = e/c** | Eccentricity ratio | Dimensionless | 0 – 1.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Insight:** The square of the ratio **(r/c)²** dominates the magnitude. Since r/c is typically 200–1000, its square is enormous (40,000 to 1,000,000), which is why S is normally very small even when μ and N are large.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Foundation: Petroff → Sommerfeld → Raimondi–Boyd

The Bearing Characteristic Number is the result of **three sequential intellectual milestones**:

1. **Petroff (1883)** — derived the *zero-eccentricity* (frictionless) torque loss of a concentric journal bearing. His equation gave the first physical relationship between viscosity, speed, load, and clearance, but it was **incomplete** because it assumed the journal is perfectly centered (ε = 0), which is unrealistic.
2. **Sommerfeld (1904)** — generalized Petroff's work by **allowing for arbitrary eccentricity** ε = e/c. He obtained the closed-form solution and defined the characteristic number that now bears his name.
3. **Raimondi & Boyd (1958)** — produced the famous **dimensionless design charts** (Universal Raimondi–Boyd Charts) that solved Sommerfeld's equations numerically and expressed **friction variable (r/c)·f**, **flow rate Q/(rcN·l)**, and **temperature rise** as functions of the eccentricity ratio ε.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** The examiner expects you to know *what* the Bearing Characteristic Number connects — namely, eccentricity ratio ε, coefficient of friction μ_f, and side flow ratio.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Derivation Logic)

The Sommerfeld equation emerges from solving the **Reynolds Equation** for an infinitely short journal bearing with an incompressible lubricant under steady-state, isothermal conditions:

1. **Geometry Setup** — The journal of radius *r* rotates with angular velocity *ω* inside a stationary bearing of radius *R*. The centers are offset by eccentricity *e*, so the minimum film thickness is $h_0 = c - e = c(1 - \varepsilon)$.

2. **Film Thickness Profile** — At any angular position *θ* measured from the line of centers (or load line):
$$h(\theta) = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

3. **Reynolds Equation** — Simplified, since the journal is short and velocity components in the axial direction are small, the pressure distribution is:
$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} = \frac{6 \mu \, r \, \omega \, \varepsilon \sin\theta \, (1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)}{(1 - \varepsilon^2)^{5/2} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^3} \cdot c^2 \cdot \text{(geometric correction)}$$

4. **Integration** — Integrating pressure over the loaded half yields the **load-carrying capacity**:
$$W = \frac{2 \, \mu \, N \, l \, r^3}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \, \varepsilon}{(1-\varepsilon^2)^{3/2}} \cdot [\text{half-load integration}]$$

5. **Rearrangement** — Isolating the dimensionless group gives:
$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu \, N}{P} = \frac{(1-\varepsilon^2)}{2\pi^2 \varepsilon} \cdot \frac{1}{f(\varepsilon)}$$
where f(ε) is a slowly varying function of eccentricity.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these nine equations. Each one has appeared in KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech question papers.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | **Bearing Characteristic Number (Sommerfeld Number)** — the *master* equation | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{W}{2 r l}$ | **Bearing pressure / load intensity** | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | $c = R - r$ | **Radial clearance** | m |
| 4 | $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ | **Minimum film thickness** | m |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{e}{c}$ | **Eccentricity ratio** | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\mu_f = \dfrac{2\pi^2 \varepsilon S}{(1 - \varepsilon^2)^{1/2} \cdot f_1(\varepsilon)}$ | **Coefficient of friction (Raimondi–Boyd form)** | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{Q}{rcN\,l} = f_2(\varepsilon)$ | **Side flow ratio** | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\Delta T}{\mu N} \cdot \dfrac{c}{P} = f_3(\varepsilon)$ | **Temperature rise function** | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_f = \dfrac{33 \, (P)^2 \, \mu \, N}{S \cdot c_0}$ (English units, Petroff) | **Frictional torque loss in a concentric journal** | N·m |

> **Notation Note:** f₁(ε), f₂(ε), f₃(ε) are *chart functions* read off the Raimondi–Boyd plots. In KTU problems, these are usually **given as numerical values** in the question statement.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bearing Characteristic Number is the **master design parameter** for:

- **Turbogenerators and steam turbines** (where 3600 rpm is standard)
- **IC engine crankshaft main bearings** (the most-examined application in KTU)
- **Gearbox shafts and rolling mill bearings**
- **Aircraft engine turbofan mainshafts** (operating at >10,000 rpm)

By computing S and then using the Raimondi–Boyd charts, a designer can:
1. Predict **minimum film thickness** (to ensure mixed/hydrodynamic lubrication).
2. Estimate **friction loss and temperature rise** (to size the oil cooler and pump).
3. Select the **optimum clearance ratio (c/r)** to balance load capacity vs. heat generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the ratio (r/c)² appear?**
> The load-carrying capacity of the oil wedge is proportional to (μN)(r⁴/c²), so dividing by the load (P × 2rl) yields a (r/c)² dependence. This is a *dimensional consequence* of the wedge geometry — the clearance has a quadratic effect on performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bearing Characteristic Number

We start with the **classical Sommerfeld assumption**: an infinitely short, rigid journal rotating eccentrically inside a rigid bearing, with a Newtonian, incompressible lubricant in laminar flow under steady, isothermal conditions.

### Step 1 — Set up the journal–bearing geometry

Let:
- *r* = journal radius
- *R* = bearing radius
- *c = R − r* = radial clearance
- *e* = offset between journal center O_j and bearing center O_b
- *ε = e / c* = eccentricity ratio (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1)
- *θ* = angular position measured from the line of centers (load line)
- *ω* = angular speed in rad/s; N = ω / (2π) rev/s

The oil film thickness at any angle θ is given by the geometric difference between the bearing inner surface and the journal outer surface:

$$h(\theta) = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

**Logic:** When θ = 0 (along the load line), the gap is largest: h = c(1 + ε). When θ = π (opposite load line), the gap is smallest: h = c(1 − ε) = h₀. This is the wedge.

### Step 2 — Velocity components in the oil film

For a thin hydrodynamic film, the Couette flow (Couette approximation, neglecting the pressure-driven Poiseuille component for simplicity) gives the tangential velocity at the film surface:

$$U = r\,\omega$$

Across the film thickness, the velocity is assumed linear (no-slip at walls):

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h(\theta)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Navier–Stokes / Reynolds equation

For a short bearing (L/D << 1), the simplified Reynolds equation reduces to:

$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} = \frac{6 \, \mu \, U \, r \, \varepsilon \sin\theta}{(1 - \varepsilon^2)^{3/2} \cdot h(\theta)^2} \cdot \text{(geometric factor)}$$

After substituting h(θ) and integrating the pressure over one-half of the bearing (from θ = 0 to θ = π), the **load-carrying capacity W** is found to be:

$$W = \frac{2 \, \mu \, l \, r^3 \, \omega}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \, \varepsilon \, [1 + (1/2)\varepsilon^2 + (1/4)\varepsilon^4]}{(1 - \varepsilon^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Logic:** The numerator is proportional to viscosity, speed, and bearing length. The r³/c² factor captures the wedge geometry. The ε/(1−ε²)^(3/2) term is the *eccentricity function*.

### Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the dimensionless group

Divide both sides by the projected area **2 r l** to obtain the bearing pressure **P = W / (2 r l)**:

$$P = \frac{\mu \, r^2 \, \omega}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \, \varepsilon \, [1 + (1/2)\varepsilon^2 + (1/4)\varepsilon^4]}{(1 - \varepsilon^2)^{3/2}}$$

Now isolate the **dimensionless group** $\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 \dfrac{\mu \, N}{P}$:

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu \, N}{P} = \frac{(1 - \varepsilon^2)^{3/2}}{\pi^2 \, \varepsilon \, [1 + (1/2)\varepsilon^2 + (1/4)\varepsilon^4]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight (KTU Board Exam Pattern):** The right-hand side is a *pure function of eccentricity ratio ε*. This means that **S uniquely determines ε**, which in turn determines *every other performance variable* (friction, flow, temperature). This is the mathematical reason the Raimondi–Boyd charts are so powerful.

### Step 5 — Petroff's equation as the special case (ε = 0)

As ε → 0, the journal is perfectly concentric. The load-carrying capacity vanishes (since the right side → 0 / 0 via L'Hôpital's rule, the *finite* Petroff limit applies). Petroff's torque-loss formula is:

$$T_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \, \mu \, N^2 \, l \, r^3}{c}$$

This gives the *friction-only* baseline used in Pet. The Bearing Characteristic Number for ε = 0 effectively becomes infinite, but the *useful* range of operation is **0.001 < S < 0.1** for typical industrial bearings.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern):**
> A full journal bearing has a journal diameter of 100 mm, length 50 mm, and supports a load of 30 kN at 1440 rpm. The radial clearance is 0.1 mm and the oil viscosity is 0.025 Pa·s. Calculate the Bearing Characteristic Number, the coefficient of friction, and the power loss.

### Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI units

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{0.100}{2} = 0.050 \text{ m} \\
l &= 0.050 \text{ m} \\
c &= 0.0001 \text{ m} \\
\mu &= 0.025 \text{ Pa·s} \\
N &= \frac{1440}{60} = 24 \text{ rev/s} \\
W &= 30{,}000 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the bearing pressure (load intensity)

$$P = \frac{W}{2 \, r \, l} = \frac{30{,}000}{2 \times 0.050 \times 0.050} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 6 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Bearing Characteristic Number S

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu \, N}{P}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \left(\frac{0.050}{0.0001}\right)^2 \times \frac{0.025 \times 24}{6 \times 10^6} \\
S &= (500)^2 \times \frac{0.6}{6 \times 10^6} \\
S &= 250{,}000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \\
S &= 0.025
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Look up eccentricity ratio from Raimondi–Boyd charts

For **S = 0.025**, the chart gives:
- **ε ≈ 0.65**
- **Coefficient of friction (r/c)·μ_f ≈ 3.5** (chart value)

So the coefficient of friction:

$$\mu_f = \frac{3.5}{(r/c)} = \frac{3.5}{500} = 0.007$$

### Step 5 — Compute power loss

Frictional torque:

$$T_f = \mu_f \cdot W \cdot r = 0.007 \times 30{,}000 \times 0.050 = 10.5 \text{ N·m}$$

Power loss:

$$P_{loss} = T_f \cdot \omega = T_f \cdot 2\pi N = 10.5 \times 2\pi \times 24 \approx 1583 \text{ W} \approx 1.58 \text{ kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - **S = 0.025** (dimensionless)
> - **μ_f = 0.007** (dimensionless)
> - **Power loss ≈ 1.58 kW**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Petroff vs. Sommerfeld)

| Feature | Petroff's Equation | Sommerfeld's Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Eccentricity | ε = 0 (concentric) | Any ε (general) |
| Load capacity | W = 0 (no load support) | $W = \dfrac{2\mu l r^3 \omega}{c^2} \cdot f(\varepsilon)$ |
| Friction result | $T_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2 \mu N^2 l r^3}{c}$ | $T_f = \mu_f \cdot W \cdot r$ |
| Use | Estimate *baseline friction loss* | **Design eccentric journal bearings** |
| Bearing Characteristic Number | Not defined (S → ∞) | **Defined: S = (r/c)² · μN/P** |
| KTU marks weightage | 2–3 marks (definition type) | **5–10 marks (calculation type)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Journal Bearing Geometry — Mermaid Functional Architecture

> *Since journal-bearing geometry is a physical drawing, we provide a Mermaid block-level architecture mapping the physical components and their functional relationships.*

```mermaid

    end
    subgraph A2[Pressure Wedge Formation]
        node6["Oil film thickness h at angle theta = c times 1 plus epsilon cos theta"]
        node7["Maximum film h max = c times 1 plus epsilon"]
        node8["Minimum film h 0 = c times 1 minus epsilon"]
        node9["Pressure peak region near 90 degrees from load line"]
        node10["Reynolds equation integrated to obtain pressure profile"]
    end
    subgraph A3[Bearing Characteristic Number Definition]
        node11["S = ratio of r to c squared times mu times N divided by P"]
        node12["P = bearing pressure = W divided by 2 r l"]

        node14["Raimondi Boyd Charts give epsilon from S"]

```

## 4.2 Information-Flow Schematic — From Geometry to Performance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["Journal r"] --> stage1["Geometry: c = R minus r"]
    in2["Bearing R"] --> stage1
    in3["Load W"] --> stage2["Pressure: P = W divided by 2 r l"]
    in4["Length l"] --> stage2
    in5["Viscosity mu"] --> stage3["Compute S = ratio of r to c squared times mu N divided by P"]
    in6["Speed N"] --> stage3
    stage2 --> stage3
    stage3 --> stage4["Read epsilon from Raimondi Boyd Chart"]
    stage4 --> stage5["Read mu f from Raimondi Boyd Chart"]
    stage4 --> stage6["Read Q ratio from chart"]
    stage4 --> stage7["Read delta T from chart"]
    stage5 --> out1["Friction Torque T f = mu f W r"]
    stage6 --> out2["Oil flow rate Q"]
    stage7 --> out3["Heat dissipation required"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q0{Bearing concentric or eccentric?}
    q0 -->|Concentric, epsilon equals 0| path1["Use Petroff's equation: T f = 4 pi squared mu N squared l r cubed divided by c"]
    q0 -->|Eccentric, epsilon greater than 0| path2["Use Sommerfeld's equation: S = ratio of r to c squared times mu N divided by P"]
    path2 --> path2a{"Raimondi Boyd charts available?"}
    path2a -->|Yes| path3["Read epsilon, mu f, Q, delta T from charts"]
    path2a -->|No, closed form needed| path4["Use Rayleigh-Raimondi closed forms with eccentricity functions"]
    path1 --> done["Result: friction torque only"]
    path3 --> done2["Result: full performance map"]
    path4 --> done2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"Define the Bearing Characteristic Number. State its physical significance in the design of hydrodynamic journal bearings."**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Bearing Characteristic Number (Sommerfeld Number)** is a dimensionless group given by:
> $$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P}$$
>
> where *r* = journal radius, *c* = radial clearance, *μ* = dynamic viscosity, *N* = journal speed, and *P* = bearing pressure.
>
> **Physical significance:** It represents the *ratio of viscous (oil-film-generating) forces to the applied load pressure*. A higher S implies a thicker hydrodynamic film and safer operation. It acts as the *master input parameter* to the Raimondi–Boyd design charts, from which eccentricity ratio, friction, flow, and temperature rise are determined.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between Petroff's equation and the Sommerfeld equation for journal bearings."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Petroff's Equation | Sommerfeld's Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Eccentricity assumption | ε = 0 (perfectly concentric) | Arbitrary ε |
| Load support | None (assumes zero load carrying) | Full hydrodynamic load support |
| Friction output | Direct analytical formula | Read from Raimondi–Boyd charts |
| Use | Friction baseline | **Bearing design** |
| Defines S? | No | **Yes** |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module 4 contains a *mandatory internal choice* between two Part-B questions, each of 14 marks. The student must answer ONE of the two. Each question is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Numerical Design Problem

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2 pattern]**

**(a)** A full journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal diameter *D* = 80 mm
- Bearing length *L* = 60 mm
- Load *W* = 25 kN
- Speed *N* = 1500 rpm
- Radial clearance *c* = 0.08 mm
- Oil viscosity *μ* = 0.02 Pa·s

Calculate:
1. The **Bearing Characteristic Number (S)**
2. The **bearing pressure (P)**
3. The **minimum film thickness (h₀)** if the eccentricity ratio ε = 0.7 (assume the chart gives this value for your computed S)

**(b)** For the same bearing, using the Raimondi–Boyd chart values at ε = 0.7, given:
- (r/c)·μ_f = 4.0
- Q/(rcN·L) = 0.50
- (ΔT · c)/(P) = 0.80

Calculate:
1. The **coefficient of friction (μ_f)**
2. The **frictional torque (T_f)**
3. The **oil flow rate (Q)** in L/min
4. The **temperature rise (ΔT)** of the oil

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

Convert all to SI units:

$$D = 0.080 \text{ m} \Rightarrow r = 0.040 \text{ m}$$

$$L = 0.060 \text{ m};\ N = 1500/60 = 25 \text{ rev/s}$$

**1) Bearing Characteristic Number:**

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P}$$

First compute P (load intensity):

$$P = \frac{W}{2rL} = \frac{25{,}000}{2 \times 0.040 \times 0.060} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.0048} = 5.208 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

Then compute S:

$$S = \left(\frac{0.040}{0.00008}\right)^2 \times \frac{0.02 \times 25}{5.208 \times 10^6}$$

$$S = (500)^2 \times \frac{0.5}{5.208 \times 10^6} = 250{,}000 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-8} = 0.024$$

**2) Bearing pressure P = 5.208 × 10⁶ Pa ≈ 5.21 MPa**

**3) Minimum film thickness** (using ε = 0.7 from chart):

$$h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon) = 0.00008 \times (1 - 0.7) = 0.00008 \times 0.3 = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 24 \text{ μm}$$

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

**1) Coefficient of friction:**

Given (r/c)·μ_f = 4.0, and r/c = 500:

$$\mu_f = \frac{4.0}{500} = 0.008$$

**2) Frictional torque:**

$$T_f = \mu_f \cdot W \cdot r = 0.008 \times 25{,}000 \times 0.040 = 8.0 \text{ N·m}$$

**3) Oil flow rate:**

$$Q = 0.50 \times r \times c \times N \times L$$

$$Q = 0.50 \times 0.040 \times 0.00008 \times 25 \times 0.060$$

$$Q = 0.50 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-6} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to L/min:

$$Q = 2.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 60 = 0.144 \text{ L/min}$$

**4) Temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{0.80 \times P}{c} = \frac{0.80 \times 5.208 \times 10^6}{0.00008} = 5.21 \times 10^{10} \text{ K·m/m}$$

> *The given dimensionless group is unconventional; the standard Raimondi–Boyd temperature-rise variable is* $\Delta T \cdot c / (P) \cdot (\mu N)$*. In a real exam, the precise chart function and its units will be specified. For this problem, treating the chart value as* $(\Delta T \cdot c) / P = 0.80$, *we get a numerical value and emphasize to students to verify the chart function form before substituting.*

Recomputing using the conventional form: $\Delta T = 0.80 \times P / c = 0.80 \times 5.208 \times 10^6 / 0.00008 \approx 5.2 \times 10^{10}$ (unphysically large). **The proper Raimondi–Boyd form is** $\Delta T \cdot c / (P \cdot \mu N) = f_3(\varepsilon)$. Using the chart value $f_3 = 0.80$ and $\mu N = 0.02 \times 25 = 0.5$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{0.80 \times P \times \mu N}{c} = \frac{0.80 \times 5.208 \times 10^6 \times 0.5}{0.00008} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{10} \text{ K}$$

The student should follow the chart's exact definition. **For the KTU exam, the question will specify the form clearly.** Assume the simpler convention: $\Delta T = (0.80 \times P) / c = 5.2 \times 10^{10}$ in chart units, which the student must interpret using the chart.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):**
> - Forgetting to convert mm → m, rpm → rev/s, kN → N. **This is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.**
> - Inverting the (r/c) ratio. Remember: **r is always journal, c is always clearance**, so r/c >> 1.
> - Treating the Raimondi–Boyd chart functions as analytical formulas. They are **purely graphical**; in a numerical question the chart values must be **given in the problem statement** or **assumed standard values**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + Numerical Mix

**(a)** **[7 marks]**
**"Derive the expression for the Bearing Characteristic Number from first principles, starting from the pressure distribution in a short journal bearing. State clearly the assumptions involved."**

**MODEL ANSWER — Derivation Sketch:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, isothermal operation
2. Newtonian, incompressible lubricant
3. Rigid journal and bearing (no elastic deformation)
4. Infinitely short bearing (L/D → 0)
5. Negligible inertia and body forces
6. No-slip condition at both walls
7. Constant viscosity μ
8. The oil film has continuous positive pressure over the converging wedge

**Derivation steps:**

(i) Film thickness:
$$h(\theta) = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

(ii) Pressure rise in the converging wedge (Reynolds, short-bearing form):
$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} = \frac{6 \mu r \omega \varepsilon \sin\theta}{(1-\varepsilon^2)^{3/2} \cdot [1 + \varepsilon\cos\theta]^2 \cdot c}$$

(iii) Integrate pressure over the loaded half-bearing (0 to π) and project onto the load direction:
$$W = \frac{2 \mu l r^3 \omega}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{(1-\varepsilon^2)^{3/2}} \cdot [1 + (1/2)\varepsilon^2 + (1/4)\varepsilon^4]$$

(iv) Rearrange to isolate the dimensionless group on one side:
$$\frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{(1-\varepsilon^2)^{3/2}}{\pi^2 \varepsilon \, [1 + (1/2)\varepsilon^2 + (1/4)\varepsilon^4]}$$

(v) Define the LHS as the **Bearing Characteristic Number**:
$$\boxed{S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \frac{\mu N}{P}}$$

**(b)** **[7 marks]**
**"With a suitable diagram, describe the Raimondi–Boyd universal design charts. How are these charts used to determine the coefficient of friction and the temperature rise for a given journal bearing?"**

**MODEL ANSWER:**

The Raimondi–Boyd charts (1958) are a set of **four dimensionless curves** plotted against the eccentricity ratio ε (x-axis from 0 to 1):

1. **Bearing Characteristic Number (r/c)·μN/P vs ε** — the Sommerfeld function
2. **Friction Variable (r/c)·μ_f vs ε** — used to compute coefficient of friction
3. **Flow Variable Q/(rcN·l) vs ε** — for oil flow rate
4. **Temperature Variable vs ε** — for temperature rise

**Procedure to use the charts:**

1. Compute S from the given design parameters: $S = (r/c)^2 \mu N / P$.
2. Multiply S by (r/c) to get the friction variable form expected on the chart x-axis: $(r/c) \cdot S$.
3. Enter the y-axis (Sommerfeld value) and read **ε** from the x-axis.
4. At the same ε, read the friction variable (r/c)·μ_f; divide by (r/c) to obtain **μ_f**.
5. Similarly, read Q/(rcN·l) and the temperature-rise variable.
6. Convert the dimensionless chart readings into physical units (W, L/min, °C).

**Why "universal"?**
- The y-axis is *dimensionless* (pure ratios).
- The x-axis is dimensionless eccentricity (0 to 1).
- Thus, the charts apply to **any** journal-bearing geometry, load, speed, or viscosity combination. This universality is why they are the workhorse of journal-bearing design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - Omitting assumptions in the derivation. **A full marks answer MUST list at least 5–6 assumptions.**
> - Confusing S with (r/c)·S. Many students enter the wrong axis on the Raimondi–Boyd chart and end up with ε > 1 (impossible!).
> - Forgetting the unit conversions in numerical sub-parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Tape it to your study wall.**

### Core Identities (must memorize)
- [ ] **Bearing Characteristic Number:** $S = \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2 \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$
- [ ] **Bearing pressure:** $P = \dfrac{W}{2 r l}$
- [ ] **Radial clearance:** $c = R - r$
- [ ] **Eccentricity ratio:** $\varepsilon = e/c$, range 0–1
- [ ] **Minimum film thickness:** $h_0 = c(1 - \varepsilon)$
- [ ] **Film thickness profile:** $h(\theta) = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$

### Conceptual Pins
- [ ] S is a **dimensionless group** — it has *no units*.
- [ ] High S ⇒ eccentric bearing tends to **float centrally** (ε small).
- [ ] Low S ⇒ bearing runs **near the wall** (ε close to 1, h₀ tiny).
- [ ] The **Raimondi–Boyd charts** connect S to all performance variables.
- [ ] **Petroff** gives friction at ε = 0; **Sommerfeld** gives load at any ε.
- [ ] The chart input is usually the *dimensionless* $(r/c) \cdot S$ or its reciprocal, NOT S directly.

### Units-Conversion Traps to Avoid
- [ ] **N (speed):** Always convert rpm → rev/s by dividing by 60.
- [ ] **r, c, l, e:** Convert all mm to meters **before** substituting.
- [ ] **P:** Comes out in **N/m² (Pa)**; convert to MPa for sanity check (1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa).
- [ ] **μ:** Pa·s = N·s/m². Do not confuse with kinematic viscosity (in cSt).

### KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Numericals
- [ ] "Compute S for given data, then read ε from the chart" — full 14 marks.
- [ ] "Find μ_f and power loss" — usually a 7-mark sub-question.
- [ ] "Define S and state its significance" — 3-mark Part-A staple.
- [ ] "Compare Petroff and Sommerfeld equations" — 3-mark Part-A definition type.

### Final Sanity Check
- [ ] Dimensionless S between **0.001 and 1.0** for industrial bearings — yes/no?
- [ ] h₀ > surface roughness × 3 — required for full hydrodynamic lubrication?
- [ ] Temperature rise < 25–30 °C above inlet oil temperature — for safe operation?

> **You are now fully equipped to tackle any KTU 2024 question on the Bearing Characteristic Number.** Good luck at the board exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Sommerfeld Number** is a fundamental **dimensionless parameter** used in the hydrodynamic analysis of journal bearings. It groups together all the operating parameters of a journal bearing (viscosity, speed, load, and geometric clearance) into a single characteristic number that uniquely describes the operating regime of the bearing.

Mathematically, the Sommerfeld Number is expressed as:

$$S = \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\mu \, N_s}{P}$$

where the symbols carry their standard meanings (detailed in the formula sheet in Section 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sommerfeld Number (KTU 2024 Module 4 Definition):**
> A dimensionless operating parameter of a hydrodynamic journal bearing, defined as the product of the **viscosity–speed term** ($\mu N_s / P$) and the **square of the radius-to-clearance ratio** ($(r/c)^2$). It governs the **load-carrying capacity**, **eccentricity ratio**, **minimum film thickness**, and **coefficient of friction** of a full journal bearing.

The Sommerfeld Number is denoted by **S** and is essentially the **inverse of the dimensionless load variable**. Bearings that operate at the **same Sommerfeld Number** will exhibit **identical performance characteristics** in terms of pressure distribution, oil flow, and frictional behavior, irrespective of their individual physical dimensions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are balancing a heavy ball (the journal) on a thin cushion of water (the oil film) on the palm of your hand (the bearing). Several factors affect how stable this balance is:

- **Viscosity of water** ($\mu$) — Thicker water holds the ball better.
- **How fast you move your hand** ($N_s$) — Faster movement creates more lift (think of a hydroplane).
- **Weight of the ball** ($P$ or $W$) — A heavier ball sinks more.
- **Thickness of the water layer** ($c$) — Thicker layer is more forgiving.

The **Sommerfeld Number** is essentially a "**regime fingerprint**" of this hydro-balance. It tells you:
- **High S** → Strong hydro-film, ball is well-centered, low friction, long bearing life.
- **Low S** → Weak hydro-film, ball is pushed to the side, high friction, risk of metal-to-metal contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world significance:** The Sommerfeld Number is the *primary design parameter* used in bearing selection charts (Raimondi–Boyd charts). An engineer picks a target Sommerfeld Number based on desired life, friction, and temperature rise, and the bearing dimensions are then derived.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sommerfeld Number range in practice:** Typically $0.001 \le S \le 1.0$ for well-designed hydrodynamic bearings.
- **Critical design value:** Usually maintained at $S \ge 0.05$ for moderate-duty applications.
- **Viscosity of lubricants at operating temperature:** $\mu \in [0.01,\ 0.10]\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ for most mineral oils.
- **Clearance ratio:** $\psi = c/r \in [0.001,\ 0.003]$ for precision bearings.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Meaning — The "Why" Behind the Definition

The Sommerfeld Number arises naturally from a **dimensional analysis** of the Reynolds equation governing hydrodynamic lubrication. Three independent dimensionless groups can be constructed from the variables of a journal bearing:

1. **Radius-to-clearance ratio:** $\dfrac{r}{c}$ (or its inverse, the clearance ratio $\psi = c/r$).
2. **Reynolds Number of the lubricant film:** characterizes inertial effects (often neglected).
3. **Sommerfeld Number:** characterizes the **balance between viscous shear forces and applied bearing load**.

The "**why**" of the Sommerfeld Number is rooted in the **load-carrying capacity equation** of a hydrodynamic bearing. The vertical force the fluid film can support is proportional to $\mu N_s L D (r/c)^2$. Dividing this by the applied load $W = PLD$ yields **dimensionless load** $W_c / W \propto 1/S$.

## 2.2 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **The journal rotates** inside the bearing at angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N_s$.
2. **The lubricant is dragged** into the converging wedge-shaped clearance by the rotating journal.
3. A **hydrodynamic pressure** is generated in the converging film, peaking where the film is thinnest.
4. The **integrated pressure** over the projected bearing area equals the applied load $W$.
5. The **degree of journal eccentricity** (i.e., the eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = e/c$) is determined solely by the **Sommerfeld Number**.
6. From $\varepsilon$, all other bearing parameters (friction, flow, film thickness) are read from **Raimondi–Boyd charts**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of the Sommerfeld Number**
> - **Bearing design:** Choosing $S$ is the first design decision; everything else follows.
> - **Bearing selection:** Catalogues often quote $S$ alongside $P$, $N$, and $\mu$ for standardized bearings.
> - **Predictive maintenance:** Falling $S$ in service (due to oil thinning, contamination, or wear) signals impending failure.
> - **Standardization:** Enables bearing interchangeability across manufacturers using a single non-dimensional descriptor.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Sommerfeld Number** | $S = \dfrac{\mu\,N_s}{P}\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^{2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Sommerfeld Number (alt.)** | $S = \dfrac{\mu\,N_s}{P\,\psi^{2}}$, $\ \psi = c/r$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Bearing pressure** | $P = \dfrac{W}{L\,D}$ | $\text{N/m}^{2}$ (Pa) |
| 4 | **Clearance ratio** | $\psi = \dfrac{c}{r} = \dfrac{2c}{D}$ | Dimensionless ($\sim 0.001$–$0.003$) |
| 5 | **Eccentricity ratio** | $\varepsilon = e/c$ | Dimensionless ($0 \le \varepsilon < 1$) |
| 6 | **Minimum film thickness** | $h_0 = c(1-\varepsilon) = r\psi(1-\varepsilon)$ | metres (m) |
| 7 | **Petroff coefficient of friction** | $f = 2\pi^{2}\,\dfrac{\mu\,N_s}{P}\cdot\dfrac{r}{c}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Petroff friction coefficient via S** | $f = 2\pi^{2}\,S\,\psi = 2\pi^{2}\,S\cdot\dfrac{c}{r}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Petroff friction torque** | $T = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}\,\mu\,N_s\,L\,D\,r^{2}}{c}$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ |
| 10 | **Petroff power loss** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\pi^{3}\,\mu\,N_s^{2}\,L\,D\,r^{2}}{c}$ | Watts (W) |
| 11 | **Friction variable (Raimondi–Boyd)** | $\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)f = 2\pi^{2}\,S$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | **Dimensionless load (Raimondi–Boyd)** | $\dfrac{W}{\mu\,N_s\,L\,D}\left(\dfrac{c}{r}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{S}$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | **Sliding velocity of journal** | $V = \pi\,D\,N_s = 2\pi\,r\,N_s$ | m/s |
| 14 | **Clearance (radial) – standard** | $c = 0.001\,r\ \text{to}\ 0.003\,r$ | metres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical pitfall in formula tables (KTU 2024):** When converting journal speed, use $N_s = N/60$ where $N$ is in **rev/min** and $N_s$ in **rev/s**. The radial clearance $c$ is **half** the diametral clearance. These two conversion errors account for $\sim 40\%$ of calculation mistakes in university answer scripts.

## 2.4 Limiting Behavior — Engineering Interpretation

| **Sommerfeld Number** | **Eccentricity Ratio** | **Bearing Behavior** | **Practical Implication** |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| $S \to 0$ | $\varepsilon \to 1$ | Journal nearly touches bearing | Boundary lubrication, high wear |
| $S \approx 0.001$ | $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$ | Heavy duty, high load | Adequate for low-speed heavy machinery |
| $S \approx 0.1$ | $\varepsilon \approx 0.5$ | Moderate duty | General-purpose bearings |
| $S \approx 1.0$ | $\varepsilon \approx 0.1$ | Light load / high speed | Centrifugal pumps, turbomachinery |
| $S \to \infty$ | $\varepsilon \to 0$ | Perfectly concentric | Theoretically frictionless |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Petroff's Equation (Foundation for Sommerfeld Number)

### Assumptions

- The journal and bearing are **perfectly concentric** (no eccentricity).
- The lubricant film has **uniform thickness** $h = c$.
- The bearing is **infinitely long** (no side leakage).
- Flow is **laminar** and the lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid**.
- The pressure variation along the direction of motion is negligible (thin-film approximation).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Velocity of the journal surface.**
The journal rotates at $N_s$ rev/s, so the surface velocity is

$$V = \pi D N_s = 2\pi r N_s$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress in the Newtonian film.**
With uniform film thickness $c$, the velocity gradient across the film is linear. The shear stress at the journal surface is

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \frac{V - 0}{c} = \frac{\mu V}{c} = \frac{2\pi \mu r N_s}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Total viscous friction force.**
The shear force on the cylindrical journal surface (area $\pi D L$) is

$$F = \tau \cdot (\pi D L) = \frac{2\pi \mu r N_s}{c} \cdot (\pi D L)$$

Substituting $D = 2r$:

$$F = \frac{2\pi \mu r N_s}{c} \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu r^{2} N_s L}{c}$$

But $r^{2} = D^{2}/4$, so equivalently

$$F = \frac{\pi^{2} \mu N_s L D^{2}}{2c}$$

**Step 4 — Friction torque.**
The friction force acts at radius $r$ from the centre, so

$$T = F \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu r^{3} N_s L}{c}$$

Or, in diameter form,

$$T = \frac{\pi^{2} \mu N_s L D^{3}}{4c}$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of friction.**
By definition, $f = F/W$, where $W$ is the applied load. Using $W = P L D$:

$$f = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu r^{2} N_s L}{c \cdot P L D} = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu r^{2} N_s}{c \cdot P \cdot 2r} = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu N_s r}{P c}$$

Regrouping gives the **Petroff's friction law**:

$$f = 2\pi^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N_s}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

**Step 6 — Expressing $f$ in terms of the Sommerfeld Number.**
Recognize that

$$\frac{\mu N_s}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} = \left(\frac{\mu N_s}{P} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{c^{2}}\right) \cdot \frac{c}{r} = S \cdot \psi$$

Therefore,

$$\boxed{f = 2\pi^{2}\,S\,\psi = 2\pi^{2}\,S \cdot \frac{c}{r}}$$

This is the **Petroff–Sommerfeld relationship** connecting friction, the clearance ratio, and the Sommerfeld Number. The product $f/\psi = 2\pi^{2} S$ is plotted as the **friction variable** in Raimondi–Boyd charts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Loss from Petroff's Equation

**Step 1 — Angular velocity of the journal.**

$$\omega = 2\pi N_s$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical power dissipated as heat.**

$$\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = T \cdot \omega = \frac{4\pi^{2} \mu r^{3} N_s L}{c} \cdot 2\pi N_s$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{4\pi^{3} \mu r^{3} N_s^{2} L}{c} = \frac{\pi^{3} \mu N_s^{2} L D^{3}}{4c}}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalently, in terms of friction coefficient and load.**

$$\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = f \cdot W \cdot V = f \cdot (PLD) \cdot (\pi D N_s) = f \pi P L D^{2} N_s$$

All three forms are dimensionally consistent and must yield the same numerical answer.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sommerfeld Number from the Short-Bearing Reynolds Solution (First Principles)

The Sommerfeld Number emerges rigorously from integrating the **Reynolds equation** in the short-bearing limit ($L \ll D$).

**Step 1 — Reynolds equation (incompressible, steady, thin film):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2 — Short-bearing approximation.**
For $L \ll D$, the $x$-derivative term dominates, giving

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^{3} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Film thickness function.**
For an eccentric journal, $h = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta) \approx c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$ for small $\varepsilon$:

$$h(\theta) = c(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate twice in $z$ with boundary conditions $p = 0$ at $z = \pm L/2$:**

$$p(\theta, z) = \frac{3\mu U}{c^{2}} \cdot \frac{(2z^{2} - L^{2}/2)}{(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)^{3}} \cdot \varepsilon \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — Load capacity (integrate pressure over the projected area):**

$$W = \int_{0}^{2\pi} p_{\max} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot L D \, d\theta$$

After non-dimensionalizing, the load capacity assumes the form

$$\frac{W}{\mu N_s L D}\left(\frac{c}{r}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{S} = \text{function of } \varepsilon \text{ only}$$

**Step 6 — Definition of Sommerfeld Number.**

By definition, since the **left-hand side is dimensionless** and depends only on $\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\mu N_s}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}}$$

This confirms that $S$ and $\varepsilon$ are **inversely related functions of one another**, which is the basis of all Raimondi–Boyd design charts.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Petroff's Analysis)

**Given (typical KTU problem):**
A journal bearing has $D = 100\ \text{mm}$, $L = 50\ \text{mm}$, $c = 0.05\ \text{mm}$, $W = 25\ \text{kN}$, $N = 1500\ \text{rpm}$, $\mu = 0.020\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Convert units.**

$$r = 0.050\ \text{m}, \quad c = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}, \quad N_s = 1500/60 = 25\ \text{rev/s}$$

**Step 2 — Bearing pressure.**

$$P = \frac{W}{L D} = \frac{25000}{0.05 \times 0.1} = 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 5\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Sommerfeld Number.**

$$S = \frac{\mu N_s}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2} = \frac{0.020 \times 25}{5 \times 10^{6}} \times \left(\frac{0.05}{5 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{2}$$

$$S = (1.0 \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{6}) = 0.10$$

**Step 4 — Petroff friction coefficient.**

$$f = 2\pi^{2}\,S \cdot \frac{c}{r} = 2\pi^{2} \times 0.10 \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05} = 2\pi^{2} \times 0.10 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$f = 1.974 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00197$$

**Step 5 — Petroff friction torque.**

$$T = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu N_s L D r^{2}}{c} = \frac{2\pi^{2} \times 0.020 \times 25 \times 0.05 \times 0.1 \times 0.0025}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$T = 2.467\ \text{N}\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Power loss.**

$$\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = T \cdot 2\pi N_s = 2.467 \times 2\pi \times 25 \approx 387.5\ \text{W}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow: Role of the Sommerfeld Number in Bearing Design

```mermaid

    A --> D[Lubricant Viscosity mu]

    C --> F
    F --> G[Compute Sommerfeld Number S]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H{S is within design range?}
    H -- No --> I[Iterate on r, c, or mu]
    I --> F
    H -- Yes --> J[Read Eccentricity Ratio epsilon from Raimondi Boyd Chart]
    J --> K[Compute Minimum Film Thickness h0]
    J --> L[Compute Friction Variable r over c times f]
    J --> M[Compute Flow Variable Q over r c N_s L]
    K --> N[Check h0 greater than h0_min]

    M --> P[Estimate Lubricant Flow Rate]
    N --> Q{Quality Criteria Met?}
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q -- No --> I
    Q -- Yes --> R[Final Bearing Design]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the Sommerfeld Number. State its physical significance in the design of a hydrodynamic journal bearing.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Sommerfeld Number** is a dimensionless parameter that characterizes the operating regime of a hydrodynamic journal bearing, defined as

$$S = \frac{\mu N_s}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^{2}$$
