## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Define the terms:**
1. Basic Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$) of a bearing.
2. Basic Static Load Capacity ($C_0$) of a bearing.

**Model Answer**:

1. **Basic Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$)**: It is defined as the constant radial load (or axial load for thrust bearings) that a rotating bearing can theoretically endure for a rated life of **$10^6$ revolutions** (one million revolutions) without exhibiting rolling contact fatigue failure. It is expressed in Newtons (N) or kiloNewtons (kN).

2. **Basic Static Load Capacity ($C_0$)**: It is defined as the maximum static radial load (or axial load for thrust bearings) that a stationary bearing can withstand without undergoing permanent deformation exceeding **0.0001 times the diameter of the rolling element** at the most heavily loaded contact zone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Distinguish between Ball Bearing and Roller Bearing with respect to the load-life exponent $p$ in the equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$.**

**Model Answer**:

| **Basis** | **Ball Bearing** | **Roller Bearing** |
|---|---|---|
| Contact Type | Point contact | Line contact |
| Load-Life Exponent $p$ | $p = 3$ | $p = 10/3 \approx 3.33$ |
| Load Carrying Capacity | Lower (per unit size) | Higher (per unit size) |
| Friction | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Typical Applications | High-speed, light loads | Heavy radial loads, low-to-medium speeds |

Because of the larger exponent, roller bearings exhibit a more gradual life degradation with increasing load, making them more suitable for heavy-duty applications.

---

### **Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> **KTU INTERNAL CHOICE**: Answer **ANY ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

A shaft rotating at $n = 1500$ rpm is supported by a pair of single-row deep groove ball bearings. The radial load on each bearing is $F_r = 4000$ N and the axial load is $F_a = 1500$ N. The bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 32{,}000$ N, $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.6$ (at $F_a/F_r = 0.375$). Required design life $L_{10h} = 20{,}000$ hours. Check whether the selected bearing is suitable. Take $p = 3$ for ball bearings.

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks  
**Compute the equivalent dynamic load and verify the dynamic load capacity.**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Identify load ratio:

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1500}{4000} = 0.375$$

Since $F_a/F_r = 0.375 > e$ (typically $e \approx 0.22$ for ball bearings), we use the given $X$ and $Y$ values.

**Step 2**: Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$$

$$P = (0.56)(4000) + (1.6)(1500)$$

$$P = 2240 + 2400 = 4640 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3**: Compute Rated Life in Hours for Given $C$ and $P$:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \times 1500} \left(\frac{32000}{4640}\right)^3$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{90000} \left(6.897\right)^3$$

$$L_{10h} = 11.11 \times 328.13$$

$$L_{10h} = 3645.9 \text{ hours}$$

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks  
**Determine the required dynamic load capacity $C_{req}$ to achieve the design life and suggest the selection.**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Convert Required Life to Revolutions:

$$L_{10} = \frac{L_{10h} \times 60 \times n}{10^6} = \frac{20000 \times 60 \times 1500}{10^6}$$

$$L_{10} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 1800 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate Required Dynamic Load Capacity:

$$C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{1/p} = 4640 \cdot (1800)^{1/3}$$

$$C_{req} = 4640 \cdot 12.18$$

$$C_{req} = 56{,}515 \text{ N} \approx 56.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3**: Comparison and Conclusion:

Since $C_{req} = 56.5$ kN $>$ $C_{available} = 32$ kN, **the selected bearing is NOT suitable**. A larger bearing with $C \geq 56.5$ kN must be chosen from the catalogue. (Typical choice: SKF 6310 with $C \approx 61.8$ kN.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

A cylindrical roller bearing is subjected to a radial load of $F_r = 8000$ N with negligible axial load. The bearing has a basic dynamic load rating $C = 60{,}000$ N, basic static load rating $C_0 = 48{,}000$ N, and operates at $n = 1000$ rpm.

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks  
**Calculate the expected life of the bearing in hours.**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: For negligible axial load, $F_a = 0$:

$$P = F_r = 8000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2**: For roller bearings, $p = 10/3$:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\frac{60000}{8000}\right)^{10/3}$$

$$L_{10} = (7.5)^{3.333}$$

Let us compute $(7.5)^{3.333}$:

$$L_{10} = 7.5^3 \times 7.5^{0.333}$$

$$L_{10} = 421.875 \times 1.957$$

$$L_{10} \approx 825.6 \text{ million revolutions}$$

**Step 3**: Convert to Hours:

$$L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{60 \times n} = \frac{825.6 \times 10^6}{60 \times 1000}$$

$$L_{10h} = \frac{825.6 \times 10^6}{60000} = 13{,}760 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{L_{10h} \approx 13{,}760 \text{ hours}}$$

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks  
**Check the bearing for static safety assuming static safety factor $S_0 = 1.5$.**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Compute Equivalent Static Load. For negligible axial load:

$$P_0 = F_r = 8000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the Ratio:

$$\frac{C_0}{P_0} = \frac{48000}{8000} = 6$$

**Step 3**: Compare with Required Safety Factor:

Since $\frac{C_0}{P_0} = 6 \geq S_0 = 1.5$, the bearing is **safe under static loading**.

$$\boxed{\text{Static Safety Factor Satisfied.}}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Wrong exponent $p$**: Students often use $p = 3$ for roller bearings. This gives inflated life. KTU strictly checks: **Ball $\rightarrow p=3$; Roller $\rightarrow p=10/3$**. (**-2 to -3 marks**)
> 
> 2. **Unit Errors in $L_{10h}$**: Forgetting to multiply $L_{10}$ (in millions of revs) by $10^6$ before dividing by $(60 \times n)$. (**-1 to -2 marks**)
> 
> 3. **Skipping Static Verification**: Even when dynamic life is sufficient, KTU mandates the $C_0/P_0 \geq S_0$ check. Omitting this = **-2 marks minimum**.
> 
> 4. **Neglecting $F_a/F_r$ Ratio**: Just using $P = F_r$ when axial load exists leads to gross underestimation. Use the catalogue's $X, Y$ values. (**-3 marks**)
> 
> 5. **Rounding Errors**: Always retain at least 4 significant digits in intermediate calculations.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> 
> - **Dynamic Load Capacity ($C$)** is associated with **rotating** bearings and **$10^6$ revolutions** rated life.
> - **Static Load Capacity ($C_0$)** is associated with **stationary** bearings and **0.0001D** plastic deformation limit.
> - **Load-Life Equation**: $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ where **$p = 3$ (ball)**, **$p = 10/3$ (roller)**.
> - **Life in hours**: $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^p$
> - **Required $C$**: $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{1/p}$
> - **Equivalent Dynamic Load**: $P = X F_r + Y F_a$
> - **Equivalent Static Load**: $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$
> - **Static Safety Check**: $C_0 / P_0 \geq S_0$
> - **Higher $p$ value** $\Rightarrow$ **slower life degradation** with increasing load (roller bearings are more robust for heavy radial loads).
> - **Typical $S_0$ values**: $S_0 = 1$ to $3$ depending on application severity.
> - **Always do BOTH checks**: dynamic (fatigue life) **AND** static (deformation safety) for full marks.
> - **Catalogue selection**: Pick a bearing with $C_{cat} \geq C_{req}$ from the manufacturer's catalogue (e.g., SKF, NTN).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stribeck's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stribeck's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Stribeck's Equation** is the empirical mathematical relationship that describes the **coefficient of friction ($f$)** in a **journal bearing** as a function of the **Hersey number** (also called the **dimensionless lubrication parameter**), defined as the ratio of viscous force to applied load.

Mathematically, the generalized Stribeck equation is expressed as:

$$f \;=\; f_{\text{boundary}} \;+\; k \left( \frac{\mu \, N}{P} \right)^{n}$$

where:
- $f$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- $f_{\text{boundary}}$ = boundary friction component (solid-to-solid contact)
- $\mu$ = **dynamic viscosity** of the lubricant (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $N$ = **rotational speed** of the journal (rev/s or rpm)
- $P$ = **bearing pressure** = load per unit projected area (N/m²)
- $k, n$ = empirically determined constants

The dimensionless group $\dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ is called the **Hersey number** ($H$), and a related parameter called the **Sommerfeld number** is defined as:

$$S \;=\; \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P}$$

where $r$ is the journal radius and $c$ is the radial clearance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine pushing a **heavy wooden log** across sand:

- **Log not moving (low speed):** You must overcome the **static friction** between wood and sand. Hard work! → This is **boundary lubrication** (high friction).
- **Log starts sliding (medium speed):** Sand grains get pushed aside, and the log glides more easily. Friction **drops**. → This is **mixed lubrication** (transition zone).
- **Log moving fast (high speed):** Sand is "fluidized" beneath the log, and the log almost **floats** on a moving sand-layer. Very low friction. → This is **hydrodynamic lubrication** (low, stable friction).

Stribeck's equation mathematically captures this exact behaviour for oil-lubricated bearings! It is essentially a **fingerprint of how a bearing behaves across all operating conditions**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key standard metrics for journal bearing analysis under KTU 2024 scheme:**
> - **Viscosity of oil** ($\mu$): typically **0.01 to 0.1 Pa·s** at operating temperature
> - **Bearing pressure** ($P$): typically **1 to 10 MPa** for general machinery
> - **Clearance ratio** ($c/r$): typically **0.001 to 0.002**
> - **Coefficient of friction** ($f$): typically **0.001 to 0.1**
> - **Hersey number** ($H = \mu N/P$): typically **$10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6}$** (in SI units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Stribeck's equation so important?**
> It allows a design engineer to **predict bearing performance** (friction, wear, heat) before manufacturing. In KTU-style problems, it is used to calculate the friction torque, power loss, and oil-flow requirement of a journal bearing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The Stribeck curve is not a single line but a **characteristic "U-shaped" or "bathtub-shaped" curve** that arises because the friction mechanism changes with the Hersey number. Let's break it down:

**Step 1: Define the Operating Regime Variable**
- The **Hersey number** $H = \mu N / P$ represents the **lubrication film strength** relative to load.
- Low $H$ → the oil film is too weak to separate the surfaces → asperities touch → high friction.
- High $H$ → the oil film is strong enough to fully separate the surfaces → low friction.

**Step 2: Identify the Three Lubrication Regimes**

| Regime | Hersey Range | Physical Behaviour | Friction Trend |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Lubrication** | Very low $H$ | Surfaces touch directly; oil film is broken | $f$ is **high and constant** (≈ $f_{\text{boundary}}$) |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | Moderate $H$ | Partial asperity contact + partial film | $f$ **decreases sharply** as $H$ increases |
| **Hydrodynamic (Full-Film) Lubrication** | High $H$ | Complete film separation, no surface contact | $f$ **increases linearly** with $H$ (viscous shearing dominates) |

**Step 3: Connect to Petroff's Equation**
- In the **hydrodynamic regime** with **concentric shafts** ($\varepsilon = 0$), Petroff's equation gives:
$$f_{\text{Petroff}} = 2\pi^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$
- This is a **linear relationship** in the hydrodynamic region of the Stribeck curve.

**Step 4: Extend to Eccentric Shafts (Sommerfeld Analysis)**
- For non-concentric (eccentric) shafts, eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = e/c$ is introduced.
- The friction coefficient becomes a function of $\varepsilon$:
$$f_{\text{Sommerfeld}} = \frac{4\pi^{2}}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} \quad \text{(long bearing)}$$
$$f_{\text{Sommerfeld}} = \frac{2\pi^{2}(1 + \varepsilon^{2})}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} \quad \text{(short bearing approx.)}$$

**Step 5: The Stribeck Synthesis**
- Stribeck **unified** the boundary, mixed, and hydrodynamic behaviours into a **single curve** by plotting $f$ vs. $H$ on log-log axes.
- The shape of the curve is governed by:

$$f = \underbrace{f_{\text{Coulomb}}}_{\text{boundary term}} + \underbrace{K \, H^{n}}_{\text{viscous term}}$$

where $n \approx 1$ in the full-film region (matches Petroff) and $n < 1$ in mixed lubrication.

### 2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Predicts bearing life:** If the operating point lies in the boundary regime, bearing wear will be severe.
- **Selects lubricant viscosity:** Designers choose $\mu$ such that the operating point is in the hydrodynamic regime.
- **Sizing of bearings:** Used in marine propeller shafts, turbo-machinery, IC engine crankshafts, gear-box shafts.
- **Energy auditing:** Friction torque $T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r$ is directly computable for power-loss estimation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Stribeck's Equation (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | **Hersey number** (dimensionless lubrication parameter) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $S = \left( \dfrac{r}{c} \right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{\mu N}{P}$ | **Sommerfeld number** (modified Hersey) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f = f_b + k H^{n}$ | **Stribeck's equation** (general form) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $f_{\text{Petroff}} = 2\pi^{2} \, H \, \dfrac{r}{c}$ | Petroff's friction coeff. (concentric, $\varepsilon = 0$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{\text{long}} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot H \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ | Sommerfeld long-bearing friction | dimensionless |
| 6 | $f_{\text{short}} = \dfrac{2\pi^{2}(1 + \varepsilon^{2})}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot H \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ | Sommerfeld short-bearing friction (approx.) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r$ | Friction torque on journal | N·m |
| 8 | $P_{\text{loss}} = 2\pi N \, T_f$ | Power loss due to friction | W |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r c^{3} N}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon (1 + \varepsilon^{2})}{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$ | Side-leakage oil flow (long bearing) | m³/s |
| 10 | $P_{\text{unit}} = \dfrac{W}{2 \, r \, L}$ | Unit bearing pressure | N/m² (Pa) |
| 11 | $h_{\min} = c (1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness | m |
| 12 | $\Lambda = \dfrac{h_{\min}}{\sigma^{*}}$ | **Lambda ratio** (film-thickness parameter) | dimensionless |

> **Notation:** $W$ = applied load (N), $L$ = bearing length (m), $\sigma^{*}$ = composite surface roughness, $h_{\min}$ = minimum oil-film thickness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stribeck's Equation from Petroff's Equation

We derive the relationship governing the **hydrodynamic branch** of the Stribeck curve, starting from the classical Petroff friction model and extending it to a generalized form.

**Step 1: Start with the Petroff Friction Torque**

For a concentric journal bearing (no eccentricity), the viscous shear stress on the journal surface is:

$$\tau = \mu \, \frac{dv}{dy} = \mu \, \frac{U}{c} = \mu \, \frac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

*where $U = 2\pi r N$ is the linear surface speed of the journal and $c$ is the radial clearance (film thickness in concentric case).*

**Step 2: Compute the Total Friction Force**

The friction force on a journal of length $L$ and radius $r$ is:

$$F_f = \tau \cdot (2\pi r \, L) = \frac{2\pi r N \mu}{c} \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{2} N \mu L}{c}$$

*This is the shearing force on the cylindrical journal surface area $2\pi r L$.*

**Step 3: Compute the Friction Torque**

$$T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{3} N \mu L}{c}$$

**Step 4: Define the Coefficient of Friction**

The coefficient of friction is defined as the ratio of friction force to normal load:

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{2} N \mu L}{c \, W}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Unit Bearing Pressure**

The unit bearing pressure is $P = \dfrac{W}{2 r L}$, so $W = 2 r L P$. Substituting:

$$f = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{2} N \mu L}{c \cdot 2 r L P} = \frac{2\pi^{2} \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{\text{Petroff}} = 2\pi^{2} \cdot \left( \frac{\mu N}{P} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)\,}$$

**Step 6: Recognize the Hydrodynamic Branch of the Stribeck Curve**

Since $H = \mu N / P$, we can rewrite this as:

$$f_{\text{hydro}} = 2\pi^{2} \, H \, \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)$$

This is the **linear (viscous) portion** of the Stribeck curve in the hydrodynamic regime. The slope on a log-log plot is **unity** (i.e., $n = 1$ in the Stribeck form $f = kH^{n}$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Stribeck's Equation with Eccentricity (Sommerfeld Extension)

**Step 1: Account for Eccentricity Ratio**

Let $\varepsilon = e / c$ be the eccentricity ratio ($0 \le \varepsilon < 1$). The film thickness varies around the journal as:

$$h(\theta) = c \,(1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)$$

**Step 2: Integrate the Variable Shear Stress**

The local shear stress is $\tau(\theta) = \mu \, U / h(\theta)$. The total friction force is:

$$F_f = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \tau(\theta) \cdot r \, L \, d\theta = \mu U r L \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{d\theta}{c (1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta)}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the Integral**

Using the standard integral result $\int_{0}^{2\pi} \dfrac{d\theta}{1 + \varepsilon \cos\theta} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}$:

$$F_f = \mu U r L \cdot \frac{2\pi}{c \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $U = 2\pi r N$ and Simplify**

$$F_f = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{2} N \mu L}{c \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Coefficient of Friction**

Using $W = 2 r L P$:

$$f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{2} N \mu L}{2 r L P \cdot c \sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} = \frac{2\pi^{2}}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{\text{long, eccentric}} = \frac{2\pi^{2}}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot H \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)\,}$$

**Step 6: Connection to the Stribeck Curve**

This is the **Sommerfeld-modified** version of the Stribeck hydrodynamic branch. As $\varepsilon \to 0$, it reduces to Petroff's equation. As $\varepsilon \to 1$ (very high load), $f \to \infty$, signalling the breakdown of hydrodynamic lubrication — exactly the transition into the mixed/boundary regime.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A journal bearing has the following data:
- Journal radius: $r = 50$ mm
- Radial clearance: $c = 0.1$ mm
- Load: $W = 10$ kN
- Speed: $N = 1500$ rpm
- Lubricant viscosity: $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s
- Eccentricity ratio: $\varepsilon = 0.6$

**Find:** (i) Hersey number (ii) Coefficient of friction using Stribeck's equation (long bearing) (iii) Friction torque (iv) Power loss.

**Solution:**

**Step (i): Hersey Number**

Convert units: $N = 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s. Unit pressure:

$$P = \frac{W}{2 r L} \text{ (assumed } L \text{ cancels; using effective } P \text{ from } W \text{ alone)}$$

Using $P = W / (2 r L)$, and noting that for a full numerical solution we would need $L$. Let's assume a **per unit length** formulation where the result is reported per metre of length. The Hersey number is:

$$H = \frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{0.05 \times 25}{10{,}000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (per metre length basis)}$$

**Step (ii): Coefficient of Friction (Long Bearing, Stribeck-Sommerfeld)**

$$f = \frac{2\pi^{2}}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot H \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)$$

$$\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}} = \sqrt{1 - 0.36} = \sqrt{0.64} = 0.8$$

$$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{50}{0.1} = 500$$

$$f = \frac{2 \times 9.8696}{0.8} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 500 = 24.674 \times 0.0625 = 1.5421$$

**Step (iii): Friction Torque**

$$T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r = 1.5421 \times 10{,}000 \times 0.05 = 771.05 \text{ N·m (per metre length)}$$

**Step (iv): Power Loss**

$$P_{\text{loss}} = 2\pi N \cdot T_f = 2\pi \times 25 \times 771.05 = 121{,}126 \text{ W} \approx 121.13 \text{ kW (per metre length)}$$

*This is unrealistically high because the problem was simplified; in practice, the actual pressure $P$ is computed using a specific $L$. The exercise illustrates the algebraic steps required in a KTU 14-mark problem.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Stribeck Curve Regimes

The Stribeck curve is fundamentally a **regime-transition diagram** for lubrication. Since the Mermaid engine cannot easily draw a smooth continuous curve with mathematical axis labels, we render a **functional block-flowchart** showing the operational states and their physical meaning.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[START: ROTATING JOURNAL IN BEARING] --> B[COMPUTE HERSEY NUMBER H = MU N OVER P]
    B --> C{H VALUE IN WHICH REGIME}
    C --> D1[BOUNDARY LUBRICATION]
    C --> D2[MIXED LUBRICATION]
    C --> D3[HYDRODYNAMIC LUBRICATION]
    D1 --> E1[ASPERITY TO ASPERITY CONTACT]
    D1 --> F1[FRICTION COEFFICIENT IS HIGH AND NEARLY CONSTANT]
    D1 --> G1[BOUNDARY LUBRICANT FILM LAYER ONLY]
    D2 --> E2[PARTIAL METAL CONTACT]
    D2 --> F2[FRICTION COEFFICIENT DECREASES WITH INCREASING H]
    D2 --> G2[OIL FILM FORMS PARTIALLY BUT IS INTERMITTENT]
    D3 --> E3[COMPLETE OIL FILM SEPARATION]
    D3 --> F3[FRICTION COEFFICIENT INCREASES LINEARLY WITH H PER STRIBECK]
    D3 --> G3[VISCOUS SHEARING DOMINATES THE LOSS]
    F1 --> H[OPERATING POINT FALLS HERE: HIGH WEAR RISK]
    F2 --> I[OPERATING POINT FALLS HERE: TRANSITION ZONE]
    F3 --> J[OPERATING POINT FALLS HERE: IDEAL FOR LOW WEAR]
    H --> K[REMEDY: INCREASE VISCOSITY OR REDUCE LOAD]
    I --> L[REMEDY: INCREASE SPEED OR VISCOSITY SLIGHTLY]
    J --> M[DESIGN GOAL: KEEP OPERATION IN THIS REGION]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

1. The central decision block `C` classifies the operating condition based on the **Hersey number**.
2. Each regime has three functional characteristics: **contact behaviour** (E), **friction trend** (F), and **lubricant film state** (G).
3. The terminal blocks (H, I, J) represent the **engineering actions** corresponding to each regime.
4. The **ideal design target** in any KTU 2024 problem is to position the operating point in block **J** (hydrodynamic regime).

### 4.2 Mapping of Stribeck Equation to Bearing Design Parameters

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[INPUT PARAMETERS] --> B[LOAD W]
    A --> C[SPEED N]
    A --> D[VISCOSITY MU]
    A --> E[GEOMETRY R AND C]
    B --> F[COMPUTE PRESSURE P]
    C --> G[COMPUTE HERSEY H]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{STRIBECK EQUATION}
    E --> H
    H --> I[COEFFICIENT OF FRICTION F]
    I --> J[FRICTION TORQUE T SUB F]
    I --> K[POWER LOSS P LOSS]
    I --> L[MINIMUM FILM THICKNESS H MIN]
    J --> M[ENGINEERING DECISIONS]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[BEARING SELECTED FOR SAFE OPERATION]
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram shows the **computational pipeline** — given the bearing inputs, the Stribeck equation produces the key performance outputs (friction, torque, power loss, film thickness) that drive the final bearing selection.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Stribeck's equation. What is the physical significance of the Hersey number in journal-bearing analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Stribeck's equation is an empirical relationship that expresses the **coefficient of friction ($f$)** in a journal bearing as a function of the **Hersey number** $H = \mu N / P$. The general form is:
> $$f = f_{\text{boundary}} + k H^{n}$$
>
> **Physical significance of the Hersey number:**
> - It is a **dimensionless parameter** representing the ratio of **viscous force** to **applied load**.
> - A **small Hersey number** indicates weak oil-film formation → surfaces touch → **high friction and wear**.
> - A **large Hersey number** indicates a strong, load-bearing oil film → surfaces are separated → **low friction and long life**.
> - The Hersey number essentially acts as a **lubrication regime indicator** on the Stribeck curve (boundary, mixed, or hydrodynamic).
>
> **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for Hersey number definition, 1 for regime significance)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*List the three lubrication regimes identified in the Stribeck curve. For each regime, state whether the coefficient of friction (a) is high and constant, (b) decreases sharply, or (c) increases linearly with the Hersey number.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Regime | Friction Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Boundary Lubrication** | (a) **High and nearly constant** — surfaces are in direct asperity contact; the lubricant film is too thin to separate them. |
| 2 | **Mixed Lubrication** | (b) **Decreases sharply** as $H$ increases — partial film formation reduces metal-to-metal contact. |
| 3 | **Hydrodynamic (Full-Film) Lubrication** | (c) **Increases linearly** with $H$ — surfaces are fully separated; friction is due to viscous shearing of the oil film (governed by Petroff's equation). |

> **(3 marks: 1 mark for each correct regime–behaviour pair)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. The question must have sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Stribeck–Petroff equation for the coefficient of friction in a **concentric journal bearing**. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A full journal bearing has the following data:
- Journal diameter $D = 100$ mm, length $L = 200$ mm
- Radial clearance $c = 0.05$ mm
- Load $W = 25$ kN
- Speed $N = 1440$ rpm
- Lubricant viscosity $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s
- Eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon = 0.5$

Calculate: (i) Unit bearing pressure (ii) Hersey number (iii) Coefficient of friction using the long-bearing Stribeck equation (iv) Friction torque and power loss. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Q-A):**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

> *Step 1 — Assumptions:*
> - The journal is **concentric** with the bearing (no eccentricity, $\varepsilon = 0$).
> - The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid** with constant viscosity $\mu$.
> - The flow is **laminar** and the oil film thickness is uniform $= c$.
> - The bearing is **infinitely long** (side-leakage neglected).
> - The pressure variation along the circumferential direction is neglected (Petroff's assumption).

> *Step 2 — Velocity gradient:*
> The journal surface moves at velocity $U = 2\pi r N$ relative to the bearing. The velocity gradient across the film is:
> $$\frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{U}{c} = \frac{2\pi r N}{c}$$

> *Step 3 — Shear stress:*
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{2\pi r N \mu}{c}$$

> *Step 4 — Friction force on journal:*
> $$F_f = \tau \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{2} N \mu L}{c}$$

> *Step 5 — Coefficient of friction:*
> Using $P = W / (2 r L)$:
> $$f_{\text{Petroff}} = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^{2} r^{2} N \mu L}{c \cdot 2 r L P} = 2\pi^{2} \cdot \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c}$$
> $$\boxed{f_{\text{Petroff}} = 2\pi^{2} H \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)}$$
> This is the **Stribeck–Petroff equation** (hydrodynamic branch with $\varepsilon = 0$).

> **(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for assumptions, 2 for shear stress derivation, 2 for coefficient of friction, 1 for final boxed equation)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

> *Given:* $D = 0.1$ m, $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.2$ m, $c = 0.05 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $W = 25{,}000$ N, $N = 1440/60 = 24$ rev/s, $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s, $\varepsilon = 0.5$.

> **(i) Unit bearing pressure:**
> $$P = \frac{W}{2 r L} = \frac{25{,}000}{2 \times 0.05 \times 0.2} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.02} = 1.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 1.25 \text{ MPa}$$

> **(ii) Hersey number:**
> $$H = \frac{\mu N}{P} = \frac{0.04 \times 24}{1.25 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{0.96}{1.25 \times 10^{6}} = 7.68 \times 10^{-7}$$

> **(iii) Coefficient of friction (long bearing, Stribeck):**
> $$\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}} = \sqrt{1 - 0.25} = \sqrt{0.75} = 0.8660$$
> $$\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.05}{0.05 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000$$
> $$f = \frac{2\pi^{2}}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot H \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right) = \frac{2 \times 9.8696}{0.8660} \times 7.68 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000$$
> $$f = 22.79 \times 7.68 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0175$$

> **(iv) Friction torque and power loss:**
> $$T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r = 0.0175 \times 25{,}000 \times 0.05 = 21.875 \text{ N·m}$$
> $$P_{\text{loss}} = 2\pi N \, T_f = 2\pi \times 24 \times 21.875 = 3297 \text{ W} \approx 3.30 \text{ kW}$$

> **(7 marks breakdown:** 2 marks each for (i) and (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for (iv))

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Sketch and explain the **Stribeck curve**. Label the three lubrication regimes and discuss how the coefficient of friction varies with the Hersey number in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Petroff's equation** and **Stribeck's equation** in terms of assumptions, applicability, and limitations. What modification did Sommerfeld introduce to handle eccentric bearings? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Q-B):**

**Part (a) — Stribeck Curve Explanation (7 marks):**

> *Sketch description (verbal — Mermaid alternative is provided in Section 4):*
> - **X-axis:** $\log H = \log(\mu N / P)$ (logarithmic scale)
> - **Y-axis:** $\log f$ (logarithmic scale)
> - **Curve shape:** A characteristic "bathtub" or "U-shape"
> - **Three labelled regions:** Boundary, Mixed, Hydrodynamic

> *Detailed explanation:*
>
> **Region I — Boundary Lubrication (low Hersey number):**
> - The oil film is incomplete; **asperities of journal and bearing touch directly**.
> - The friction coefficient is governed by the **boundary lubricant's properties** (e.g., fatty acid additives), not by viscosity.
> - On the log-log plot, this region appears as a **horizontal line** ($f \approx \text{constant} = f_{\text{boundary}}$).
> - **Engineering implication:** High wear; not a desirable operating zone.

> **Region II — Mixed Lubrication (intermediate Hersey number):**
> - The oil film begins to form but is **intermittent**; partial asperity contact remains.
> - The friction coefficient **decreases steeply** as $H$ increases (a near-vertical drop on log-log axes).
> - **Engineering implication:** Unstable; rapid wear transitions occur here.

> **Region III — Hydrodynamic Lubrication (high Hersey number):**
> - A **complete oil film** separates the journal from the bearing.
> - Friction is purely due to **viscous shearing** of the oil.
> - On the log-log plot, this is a **straight line with slope ≈ 1** (i.e., $f \propto H$).
> - **Engineering implication:** Ideal operating zone; low wear, predictable friction.

> **(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for sketch with axes, 2 for naming regimes, 3 for detailed behaviour explanation)

---

**Part (b) — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Petroff's Equation | Stribeck's Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Analytical (closed-form) | Empirical (curve-fitted) |
| **Eccentricity** | Assumes **concentric** journal ($\varepsilon = 0$) | Valid for **all** eccentricities (uses $\varepsilon$ via Sommerfeld extension) |
| **Friction Expression** | $f = 2\pi^{2} H (r/c)$ | $f = f_b + k H^{n}$ |
| **Lubrication Regimes Covered** | Only **hydrodynamic** | All three: boundary, mixed, hydrodynamic |
| **Load Capacity** | Cannot predict load capacity (no $\varepsilon$ dependence) | Can predict load capacity (via $P = W / 2rL$) |
| **Applicability** | Quick preliminary checks | Comprehensive design analysis |
| **Limitation** | Unrealistic for loaded bearings | Requires empirical constants $k, n$ |

> **Sommerfeld's Modification:**
> - Sommerfeld extended Petroff's equation to handle **eccentric** bearings by introducing the **eccentricity ratio** $\varepsilon = e/c$.
> - The modified long-bearing Stribeck–Sommerfeld equation:
> $$f = \frac{2\pi^{2}}{\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}} \cdot H \cdot \left( \frac{r}{c} \right)$$
> - As $\varepsilon \to 0$, this reduces to Petroff's equation; as $\varepsilon \to 1$, the friction blows up, signalling the **transition out of hydrodynamic lubrication**.

> **(7 marks breakdown:** 4 marks for the comparison table, 3 marks for Sommerfeld modification)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** $N$ must be in **rev/s** (not rpm) when using SI units. Mixing rpm and rev/s is the **#1 reason** for numerical answer mismatch in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Forgetting $\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$:** In the long-bearing Stribeck equation, students often write $f = 2\pi^{2} H (r/c)$ even when $\varepsilon \ne 0$. **Always check the eccentricity!**
> 3. **Confusing Hersey and Sommerfeld numbers:** Hersey $H = \mu N / P$ is a property of the operating point; Sommerfeld $S = (r/c)^{2} H$ also incorporates the **geometry**. They are **not interchangeable** in formulas.
> 4. **Skipping the Stribeck branch identification:** When asked to "explain the curve," students often describe only the hydrodynamic branch. The **boundary and mixed regimes are mandatory** for full marks.
> 5. **Assuming $\varepsilon = 0$ in design problems:** In a real loaded bearing, $\varepsilon > 0$. Using Petroff's equation for a heavily loaded bearing will give a **grossly underestimated** friction coefficient.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Stribeck's Equation (KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- **Stribeck's equation** is fundamentally: $f = f_{\text{boundary}} + k H^{n}$, where $H = \mu N / P$ is the **Hersey number**.
- The **Hersey number** represents the **strength of the oil film** relative to the applied load.
- The **Sommerfeld number** is the geometrically scaled Hersey number: $S = (r/c)^{2} H$.
- **Three lubrication regimes** exist on the Stribeck curve:
  - **Boundary:** $f$ is high and constant — direct asperity contact.
  - **Mixed:** $f$ decreases sharply — partial film formation.
  - **Hydrodynamic:** $f$ increases linearly with $H$ — full film, viscous shearing.
- **Petroff's equation** ($f = 2\pi^{2} H r/c$) is the special case of Stribeck's equation for **concentric** ($\varepsilon = 0$) bearings in the **hydrodynamic** regime.
- **Sommerfeld's modification** introduces the eccentricity ratio $\varepsilon$ and the factor $1/\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon^{2}}$ to account for non-concentric loading.
- **Friction torque** is computed as $T_f = f \cdot W \cdot r$.
- **Power loss** in the bearing is $P_{\text{loss}} = 2\pi N \cdot T_f$.
- **Engineering design goal:** Always operate the bearing in the **hydrodynamic regime** (high $H$) to minimize wear.
- **Common exam traps:** Mixing rpm with rev/s; using Petroff's equation when $\varepsilon \ne 0$; confusing $H$ with $S$.
- **Key geometric quantity:** $h_{\min} = c(1 - \varepsilon)$ — the **minimum film thickness**, used to verify that surface asperities do not collide.
- **Lambda ratio** $\Lambda = h_{\min} / \sigma^{*}$ is the design criterion: $\Lambda > 3$ for full-film lubrication, $1 < \Lambda < 3$ for mixed, $\Lambda < 1$ for boundary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Bearings
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛞 SELECTION OF BEARINGS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT403 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selection of Bearings

**Bearing Selection** is the systematic engineering process of choosing the most appropriate bearing (rolling element or sliding contact) for a specific mechanical application by matching **operating loads**, **rotational speeds**, **expected service life**, **environmental conditions**, and **lubrication requirements** against the manufacturer's rated performance parameters such as **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)** and **Basic Static Load Rating (C₀)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, *bearing selection* is a design decision that directly affects machine **reliability**, **efficiency (η)**, **rotational accuracy**, and **mean time between failures (MTBF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The selection of bearings involves the application of **ISO 15:2017** and **ISO 281:2007** standards for dynamic load rating and modified life calculations. Students must be conversant with the **dynamic equivalent load (P)** and the **modified rating life (Lₙₘ)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tyre Analogy" 🚗

Imagine you are buying **tyres for a vehicle**. You do NOT randomly pick the first tyre on the shelf. Instead, you consider:

- 🏋️ **How heavy is the vehicle?** → *Load carrying capacity*
- 🏎️ **How fast will you drive?** → *Speed rating*
- 🛣️ **Rough city roads or smooth highway?** → *Operating environment*
- ⏳ **How many kilometres do you expect them to last?** → *Service life*

**Bearing selection works EXACTLY the same way.** A bearing is a "rotating tyre" supporting a shaft, and the engineer must systematically match the bearing's **rated capacity** to the **service demands** of the application.

A tiny **bicycle wheel bearing** cannot support a **railway axle load**, just as a Formula-1 slick tyre is unsuitable for a heavy truck.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Bearing:**
> A bearing is a machine element that **constrains relative motion** to only the desired motion (typically rotation) and **reduces friction** between moving parts, thereby transmitting **loads** while permitting **smooth relative motion**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Bearings (Foundational Recap)

Bearing selection begins with choosing the **type**, guided by operational parameters.

| **Category** | **Sub-Types** | **Key Characteristic** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Rolling Contact Bearings** | Deep Groove Ball, Angular Contact, Cylindrical Roller, Tapered Roller, Spherical Roller, Needle Roller, Thrust Ball, Thrust Roller | Low starting friction, standardized dimensions, high speed capability |
| **Sliding Contact Bearings** | Journal (Sleeve), Thrust, Tilting Pad, Hydrostatic, Hydrodynamic | High load capacity, dampening, low noise, suitable for very high radial loads |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exam:**
> - **Ball Bearings** → primarily radial + some thrust load, higher speeds.
> - **Roller Bearings** → heavy radial loads, line contact gives higher capacity.
> - **Tapered Roller / Angular Contact** → combined radial + thrust loads.
> - **Thrust Bearings** → purely axial loads.
> - **Journal Bearings** → very heavy loads, low-to-moderate speeds, or where damping is critical.

---

## 1.4 The "Five Pillars" of Bearing Selection

According to **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4** outcomes, the selection process rests on **five primary parameters**:

1. **Magnitude and Direction of Load** (Radial `F_r`, Thrust `F_a`, Combined)
2. **Operating Speed** (n in rpm) → dictates permissible `dn` value & lubrication regime
3. **Required Life** (L₁₀ in hours or revolutions) → dictates required dynamic load rating `C`
4. **Operating Temperature** → affects lubricant viscosity & material selection
5. **Misalignment & Mounting Constraints** → dictates self-aligning vs. rigid types

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:**
> Students often confuse the **Basic Dynamic Load Rating (C)** with the **Applied Load (P)**. Remember: **C is the *capacity* of the bearing**, while **P is the *demand* from the application**. The bearing is correctly selected only when **C > P** (typically **C ≥ P × safety factor**).

---

## 1.5 Bearing Designation System (Standardised Identification)

Bearing designations are the "model numbers" used to identify a specific bearing from the manufacturer's catalogue (e.g., **SKF 6205**, **NTN 6308**).

A standard designation (e.g., **6205-ZZ**) decodes as:

| **Digit** | **Code** | **Meaning** |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1st | **6** | Bearing type: Deep Groove Ball Bearing |
| 2nd | **2** | Dimension series: Light Series 02 |
| 3rd | **0** | Width series: Normal (omitted in some systems) |
| 4th-5th | **05** | Bore code: 05 = 25 mm bore (code 00=10mm, 01=12mm, 02=15mm, 03=17mm, 04=20mm, 05=25mm) |
| Suffix | **ZZ** | Shields on both sides (metal shields) |

> Common suffixes: **2RS** = Rubber Seals on both sides, **N** = Snap ring groove, **P6** = Precision class.

---

## 1.6 Why Selection Matters — Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace** → Ultra-high precision, high-speed bearings (ceramic hybrid, ABEC-7+)
- **Automotive** → Tapered roller for wheel hubs, deep groove for electric motors
- **Heavy Industry** → Spherical roller bearings for crushers, rolling mills
- **Turbomachinery** → Hydrodynamic journal bearings for high-speed steam/gas turbines
- **Precision Machine Tools** → Preloaded angular contact bearings for spindles

The **correct bearing selection** ensures:
- ✅ Predictable **L₁₀ fatigue life**
- ✅ Minimal **frictional losses** (improved **mechanical efficiency**)
- ✅ Reduced **downtime** and **maintenance costs**
- ✅ Compliance with **safety standards** (ISO, AGMA, DIN)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Selection Methodology — A Step-by-Step Engineering Framework

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to follow a **structured analytical procedure** when solving bearing selection problems. The methodology is:

### **STEP 1: Define the Application Requirements**
- Radial load: **F_r** (N)
- Axial / Thrust load: **F_a** (N)
- Rotational speed: **n** (rpm)
- Desired life: **L₁₀ₕ** (hours) or **L₁₀** (revolutions)
- Temperature: **T** (°C)
- Shaft & housing fits, lubrication type, sealing arrangement

### **STEP 2: Determine the Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)**

The actual load on a bearing is often a **combination of radial and thrust forces**. Manufacturers cannot test every combination, so they publish ratings for a **pure radial load** equivalent. The **Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)** is the constant radial load that, if applied, would give the **same bearing life** as the actual combined load.

For most radial & angular contact bearings:

$$
P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a
$$

where:
- **X** = Radial load factor (dimensionless)
- **Y** = Axial / Thrust load factor (dimensionless)
- **F_r** = Applied radial load (N)
- **F_a** = Applied thrust load (N)

The factors **X** and **Y** are read from the **manufacturer's catalogue** based on the ratio **F_a / F_r** and the bearing type.

For **pure radial contact** bearings (cylindrical roller, deep groove ball with F_a = 0):

$$
P = F_r
$$

For **pure thrust bearings**:

$$
P = F_a
$$

### **STEP 3: Convert Required Life to Revolutions**

If life is specified in **hours (L₁₀ₕ)**:

$$
L_{10} = L_{10h} \times 60 \times n \quad \text{[revolutions]}
$$

If life is specified in **millions of revolutions (L₁₀ₘ)**:

$$
L_{10} = L_{10m} \times 10^6 \quad \text{[revolutions]}
$$

### **STEP 4: Calculate the Required Dynamic Load Rating (C_required)**

The fundamental **life-load relationship** derived by **Lundberg & Palmgren (1947)** is:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \times 10^6 \quad \text{[revolutions]}
$$

Solving for **C_required**:

$$
C_{required} = P \times \left(\frac{L_{10}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}
$$

where:
- **p = 3** for **ball bearings**
- **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.333** for **roller bearings**

### **STEP 5: Select the Bearing from the Catalogue**

Choose the bearing whose **Basic Dynamic Load Rating C** (from the catalogue) is **≥ C_required**.

### **STEP 6: Check the Static Load Rating (C₀)**

If the bearing is stationary or rotating very slowly (n < 10 rpm), the failure mode is **permanent indentation** at the contact zone, governed by the **static load rating**:

$$
C_0 \geq P_0 \times S_{min}
$$

where:
- **P₀** = Equivalent static load = **X₀·F_r + Y₀·F_a**
- **S_min** = Static safety factor (typically **1.0 to 2.0**, depending on shock loading)

### **STEP 7: Verify Lubrication and Speed Limits**

Check the **limiting speed (n_limit)** for the chosen bearing against the **operating speed (n_op)**. A safety factor on limiting speed should be applied:

$$
n_{op} \leq 0.5 \text{ to } 0.8 \times n_{limit}
$$

The **viscosity ratio κ (kappa)** must be ≥ 1 at operating temperature to ensure hydrodynamic film separation.

---

## 2.2 Modified Rating Life (ISO 281:2007)

Modern bearing selection per **ISO 281:2007** uses a **modified rating life** that accounts for:
- Lubrication condition (contamination, viscosity ratio κ)
- Fatigue load limit (C_u)

$$
L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}
$$

where:
- **a₁** = reliability factor (e.g., 1.00 for 90%, 0.62 for 95%, 0.21 for 99%)
- **a_ISO** = life modification factor (function of κ, contamination, fatigue limit)

For **KTU 2024 Module 4**, the basic Lundberg-Palmgren formulation is the primary expected answer, with the **a_ISO** factor mentioned as a refinement.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> **Table 2.1: High-Yield Formula Reference for Bearing Selection**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load | **N** |
| 2 | $P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent static load | **N** |
| 3 | $L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \cdot 10^6$ | Basic rating life (Lundberg-Palmgren) | **revolutions** |
| 4 | $C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{L_{10}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}$ | Required dynamic load rating | **N** |
| 5 | $L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Life in hours | **hours** |
| 6 | $L_{10m} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$ | Life in millions of revolutions | **dimensionless** |
| 7 | $p = 3$ (ball) ; $p = 10/3$ (roller) | Load-life exponent | **—** |
| 8 | $P_{eq, axial} = 0.4 \cdot F_r + K_F \cdot F_a$ | Equivalent load for thrust bearings | **N** |
| 9 | $a_{c} = 0.1 \cdot \kappa^{0.7}$ | Contamination factor (approx.) | **—** |
| 10 | $C_0 \geq S_{min} \cdot P_0$ | Static load check | **N** |
| 11 | $\kappa = \frac{\nu_{actual}}{\nu_{required}}$ | Viscosity ratio | **dimensionless** |
| 12 | $f_{cm} = \frac{C}{P}$ | Load ratio (must be ≥ 1) | **—** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark-Winning Tip:** Always state the **units** in your final answer and clearly indicate whether you are using **p = 3 (ball)** or **p = 10/3 (roller)**. Examiners deduct marks when these are missing.

---

## 2.4 Application of the Concept in Real Engineering

| **Application** | **Typical Bearing** | **Reason** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Electric Motor (general) | Deep Groove Ball (e.g., 6205) | Handles radial + small thrust, high speed, low cost |
| Automotive Wheel Hub | Tapered Roller Bearing | Combined radial + thrust, adjustable clearance |
| Railway Axlebox | Cylindrical Roller + Tapered Roller | Very heavy radial + some thrust, line contact |
| Helical Gearbox Shaft | Angular Contact Ball (paired) | Resists axial thrust from gear meshing |
| Centrifugal Pump | Deep Groove Ball or Cylindrical Roller | Moderate loads, moderate speeds |
| Steam Turbine (large) | Hydrodynamic Journal | Very high speeds, smoothness, dampening |
| Concrete Mixer | Spherical Roller | Heavy load + significant shaft misalignment |
| Bicycle Wheel Hub | Loose ball bearings or cartridge | Light load, low speed, low cost |

The **selection** is therefore an optimization across multiple, often competing, requirements.

---

## 2.5 Selection Flowchart Logic (Textual)

```
START → Define Loads (Fr, Fa)
      → Define Speed (n) and Life (L10h)
      → Identify Bearing Type (Ball/Roller/Thrust)
      → Calculate P = X·Fr + Y·Fa
      → Convert Life to Revolutions
      → Calculate C_required
      → Catalogue Lookup → Choose bearing with C ≥ C_required
      → Check C₀ ≥ Sm × P₀
      → Check n_op ≤ k × n_limit
      → Check κ ≥ 1 (Lubrication)
      → FINAL SELECTION
```

This **decision tree** is the exact procedure expected in KTU 2024 ESE Part B answers.

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lundberg-Palmgren Life Equation

The fundamental equation used in **all rolling bearing life calculations** is the Lundberg-Palmgren equation, derived empirically and theoretically from the **fatigue of material** under Hertzian contact stress.

### **Starting Point: Hertzian Contact Stress Theory**

For two bodies in line or point contact, the **maximum Hertzian contact pressure** is:

$$
p_0 = \frac{3F}{2\pi \cdot a \cdot b} \quad \text{[Pa]}
$$

where **a** and **b** are the semi-axes of the contact ellipse, and **F** is the applied contact force.

### **Step 1: Stress Cycle and Fatigue Hypothesis**

When a bearing rotates, each rolling element experiences a **cyclic stress** as it enters and exits the load zone. Lundberg and Palmgren hypothesised that **fatigue initiates subsurface** at a depth **z₀** below the contact surface, where the **maximum alternating shear stress τ₀** acts.

The number of stress cycles **N** to cause a fatigue spall of unit area was found experimentally to follow:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{1}{N}\right) \propto \frac{\tau_0^{c} \cdot z_0^{1-c}}{K}
$$

where:
- **c ≈ 9 for ball bearings, c ≈ 6 for roller bearings** (stress-life exponent)
- **K** = material constant
- **z₀** = depth of maximum shear stress

### **Step 2: Relating Contact Stress to Applied Load**

For **ball bearings** (point contact):

$$
\tau_0 \propto \left(\frac{F}{d^2}\right)^{1/2} \quad \text{and} \quad z_0 \propto d
$$

For **roller bearings** (line contact):

$$
\tau_0 \propto \left(\frac{F}{\ell \cdot d}\right)^{1/2} \quad \text{and} \quad z_0 \propto d
$$

where **d** is the ball/roller diameter and **ℓ** is the roller length.

### **Step 3: Integration Over All Rolling Elements**

The total fatigue life of the bearing is determined by the **weakest of the contact points**, after integrating the stress over the rolling elements. The result is:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \cdot 10^6 \quad \text{[revolutions]}
$$

with:
- **p = 3** for ball bearings
- **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.333** for roller bearings

> **Why is p different for balls and rollers?**
> The exponent **p** reflects the sensitivity of fatigue life to load. In **line contact** (rollers), the contact area is **larger** for the same load, and the **stress gradient** is shallower — leading to a slightly different (and more sensitive) load-life relationship. Roller bearings therefore have a **steeper life penalty** with overload.

### **Step 4: Conversion to Hours**

Since engineers commonly specify life in **hours of operation**, we convert revolutions to hours using the rotational speed **n** (rpm):

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

Or in **millions of revolutions**:

$$
L_{10m} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

This is the form most commonly used in KTU problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Required Dynamic Load Rating

Rearranging the Lundberg-Palmgren equation to find the **minimum required dynamic load rating** for a given life and load:

### **Starting Equation:**

$$
L_{10m} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

### **Step 1: Take the p-th root on both sides**

$$
\left(L_{10m}\right)^{1/p} = \frac{C}{P}
$$

### **Step 2: Isolate C**

$$
C = P \cdot \left(L_{10m}\right)^{1/p}
$$

### **Step 3: For a Life Specified in Hours**

Recall that **L₁₀ₘ = (60·n·L₁₀ₕ) / 10⁶**, hence:

$$
C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}
$$

This is the **master equation** for KTU Part B 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **[Worked Problem]**
> A **deep groove ball bearing** in a gearbox operates under the following conditions:
> - Radial load: **F_r = 4000 N**
> - Thrust load: **F_a = 1500 N**
> - Speed: **n = 1450 rpm**
> - Desired life: **L₁₀ₕ = 10,000 hours**
> - Catalogue factors (for F_a/F_r = 0.375 and the bearing series): **X = 0.56, Y = 1.15**
>
> Calculate the **required Basic Dynamic Load Rating C** and **select a suitable bearing** from the catalogue (given catalogue values: 6208 → C = 32,500 N, 6209 → C = 41,000 N, 6210 → C = 47,500 N).

### **Solution — Step-by-Step:**

### **Step 1: Calculate Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)**

$$
P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a
$$

$$
P = (0.56)(4000) + (1.15)(1500)
$$

$$
P = 2240 + 1725 = 3965 \text{ N}
$$

### **Step 2: Convert Life to Millions of Revolutions**

$$
L_{10m} = \frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}
$$

$$
L_{10m} = \frac{60 \times 1450 \times 10000}{10^6}
$$

$$
L_{10m} = \frac{870,000,000}{10^6} = 870
$$

### **Step 3: Apply the Life Equation (p = 3 for ball bearings)**

$$
C_{req} = P \cdot \left(L_{10m}\right)^{1/p} = P \cdot (L_{10m})^{1/3}
$$

$$
C_{req} = 3965 \cdot (870)^{1/3}
$$

### **Step 4: Calculate the Cube Root**

$$
(870)^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{870}
$$

Let us estimate: $9^3 = 729$, $9.5^3 = 857.4$, $9.55^3 = 870.9$

So $(870)^{1/3} \approx 9.547$

$$
C_{req} = 3965 \times 9.547 \approx 37{,}854 \text{ N}
$$

### **Step 5: Catalogue Selection**

| **Bearing** | **Catalogue C (N)** | **Suitable?** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 6208 | 32,500 | ❌ (insufficient) |
| **6209** | **41,000** | ✅ **SELECTED** |
| 6210 | 47,500 | ✅ (oversized, higher cost) |

### **Conclusion:**
**Bearing 6209** is the **smallest, most economical choice** satisfying the life requirement.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** Most students make errors in the **unit conversion** between hours and revolutions. Always write out: **L₁₀ₘ = (60·n·L₁₀ₕ)/10⁶** explicitly to avoid losing 2-3 marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Ball vs. Roller Bearings

| **Parameter** | **Ball Bearing** | **Roller Bearing** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Contact type | Point | Line |
| Load capacity | Moderate to high | Very high |
| Speed capability | Higher | Lower (due to higher friction) |
| Radial load handling | Good | Excellent |
| Thrust load handling | Limited (depends on type) | Limited (most types) |
| Misalignment tolerance | Low (except self-aligning) | Low (except spherical) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Friction torque | Lower | Higher |
| Manufacturing precision required | High | High |
| Load-life exponent **p** | 3 | 10/3 |
| Typical application | Electric motors, gearboxes | Rolling mills, heavy machinery |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Selection from Catalogue Using Life Equation

The general selection problem can be **inverted** in three ways depending on what is given:

### **Case A: Given Load and Life → Find C**

$$
C_{req} = P \cdot \left(\frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}
$$

### **Case B: Given C and Life → Find Permissible Load (P)**

$$
P_{allowable} = \frac{C}{\left(\frac{60 \cdot n \cdot L_{10h}}{10^6}\right)^{1/p}}
$$

### **Case C: Given C and Load → Find Achievable Life**

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{10^6}{60 \cdot n} \cdot \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p
$$

> These three forms are **the most common KTU Part B problem templates**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Solution: Case C Example

> **Problem:** A cylindrical roller bearing has **C = 80,000 N**, supports **P = 20,000 N**, and operates at **n = 1000 rpm**. Find the life in hours.

### **Solution:**

For roller bearing, **p = 10/3**

$$
L_{10m} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} = \left(\frac{80{,}000}{20{,}000}\right)^{10/3}
$$

$$
L_{10m} = (4)^{10/3}
$$

$$
L_{10m} = 4^{10/3} = (2^2)^{10/3} = 2^{20/3} = 2^{6.6667}
$$

$$
2^{6.6667} = 2^6 \times 2^{0.6667} = 64 \times 1.5874 \approx 101.6
$$

So **L₁₀ₘ ≈ 101.6 million revolutions**

Now convert to hours:

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{10^6 \times L_{10m}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{10^6 \times 101.6}{60 \times 1000}
$$

$$
L_{10h} = \frac{1.016 \times 10^8}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
L_{10h} \approx 1693 \text{ hours}
$$

> The bearing will achieve a 90% reliability life of **~1693 hours** at the given operating conditions.
