# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"State the constructional features of a needle bearing and list two situations where it is preferred over a deep-groove ball bearing."**

**Model Answer:**

A needle bearing consists of **long, slender cylindrical rollers (needles)** with a length-to-diameter ratio between **3:1 and 10:1**, held in position by either a **cage** (caged type) or in direct contact (full-complement type). The needles run parallel to the shaft axis, providing **line contact** with the inner and outer raceways. There are three principal configurations: **drawn-cup type** (no inner ring, shaft acts as inner race), **machined-ring type** (precision inner and outer rings), and **full-complement type** (no cage, maximum needle density).

**Two situations where needle bearings are preferred:**

1. **Limited radial space with high radial load:** In gearboxes and connecting rods, the radial envelope is constrained but the radial load is high. A needle bearing of the same bore diameter has a **60–80 % higher load capacity** than a deep-groove ball bearing of the same size, because line contact distributes stress over a larger area than point contact.

2. **Hardened-shaft applications where a separate inner ring is undesirable:** In drawn-cup needle bearings, the shaft itself is the inner raceway after appropriate hardening (≥ 60 HRC) and grinding. This reduces the number of components, cost, and axial length — for example, rocker-arm pivots in automotive engines.

*(Examiner credit: 1 mark for construction, 1 mark for each of two situations = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"Define equivalent dynamic radial load and equivalent static radial load for a rolling-contact bearing. Why is the static safety factor important for needle bearings?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **equivalent dynamic radial load** $P$ is defined as that constant radial load (purely radial, no axial component) which, if applied to the bearing, would give the same rating life as the *actual* combined radial and thrust load the bearing experiences. Mathematically:

$$ P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a $$

where $X$ and $Y$ are the radial and thrust factors obtained from the manufacturer's catalogue. For needle bearings supporting purely radial load ($F_a = 0$), $P = F_r$.

The **equivalent static radial load** $P_0$ is similarly defined for *static* load conditions:

$$ P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a $$

It is the load used to check whether the bearing can safely support the applied load **without permanent brinelling** (indentation) exceeding the elastic limit of the raceway material.

**Importance of static safety factor for needle bearings:**

The **static safety factor** is $f_s = C_0 / P_0$. It is critical for needle bearings because:
- Line contact produces **higher peak contact pressure** than point contact for a given static load.
- Needle rollers are long and slender, so **edge loading** (a small axial misalignment) dramatically increases the local stress.
- Recommended values: $f_s \geq 1.5$ for smooth operation, $\geq 2.0$ for shock loads. Without adequate $f_s$, brinelling occurs at standstill (e.g., during shipment or pre-load), permanently degrading the bearing.

*(Examiner credit: 1 mark each for $P$ and $P_0$ definitions, 1 mark for the safety-factor justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Needle Bearing Problems**
> 1. Students often **confuse the life exponent**: use $p = 3$ (ball) instead of $p = 10/3$ (needle/roller) — *loses 2 marks*.
> 2. Many forget to **convert revolutions → hours** correctly; the factor is $60 \times n$, not $60 / n$.
> 3. In the modified life equation, omitting $a_1$ (reliability factor) and $a_{ISO}$ (lubrication factor) forfeits the **3-mark** ISO 281 sub-part.
> 4. The unit of $C$ must be **N or kN consistently**; mixing N and kN in a single equation is a **valuation killer**.
> 5. Always state the **assumed lubrication regime** (oil bath, drip, grease) before quoting the limiting speed.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`

**A full-complement needle bearing is required for the output shaft of an industrial gearbox. The operating conditions are:**

- Radial load on bearing: $F_r = 12\,\text{kN}$
- Axial load on bearing: $F_a = 1.5\,\text{kN}$
- Shaft speed: $n = 800\,\text{rpm}$
- Required minimum life: $L_{10h,req} = 8\,000\,\text{hours}$
- Reliability target: 95 %
- Lubrication: oil-bath, clean, normal temperature
- Catalogue factors for the chosen series: $X = 0.56$, $Y = 1.45$ (for $F_a / F_r \leq 0.25$), $X_0 = 0.6$, $Y_0 = 1.0$

**(a)** Calculate the **equivalent dynamic radial load** $P$ and determine the **minimum required dynamic load rating** $C_{min}$ for the bearing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The manufacturer offers a needle bearing with $C = 35\,\text{kN}$ and $C_0 = 41\,\text{kN}$. Verify (i) whether the basic rating life exceeds the requirement, and (ii) the **static safety factor** $f_s$. Comment on the design's suitability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Equivalent Load and Minimum C**

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio $F_a / F_r$ to confirm the $X, Y$ factors are applicable.

$$ \frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{1.5}{12} = 0.125 $$

Since $0.125 \leq 0.25$, the given factors $X = 0.56$ and $Y = 1.45$ are valid. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Compute the equivalent dynamic radial load $P$.

$$ P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a = (0.56)(12) + (1.45)(1.5) $$

$$ P = 6.72 + 2.175 = 8.895\,\text{kN} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Use the ISO 281 rating life equation to find $C_{min}$. Rearrange for $C$:

$$ L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \, n} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} $$

$$ 8\,000 = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 800} \left(\frac{C}{8.895}\right)^{10/3} $$

Compute the speed-life factor:

$$ \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 800} = \frac{10^{6}}{48\,000} = 20.833 \text{ hours} $$

Therefore:

$$ 8\,000 = 20.833 \times \left(\frac{C}{8.895}\right)^{10/3} $$

$$ \left(\frac{C}{8.895}\right)^{10/3} = \frac{8\,000}{20.833} = 384.0 $$

$$ \frac{C}{8.895} = (384.0)^{3/10} = 384.0^{0.3} $$

Compute:

$$ 384.0^{0.3} = \exp(0.3 \times \ln 384.0) = \exp(0.3 \times 5.9506) = \exp(1.7852) \approx 5.961 $$

$$ C_{min} = 5.961 \times 8.895 = 53.02\,\text{kN} $$

**Rounded up to the next catalogue size: $C_{min} \approx 54\,\text{kN}$**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4:** **Modified life factors** — for 95 % reliability, $a_1 = 1.62$ (note: ISO 281 : 2007 uses $a_1 > 1$ for *higher* than 90 % reliability; 95 % → $a_1 \approx 1.62$). For clean oil-bath lubrication at moderate speed, $a_{ISO} \approx 1.0$. Therefore:

$$ C_{min,\text{modified}} = C_{min} \times (a_1 \cdot a_{ISO})^{3/10} = 53.02 \times (1.62)^{0.3} $$

$$ (1.62)^{0.3} = \exp(0.3 \times \ln 1.62) = \exp(0.3 \times 0.4824) = \exp(0.1447) \approx 1.156 $$

$$ C_{min,\text{modified}} = 53.02 \times 1.156 \approx 61.3\,\text{kN} $$

**Thus the bearing must be selected for at least $C = 61.3\,\text{kN}$ to meet 8 000 hours at 95 % reliability.** **(2 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Verification of $C = 35\,\text{kN}$ Bearing**

**Step 5:** Calculate the achieved basic rating life with the offered $C = 35\,\text{kN}$.

$$ L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 800} \left(\frac{35}{8.895}\right)^{10/3} $$

$$ \frac{35}{8.895} = 3.935 $$

$$ (3.935)^{10/3} = \exp\!\left(\frac{10}{3}\ln 3.935\right) = \exp(3.3333 \times 1.3694) = \exp(4.5646) \approx 96.04 $$

$$ L_{10h} = 20.833 \times 96.04 \approx 2\,001 \text{ hours} $$

Since $2\,001\,\text{h} \ll 8\,000\,\text{h}$, the **basic rating life requirement is NOT met**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 6:** Compute the static safety factor.

$$ P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a = (0.6)(12) + (1.0)(1.5) = 7.2 + 1.5 = 8.7\,\text{kN} $$

$$ f_s = \frac{C_0}{P_0} = \frac{41}{8.7} \approx 4.71 $$

For smooth gearbox operation, $f_s \geq 1.5$ — so the **static safety criterion IS satisfied**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 7:** **Design Comment (3 marks):**

- The static safety is excellent ($f_s = 4.71 \gg 1.5$), so the bearing will not brinell.
- However, the dynamic life is **only 25 %** of the 8 000-hour requirement — the bearing will fail by rolling-contact fatigue after roughly **83 days** of continuous 24/7 operation.
- The 95 % reliability modification worsens this further.
- **Recommendation:** Select a needle bearing with $C \geq 62\,\text{kN}$ (next standard size above the calculated minimum) to meet the life and reliability targets. If a smaller bearing must be used, the radial load must be reduced to $\leq 6.3\,\text{kN}$ or the speed lowered — neither is desirable for the gearbox.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**A drawn-cup needle bearing (HK-2010 type) is selected for the rocker-arm pivot of an internal combustion engine. Catalogue data:**

- Dynamic load rating: $C = 11.2\,\text{kN}$
- Static load rating: $C_0 = 16.5\,\text{kN}$
- Effective needle length: $l_{ef} = 10\,\text{mm}$
- Needle diameter: $D_w = 3.5\,\text{mm}$
- Modulus of elasticity of raceway steel: $E = 2.07 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$
- Poisson's ratio: $\nu = 0.3$

**Operating conditions:**

- Radial load: $F_r = 3.0\,\text{kN}$ (purely radial, oscillating)
- Rotational speed of cam follower: $n = 600\,\text{rpm}$
- Required life: $L_{10h,req} = 5\,000\,\text{hours}$
- Lubrication: drip-feed oil, ambient temperature 60 °C

**(a)** Compute (i) the **Hertzian peak contact pressure** $p_0$ between a single needle and the outer-race raceway, assuming the load is uniformly distributed over all 14 needles. **(ii) Comment on whether this pressure is within the typical yield-safe range for bearing steel (σ_y ≈ 2 000 N/mm²). (7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify whether the bearing meets the **5 000-hour life requirement** with a reliability of 90 % under the given load. If not, suggest a design change. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Hertzian Peak Contact Pressure**

**Step 1:** Compute the **line load** $Q$ on a single needle. If load is uniformly distributed over 14 needles and the load is purely radial (axial force on each needle = 0):

$$ F_{\text{per needle}} = \frac{F_r}{14} = \frac{3\,000}{14} = 214.29\,\text{N} $$

The **line load** (force per unit effective length):

$$ Q = \frac{F_{\text{per needle}}}{l_{ef}} = \frac{214.29}{10} = 21.43\,\text{N/mm} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Compute the **effective modulus** $E'$ for identical steel-on-steel contact:

$$ E' = \frac{E}{2(1-\nu^{2})} = \frac{2.07 \times 10^{5}}{2(1-0.3^{2})} = \frac{2.07 \times 10^{5}}{2 \times 0.91} = \frac{2.07 \times 10^{5}}{1.82} \approx 1.1374 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2} $$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Compute the **effective radius** $r$ for a cylindrical roller (needle) against a flat raceway:

$$ r = \frac{D_w}{2} = \frac{3.5}{2} = 1.75\,\text{mm} $$

**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 4:** Apply the **Hertzian line-contact peak pressure** formula:

$$ p_0 = 0.564 \sqrt{\frac{Q \cdot E'}{l_{ef} \cdot r}} $$

Substitute:

$$ p_0 = 0.564 \sqrt{\frac{21.43 \times 1.1374 \times 10^{5}}{10 \times 1.75}} $$

$$ p_0 = 0.564 \sqrt{\frac{21.43 \times 1.1374 \times 10^{5}}{17.5}} $$

Compute the numerator:

$$ 21.43 \times 1.1374 \times 10^{5} = 2.4374 \times 10^{6} $$

Divide by denominator:

$$ \frac{2.4374 \times 10^{6}}{17.5} = 1.3928 \times 10^{5} $$

Take the square root:

$$ \sqrt{1.3928 \times 10^{5}} = \sqrt{139\,280} \approx 373.2 $$

Multiply by 0.564:

$$ p_0 = 0.564 \times 373.2 \approx 210.5\,\text{N/mm}^{2} $$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 5:** **Comment on yield safety (1.5 marks):**

The peak Hertzian pressure $p_0 \approx 210.5\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ is well **below the yield strength** of bearing steel ($\sigma_y \approx 2\,000\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$). The safety factor against yielding is approximately:

$$ n_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{p_0} = \frac{2\,000}{210.5} \approx 9.5 $$

This is **excellent** — the contact is well within the elastic regime, so there is no risk of immediate plastic deformation. Note that this is a *static* Hertzian check; for *dynamic* fatigue, the relevant criterion is the rolling-contact fatigue limit, typically $p_{0,\text{fatigue}} \approx 1\,500\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ for through-hardened steel, which is also satisfied comfortably.

---

**Part (b) — Life Verification and Design Recommendation**

**Step 6:** Since the load is purely radial, the equivalent dynamic load equals the radial load:

$$ P = F_r = 3.0\,\text{kN} $$

**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 7:** Apply the ISO 281 rating life equation for needle bearings ($p = 10/3$):

$$ L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \, n} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3} $$

$$ L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{60 \times 600} \left(\frac{11.2}{3.0}\right)^{10/3} $$

$$ L_{10h} = \frac{10^{6}}{36\,000} \times (3.7333)^{10/3} $$

$$ L_{10h} = 27.778 \times (3.7333)^{3.3333} $$

Compute the exponent:

$$ (3.7333)^{10/3} = \exp\!\left(\frac{10}{3}\ln 3.7333\right) = \exp(3.3333 \times 1.3170) = \exp(4.3901) \approx 80.79 $$

$$ L_{10h} = 27.778 \times 80.79 \approx 2\,244\,\text{hours} $$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 8:** **Apply reliability correction** (90 % reliability ⇒ $a_1 = 1.00$, so no change). However, the elevated temperature of 60 °C and the dripping lubrication suggest $a_{ISO} \approx 0.8$ to 0.9. For conservative KTU valuation, take $a_{ISO} = 0.85$:

$$ L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10h} = 1.00 \times 0.85 \times 2\,244 = 1\,907\,\text{hours} $$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 9:** **Comparison and recommendation (2 marks):**

- $L_{nm} = 1\,907\,\text{hours} < L_{10h,req} = 5\,000\,\text{hours}$ — **the bearing fails the life requirement**.
- The life ratio: $5\,000 / 1\,907 \approx 2.62$ — life is **only 38 %** of the requirement.
- **Design options:**
  1. **Increase the bearing size:** A bearing with $C_{new}$ such that $(C_{new}/3.0)^{10/3} = 2.62 \times (11.2/3.0)^{10/3}$ — solve:

$$ C_{new} = 3.0 \times \left[2.62 \times (3.7333)^{10/3}\right]^{3/10} = 3.0 \times [2.62 \times 80.79]^{0.3} = 3.0 \times [211.7]^{0.3} $$

$$ (211.7)^{0.3} = \exp(0.3 \times \ln 211.7) = \exp(0.3 \times 5.3538) = \exp(1.6061) \approx 4.984 $$

$$ C_{new} \approx 3.0 \times 4.984 = 14.95\,\text{kN} $$

Select the **next catalogue size** with $C \geq 15\,\text{kN}$ (e.g., HK-2515 or HK-2520).

  2. **Reduce the load** by relocating the cam-contact point closer to the pivot, halving the moment-arm. This roughly halves the radial load, but typically requires a major design change.
  3. **Improve lubrication** to raise $a_{ISO}$ above 1.0 — but realistic maximum for drip-feed at 60 °C is $a_{ISO} \approx 1.0$. The other options (oil-mist, forced circulation) are impractical for a rocker arm.

- **Conclusion:** The most practical recommendation is **upsizing to HK-2515** ($C \approx 15.4\,\text{kN}$), which restores the 5 000-hour life with a comfortable safety margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Needle Bearings (Module 4)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Rolling-element bearing with cylindrical rollers of L/D ratio **3:1 to 10:1**, providing **line contact** (not point contact like balls).
- ☐ **Construction variants:** Drawn-cup (no inner ring), machined-ring (both rings), full-complement (no cage), thrust, combined.
- ☐ **Life exponent:** Always use **$p = 10/3$** for needle bearings (line contact). Confusing with $p = 3$ (ball) is the #1 mark-loser.
- ☐ **Rating life equation:** $L_{10h} = \dfrac{10^{6}}{60\,n}\!\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3}$; in millions of revolutions: $L_{10} = 10^{6}\!\left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3}$.
- ☐ **Modified life (ISO 281 : 2007):** $L_{nm} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$; $a_1 > 1$ for reliability **above** 90 %, $a_1 < 1$ for reliability **below** 90 %.
- ☐ **Equivalent load:** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ (dynamic); $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ (static).
- ☐ **Static safety factor:** $f_s = C_0 / P_0 \geq 1.5$ smooth, $\geq 2.0$ shock; needle bearings are **more sensitive** than ball bearings because of line-contact edge stresses.
- ☐ **Hertzian peak pressure (line contact):** $p_0 = 0.564 \sqrt{\dfrac{Q E'}{l_{ef} r}}$ — proportional to $\sqrt{Q}$, *not* $Q$.
- ☐ **Hertzian half-contact-width:** $b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 Q r}{\pi \, l_{ef} E'}}$.
- ☐ **Speed limit indicator:** $n \cdot D_m$ (full-complement limit $\approx 2.5 \times 10^{5}$ to $4 \times 10^{5}$ mm·rpm); caged bearings run faster.
- ☐ **Advantages:** Highest radial load capacity per unit radial space; low friction; self-contained (drawn-cup type).
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** Low limiting speed; sensitive to misalignment (edge loading); higher starting torque in full-complement type; not suited to thrust load alone.
- ☐ **Key applications:** Gearbox pinions, rocker-arm pivots, connecting-rod big ends, universal joints, hydraulic pumps, helicopter rotor masts.
- ☐ **Always state:** Lubrication regime, reliability target, operating temperature — these govern $a_{ISO}$ and hence the final design verdict.
- ☐ **Sanity check:** If $L_{10h}$ computed is less than half the required, **upsize** the bearing rather than tweaking lubrication — much safer mark in KTU valuation.
